Name: Meadow Management at Adelaide Local Nature Reserve
Description: Join in for a session of nature conservation and habitat management activities. The Green Gym is a unique way to get fit, transform the environment, meet new people and learn new skills. Each session includes a range of activities, designed to suit all abilities. We provide hot drinks and other refreshments during the tea break.

Name: Capenhurst Meadow Management
Description: Cutting the sward and raking off the arisings to be composted. Timber compost bays to be built. Cutting back encroaching scrub. Scarification and/or harrowing of ground in preparation for hand seeding and planting of wildflowers.

Name: Hastings Biodiversity Action Team
Description:
If you love being outside and active, if you enjoy nature and want to help the environment, if you like being part of a team and making a difference in your area, why don’t you join the Biodiversity Action Team?

They have conservation activities every weekday, mainly around the Hastings, Rother and Wealden area. There’s a range of tasks, such as woodland management, pond maintenance, footpath clearance and litter picking… all helping to make our local green spaces nicer for everyone, to improve the environment, and to help you to connect with nature and boost health and wellbeing.

Sessions are fun, free and friendly, and tasks can be adapted for different levels of ability or knowledge. You don’t need to have previous experience and you don’t need to bring anything, just wear rough clothing and sturdy footwear. They’ll bring the tools and equipment, the gloves and the guidance, and there will be support and training for each type of activity. And free tea and coffee!

Booking required so they can make sure they have enough equipment, please contact the relevant Project Officer.

Name: Lower Hampen Farm
Description:
Lower Hampen Farm sits on 330 acres in the Cotswold AONB near Cheltenham. After ash dieback affected veteran trees, the project is replanting ten acres of mixed native woodland, restoring historic limestone-wall hedgerows and sowing wildflower meadows to boost pollinator habitat, while grassland management focuses on traditional cattle grazing to improve soil health and wildlife diversity.

Protect Earth have partnered with 30x30 UK to provide volunteer days for teams around the UK. Typical activities include tree planting, woodland maintenance, invasive species removal, and leaky dam building. Let us know if you have a preference and we’ll do our best to accommodate it. Pricing includes tea and biscuits throughout the day, and covers our management costs and additional workers in case the work on the day is only partially complete.

Name: Donkeywell Farm
Description:
Donkeywell Farm in Quenington is transforming arable fields into a network of native woodland corridors and flowering shelterbelts. Since January 2021, over 1,300 trees have been planted—hawthorn, hazel and blackthorn—and sections of existing hedgerow have been restored. Managed grazing by rare cattle breeds improves soil structure, while wildflower margins and scrub clearance create new habitat for birds, insects and small mammals.

Protect Earth have partnered with 30x30 UK to provide volunteer days for teams around the UK. Name: Dyrham Wood
Description:
Dyrham is a beautiful, diverse, historic stretch of land. From Dyrham Park on the west boundary, to Dyrham Woods, this project is filling in the gaps on a large farm between the two. Hikers familiar with the Cotswold Way might recognize it, as the path travels through this farm north to south, and another public footpath intersects it east to west, making this project a treat for future generations of hikers. The land has been farmed since Medieval times, featuring lynchets—old hand-dug terraces where different families would each own their own terraced ridge and plow it to grow crops since the 13th century.

Currently the land being planted is used for grazing by horses and cows, and for organic crop growing to feed the livestock. The initial phase focuses on joining up patches of woodland to create habitat corridors, and creating shelter from strong winds coming across the Avon valley, which will improve crop yields and make life a little more pleasant for the animals.

Name: Wadswick Green
Description:
Wadswick Green is a 20-year-old planted woodland set just a stone’s throw from an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. We were brought in to replace dying ash with a more resilient mix of local native species, including hornbeam, silver birch, field maple and oak, to bolster ecosystem health. Our work this season focuses on diversifying the understorey with hawthorn, blackthorn, hazel and guelder rose to support birds and small mammals and to build long-term resilience.

Name: Ickleford Mini Forest
Description:
The sites for the forest lie on a thin strip of greenbelt between Ickleford village and Hitchin’s industrial area. The landowner has opened the space up to the public and is improving facilities for access, including new allotments, orchards, cycle paths and dog-walking routes. A disused railway runs through the site with a thin strip of wild woodland; two of our planting areas lie alongside it to deepen and enrich the habitat. A chalk stream flows through the property in good condition, and local residents frequently use the space for walks, welcoming the Dream For Trees plan to add more native woodland.

Name: Hazeland Wood
Description:
In partnership with Avon Needs Trees, we have added 1,000 native trees to a 34-acre valley in the Marden Valley between Chippenham and Calne. This high-catchment planting helps protect downstream communities from flooding, benefiting towns including Melksham, Bradford-on-Avon, Bath and Bristol. The first phase used cardboard guards from Grown Green, and this season we trialled Tubex Nature Guards. We worked with local universities and the Woodland Trust to test mulching techniques and guard types for optimal survival.

Name: Pantpurlais
Description:
Pantpurlais lies in the heart of Powys, Wales, on the slopes above the River Wye. This site restores native woodland on former sheep pasture, planting predominantly oak, rowan and birch to reconnect fragmented oakwoods and enrich the understory with hazel, holly and spindle. Positioned within walking distance of Llandrindod Wells, the new woodland will capture mist and rain, reduce downstream flooding and provide habitat for red squirrels and woodland birds.

Description:
Caberston lies in the Scottish Borders above the River Tweed. This project enriches mixed conifer plantations by felling non-native spruce and opening up glades for natural regeneration of birch and rowan. Woodland rides are being widened and cleared to let light in, boosting populations of woodland butterflies, moths and ground flora.

Name: Hawling
Description:
Hawling sits in the North Cotswolds AONB near Cheltenham. This ancient mosaic of grassland, ash woodland and limestone outcrops is being buffered with new native hedgerows, whip planting and strips of wildflower seed to improve insect corridors. Over 2,000 hedge whips—hawthorn, blackthorn and field maple—will knit together isolated copses, while improved grazing management will open the woodland floor to primroses, wood anemone and ferns.

Name: Highchurch Farm
Description:
Highchurch Farm sits on reclaimed coal-mining land near Radstock, Somerset. Our team is transforming arable fields and gorse scrub into acid-loving woodland, mixing silver birch, downy birch and scots pine. Deer-proof guards and soil-improvement techniques ensure young trees establish quickly. Over time this new woodland will filter water heading into the River Avon, provide carbon capture and link to existing Ancient Semi-Natural Woodland nearby.

Name: High Wood
Description:
High Wood lies on the fringes of Bodmin Moor in Cornwall. This Rare Atlantic Rainforest remnant is being enlarged through native laurel, holly and oak planting on adjoining pasture. We’re also opening the canopy in older wood to let sunlight reach the forest floor, boosting mosses, lichens and ferns. The work strengthens connectivity between ancient oak stands and peat-bog habitat, improving resilience to climate extremes.

Name: Wraxall
Description:
Wraxall sits on shallow soils above the River Avon near Bristol. This project restores ancient woodland fragments by removing non-native laurel and maple, then planting native species such as beech, hornbeam and hazel. Volunteers help protect young trees with guards and spread local wildflower seed, creating nectar sources for bees and butterflies. Over time the restored woodland will link existing copses, aiding dormouse and dormouse corridors.

Protect Earth have partnered with 30x30 UK to provide volunteer days for teams around the UK. Name: Bubbenhall
Description:
Bubbenhall lies on the edge of the River Avon floodplain near Coventry. This project enlarges remnant floodplain oakwood by planting pedunculate oak, alder and willow. Volunteers help clear bramble and ivy in glades, then plant whips in clusters to mimic natural regeneration patterns. The expanded woodland will slow floodwaters, filter agricultural runoff and provide nesting sites for warblers and woodpecker.

Name: Howard Court
Description:
Howard Court sits on an old ironstone quarry edge near Great Ayton. This project is transforming steep spoil heaps into acid-loving woodland, planting downy birch and rowan to stabilise the slopes. Volunteers help build stone-lined bunds to slow surface water, letting moisture sink in and preventing downstream flash floods. Over time the new woodland will anchor soils, enrich soil microbes and create foraging habitat for woodcock and red squirrel.

Name: Burnsall
Description:
Burnsall sits in the golden limestone dale of North Yorkshire, overlooking the River Wharfe. This project is re-establishing ancient ash–oak woodland on historic pasture, using local seed sources to ensure genetic match. Volunteers help clear invasive rhododendron and plant native saplings beneath veteran oaks, creating a multi-layered canopy that will shelter woodland flora and mosses and support returning populations of pied flycatcher and redstart.

Name: Grove Farm
Description:
Grove Farm lies on south-facing slopes outside Corsham. Once an arable field, it’s now transitioning to native mixed woodland and wildflower margins. Teams plant silver birch, oak and hawthorn along new hedgerows, then sow wildfoxgloves, knapweed and oxeye daisy to bolster pollinators. The restored hedges also screen the fields from prevailing winds, reducing soil erosion and creating safe corridors for hedgehogs and bats.

Description:
Guiting Manor Farm lies beneath the limestone escarpment of the Windrush Valley. This project opens up veteran ash woodland by coppicing and planting hornbeam, silver birch and alder to increase structural diversity. Volunteers help clear ivy, plant whips in small groups and build deadwood habitats to support saproxylic beetles and woodpeckers.

Description:
Stainswick Farm occupies chalk downland overlooking the River Thames near Shrivenham. Here we’re creating a new beech–scots pine wood on former arable fields, using local whip stock to ensure provenance. Volunteers assist with laying dead hedges and planting clusters of native shrubs—hawthorn, blackthorn and spindle—to provide nesting sites for willow warbler and yellowhammer.

Description:

Volunteers help remove scrub, plant saplings in staggered patterns and weave live willow hurdles to provide shelter. The revived hedgerow will link remnant copses and support farmland birds and small mammals.

This project enhances existing ash–oak woodland along the River Windrush, replacing non-native sycamore with native alder, field maple and hazel. Volunteers help create glades to increase light levels for bluebell carpets and plant sedges and rushes at the woodland edge to filter river runoff.

Description:

This project restores a field corner to ancient meadow, sowing a locally sourced wildflower mix rich in oxeye daisy, meadow buttercup and knapweed. Volunteers help mulch and seed the area, then install small log piles and bee bricks to create insect habitat and encourage ground-nesting birds.

Volunteers assist with felling non-native spruce and planting pedunculate oak, silver birch and rowan. The new woodland will improve landscape connectivity and provide feeding sites for wintering thrushes and woodcock.

Volunteers help remove bramble in glades, then plant clusters of field maple, hornbeam and blackthorn to boost structural diversity and insect habitat.

Volunteers help scarify ground and sow wildflower seed mixes containing horseshoe vetch, salad burnet and pyramidal orchid. The new meadow will support brimstone butterflies and nearly 30 other chalk grassland specialists.

Description:
Honeydale Farm is restoring a former hay meadow to traditional floodplain grassland. Volunteers help spread green hay from a donor site rich in meadow foxtail, meadow foxtail, yellow rattle and ragged robin. By reintroducing local seed, the meadow will support skylarks, lapwings and a range of specialist pollinators once again.

Volunteers help dig planting pits, install tree guards and build small bunds to retain water. Over time the new wet wood will filter river water and create habitat for kingfishers and amphibians.

Volunteers assist with planting whips in irregular clusters, building rabbit guards and laying dead hedges to define new glades. Over time the woodland will mature into habitat for tawny owls, dormice and bluebell carpets each spring.

This project opens up rides, fells conifers and plants native species such as oak, silver birch and rowan. Volunteers help water young trees, clear competing bramble and install bat boxes to increase habitat diversity for birds and bats.

Volunteers assist with rotavation, seeding with yellow rattle and meadow foxtail, and installing simple wooden water bars to retain surface water. Within a season the site will hum with sedge warblers and reed buntings as moisture-loving plants take hold.

Volunteers spread green hay from a local donor meadow rich in devil’s-bit scabious and field scabious, then rake and roll to embed the seed. The restored meadow will support skylarks, harvest mice and a colorful display of wildflowers each midsummer.

Volunteers help plant native alder, willow and hazel along eroded banks, weaving branches into living fascines to reduce scour. As the trees establish, the new woodland will filter runoff, improve water quality and create bank cover for otters and kingfishers.

Volunteers plant hawthorn, spindle and blackthorn whips, then sow wild carrot and oxeye daisy along the edge. Over time these linear habitats will offer nesting sites for yellowhammers and corridors for hedgehogs to roam safely.

Description:
Woodlea is a riverside meadow on the Kennet near Newbury where we are restoring floodplain wet grassland. Volunteers help rotavate and seed the area with sedges, rushes and meadow foxtail to trap silt and improve riverbank stability. In late summer the new meadow will shimmer with marsh marigold and ragged robin, supplying pollen and nectar to bees and hoverflies.

Volunteers plant hawthorn, spindle and hazel whips, then sow meadow vetchling and red clover. Within a few seasons these linear habitats will bloom with cow parsley and provide nesting cover for yellowhammers.

Description:
Pengover Farm comprises mixed pasture and ancient hedgerows near Liskeard. Volunteers help plant native saplings—oak, rowan and silver birch—in carefully prepared pits. They install tree guards, weave protective hedging and clear invasive laurel to let light reach young growth. Over time this will regenerate old hedgerows into bushy wildlife corridors for birds and small mammals.

Protect Earth have partnered with 30x30 UK to provide volunteer days for teams around the UK. Typical activities include tree planting, woodland maintenance, invasive species removal, and leaky dam building. Let us know if you have a preference and we’ll do our best to accommodate it. Pricing includes tea and biscuits throughout the day, and covers our management costs and additional workers in case the work on the day is only partially complete.

Please note the photo is from a different project while we wait for one from the project.

Name: Hutton Rudby
Description:
Hutton Rudby project restores a wildflower meadow on the edge of Stokesley. Volunteers spread green hay from a nearby species-rich field, then rake and roll seed into the soil. They cut and remove rush clumps to reduce competition and install simple wooden bug hotels. Next summer the meadow will burst with oxeye daisy, lady’s bedstraw and buzzing pollinators like bumblebees.

Name: South Lodge
Description:
South Lodge project enhances a small woodland on sandy soils near Horsham. Volunteers help clear invasive rhododendron and laurel, then plant native birch, oak and wild cherry into the understory. They install bird boxes and batt boxes to encourage hole-nesting species. As the wood regenerates, it will support woodland butterflies and nesting warblers in summer.

Name: Haydon Bridge
Description:
Haydon Bridge sits on the River South Tyne floodplain near Hexham. This project reconnects old river channels by digging shallow scrapes and planting wetland sedges. Volunteers build simple weirs from brushwood to hold water and create puddling zones. Once established, the new wetlands will host amphibians, water voles and wintering waders along quiet backwaters.

Name: Rhydwen
Description:
Rhydwen near Cardigan in Ceredigion is rolling pasture with remnant oak copses. Volunteers help plant wood pasture saplings—oak, ash and hazel—in veteran tree glades, then lay hazel hurdles to protect seedlings from grazing. As trees mature, this will become a restored wood pasture for dormice and woodpeckers to thrive.

Name: Hemyock
Description:
Hemyock project sits on sandstone uplands above Tiverton. Volunteers clear bramble and re-lay drystone walls to open glades, then plant acid-loving heathers and bilberry. They install reptile hibernacula from stone piles to boost amphibian shelter. Over time these heathland glades will support nightjars, adders and rare heath fritillary butterflies.

Name: Glebe farm
Description:
Glebe Farm near West Haddon in Northamptonshire features ancient limestone grassland. Volunteers sow green hay from a donor meadow rich in cowslip and autumn gentian, then scarify and press the seed in. They plant hedge whips of spindle and dogwood to create new boundary features. In spring the grassland will burst with yellow rattle and attract rare limestone-specialist bees.

Name: Newcastle Emlyn (Penralltffynnon)
Description:
Penralltffynnon is a farmed flood meadow on the River Teifi at Newcastle Emlyn in Ceredigion. Volunteers help dig shallow scrapes to reconnect backwaters, then plant sedge and rush plugs in wet hollows. These wetland features will provide spawning habitat for lamprey and shelter for overwintering wildfowl.

Name: Thorncombe Street
Description:
Thorncombe Street lies on chalk downland near Godalming in Surrey. Volunteers plant native chalk-hill flowers such as salad burnet and cowslip in micro-reserves carved from former arable strips, then scrape and seed areas to expose bare chalk. Over time these patches will bloom with orchids and support wild butterflies like the green hairstreak and dingy skipper.

Name: Hardknott Forest
Description:
Hardknott Forest in the Lake District lies on steep fell sides where pines once dominated. Volunteers will excavate pits for native Scots pine and rowan, then protect each sapling with brushwood shelters to guard against sheep. Over time these young trees will knit together into a resilient upland forest habitat for capercaillie and red squirrels.

Name: Pennyhill Park
Description:
Pennyhill Park estate in Bagshot is restoring a former ornamental woodland. Volunteers plant native blaeberry and sessile oak beneath veteran limes, then clear invasive rhododendron to open rides. In a few seasons these rides will burst with wild orchids and herald returning nightingales.

Typical activities include tree planting, woodland maintenance, invasive species removal, and leaky dam building. Let us know if you have a preference and we’ll do our best to accommodate it. Pricing includes tea and biscuits throughout the day, and covers our management costs and additional workers in case the work on the day is only partially complete.

Name: Lainston
Description:
Lainston Hotel near Winchester has medieval parkland where veteran oaks need new saplings planted in their glades. Volunteers plant oak and wild cherry whips beneath ancient canopies, then fence off young trees from deer. Over the years this will secure the next generation of parkland giants and boost habitat for nuthatches and glowing bioluminescent fungi.

Protect Earth have partnered with 30x30 UK to provide volunteer days for teams around the UK. Typical activities include tree planting, woodland maintenance, invasive species removal, and leaky dam building. Let us know if you have a preference and we’ll do our best to accommodate it. Pricing includes tea and biscuits throughout the day, and covers our management costs and additional workers in case the work on the day is only partially complete.

Name: Swinburne Recreation Ground
Description:
Swinburne Recreation Ground in Hitchin is being transformed from mown grass into a wildflower-rich meadow. Volunteers help rotovate small trial plots, broadcast a diverse pollinator seed mix, then rake and roll in the seed. Within weeks the meadow will shimmer with oxeye daisy and knapweed, supporting bumblebees and meadow brown butterflies.

Protect Earth have partnered with 30x30 UK to provide volunteer days for teams around the UK. Typical activities include tree planting, woodland maintenance, invasive species removal, and leaky dam building. Let us know if you have a preference and we’ll do our best to accommodate it. Pricing includes tea and biscuits throughout the day, and covers our management costs and additional workers in case the work on the day is only partially complete.

Name: Wild Walsall
Description: An ambitious nature recovery project run by Birmingham and Black Country Wildlife Trust targeting 1% of land in the Walsall borough.

Name: Stump Up For Trees
Description: A community charity planting one million trees in the Brecon Beacons area of south-east Wales.

Name: Rothbury for Nature and the Nation
Description: Northumberland Wildlife Trust has partnered to buy and care for the 3,800-hectare Rothbury Estate and showcase nature recovery on a vast scale.

Name: Wild Woodbury
Description: A Dorset Wildlife Trust's community project rewilding 150 hectares near Bere Regis.

Name: Restoring Seagrass & Oysters to North West Scotland
Description: The Scottish charity, Seawilding, is working with communities to restore native oyster beds and seagrass meadows in Loch Craignish.

Name: Northey Island Saltmarsh Restoration
Description: Restoring lost saltmarsh on a remote island in Essex, cared for by the National Trust.

Name: Wilder Blean
Description: First UK re-introduction of the European Bison. As ecosystem engineers they play a vital role in nature's recovery.

Name: Heal Somerset
Description: Transforming a site in Somerset from nature-poor to nature-rich, creating an inspiring place for wildlife and people.

Name: Cambridge City Chalk Streams Project
Description: Restoring Cambridge's chalk streams into thriving, resilient ecosystems and reviving wildlife habitats.

Name: Northern Devon Natural Solutions
Description: Working with farmers and landowners to return rivers to their natural state, restore wildflower-rich grasslands, and create new areas of woodland in North Devon.

Name: Benshaw Moor
Description: Northumberland Wildlife Trust is restoring blanket bog, heath and woodland at Benshaw Moor to boost peatland habitats and upland biodiversity.

Name: Allestree Park Rewilding
Description: Derbyshire Wildlife Trust is transforming Allestree Park into a flagship urban rewilding site with wood pasture, wetlands and community engagement.

Name: Broughton Sanctuary Nature Recovery
Description: Broughton Sanctuary in Yorkshire is converting 400ha of parkland to wood pasture, floodplain meadow and native woodland for wildlife and wellbeing.

Name: Dundreggan Estate
Description: The 10,000‑acre Dundreggan Estate in Glenmoriston is Trees for Life’s pioneering rewilding site, expanding Caledonian pinewood, restoring peatlands and running a public rewilding centre for research and education.

Name: Boothby Wildland
Description: Nattergal is rewilding Boothby Graffoe estate in Lincolnshire, restoring wood pasture, wetlands and species-rich grassland for nature recovery.

Name: Eastern Moors Partnership
Description: A partnership of National Trust and RSPB managing 7,800 acres of moorland in the Peak District for waders, heathland and low-intensity grazing.

Name: South Arran MPA
Description: South Arran Marine Protected Area, Scotland’s first community‑led MPA, safeguards seagrass, maerl beds and nursery grounds around the Isle of Arran and bans bottom‑trawling to let marine life recover.

Name: The Grange Project
Description: The Grange Project is transforming former pasture into a diverse agroforestry and rewilding hub with orchards, wood pasture and community events in the Malvern Hills.

Name: Harold's Park Wildland
Description: Nattergal’s Harold’s Park estate on the Essex‑Hertfordshire border is transitioning from arable to dynamic wood‑pasture, wetlands and natural grazing for biodiversity credits and public engagement.

Name: Helman Tor Nature Reserve
Description: Cornwall Wildlife Trust is rewilding Helman Tor’s granite uplands and wetlands with beavers, semi‑free‑roaming cattle and ponies to create wood pasture and wetland mosaics.

Name: WildWood Living Landscape
Description: Norfolk Wildlife Trust’s WildWood project near Lenwade is recreating wood pasture, heath and wetlands and reconnecting people with nature through volunteering and education.

Name: Hepple Wilds
Description: Hepple Wilds in Northumberland is restoring hill‑ground heath, riparian woodland and juniper scrub alongside regenerative sheep farming and award‑winning gin distilling.

Name: Gilfach Nature Reserve
Description: Radnorshire Wildlife Trust’s Gilfach is a 410‑acre upland farm turned reserve, famous for salmon leaping, ancient oak woodland and hay meadows rich in orchids.

Name: Geltsdale
Description: RSPB Geltsdale in the North Pennines uplands combines blanket bog, hay meadows and heathland management to support hen harriers, black grouse and curlews.

Name: Hesketh Out Marsh
Description: RSPB Hesketh Out Marsh on the Ribble Estuary is one of the UK’s largest recent realignment projects, restoring saltmarsh for thousands of wintering waders and wildfowl.

Name: High Fen Wildland
Description: High Fen Wildland straddles the Suffolk‑Cambridgeshire claylands where Nattergal is restoring wood pasture, ponds and fen meadow for landscape‑scale biodiversity recovery and nature finance.

Name: Knepp Estate
Description: The pioneering 3,500‑acre rewilding project in West Sussex where free‑roaming livestock drive natural processes, supporting nightingales, turtle doves, purple emperor butterflies and thriving biodiverse wetlands.

Name: Mapperton Wildlands
Description: A 1,600‑acre rewilding initiative on the Mapperton Estate in West Dorset where White Park cattle, Exmoor ponies and beavers are helping to regenerate wood‑pasture, wetlands and species‑rich grassland.

Name: Steart Marshes
Description: WWT’s flagship 490‑ha managed realignment on the Severn Estuary transforming arable farmland into saltmarsh and freshwater wetlands that protect homes from flooding and host thousands of waterbirds.

Name: Lyscombe Farm
Description: Dorset Wildlife Trust’s 250‑ha working farm in the Purbeck Hills converting to nature‑friendly grazing, chalk grassland restoration and hedgerow expansion to boost rare orchids, butterflies and grey partridge.

Name: Moor Barton
Description: A 120‑acre regenerative farm and rewilding project on the edge of Dartmoor where agroforestry, pasture‑fed cattle and river restoration are bringing back wildlife and engaging volunteers.

Name: Purbeck Heaths NNR
Description: A 3,400‑ha ‘super reserve’ uniting RSPB Arne, National Trust and other partners to reconnect heathland, saltmarsh and dunes on Dorset’s Isle of Purbeck for nightjars, reptiles and pollinators.

Name: Sunart Fields
Description: Regenerative croft on the shores of Loch Sunart, Highlands, where native woodland planting, no‑dig veg and rare-breed cattle are restoring soil health and biodiversity while supplying the local community.

Name: Wicken Fen NNR
Description: National Trust’s oldest nature reserve – 880 ha of lowland fen, wet grassland and meres near Ely managed by Konik ponies and Highland cattle, home to more than 9,000 recorded species.

Name: Belmont Estate
Description: Historic 500‑ha estate in the Gordano Valley near Bristol transitioning to agroecology, wood‑pasture and wet meadow creation while offering nature education and venison from wild fallow deer.

Name: Borders Forest Trust
Description: Charity restoring native woodland at Carrifran Wildwood, Talla & Gameshope and Corehead in the Southern Uplands – over 7,000 acres of peatland, montane scrub and river habitat for eagles to return.

Name: Wild Ken Hill
Description: Norfolk coastal estate combining regenerative farming, rewilding and traditional conservation across 1,500 ha of heath, woodland, wetland and arable re‑sown with herbal leys.

Name: Wild Ingleborough
Description: Landscape‑scale WWF & Yorkshire Wildlife Trust project restoring 1,200 ha of limestone grassland, blanket bog and woodland around Ingleborough to link valley floor to mountain top.

Name: Creating Wilder Forests
Description: Forestry England’s national programme to restore wilder processes—natural regeneration, dynamic rivers, large herbivores—across the public forest estate’s 250,000 ha.

Name: Wilder Doddington
Description: Historic Lincoln estate dedicating 770 ha to scrub, wetland and low‑input grazing, with beaver release planned and community science mapping swift and barn‑owl recovery.

Name: Wild Tolworth
Description: Citizen Zoo project re‑imagining suburban green spaces along the Hogsmill River in Kingston, with beavers licensed in 2024 and community meadows boosting pollinators.

Name: Pentwyn Vision
Description: Radnorshire Wildlife Trust’s plan to restore 130 ha of upland pasture near Llanbister to species‑rich hay meadows, wood‑pasture and a haven for marsh fritillaries.

Name: Wild Ennerdale
Description: Partnership of Forestry England, National Trust & United Utilities letting natural processes shape 4,700 ha of valley woodland, river and lake fringes in the western Lake District.

Name: Wild Kielder
Description: Forestry England initiative restoring bogs, riparian woodland and montane scrub in 62,000‑ha Kielder Forest & Water Park, Northumberland, benefiting ospreys, redsquirrels & water voles.

Name: Merseyside Midweek Group
Description:
The Merseyside Midweek volunteer group operates across the Liverpool, Wirral, Cheshire West, Sefton and West Lancashire region. 

Hosted by The Conservation Volunteers, they organise a minibus at 9:30am in Liverpool City Centre every Tuesday, Wednesday and Thursday - driving from there to site to deliver conservation work in the local work.

Name: Practical Conservation Activity
Description: Tasks include Coppicing, bramble clearing, path clearing, boarding, clearing scrub to improve habitat

Name: The BUG - Green Gym
Description: The BUG Green Gym is designed to bring members of the community together to a beautiful green space. It provides opportunities to get outside, connect with nature, learn new skills, grow your own veg and make new friends.

Name: Countess Friday Conservation Session
Description: Here the group will be undertaking various conservation and management task around the park

Name: Port Sunlight River Park
Description: Various conservation tasks on and around the park concentrating on gardening and plant based activities mainly

Name: Glasgow Biodiversity Action Team
Description: Enhance the biodiversity of Glasgow’s many fantastic parks and greenspaces. Help care for the local woodlands and wildlife areas, plant trees and hedges, create wildflower meadows, clear invasive plant species, and much more.

Name: Newton Park West
Description: River management at Newton Park West, cutting scallops and clearing reedmace.

Name: Practical Conservation
Description: The work varies from season to season. In winter you could be tree planting, hedge laying, or creating and maintaining ponds and wetlands; whilst in summer you could be constructing wildlife areas in schools, creating footpaths, making hay, or undertaking invasive species control.

Name: Midweek Group Activities
Description: The activities vary and could include: - habitat management - countryside furniture construction - waterways and pond work - green skills like coppicing and hedge-laying.

Name: Practical Conservation Projects
Description: Transport provided from TCV Humber field office. No experience necessary. The work varies from season to season. In winter you could be tree planting, hedge laying, or creating and maintaining ponds and wetlands; whilst in summer you could be constructing wildlife areas in schools, creating footpaths, making hay, or undertaking invasive species control.

Name: Wildflower Organic Grassland Management
Description: Pre-season grassland preparation work, removing ragwort and thistle in readiness for the annual hay cut starting in August. This is a rewarding project for volunteers who are well looked after on this Anglian Water site, a good opportunity to enjoy the beautiful Rutland countryside.

Name: Forth Valley Biodiversity Action Team
Description: Undertake various practical conservation tasks and improve green spaces across Falkirk and Stirling. Activities include woodland management, pond clearance, maintaining paths and sowing wildflower seeds.

Name: Rhododendron cutting/clearance
Description: Cutting and clearance activities

Name: EPIC Volunteers
Description: Practical conservation activities across Clackmannanshire. looking after path network, making greenspaces better for wildlife through biodiversity improvements.

Name: Swadlincote Woods
Description:
Various outdoor practical conservation activities focused on improving the site for both wildlife and local people. 

Hosted by The Conservation Volunteers, the day runs from 10-3 with breaks in-between. (under 18s welcome by prior arrangement only).

Name: Milebrook Hall Community Garden
Description: Milebrook Hall provides space and community to easily grow food.All tools and gloves etc are provided for you.

Name: Horam Green Gym
Description: Various outdoor tasks, to improve health and the local environment, all based on the grounds of the crematorium. Tasks include general horticultural maintenance, and woodland maintenance

Name: Shoresearch Volunteer (East) for Kent Wildlife Trust
Description:
Shoresearch volunteer citizen scientists collect data across intertidal zones along the Kent coast which help to provide invaluable insights into the health of our coastal ecosystems and the species that inhabit them. Here in Kent, we utilise the intertidal rocky shore survey technique, owing to our internationally important chalk reefs. We carry out these surveys throughout the year all around Kent's magnificent coastline. Our main aim is to record the species we find along our coast (species presence surveys). We also carry out more detailed surveys looking at species abundance and invasive non-native species. The data we collect goes into The Wildlife Trust's National data base which can be used to inform marine conservation decisions. By collecting vital data, the project helps safeguard the UK’s coastal environments for future generations. 

Shoresearch surveys in Kent are run by Kent Wildlife Trust throughout the year, usually with one survey taking place a month. They are the perfect opportunity for anyone to get involved in the marine environment and start having fun identifying and recording the animals and algae communities found on our rocky shores. You are guaranteed to meet new and interesting people with a wealth of knowledge and experience!

Name: Kent Dolphin Landwatch
Description:
The Kent Dolphin Project, a collaborative effort between the Sussex Dolphin Project and Adonis Blue Environmental Consultants, part of the Kent Wildlife Trust Group, is working to better understand and protect whales, dolphins and porpoises (known collectively as cetaceans) in the county’s waters.

Without volunteers we would not be able to have significant effort and gather sufficient data to inform decision making.

What will you be doing?
As a volunteer, you will be taking part in Landwatch surveys. During Landwatch we monitor cetacean activity in a specific area
.

Name: Biodiversity Action Team - Woodland Trust
Description:
Help the East Sussex Biodiversity Action Team with woodland management.

33 acres of precious ancient woodland, newly planted native woodland, wide open rides and fabulous glimpses into the surrounding Wealden countryside. Ancient woodland species include bluebells, wood anemones and a wild service tree.

Pick up from their office on Fearon Road at 9.15am or meet on site about 10am.

Name: Conservation tasks with East Sussex Biodiversity Action Team
Description:
Help out for a day on an organic farm.
Owners David & Marian are keen conservationists and bird watchers. They have created 8acres of reedbed for the expressed purpose of encouraging Bitterns to nest. The site already has nesting Marsh Harriers and Snipe.

Name: Dungeness
Description: Big shingle peninsula with freshwater pits, wet grassland and wildflower meadows – flagship birding on Romney Marsh.

Name: Cliffe Pools
Description: Lagoons and pools on the Thames estuary with masses of water birds and big skies.

Name: Pulborough Brooks
Description: Arun Valley wetlands and wooded heath. Family friendly trails and hides with seasonal spectacles from nightingales and dragonflies to winter flocks of waders and ducks.

Name: Pagham Harbour LNR
Description: Sheltered inlet of saltmarsh, mudflats, lagoons and shingle beach south of Chichester. Waymarked routes from the visitor centre with year round coastal birding.

Name: Broadwater Warren
Description: Restored lowland heath and woodland on the High Weald ridge near Tunbridge Wells. All ability loop plus longer trails with chances of woodlark, nightjar and adders.

Name: Medmerry
Description: Managed realignment creating vast saltmarsh and pools behind the shingle bank. 10 km of level trails for walking and cycling with avocets, harriers and migrating waders.

Name: Blean Woods
Description: Ancient coppice woodland north of Canterbury with glades and heathy rides. A stronghold for the rare heath fritillary plus nightingales, woodpeckers and spring bluebells.

Name: Fore Wood
Description: Peaceful ghyll woodland at Crowhurst with steep, fern lined ravines and spring carpets of bluebells. Short waymarked trails through coppice, oak and hornbeam.

Name: White Cliffs of Dover
Description: Iconic chalk cliffs with coastal paths, wildlife-rich grassland and wartime tunnels, starting from the National Trust visitor centre east of Dover with sweeping Channel views.

Name: Chartwell
Description: Winston Churchill’s family home set in the Weald of Kent, with rooms preserved as he left them, hillside gardens, woodland trails and lakes offering views across the countryside.

Name: Scotney Castle
Description: Romantic moated 14th century castle ruins set within a picturesque garden and 19th century house, woodland and parkland on the Kent-Sussex border near Lamberhurst.

Name: Devil’s Dyke
Description: A spectacular dry valley on the South Downs with panoramic views, Iron Age ramparts and classic walking routes. Popular for picnics, kite-flying and paragliding.

Name: Emmetts Garden
Description: A hillside Edwardian garden famed for spring blossom, bluebells and far-reaching views over the Weald, with rock garden, wildflower meadows and family waymarked trails.

Name: Black Down
Description: The highest point in Sussex - an expanse of heath, woodland and sunken lanes above Haslemere with long vistas, wildlife and the Temple of the Winds viewpoint.

Name: Slindon Estate
Description: A 3,500-acre estate in the South Downs with ancient woodland, chalk downland, orchards and miles of tracks from the village, perfect for walking, cycling and wildlife.

Name: Petworth House and Park
Description: Grand Baroque house famed for its art and sculpture collections, set within Capability Brown’s deer park, with sweeping views, lakes and miles of waymarked paths.

Name: Sissinghurst Castle Garden
Description: World-famous garden created by Vita Sackville-West and Harold Nicolson, with intimate garden rooms, tower views, surrounding farmland and nature-rich estate in the High Weald.

Name: Standen House and Garden
Description: Arts and Crafts family home near East Grinstead with Morris & Co. interiors, hillside garden, woodlands and views over the Medway valley with year-round walks.

Name: Cissbury Ring
Description: One of Britain’s largest hillforts above Worthing with Neolithic flint mines, chalk grassland wildlife and far-reaching coastal and Wealden views along open downland.

Name: Smallhythe Place
Description: Former home of Victorian actor Ellen Terry near Tenterden, with intimate cottage rooms, rose-filled gardens, working theatre barn and exhibitions celebrating her stage career.

Name: Sheffield Park and Garden
Description: Historic landscape garden famed for autumn colour, lakes and vistas, linked to the wider estate and Bluebell Railway, with year-round walks and accessible routes.

Name: Knole
Description: A vast 15th century house set within a 1,000-acre medieval deer park in Sevenoaks, with showrooms, Gatehouse Tower, conservation studio and miles of public parkland to explore.

Name: Woolbeding Gardens
Description: A horticultural haven near Midhurst with garden rooms, follies, a contemporary glasshouse and riverside walks - open by pre-booked ticket on selected days.

Name: Nymans
Description: Romantic house ruins and famed gardens at Handcross created by the Messel family, with woodland and meadow, seasonal borders, and vistas across the High Weald AONB.

Name: Otmoor
Description: Oxfordshire wetland restoration with lapwings, curlew, and starling murmurations.

Name: Stour Estuary
Description: Ancient woodland and estuarine shorelines rich in fungi, bluebells, and wintering birds.

Name: The Lodge
Description: Headquarters reserve with heathland, ancient woodland, and gardens attracting nuthatches and green woodpeckers.

Name: Bempton Cliffs
Description: Dramatic chalk cliffs hosting England’s largest mainland seabird colony including gannets and puffins.

Name: Surlingham Church Marsh
Description: Tranquil Broadland reserve with circular trails through wet woodland and reedbeds beside the Yare.

Name: Sherwood Forest
Description: Historic ancient woodland, home to the Major Oak and hundreds of veteran trees supporting wildlife.

Name: St Bees Head
Description: Dramatic sea cliffs on the Cumbrian coast, the only breeding cliff colony of seabirds in northwest England.

Name: Campfield Marsh
Description: Solway Firth coastal wetlands with saltmarsh, peat bog, and reedbeds important for barnacle geese.

Name: Winterbourne Downs
Description: Chalk grassland restoration in Wiltshire bringing back skylarks and butterflies like the Adonis blue.

Name: Brading Marshes
Description: Island wetland on the Isle of Wight, featuring reedbeds, grassland, and saline lagoons.

Name: Beckingham Marshes
Description: Restored wet grassland on the River Trent, providing nesting habitat for lapwing and redshank.

Name: Haweswater
Description: Upland valley in the Lake District where woodland and peatland restoration supports red squirrels and golden eagles.

Name: Titchwell Marsh
Description: Coastal wetland and lagoons on the North Norfolk coast supporting avocets, bearded tits, and harriers.

Name: Sandwell Valley
Description: Oasis of wetland and woodland near Birmingham supporting kingfishers, lapwings, and otters.

Name: Havergate Island
Description: Suffolk estuary island, breeding site for avocets and terns, accessible only by pre-booked boat trips.

Name: Arne
Description: Iconic heathland and woodland on Poole Harbour with diverse species including sika deer and Dartford warbler.

Name: Ham Wall
Description: Somerset Levels reedbed reserve, flagship site for bittern, marsh harrier, and great white egret.

Name: Bowling Green Marsh
Description: Estuarine wetland at the Exe Estuary, ideal for viewing waders and ducks from accessible hides.

Name: Langford Lowfields
Description: Gravel pits restored to reedbeds and lagoons for bitterns and warblers along the River Trent.

Name: Leighton Moss
Description: Lancashire’s largest reedbed and a haven for bitterns, bearded tits, and otters.

Name: Lakenheath Fen
Description: Created from farmland into vast reedbeds and poplar woodland, home to bitterns, cranes, and otters.

Name: Middleton Lakes
Description: River Tame wetlands of reedbeds and meadows, ideal for kingfishers, bitterns, and dragonflies.

Name: Lodmoor
Description: Coastal wetland with reedbeds and saline lagoons supporting avocets, terns, and marsh harriers.

Name: Garston Wood
Description: Ancient woodland in Dorset supporting bluebells, dormice, and great spotted woodpeckers.

Name: Marshside
Description: Coastal grassland on the Ribble Estuary hosting breeding waders and large wintering flocks of geese.

Name: Aylesbeare Common
Description: Lowland heath supporting silver-studded blue butterflies and nightjars; part of the East Devon Pebblebed Heaths.

Name: Rainham Marshes
Description: Urban-edge reserve along the Thames, a mix of grazing marsh, scrapes, and boardwalk trails.

Name: Strumpshaw Fen
Description: Broadland reedbed reserve renowned for swallowtail butterflies, bitterns, and marsh harriers.

Name: Saltholme
Description: Wetland mosaic of lakes and grassland on Teesside, with family-friendly hides and paths.

Name: Blacktoft Sands
Description: Freshwater lagoons on the Humber estuary famous for marsh harriers, avocets, and bearded tits.

Name: Dearne Valley Bolton Ings
Description: Restored industrial wetland supporting wildfowl, waders, and reedbed specialists in South Yorkshire.

Name: Fairburn Ings
Description: Restored open-cast mine transformed into lagoons, reedbeds, and woodland alive with waterfowl.

Name: Valley Wetlands
Description: Valley Wetlands is home to one of the most important reedbeds in Wales, which alongside the surrounding marshes, make this patch perfect for a rich variety of wetland birds, plants and bugs.

Name: Buckenham Marshes
Description: Broadland grazing marshes supporting winter flocks of wigeon and pink-footed geese.

Name: Hodbarrow
Description: Coastal lagoon on the Cumbrian coast with tern islands, wildflowers, and panoramic views of the Duddon Estuary.

Name: Frampton Marsh
Description: Coastal freshwater and saltmarsh habitats attracting avocets, godwits, and ruffs.

Name: Nagshead
Description: Ancient oak woodland in the Forest of Dean, known for pied flycatchers and wood warblers.

Name: Dove Stone
Description: Upland reservoir landscape with moorland and woodland habitats, supporting peregrines and red grouse.

Name: Dearne Valley Old Moor
Description: Wetland and meadow complex within the South Yorkshire coalfield, home to bittern and kingfisher.

Name: Exminster & Powderham Marshes
Description: Twin wetland reserves on the Exe Estuary supporting overwintering ducks and breeding waders.

Name: Berney Marshes
Description: Broadland grazing marsh supporting breeding waders, wildfowl, and roosting pink-footed geese.

Name: Boyton Marshes
Description: Coastal grazing marsh in Suffolk adjacent to Hollesley Marshes, home to avocets and redshank.

Name: Labrador Bay
Description: Coastal cliffs and grasslands with rare cirl buntings and sweeping sea views.

Name: Ouse Washes
Description: Seasonal floodplain between river embankments attracting thousands of ducks and waders in winter.

Name: Fowlmere
Description: Small chalk stream-fed wetland with clear pools supporting kingfishers and water voles.

Name: Sydenham Hill Wood & Cox’s Walk
Description: A unique mix of new and ancient woodland in Dulwich, with remnants of Victorian gardens, can be found at the Trust's oldest nature reserve.

Name: Hutchinson's Bank
Description: Chalk grassland on London’s edge in Croydon with orchids and butterflies - regular habitat workdays.

Name: Tottenham Marshes (Lee Valley)
Description: Lea Valley floodplain meadows and wetlands with regular habitat tasks via Lee Valley volunteering.

Name: Woodberry Wetlands
Description: 11 ha reed-fringed reservoirs in Hackney with regular habitat tasks and citizen science.

Name: Crane Park Island
Description: River Crane island reserve in Whitton with woodland, reedbed and ponds - good for team tasks.

Name: Gunnersbury Triangle
Description: 3 ha wet woodland and grassland near Chiswick Park with active volunteer days and education.

Name: Walthamstow Wetlands
Description: Europe’s largest urban wetland with reservoirs, hides and trails, ideal for corporate and individual volunteering.

Name: WWT London Wetland Centre (Barnes)
Description: Flagship urban wetland in Barnes with structured corporate and individual volunteering.

Name: Epping Forest (City of London)
Description: Ancient woodland on London’s NE fringe with conservation workdays and surveys.

Name: Camley Street Natural Park
Description: Central London urban nature park at King’s Cross with established individual and corporate volunteering.

Name: Richmond Park (The Royal Parks)
Description: Large National Nature Reserve with practical conservation and ranger-style volunteering.

Name: Rodley Nature Reserve
Description: Volunteer-managed wetland in west Leeds, created from a former water treatment works.

Name: Ashton Court Estate
Description: 850-acre park on Bristol’s edge with deer park, grassland and woodland volunteering.

Name: Brandon Marsh
Description: Flagship wetland near Coventry with reedbeds, pools and an active volunteer community.

Name: The Wolseley Centre
Description: HQ and nature gardens of Staffordshire Wildlife Trust on the River Trent - frequent volunteer days.

Name: North Cave Wetlands
Description: Expansive former quarry turned wetland near Hull; regular habitat works and citizen science.

Name: Chorlton Water Park
Description: Local nature reserve with lake, woodland and grassland on Manchester’s fringe; TCV sessions common.

Name: Blacka Moor
Description: Sheffield’s wild moorland fringe with grazing and path management volunteer days.

Name: Saltram Park
Description: National Trust estate on Plymouth’s edge with woodland and estuary habitats; conservation volunteer days.

Name: Willington Wetlands
Description: Derbyshire Trent Valley wetlands with hides and regular habitat management tasks.

Name: Potteric Carr
Description: Large wetland and woodland near Doncaster with established volunteer programme.

Name: Whitlingham Country Park
Description: Broads-edge park on Norwich fringe with woodland and lakes; conservation and litter action days.

Name: Rainton Meadows Nature Reserve
Description: Restored opencast site near Sunderland with wetlands and meadows; active community volunteering.

Name: Cossington Meadows
Description: River Soar floodplain meadows north of Leicester; practical conservation and survey volunteering.

Name: Gosforth Nature Reserve
Description: Urban-edge reserve with woodland, reedbeds and lake on Newcastle’s northern fringe; regular habitat workdays.

Name: Portrack Marsh
Description: Urban wetland on the River Tees between Stockton and Middlesbrough, ideal for corporate tasks.

Name: Attenborough Nature Reserve
Description: Iconic gravel pit wetlands on the Trent by Nottingham; extensive volunteering and education.

Name: Ferry Meadows (Nene Park)
Description: Large country park in Peterborough with rivers, lakes and meadows - structured corporate days.

Name: Brockholes Nature Reserve
Description: Converted quarry on the Ribble near Preston with boardwalks and regular conservation tasks.

Name: Ludwell Valley Park (Exeter Valley Parks)
Description: Urban valley park with meadows, orchards and stream corridors; DWT volunteer tasks.

Name: New Ferry Butterfly Park
Description: Post-industrial urban nature reserve on the Wirral with active volunteer group.

Name: Paxton Pits Nature Reserve
Description: Gravel pit lakes, reedbeds and riverside meadows near St Neots with strong volunteer programme.

Name: WWT Washington Wetland Centre
Description: Sunderland wetlands with structured volunteer roles and corporate conservation days.

Name: Kingsbury Water Park
Description: Lakes and wetlands in the Tame Valley offering habitat management and access volunteering.

Name: Rushcliffe Country Park
Description: Green Flag award park near Nottingham with habitat work, orchard care and community days.

Name: Fletcher Moss Park & Parsonage Gardens
Description: Riverside park in Didsbury with wetlands, gardens and community conservation days.

Name: Staveley Nature Reserve
Description: Extensive reedbeds, lagoons and meadows near Harrogate; practical tasks and surveys.

Name: Thorpe Marshes
Description: Broadland reserve on Norwich’s edge with boardwalks, fen and marshland habitats.

Name: Watermead Country Park
Description: Linked lakes and meadows along the River Soar and Wreake with active volunteer groups.

Name: Stover Country Park
Description: Devon’s popular country park with lake, heathland and woodland; regular conservation tasks.

Name: Askham Bog
Description: Doncaster lowland bog and fen with specialist habitat management volunteering.

Name: Low Barns Nature Reserve
Description: Wetland and woodland near Bishop Auckland with hides and active community volunteering.

Name: Sutton Park National Nature Reserve
Description: Expansive heathland, wetlands and woodlands in north Birmingham; ranger-supported volunteer tasks.

Name: Monkwood Nature Reserve
Description: Ancient woodland near Worcester with bluebells and coppice rides; monthly practical conservation tasks.

Name: Whisby Nature Park
Description: Post-quarry wetlands near Lincoln with boardwalks and reedbeds; strong volunteer programme.

Name: Caldecotte Lake
Description: Linked lakes and grasslands with orchards and bat roosts on MK’s southern edge; conservation workdays.

Name: Hartlebury Common NNR
Description: Lowland heath with rare invertebrates and reptiles; path, scrub and habitat work parties.

Name: Stony Stratford Nature Reserve
Description: Ouse Valley wetlands with boardwalks and meadows on MK’s northwest fringe; active volunteer team.

Name: Ufton Fields Nature Reserve
Description: Warwickshire limestone grassland and scrub with ponds; established weekday work parties.

Name: Ryton Pools Country Park
Description: Lakes, meadows and woods east of Coventry; ranger-supported conservation volunteering.

Name: Bodenham Lake Nature Reserve
Description: Herefordshire’s largest body of open water with orchard and wetlands; regular volunteer days.

Name: Howe Park Wood
Description: Ancient woodland SSSI in south-west MK with rides, glades and regular habitat tasks.

Name: Willow Tree Fen Nature Reserve
Description: Reclaimed arable land turned fen with reedbeds and grazing; regular practical conservation tasks.

Name: Gwen Finch Wetland Reserve
Description: Reedbeds and shallow lagoons by the Avon near Nafford; habitat management and survey volunteering.

Name: EcoPark
Description: Hidden within a built-up inner city area, this ground-breaking environmental education centre features ponds, woodland, and lowland meadows that provide a vital oasis for smooth newts, frogs, and urban birds.

Name: Portway Hill
Description: Part of the Rowley Hills, this elevated grassland reserve offers stunning views across Birmingham. It supports a wealth of wildflowers including the bee orchid, and provides habitat for the marbled white butterfly.

Name: Moseley Bog & Joy's Wood
Description: This local nature reserve is famously known as the childhood playground of J.R.R. Tolkien. It features a surviving fragment of primeval wildwood, lowland fen, and spectacular displays of spring bluebells.

Name: Deer's Leap Wood
Description: This small urban reserve features a wonderful mix of informal woodland, boundary brooks, ponds, and newly established meadows. It provides an essential home for badgers, foxes, and great spotted woodpeckers.

Name: Abberton Reservoir Nature Discovery Park
Description: Abberton Reservoir Nature Discovery Park is a globally important Ramsar site, known for its significant wildfowl populations. It offers bird hides, trails, and a visitor centre, supporting overwintering ducks and breeding terns through active conservation management by Essex Wildlife Trust.

Name: Abbot's Wood
Description: Abbot's Wood, managed by Forestry England, is a mixed woodland in East Sussex offering walking trails, an accessible path, and a children's play area. It is noted for its diverse butterfly population, including the Pearl-bordered Fritillary, alongside open spaces and a pond.

Name: Abbots Moss
Description: Abbots Moss is a Cheshire Wildlife Trust reserve, safeguarding a unique ombrotrophic bog. This significant wetland is home to rare Sphagnum mosses, peat-forming plants, and diverse insect populations, including dragonflies and butterflies, crucial for local biodiversity.

Name: Abbotts Hall Farm
Description: Abbotts Hall Farm is the Essex Wildlife Trust headquarters, a coastal farm demonstrating sustainable farming and wildlife conservation. It features diverse habitats, including ancient woodland, saltmarsh, and traditional orchards, offering educational walks and events.

Name: Abercamlo Bog (Wet Heath & Basin Mires)
Description: Abercamlo Bog, near Llandrindod Wells, features a wet heath and basin mires, home to rare plants like bog rosemary. The reserve also includes species-rich acidic grassland and flush areas, providing a diverse environment for local wildlife.

Name: Aberduna (Limestone Woods)
Description: Aberduna is a 20-hectare nature reserve in Flintshire, Wales, protecting limestone grassland, ancient woodland, and disused quarry faces as a Site of Special Scientific Interest. It supports wildflowers like orchids and butterflies such as the small pearl-bordered fritillary.

Name: Aberithon (River Ithon Corridor)
Description: Aberithon nature reserve is a tranquil haven situated along the River Ithon corridor in Powys, Wales. It features wet woodland, marsh, and grassland habitats, supporting diverse wildlife including otters, birds, and invertebrates. The reserve is a significant site for biodiversity conservation.

Name: Abernethy Forest (Cairngorms)
Description: Abernethy Forest is a remnant of the ancient Caledonian pine forest in the Cairngorms National Park. It hosts rare species like capercaillie and Scottish crossbill, alongside red squirrels and pine martens. The RSPB manages the reserve, focusing on habitat restoration and protecting its unique biodiversity.

Name: Abington Meadows
Description: Abington Meadows is a riverside meadow north-east of Northampton, bordering the River Nene. Part of Kingsthorpe Meadows Local Nature Reserve, it supports wildflowers including common spotted orchid and knapweed, and various invertebrates. The site is managed for biodiversity.

Name: Abney Park Cemetery (Hackney)
Description: Abney Park Cemetery, a Grade II* listed woodland memorial park and nature reserve, is one of London's 'Magnificent Seven'. Located in Stoke Newington, Hackney, it functions as a significant ecological and historical site for the community.

Name: Abney Park Cemetery (Volunteer Partner)
Description: Abney Park Cemetery is a historic Victorian arboretum and nature reserve located in Hackney, London. It is one of London's 'Magnificent Seven' cemeteries. The site serves as a vital green space, managed in partnership to conserve its unique heritage and biodiversity for public enjoyment.

Name: Abram Flash
Description: Abram Flash is a key wetland in Greater Manchester, formed by mining subsidence. It features open water, extensive reedbeds, and grasslands. The site supports diverse birdlife, including overwintering wildfowl and breeding waders. Managed for wildlife and access.

Name: Acorn Bank (Crowdundle Beck)
Description: Acorn Bank, near Penrith, Cumbria, features extensive traditional fruit orchards and one of the largest medicinal and culinary herb collections in northern England. The tranquil estate includes Crowdundle Beck, a historic watermill, and gardens with woodland walks, offering visitors a peaceful natural experience.

Name: Adcombe Wood
Description: Adcombe Wood is an ancient woodland with a diverse range of trees including oak, ash, and hazel. It features a varied ground flora, stream, and coppiced areas. The site is a habitat for dormice, bats, and various bird species.

Name: Addiewell Bing
Description: Addiewell Bing is a large red shale bing, formed from waste from the Addiewell Oil Works, overlooking Addiewell village. It is transforming into grassland and heathland, supporting various birds and invertebrates.

Name: Adel Dam
Description: Adel Dam Nature Reserve is a small but diverse woodland site in Leeds. It features a large pond with a bird hide, mature trees, and wetland areas. The reserve provides important habitat for birds, including kingfishers and mandarin ducks, and other wildlife.

Name: Adventurers' Fen (Wicken Fen)
Description: Wicken Fen is one of Europe's most important wetlands, offering a rich habitat for over 9,000 species. Visitors can explore boardwalks, enjoy boat trips, cycle routes, and views from the tower. Adventurers' Fen is part of this diverse nature reserve.

Name: Affpuddle Forest
Description: Affpuddle Forest, near Affpuddle in Dorset, is a mixed woodland providing a quiet walking experience. The site features broadleaf and conifer trees, with some areas managed for timber. Informal paths allow visitors to explore and observe local wildlife.

Name: Agden Bog (Rare Peak District Mire)
Description: Agden Bog is a rare Peak District mire, featuring sphagnum mosses, cotton grasses, and carnivorous sundew plants. It supports diverse invertebrates, snipe, and red grouse. Conservation efforts manage water levels and control invasive species.

Name: Ail Meadow (Woolhope Dome)
Description: Ail Meadow, within the Woolhope Dome SSSI in Herefordshire, is a species-rich grassland. It supports diverse wildflowers including common and green-winged orchids. The site provides vital habitat for insects and small mammals, managed for its significant biodiversity value.

Name: Ainsdale Sand Dunes NNR (Natural England)
Description: Ainsdale Sand Dunes NNR is a coastal dune system in Merseyside, covering 500 hectares. It protects rare wildlife, including natterjack toads and sand lizards. The reserve features diverse habitats like dunes, slacks, and pine woodland, supporting a wide array of plant and insect species.

Name: Aira Force & Ullswater (Riparian Recovery)
Description: Aira Force waterfall cascades 65ft amidst ancient woodland in the Ullswater valley, Lake District. Conservation efforts protect red squirrel habitats and riparian areas. Visitors explore walking trails and an arboretum.

Name: Aisholt Wood
Description: Aisholt Wood is an ancient woodland reserve in the Quantock Hills, supporting diverse tree species, wildlife, and a stream. It provides crucial habitats for dormice and various bird species.

Name: Albany Park River Restoration
Description: Albany Park River Restoration enhances the River Lea in Enfield, north London. It improves river health and biodiversity through habitat creation, natural channel design, and increased public access. The project forms part of a larger initiative to restore Enfield's rivers.

Name: Aldbury Nowers
Description: Aldbury Nowers is a Hertfordshire nature reserve featuring ancient woodland and chalk grassland. It supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare butterflies like the Duke of Burgundy. Management maintains its crucial habitats.

Name: Alde Mudflats (Iken)
Description: Alde Mudflats, part of the Alde-Ore Estuary SSSI, protects extensive intertidal mudflats and saltmarsh. This habitat supports diverse wading birds and wildfowl, with nationally significant numbers present during migration and winter. Key species include avocet and brent goose.

Name: Aldercar Flash (Erewash Meadows)
Description: Aldercar Flash is a rich wetland habitat within the Erewash Valley. The reserve features open water, reedbeds, and willow scrub, supporting diverse bird species throughout the year. Winter hosts wildfowl, while summer attracts warblers and wading birds. It is a significant site for biodiversity.

Name: Alderfen Broad and Marshes
Description: Alderfen Broad and Marshes is a Norfolk Wildlife Trust reserve located within the Broads National Park. It features open water, extensive reedswamp, and wet woodland habitats, part of the River Ant system. The site supports a variety of wildlife, including the rare swallowtail butterfly and numerous scarce plants.

Name: Alderley Edge (Geological SSSI)
Description: Alderley Edge is a dramatic red sandstone escarpment in Cheshire, offering panoramic views. It is a geological SSSI, known for its ancient copper mines and unique mineralisation. The site is also linked to the legend of the Wizard of Alderley.

Name: Aldouran Glen (Atlantic Rainforest)
Description: Aldouran Glen is an ancient Atlantic Rainforest near Leswalt, Dumfries & Galloway. This steep-sided glen offers a rich habitat for wildlife and fungi. The woodland features well-maintained paths for visitors to explore its diverse ecosystem.

Name: Aldred's Lea
Description: Aldred's Lea is a small woodland in Ashton Hayes village. It supports diverse wildlife, including bats and butterflies. Mature trees create a canopy, with ground flora like bluebells in spring. Public footpaths allow visitors to explore the reserve.

Name: Alexandra Park (Oldham)
Description: Alexandra Park in Oldham is a Grade II listed Victorian park, opened in 1865. It features a boating lake, conservatory, children's play area, and various sports facilities. The park offers walking paths and formal gardens, providing a community green space.

Name: Alice Holt Forest
Description: Alice Holt Forest in Surrey is a managed woodland, offering extensive walking and cycling trails, play areas, and a visitor centre. This popular site provides diverse habitats for wildlife and supports timber production and environmental education.

Name: Allan Bank (Grasmere Ecosystem)
Description: Allan Bank is a National Trust property near Grasmere, Lake District, linked to William Wordsworth and Canon Hardwicke Rawnsley. The estate features a house, woodlands, and walled garden, offering public access and views across the Grasmere valley.

Name: Allen Banks and Staward Gorge (Ancient Wood)
Description: Allen Banks and Staward Gorge is an extensive area of woodland in the North Pennines, home to a wide range of wildlife. The River Allen flows through the gorge, creating a diverse habitat. It features a Victorian viewing tower and walking trails.

Name: Aller & Beer Woods
Description: Aller & Beer Woods is an ancient woodland reserve near Langport, Somerset, managed by Somerset Wildlife Trust. Covering 23 hectares, it features mature oak, ash, and hazel trees. The diverse habitats support dormice, various bird species, and rare flora, contributing significantly to local biodiversity.

Name: Allerthorpe Common
Description: Allerthorpe Common is a 60-hectare lowland heath and woodland reserve in East Yorkshire, managed by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust. It supports nationally rare species like the dark bordered beauty moth and nightjar. Habitats include wet/dry heath, birch woodland, and wetlands.

Name: Allexton Wood (Ancient Upland Wood)
Description: Allexton Wood is a 38-hectare ancient woodland in East Leicestershire. It features diverse tree species like oak, ash, field maple, and hazel coppice. A rich understory of wildflowers, including bluebells, supports a variety of birds and insects.

Name: Alliance for Scotland's Rainforest (Coastal Hub)
Description: The Alliance for Scotland's Rainforest is a collaborative partnership dedicated to conserving, restoring, and expanding Scotland's unique temperate rainforest along its western coast. The initiative focuses on protecting vital habitats and species through coordinated efforts and community engagement.

Name: Allt-yr-Yn (Newport Urban Gateway)
Description: Allt-yr-Yn is a 3.5-hectare urban nature reserve in Newport, South Wales. It features ancient woodland, grassland, and ponds, providing habitat for diverse wildlife. The site is actively managed for biodiversity, offering public walking trails.

Name: Alpine Meadow
Description: Alpine Meadow is a small nature reserve in Tring that features a rare chalk grassland. It supports a diverse range of plant and insect species, including several orchids and butterflies. The site is managed for conservation, maintaining its unique biodiversity.

Name: Altar Stones
Description: Altar Stones Nature Reserve, a former quarry site, features diverse habitats including grassland, woodland, and heathland. It supports a wide array of wildlife, notably rich flora and fauna, providing a haven for biodiversity in Leicestershire.

Name: Alvecote Pools
Description: Alvecote Pools is a former opencast coal mine, now an important wetland site with diverse habitats including open water, reedbeds, wet woodland, and meadows. It supports a rich variety of bird species, dragonflies, and other invertebrates.

Name: Alvecote Pools (Staffordshire/Warwickshire Border)
Description: Alvecote Pools, a Warwickshire Wildlife Trust reserve, features open water, reedbeds, grassland, and woodland habitats. It provides vital refuge for diverse wildlife, notably bird species like waders and wildfowl, and supports invertebrate populations.

Name: Alverstone Mead (IOW Wetland Complex)
Description: Alverstone Mead is a small wetland reserve in the Eastern Yar Valley, Isle of Wight. It features wet woodland, ponds, and meadows, forming part of a larger wetland complex. The site supports diverse birdlife, including kingfishers, and provides habitat for water voles.

Name: Amberley Wildbrooks
Description: Amberley Wildbrooks, a large grazing marsh and wet grassland alongside the River Arun, is vital for overwintering wildfowl and waders including wigeon and teal. It supports diverse aquatic invertebrates and is part of the Arun Valley Special Protection Area.

Name: Amwell
Description: Amwell is a former gravel pit that now forms a wetland nature reserve in Hertfordshire, home to a wide range of birds. This site features open water, reedbeds, and woodland, providing diverse habitats for wildlife. It is particularly known for its wintering wildfowl and breeding waders, attracting birdwatchers.

Name: Amwell Nature Reserve (Partner)
Description: Amwell Nature Reserve, near Hertford, is a Site of Special Scientific Interest, internationally recognised for its diverse wildlife. Managed by Lee Valley Regional Park Authority and Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust, it offers excellent birdwatching, especially for wintering wildfowl and Bittern, alongside dragonflies and unique flora.

Name: Ancaster Valley
Description: Ancaster Valley is a narrow, steep-sided limestone valley with ancient woodland and grassland. It supports species like fly orchid and hazel dormouse. Managed by Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust, it is a Site of Special Scientific Interest.

Name: Ancells Farm
Description: Ancells Farm Nature Reserve, near Fleet, Hampshire, is managed by Hampshire and Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust. The urban fringe site features species-rich grassland, ancient woodland, scrub, and wetland. Paths provide year-round access for visitors.

Name: Anderby Marsh
Description: Anderby Marsh is a wildlife-rich wetland on the Lincolnshire coast. Formerly a grazing marsh, it now features scrapes, ditches, and ponds. The reserve supports diverse birdlife and invertebrates. It forms part of the wider Coastal Grazing Marsh Project.

Name: Andrew's Wood
Description: Andrew’s Wood is a mix of ancient woodland, grassland and wet meadows, covering 55 hectares near Loddiswell. The reserve supports varied wildlife, including dormice, otters, and various bird species. Devon Wildlife Trust manages the site for conservation.

Name: Anglesey Abbey (Fen-edge Parkland)
Description: Anglesey Abbey is a former priory, country house, and extensive gardens in Lode, Cambridgeshire. The site includes a working watermill, a landscaped parkland, and a significant collection of art and artefacts. Visitors explore the house, formal gardens, and wider estate.

Name: Anglian Water Birdwatching Centre (Egleton)
Description: Rutland Water Nature Reserve, managed by Leicestershire and Rutland Wildlife Trust, is an internationally important wetland. It features diverse habitats, including lagoons and woodlands, supporting numerous bird species and wildlife. Visitors can observe nature from various hides.

Name: Annesley Woodhouse Quarry
Description: Annesley Woodhouse Quarry is a former railway cutting and limestone quarry in Nottinghamshire. The reserve features grassland, scrub, and woodland habitats. It supports wildflowers like pyramidal and bee orchids, and butterflies such as the dingy skipper.

Name: Annet (The Bird Island Sanctuary)
Description: Annet, The Bird Island Sanctuary, is the largest seabird breeding site in the Isles of Scilly. It hosts puffins, storm petrels, and shearwaters. Landing is restricted to prevent disturbance to ground-nesting birds. The island can be observed from the sea.

Name: Annstead Dunes
Description: Annstead Dunes is a Northumberland Wildlife Trust reserve in Chathill, Northumberland. This narrow coastal strip comprises sand dunes and dune grassland. The site supports important populations of rare plants and various insect species, contributing to local biodiversity.

Name: Ardingly Reservoir (Wetland sections)
Description: Ardingly Reservoir Wetlands is a Sussex Wildlife Trust nature reserve within the South East Water-owned Ardingly Reservoir site. It offers excellent birdwatching opportunities, with diverse species utilising its open water, reedbeds, and wet woodlands.

Name: Ardley Quarry
Description: Ardley Quarry, near Bicester, Oxfordshire, is a former railway quarry. Now a nature reserve, it features species-rich limestone grassland, scrub, and woodland. Diverse habitats support butterflies, insects, wildflowers, lizards, and slow-worms.

Name: Arger Fen & Spouse's Vale
Description: Arger Fen & Spouse's Vale is an ancient woodland, stream, and meadowland site in Suffolk, managed by Suffolk Wildlife Trust. It supports diverse wildlife, including dormice, bats, and rare fungi, offering peaceful walks and birdwatching opportunities.

Name: Arger Fen and Spouse's Vale
Description: Arger Fen and Spouse's Vale is an ancient woodland and nature reserve in Suffolk. It features coppiced woods, meadows, and streams, providing habitats for dormice, nightingales, and various rare plants. The reserve offers walking trails for visitors.

Name: Argill Woods (Waterfalls & Dippers)
Description: Argill Woods is a tranquil ancient woodland reserve near Brough, Cumbria, featuring a steep-sided gill with waterfalls and diverse flora. The site is notable for dippers along the beck and various woodland birds. Mature oak, ash, and hazel trees dominate the canopy, supporting a rich understorey.

Name: Arkaig Community Forest
Description: Arkaig Community Forest manages 1,215 hectares of native pinewood and woodland near Spean Bridge, Loch Arkaig, in the Scottish Highlands. The charity works to restore native pinewoods and manages them for community benefit and sustainable income.

Name: Arle Grove
Description: Arle Grove is a 27-hectare ancient semi-natural woodland near Cheltenham, Gloucestershire. Predominantly oak, ash, and beech, it supports diverse flora and fauna, including woodland birds and invertebrates. The reserve is actively managed for biodiversity.

Name: Arlesey Old Moat and Glebe Meadows
Description: Arlesey Old Moat and Glebe Meadows is a historic site featuring a medieval moated manor house, now a tranquil woodland and wetland area. Glebe Meadows provides flower-rich grassland habitat.

Name: Arlington Court (Exmoor Fringe Woodland)
Description: Arlington Court is a Regency house and estate in North Devon, situated on the edge of Exmoor. It features a picturesque Victorian garden, extensive woodlands, and houses the National Trust Carriage Museum. The estate offers diverse walking trails.

Name: Armstrong Wood
Description: Armstrong Wood is a nature reserve near Bodmin, Cornwall, covering five hectares. It features ancient and wet woodland, meadows, and borders the River Camel. The site supports species like otter, kingfisher, and brown trout.

Name: Arnold's Meadow
Description: Arnold's Meadow is a grassland reserve located near Gainsborough, Lincolnshire. It features a variety of wildflowers and provides habitat for invertebrates. The site is managed by Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust.

Name: Arnside Knott (Coastal Limestone)
Description: Arnside Knott is a prominent limestone hill offering panoramic views of Morecambe Bay and the Lake District Fells. Managed by the National Trust, this site features ancient woodlands, wildflower-rich grasslands, and diverse wildlife. Public footpaths allow access to its summit.

Name: Arreton Down
Description: Arreton Down is a chalk grassland nature reserve on the Isle of Wight. It features steep slopes, rich wildflowers like pyramidal orchids, and various butterflies, including chalkhill blues. The site offers panoramic views.

Name: Arreton Down (IOW Chalk Grassland)
Description: Arreton Down, a nationally important chalk grassland on the Isle of Wight, is a Site of Special Scientific Interest. It supports a rich diversity of wildflowers and insects. Unique geological formations and panoramic views are present. The site is managed for biodiversity.

Name: Ash Moor
Description: Ash Moor, near South Molton, is a 117-hectare nature reserve featuring internationally important culm grassland. It supports many rare and threatened species, alongside heathland, woodland, scrub, and wetlands. The site offers a rich mosaic of habitats for wildlife.

Name: Ash Ranges
Description: Ash Ranges is a 600-hectare lowland heathland in Surrey, designated an SSSI and part of the Thames Basin Heaths SPA. Managed by Surrey Wildlife Trust and the MOD, it supports rare wildlife like Dartford warblers, nightjars, and adders. Military training takes place here.

Name: Ash Ranges (MOD Training Area)
Description: Ash Ranges is a military training area that also serves as a significant nature reserve. It features extensive heathland, supporting rare bird species like woodlark and nightjar, along with reptiles such as sand lizards. The site is managed for biodiversity alongside its primary MOD use.

Name: Asham Meads
