Description: Brancaster Staithe is a harbour village on the North Norfolk coast, managed by the National Trust. It features extensive saltmarsh and mudflats, home to diverse wading birds like oystercatchers and curlews. The area is popular for sailing and provides vital habitat.

Name: Brandlehow Woods (Ancient Wood)
Description: Brandlehow Woods is an ancient woodland on the western shore of Derwent Water in the Lake District. The National Trust acquired it in 1902, making it their first property. It provides public access and walking routes near the lake.

Name: Brandon Hill Nature Reserve
Description: Brandon Hill is Bristol's oldest park, rising steeply behind Park Street. The site offers panoramic city and harbour views, with wildflower meadows, mature trees, and a pond. It is managed by Avon Wildlife Trust to enhance biodiversity for urban wildlife.

Name: Brandon Park (Thetford Forest)
Description: Brandon Country Park, within Thetford Forest, Suffolk, covers 30 acres of parkland and 300 acres of woodland. It features an arboretum, lake, mausoleum, visitor centre, and a tea room. Miles of trails are available for walking, cycling, and riding.

Name: Brandon Reach (Ancient Woodland & Grassland)
Description: Brandon Reach is a Warwickshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve near Coventry. It protects ancient woodland and species-rich grassland, home to mature oak and ash trees. The site supports diverse wildlife, including birds and invertebrates, and provides walking trails for visitors.

Name: Brandon Wood (Former Golf Course Rewilding)
Description: Brandon Wood, a former golf course near Coventry, undergoes rewilding by Warwickshire Wildlife Trust. It creates a diverse mosaic of woodland, grassland, and wetland habitats, supporting various wildlife and aiding ecological recovery efforts locally.

Name: Brankley Pastures (Ancient Wood Pasture)
Description: Brankley Pastures is an ancient wood pasture, featuring venerable oak and ash trees. It supports a diverse range of invertebrates, fungi, and lichens, including nationally rare species. The site is managed for conservation, with grazing maintaining the open woodland structure.

Name: Branscombe to Beer Cliffs
Description: Branscombe to Beer Cliffs is a dramatic coastal area in East Devon, managed by the National Trust. It features striking cliffs, secluded beaches, and ancient woodlands. Part of the Jurassic Coast World Heritage Site, it offers diverse wildlife and geological formations.

Name: Brassey
Description: Brassey is a grassland nature reserve in the Cotswolds, managed by Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust. It protects diverse limestone grassland, wildflowers, and insects, including rare species like the Pasqueflower and Duke of Burgundy butterfly. The reserve is a significant site for biodiversity.

Name: Brassey Nature Reserve (River Eye Headwaters)
Description: Brassey Nature Reserve is a diverse mosaic of grassland, scrub, and woodland in the Cotswolds, protecting the headwaters of the River Eye. It supports rare flora like moonwort and adders-tongue fern. The limestone grassland is grazed to maintain rich biodiversity.

Name: Brean Down (Somerset Coast & Grassland)
Description: Brean Down is a prominent limestone headland in Somerset, offering panoramic views over the Bristol Channel. It features an Iron Age hill fort and a 19th-century fort. The site is known for its diverse wildlife and coastal habitats, providing historical walks and natural beauty.

Name: Bredhurst Wood
Description: Bredhurst Wood is a 64-hectare ancient woodland Site of Special Scientific Interest in Kent. Managed by Kent Wildlife Trust, it features diverse oak, hornbeam, and sweet chestnut trees. The site supports fungi, invertebrates, butterflies, and dormice.

Name: Breney Common (Wetland Mosaic)
Description: Breney Common is a designated SSSI and SAC in Cornwall, forming part of the Mid Cornwall Moors. This lowland wet heath and valley mire supports diverse wetland flora and fauna, including rare butterflies and birds, managed for conservation.

Name: Brentmoor Heath & Folly Bog
Description: Brentmoor Heath & Folly Bog is an SSSI near West End, Woking. This nature reserve features a mosaic of open heathland, bog, wet and dry grassland, and woodland. It supports diverse wildlife including Dartford warbler, nightjar, woodlark, and great crested newts.

Name: Brett's Wood (Woodland Restoration)
Description: Brett's Wood is a 33.5-hectare nature reserve near Watton, Norfolk. It comprises former arable fields undergoing restoration to woodland and acid grassland. The site features planted oak, sweet chestnut, and hornbeam, creating new habitats for local wildlife.

Name: Brettenham Heath
Description: Brettenham Heath is an important area of heathland, woodland, and acid grassland in Suffolk. It supports breeding woodlark, tree pipit, nightjar, and stone-curlew. Reptiles include slow-worm, common lizard, and adder. Over 170 vascular plant species are recorded.

Name: Brickfield Meadow
Description: Brickfield Meadow is a small, quiet nature reserve in south Haywards Heath, West Sussex. It features an open wildflower meadow, a pond, and mature oak trees. The site supports various insects, amphibians, and bird species, providing a valuable local green space.

Name: Bridestones (National Trust Moorland)
Description: Bridestones, Crosscliff and Blakey Topping presents dramatic moorland in the North York Moors National Park. Wind and ice shaped the spectacular rock formations, including the Bride and Groom. The site features ancient woodland, heather moorland, and rich biodiversity, offering walking trails and scenic views. Managed by the National Trust.

Name: Bridgham Heath
Description: Bridgham Heath, a fragment of Breckland heathland in Norfolk, features dry acid grassland, heather, and small copses. It supports diverse insects, common lizards, and various bird species, actively managed for conservation.

Name: Brierdene LNR
Description: Brierdene LNR is a coastal denemouth in Whitley Bay, Northumberland, managed by Northumberland Wildlife Trust. It encompasses woodland, grassland, and freshwater habitats, supporting diverse wildlife. The dene connects directly to the beach, offering varied coastal and woodland environments.

Name: Brilley Green Dingle
Description: Brilley Green Dingle is a small but important ancient woodland nature reserve in Herefordshire. It features sessile oak, ash, and hazel, with a rich ground flora including bluebells and wood anemones. The dingle structure creates a sheltered habitat.

Name: Brimham Rocks (Moorland & Geology)
Description: Brimham Rocks, a biological SSSI in North Yorkshire, features unique rock formations sculpted over 320 million years by natural erosion. This moorland landscape offers free access and supports diverse wildlife.

Name: Brimley Hill Mire
Description: Brimley Hill Mire is a small acidic mire and wetland system on Greensand in East Devon. It supports rare mosses, diverse dragonflies including the golden-ringed dragonfly, and plants like bog asphodel. Managed via grazing and scrub control.

Name: Brinsley Headstocks (Heritage & Nature)
Description: Brinsley Headstocks is a 2-hectare Local Nature Reserve near Eastwood, Nottinghamshire. The site preserves former colliery structures and offers diverse habitats, including grassland and wetlands, supporting varied wildlife. It is managed for both heritage and biodiversity.

Name: Brixham & Mansands
Description: Brixham and Mansands features dramatic cliffs and a shingle beach along the Devon coast. This National Trust managed area offers quiet coves and coastal path walks, rich in wildlife between Brixham and Kingswear.

Name: Broad Oak Orchard
Description: Broad Oak Orchard is a small, traditional orchard near Stourpaine, Dorset. It features various apple, pear, and plum trees, providing habitat and food for local wildlife. The site is actively managed to enhance biodiversity and preserve traditional fruit varieties.

Name: Broadham Down
Description: Broadham Down is a small chalk grassland nature reserve near Oxted, Surrey. Its south-facing slope provides superb views across the Weald and supports a rich variety of chalk-loving plants. Pyramidal orchids and wild thyme attract diverse butterfly species.

Name: Broadhead Clough
Description: Broadhead Clough is a small, wooded valley with a stream flowing to the River Calder. This ancient woodland reserve features mature oak, ash, and birch, supporting diverse flora. Notable species include bluebells and wood anemones. The site is actively managed for conservation.

Name: Broadhurst Edge Wood
Description: Broadhurst Edge Wood is a steep-sided woodland in Derbyshire, dominated by mature sessile oak, birch, and hazel. Ground flora includes bluebell, wood sorrel, and dog's mercury. The site is important for woodland birds and invertebrates. A public footpath runs through the site.

Name: Broadmead
Description: Broadmead is a small wetland nature reserve located east of Colchester, near Elmstead. It features a mosaic of habitats including wet woodland, reedbeds, scrub, and grassland, supporting diverse wildlife. The reserve provides an important habitat for water voles and other species.

Name: Broadstreet Backside & Rydes Commons
Description: Broadstreet Backside & Rydes Commons is a Surrey nature reserve featuring heathland, woodland, and ponds. It supports rare marsh gentian and diverse birdlife including Dartford warblers and nightjars. This site is a designated Site of Special Scientific Interest.

Name: Broadwater Lake (Colne Valley)
Description: Broadwater Lake is a large lake with extensive reedbeds, wet woodland, and grassland habitats, supporting a diverse array of wildlife. It is home to significant bird populations and various invertebrates, serving as a key site for nature conservation and observation.

Name: Broadwater Lake SSSI
Description: Broadwater Lake SSSI is a former gravel extraction site. It supports significant populations of wildfowl, including wintering gadwall, pochard, and shoveler. Important aquatic vegetation thrives here, contributing to diverse wetland habitats.

Name: Broaks Wood
Description: Broaks Wood is a mature ancient woodland in Essex, featuring hornbeam, ash, and oak trees. It contains an ancient stream, medieval fishpond remains, and seasonal ponds. Wildflowers like wood anemone, bluebell, and ramsons thrive here, managed for biodiversity.

Name: Brockadale (Magnesian Limestone)
Description: Brockadale is a nationally important magnesian limestone woodland and grassland reserve in West Yorkshire's Went Valley. It features ancient woodland, wildflower-rich grassland, and the River Went, supporting rare orchids, butterflies, and diverse bird species.

Name: Brockhampton Estate
Description: Brockhampton Estate is a medieval manor house and estate set in a secluded valley near Bringsty, Herefordshire. The property features ancient orchards, woodlands, and a working farm. Visitors can explore the timber-framed house, a moat, and 1,000 acres of parkland.

Name: Brockholes Wood
Description: Brockholes Wood is an ancient woodland in Derbyshire, managed by Derbyshire Wildlife Trust. Mature oak, ash, and hazel trees support diverse bird species and wildflowers. Public footpaths provide access to this valuable wildlife habitat.

Name: Brodsworth Community Woodland (Restored Colliery)
Description: Brodsworth Community Woodland is a former colliery site in South Yorkshire. TCV supports its management, with ongoing activities including path maintenance, tree planting, and coppicing. The woodland offers views across the Dearne Valley.

Name: Bromeswell Green
Description: Bromeswell Green, managed by Suffolk Wildlife Trust, is a nature reserve in Suffolk. It features wet grassland, reedbed, and wet woodland habitats, supporting diverse birds and invertebrates. The site is a key area for local wildlife conservation.

Name: Bromeswell Green (Woodland & Saltmarsh)
Description: Bromeswell Green, near Woodbridge, Suffolk, features mixed woodland and saltmarsh habitat. Managed by Suffolk Wildlife Trust, it supports diverse bird and plant life. Visitors enjoy peaceful walks, observing local wildlife. A vital area for biodiversity.

Name: Brompton Cemetery (Nature & Heritage)
Description: Brompton Cemetery is a Grade I listed garden cemetery established in 1840 in West London. It features over 20,000 monuments, including notable figures like Emmeline Pankhurst, and a diverse range of trees and wildlife. Managed by The Royal Parks, it is a site of special historic interest.

Name: Brook Vessons
Description: Brook Vessons is a Shropshire Wildlife Trust reserve featuring upland heathland, acid grassland, woodland, and wetlands. It supports diverse species, including curlew, snipe, and raft spiders. Bog and fen areas are crucial for invertebrates and specialised plants, contributing to its rich flora and fauna.

Name: Brookes Nature Reserve (Coppice Wood)
Description: Brookes Nature Reserve, also known as Coppice Wood, is a small ancient woodland in North Stifford. It features mature trees, a hazel coppice, and a small pond, supporting diverse wildlife. The reserve offers peaceful walking trails.

Name: Brookheys Covert
Description: Brookheys Covert is a small woodland reserve in Hale Barns, Cheshire, acquired by the Trust in 2011. It features mature oaks, sycamores, and a diverse ground flora including bluebells. A stream supports invertebrates, attracting various birds and mammals.

Name: Brookwood Lye
Description: Brookwood Lye is a wet woodland and grassland reserve in the Hoe Stream valley, a River Wey tributary. The site features alder woodland, ditches, and wet grassland, supporting species like sedge warbler and water vole.

Name: Broom Marsh (River Arrow Floodplain)
Description: Broom Marsh is a wet grassland and marsh site, part of the River Arrow floodplain. Managed with cattle grazing and traditional hay meadows, it supports fen meadow species, wintering wildfowl, and otters within its diverse wetland habitats.

Name: Brotheridge Green (Railway Corridor)
Description: This linear reserve follows a disused railway line near Upton-upon-Severn. It features diverse habitats including wildflower meadows, ancient hedgerows, and woodland. It supports various butterflies and birds, acting as an important wildlife corridor.

Name: Brough Law (Cheviot Grassland Recovery)
Description: Brough Law is a Cheviot grassland site within Northumberland National Park undergoing peatland restoration. Volunteers remove non-native conifers and plant sphagnum moss to restore natural habitat and biodiversity. This project enhances the peatland ecosystem.

Name: Brough Law (Cheviot Hillfort & Grassland)
Description: Brough Law is an Iron Age hillfort located within the Cheviot Hills, Northumberland National Park. The site features multiple well-preserved ramparts and ditches, indicating significant ancient occupation. It is surrounded by rough grassland.

Name: Broughton Down
Description: Broughton Down features colourful chalk downland flowers and butterflies such as Adonis blue. The site offers views across the Test Valley. Restoration efforts convert scrub to chalk grassland, managed by the Hampshire & Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust.

Name: Broughton Down (Chalk Escarpment)
Description: Broughton Down in Hampshire is a nationally important chalk grassland. Its varied topography includes steep scarp slopes and undulating plateaus. Wildflowers, including cowslip and orchids, thrive here, attracting butterflies like chalkhill blue. Grazing helps maintain its diverse sward.

Name: Broughton Heath
Description: Broughton Heath is a lowland heathland reserve near Church Broughton. It features heather and acid grassland, supporting invertebrates like mining bees and heathland moths. Common lizards are present. Scrub clearance helps maintain this vital open habitat.

Name: Brown Robin (Limestone Wood)
Description: Brown Robin is a nature reserve near Grange-over-Sands, Cumbria. It protects ancient semi-natural woodland thriving on limestone, supporting rich flora including helleborines and bluebells. Diverse bird species and invertebrates inhabit the site, which also features a public footpath.

Name: Brown's Folly
Description: Brown's Folly is a nature reserve featuring limestone grassland and ancient woodland, located near Bath. The site supports diverse wildflowers, rare bat species, and has disused quarry faces. A folly tower provides panoramic views.

Name: Brown's Hill Quarry (Holwell Limestone)
Description: Holwell Nature Reserve, near Melton Mowbray, encompasses Brown's Hill Quarry, a former limestone quarry, and an ancient woodland. It is notable for rare plants, diverse butterflies, and significant geological features. The site holds a designated Site of Special Scientific Interest.

Name: Brownsea Island (Red Squirrel Sanctuary)
Description: Brownsea Island, a National Trust property in Poole Harbour, Dorset, is a red squirrel sanctuary and wildlife haven. It features diverse habitats, walking trails, and a lagoon. Access is by ferry, supporting its conservation efforts.

Name: Brownsham & Mouthmill (Temperate Rainforest)
Description: Brownsham and Mouthmill, part of North Devon Coast AONB, feature ancient temperate rainforest and dramatic coastal cliffs. The area includes wildflower meadows and a hanging valley with a rock arch and waterfall. Brownsham Mill is a Grade II listed building.

Name: BrueCREW River Restoration (Bruton)
Description: BrueCREW is a community group in Bruton, Somerset, focused on restoring, enhancing, and protecting the River Brue. They remove obstructions, create habitats, and manage invasive species to improve biodiversity for people and wildlife.

Name: Brynmawr (Upland Grassland)
Description: Brynmawr is an upland grassland nature reserve in Powys, Wales, managed by Shropshire Wildlife Trust. The site features acidic grassland, heather, and wildflowers, supporting a diverse array of insects and birds. Sheep grazing maintains the habitat, and the reserve offers views of the Kerry Hills.

Name: Brynna Woods & Llanharan Marsh
Description: Brynna Woods and Llanharan Marsh, a nature reserve in South Wales, features ancient woodlands and a lowland fen. The site supports diverse wildlife, including otters and dormice. Habitats include wet woodland, ponds, and marshland, providing a rich environment for various species.

Name: Bubbenhall Wood & Meadow (Dunsmore Core)
Description: Bubbenhall Wood & Meadow, near Coventry, is an ancient woodland and grassland site. It features oak, ash, and hazel trees, diverse ground flora, and a wildflower meadow. The River Sowe flows through, supporting varied wildlife.

Name: Bubwith Acres
Description: Bubwith Acres is a 6.2 hectare nature reserve, owned and managed by Somerset Wildlife Trust. It comprises ancient broadleaved woodland, grassland, and a stream, providing a habitat for diverse wildlife including bats and butterflies. The site offers peaceful walking trails.

Name: Buckland Abbey & Tavy Valley
Description: Buckland Abbey is a historic 700-year-old abbey in the Tavy Valley, Devon, initially a Cistercian monastery. It later became home to Sir Richard Grenville and Sir Francis Drake. The site features gardens, orchards, and ancient woodlands.

Name: Buckland Lake Nature Reserve (Clifftop & Wetland)
Description: Buckland Lake Nature Reserve, a 100-acre SSSI in West Kent, features a large spring-fed lake and diverse woodland. Once a chalk quarry, it supports varied flora and fauna, offering birdwatching and walking trails.

Name: Budby South Forest
Description: Budby South Forest is a significant remnant of Sherwood Forest's ancient woodland. This RSPB reserve features open heathland, acid grassland, and mature trees, supporting species such as nightjars, woodlarks, and various heathland plants. Management focuses on restoring and maintaining diverse habitats.

Name: Bude Meadows and Woods
Description: Bude Meadows and Woods is a new woodland creation project in Cornwall. Over 27,000 trees have been planted on the site, which also features existing ancient woodland and open meadows. Visitors can explore diverse habitats via marked footpaths.

Name: Bugbrooke Meadow
Description: Bugbrooke Meadow is a wet meadow on the River Nene floodplain in Northamptonshire. It features diverse grasses, sedges, and wildflowers, including ragged robin and great burnet. The site supports various insects and amphibians, providing valuable habitat.

Name: Bugdens Meadows
Description: Bugdens Meadows is a small, quiet nature reserve near Stalbridge. This damp wildflower meadow borders the River Stour. It supports orchids, marsh marigolds, and ragged-robin.

Name: Bull's Wood
Description: Bull's Wood is an ancient woodland reserve in Suffolk, managed by Suffolk Wildlife Trust. It is nationally important for its diverse flora, including early-purple orchid, and features bluebell carpets in spring. A variety of trees, birds, and mammals inhabit the site. Access is available from a small car park.

Name: Bull's Wood (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Bull's Wood is an ancient woodland reserve in Suffolk, known for its diverse flora and fauna. It features a rich understorey of hazel and hornbeam, supporting species like dormice and various woodland birds. The site provides a habitat for wildflowers including bluebells and wood anemones.

Name: Bumble Hole & Warrens Hall (Canal Junction)
Description: Bumble Hole and Warrens Hall Local Nature Reserves sit at a historic canal junction in the West Midlands. The site features restored canals, diverse wildlife habitats, and walking trails, offering a valuable green space for nature enthusiasts.

Name: Bunny Old Wood
Description: Bunny Old Wood is an ancient woodland, featuring mature oak, ash, hazel, and field maple trees. The site supports a diverse ground flora, including bluebells and wood anemones in spring, and provides habitats for various bird species and other wildlife.

Name: Bunny Old Wood (Ancient Coppice)
Description: Bunny Old Wood is an ancient coppice wood, designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI). It features oak, ash, and field maple with a rich ground flora, including bluebells and ramsons. The site supports diverse birdlife and invertebrates.

Name: Bunny Old Wood (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Bunny Old Wood is an ancient woodland, dating to at least 1600. It features mature oak, ash, and field maple with a rich understorey of hazel, hawthorn, and blackthorn. The site supports diverse ground flora including dog's mercury, wood anemone, and yellow archangel, providing habitat for various birds and invertebrates.

Name: Bure Marshes
Description: Bure Marshes, a National Nature Reserve and SSSI, comprises six sites along the River Bure. It features extensive fen and reedbed, supporting rare invertebrates like the Swallowtail butterfly and nationally important bird populations including Bitterns and Marsh Harriers. The site is a key component of the Broads wetland system.

Name: Bure Marshes (managed sections)
Description: Bure Marshes, a National Nature Reserve in the Norfolk Broads, features floating fen, wet woodland, and open water habitats. It supports rare plants, diverse insects, and various bird species. Active conservation efforts are ongoing.

Name: Burfa Bog (Grassland & Woodland Mosaic)
Description: Burfa Bog is a superb example of a rich wet grassland and wetland mosaic, located in Powys, Wales. It is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI), home to diverse wildflowers, insects, and notable species such as otters and curlew. The site represents an important habitat for biodiversity.

Name: Burgess Field (Oxford)
Description: Burgess Field is a 4.9-hectare nature reserve on Oxford's northern edge. It features open areas, scrub, and mature trees by the River Cherwell. Habitats include fen meadow, wetland, and acid grassland, supporting diverse wildlife like dragonflies and birds.

Name: Burham Marsh (River Medway Tidal Reedbed)
Description: Burham Marsh is a large area of tidal reedbed bordering the River Medway. It supports a variety of wildlife, including various bird species, insects, and rare plants. The reserve is managed for biodiversity and acts as a flood plain. Access is restricted for conservation purposes.

Name: Burledge Hill
Description: Burledge Hill is a species-rich unimproved limestone grassland in the Mendip Hills. It provides a sanctuary for diverse flora and fauna, including rare wildflowers and various butterfly species.

Name: Burnham Beeches
Description: Burnham Beeches is a National Nature Reserve and Site of Special Scientific Interest in Buckinghamshire. Managed by the City of London Corporation, it features ancient woodland, heathland, and ponds, supporting diverse wildlife. It is popular for walking and nature observation.

Name: Burnham Beeches NNR (London Fringe Hub)
Description: Burnham Beeches NNR is an ancient woodland of beech and oak, managed by the City of London Corporation since 1880. This Site of Special Scientific Interest provides important habitats for rare species and is popular with visitors.

Name: Burns Beck Moss
Description: Burns Beck Moss is a lowland raised mire near Bowscar, Penrith, supporting diverse peatland plants including sphagnum mosses, cotton grass, and heather. The site provides habitat for various insects and is managed for wetland ecosystem restoration.

Name: Burntollet Wood (Faughan Valley)
Description: Burntollet Wood spans a steep valley slope overlooking the River Faughan. Mature broadleaf trees dominate, supporting diverse wildlife. Walking trails lead through this ancient woodland, designated an Area of Special Scientific Interest.

Name: Burtle Moor
Description: Burtle Moor is a species-rich wet grassland site, part of the extensive Somerset Levels. It features a diverse range of plant and insect life, including meadowsweet and ragged-robin, along with various dragonflies and butterflies. The reserve provides an important habitat for wetland species within a traditional agricultural landscape.

Name: Burtle Moor (Wet Grassland & Rush Pasture)
Description: Burtle Moor is a wet grassland and rush pasture site with wildflowers and drainage ditches. It supports birds like curlew, lapwing, and snipe. Traditional grazing maintains this unique habitat.

Name: Burton and Chingford Ponds
Description: Burton Pond, a large expanse within the South Downs National Park, offers diverse habitats including woodland and heathland. It supports all 17 Sussex breeding dragonfly and damselfly species. Birdlife includes grebes and gadwall, with fungi in the woodlands. An Iron Age fort remains are also present.

Name: Burton Bradstock & Hive Beach
Description: Burton Bradstock is a National Trust site on the Jurassic Coast in Dorset, offering Hive Beach for swimming, fossil hunting, and rockpooling. The soft sandstone cliffs are part of the South West Coast Path and are subject to erosion. A café is available.

Name: Burton Leonard Lime Quarries
Description: Burton Leonard Lime Quarries nature reserve is a disused quarry site in North Yorkshire with flower-rich grassland, scrub, and woodland. It supports a variety of wildlife, including orchids, butterflies, and breeding birds.

Name: Burton Riggs
Description: Burton Riggs is a former gravel quarry, now a Yorkshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve located near Scarborough. It features open water, reedbeds, scrub, and woodland habitats. The site supports a variety of bird species and invertebrates, offering accessible walking trails for visitors.

Name: Burwell Fen (Wicken Fen Expansion)
Description: Burwell Fen is a significant expansion area of Wicken Fen, forming part of a 60-year vision to restore wetland habitats. Managed by the National Trust, it actively contributes to fenland landscape restoration and biodiversity near Burwell and Reach, Cambridgeshire.

Name: Bury Lodge Wetlands
Description: Bury Lodge Wetlands in Enfield is undergoing restoration work led by Thames21. Efforts focus on improving water quality and increasing biodiversity within the Salmons Brook. Community volunteering events regularly take place to support these ecological enhancements.

Name: Buscot and Coleshill Estates
Description: Buscot and Coleshill Estates encompass traditional villages, farmland, and historic properties in Oxfordshire. The National Trust manages this diverse landscape, offering visitors walking trails, gardens, and preserved rural heritage.

Name: Bushy Park - Water Gardens & Pheasantry
Description: Bushy Park's historic Water Gardens feature canals, ponds, and cascades, creating diverse habitats for birds and amphibians. The nearby Pheasantry area supports various ground-nesting birds and woodland species.

Name: Bushy Park SSSI
Description: Bushy Park is London's second largest Royal Park, covering 445 hectares. It features woodlands, formal gardens, and diverse wildlife, including free-roaming herds of deer. The park holds Site of Special Scientific Interest status.

Name: Butcher's Wood (Tayside)
Description: Butcher's Wood is a small, ancient woodland in West Sussex, near Graffham village. It features diverse broadleaf trees and valuable ground flora, including bluebells. The wood offers peaceful walking trails, contributing to local green spaces.

Name: Butcherlands
Description: Butcherlands is a tranquil woodland and wetland nature reserve, forming part of the Pevensey Levels SSSI. Managed to enhance biodiversity, it offers peaceful walks and opportunities to observe local wildlife near Hailsham, East Sussex.

Name: Butterburn Flow (Great North Bog)
Description: Butterburn Flow, a National Nature Reserve and Special Area of Conservation near the Scottish border, is one of England's largest and most intact peatlands. It supports diverse wetland flora and fauna, forming a key part of the Great North Bog.

Name: Butterburn Flow (The 10-Metre Deep Bog)
Description: Butterburn Flow is a significant deep peat bog located within Northumberland National Park. It forms part of the Border Mires complex and holds Site of Special Scientific Interest status. The bog reaches depths of up to 10 metres.

Name: Butterdon Wood
Description: Butterdon Wood is a young broadleaf woodland in Devon, featuring oak and ash trees. It provides habitat for various woodland birds and wildflowers. The site offers a good network of accessible paths, suitable for wheelchairs, and links to local trails near Buckland Monachorum.

Name: Buxton Heath
Description: Buxton Heath is a prime Norfolk heathland example, dominated by heather and scattered silver birch and oak. It supports woodlark, nightjar, common lizards, and adders. Management involves grazing and scrub removal to preserve its open character.

Name: Bysing Wood (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Bysing Wood, near Faversham, is ancient woodland managed by Kent Wildlife Trust. It features a diverse mix of trees, shrubs, and plants. In spring, the wood is noted for its extensive bluebell displays. The site provides tranquil walking trails for visitors.

Name: Cabilla and Redrice Woods (Ancient Oak)
Description: Cabilla and Redrice Woods is a Cornwall Wildlife Trust nature reserve. It contains ancient oak woodland within steep-sided valleys and a fast-flowing river. The site supports a temperate rainforest restoration project, providing habitat for diverse lichens, ferns, and mosses. Management focuses on expanding this unique ecosystem.

Name: Cable Street Community Garden
Description: Cable Street Community Gardens provides green spaces in Tower Hamlets, East London, where residents grow food, enjoy nature, and connect. A network of garden sites host regular events and workshops, promoting biodiversity and community engagement for local residents.

Name: Cadishead & Little Woolden Moss
Description: Cadishead & Little Woolden Moss is a regenerating peat bog in Greater Manchester, part of the Manchester Mosses SSSI. It is a key area for carbon capture and biodiversity, home to sphagnum mosses, cotton grasses, and rare invertebrates. Active restoration is ongoing.

Name: Cadishead Moss (Peatland Restoration)
Description: Cadishead Moss, a lowland raised peat bog in Greater Manchester, is part of the Manchester Mosses complex. Lancashire Wildlife Trust manages this nature reserve, actively restoring it to re-establish a healthy and biodiverse peatland ecosystem.

Name: Caeau Tan-y-bwlch (Wildflower Ark)
Description: Caeau Tan-y-Bwlch is a traditional hay meadow in the Aber Valley, Gwynedd. It is home to orchids and other wildflowers like meadow vetchling. The site also supports diverse insect life, including marbled white butterflies. Sheep graze the meadow after the hay harvest.

Name: Caer Brân
Description: Caer Brân is an Iron Age hillfort and settlement in west Cornwall, a Scheduled Monument. Visible remnants of roundhouses are within its ramparts. The surrounding heathland supports diverse flora, including rare lichens and invertebrates. Panoramic views are offered.

Name: Caerlaverock NNR (Solway Saltmarsh)
Description: Caerlaverock NNR protects expansive saltmarsh and mudflats on the Solway Firth, a critical wintering ground for barnacle geese and other wildfowl. The site supports diverse coastal habitats, offering birdwatching and walking opportunities.

Name: Cairngorms Connect
Description: Cairngorms Connect is a partnership of land managers working across 600 square kilometres of the Cairngorms National Park in Scotland. The project focuses on restoring habitats, enhancing species, and improving ecological processes over a 200-year timescale.

Name: Calke Abbey (Ancient Oak Trees)
Description: Calke Abbey, a National Trust property in Derbyshire, showcases a preserved country estate with a focus on natural decay. It features ancient oak trees, a deer park, walled gardens, and historic collections, offering insights into its unique past.

Name: Calley Heath
Description: Calley Heath Nature Reserve in South Yorkshire is a mosaic of habitats, including acid grassland, heathland, and wetland areas. It supports diverse wildlife, notably dingy skipper butterflies, dragonflies, and various bird species. The site is managed for conservation to enhance its biodiversity.

Name: Calshot Marshes (managed interface)
Description: Calshot Marshes, a coastal reserve in Hampshire, features saltmarsh, reedbed, and freshwater lagoons. It provides habitat for bird species. The site is managed to mitigate coastal squeeze and adapt to sea-level rise.

Name: Calthorpe Broad
Description: Calthorpe Broad is a fenland area within the Hickling Broad and Marshes nature reserve, located in the Norfolk Broads. Norfolk Wildlife Trust manages this site, part of a nationally important wetland ecosystem designated for its diverse wildlife habitats.

Name: Calvert Jubilee
Description: Calvert Jubilee is a BBOWT nature reserve established in 1977. It features varied habitats including wildflower meadows, mature woodlands, and a lake, supporting diverse bird species such as great crested grebes and kingfishers. The reserve offers walking trails.

Name: Cambourne Nature Reserve
Description: Cambourne Nature Reserve is a 170-hectare site featuring woodlands, wetlands, and grasslands. Three large lakes and ponds support diverse wildlife, including birds, insects, and mammals. Managed by Wildlife BCN, the reserve provides walking trails and birdwatching.

Name: Cambus o'May (Pinewood Restoration)
Description: Cambus o'May is an ancient Caledonian pinewood forest between Ballater and Dinnet, north of the River Dee. Restoration returns it to a natural state, rich in native Scots pine through non-native species removal. Waymarked trails are available.

Name: Canal & River Trust - Grand Union Canal (Blue Corridor)
Description: The Canal & River Trust manages the Grand Union Canal's 'Blue Corridor', a vital waterway supporting diverse wildlife habitats. It provides homes for various species, connecting urban and rural landscapes and enhancing biodiversity across its extensive length.

Name: Candlesby Hill Quarry
Description: Candlesby Hill Quarry, a former chalk quarry in Lincolnshire, features chalk grasslands, exposed faces, and scrub. The site supports diverse wildflowers, including pyramidal and bee orchids, and various insects. It is managed to maintain its unique chalk flora and fauna.

Name: Canna & Sanday
Description: Canna and Sanday are Inner Hebridean islands, a National Scenic Area owned by the National Trust for Scotland. They form a crofting community, known for birdlife, unique geology, and historical sites. Sanday connects by a bridge.

Name: Cannock Chase Forest
Description: Cannock Chase Forest offers a range of activities including walking, cycling, Go Ape, and a children's play area. Managed by Forestry England, it features diverse woodlands, open heathland, and heritage trails. The site provides various trails suitable for all ages and abilities.

Name: Cannop Bridge Marsh
Description: Cannop Bridge Marsh is a small wet woodland and marshland site within the Forest of Dean, Gloucestershire. It features willow carr and open fen habitats, supporting diverse plant species. Fed by springs, the marsh maintains a consistently damp environment, managed to conserve its unique wetland ecology.

Name: Canon Tump Common
Description: Canon Tump Common, a small Herefordshire reserve near Pontrilas, protects ancient oak and birch woodland plus acid grassland. The site supports green woodpeckers and woodcock, with management enhancing biodiversity.

Name: Canons Park (Ecological Corridor)
Description: Canons Park in Harrow is a Green Flag award-winning park featuring formal gardens, woodlands, and a lake. It serves as a vital ecological corridor, linking local green spaces and supporting diverse wildlife. The park offers recreational opportunities for the community.

Name: Canvey Wick
Description: Canvey Wick is a unique brownfield site, renowned for its diverse invertebrate population, including rare bees and moths. It offers grassland and scrub habitats on former oil refinery land, now a Site of Special Scientific Interest.

Name: Canvey Wick (The 'Brownfield Rainforest')
Description: Canvey Wick is a former oil refinery site on Canvey Island, Essex, now a nationally important nature reserve. Recognised for its biodiversity, it supports rare invertebrates, plants, grassland, scrub, and wetland habitats.

Name: Cape Cornwall
Description: Cape Cornwall is a prominent headland near St Just in Penwith, Cornwall, marking England's only cape. It is a former tin mine site offering coastal walks, wildlife viewing, and views of the Brisons rocks, where Atlantic currents divide.

Name: Capel-le-Ferne (White Cliffs Habitat)
Description: Capel-le-Ferne nature reserve safeguards coastal chalk grassland and scrub habitats on the White Cliffs. This site supports a diverse range of wildflowers, butterflies, other insects, and birds, forming a crucial wildlife corridor along the Kent coast.

Name: Captain's Wood (Ancient Trees & Deer)
Description: Captain's Wood is an ancient woodland with impressive oak and hornbeam trees, some believed to be over 400 years old. It is home to fallow deer, a herd of around 200, which are visible year-round. The reserve also supports a variety of woodland birds and wildflowers.

Name: Carbrook Ravine (Urban Wildlife Corridor)
Description: Carbrook Ravine is a narrow urban green space in Sheffield, connecting community parks and providing a crucial wildlife corridor. It supports various birds, insects, and plant life, including ancient woodland species. Maintained for biodiversity, it offers walking paths for local residents.

Name: Cardigan Island (Coastal Grazing Hub)
Description: Cardigan Island, off Ceredigion, West Wales, is an uninhabited nature reserve and Special Protection Area. It supports breeding seabirds like kittiwakes, guillemots, and razorbills. Grey seals frequent the waters. A mainland grazing hub assists conservation.

Name: Carding Mill Valley & The Long Mynd
Description: Carding Mill Valley, within the Shropshire Hills AONB, is managed by the National Trust. It features a deep valley with a stream, leading to the heather-clad Long Mynd moorland. The site offers walking trails, diverse wildlife, and panoramic views.

Name: Cardinham Woods
Description: Cardinham Woods, near Bodmin in Cornwall, provides walking and mountain biking trails. Visitors can explore historical features like a clapper bridge and charcoal platforms. Facilities include a cafe, cycle hire, and a children's play area within the woodland setting.

Name: Carlton Marshes
Description: Carlton Marshes, a Suffolk Wildlife Trust reserve on the River Waveney near Lowestoft, features fens, marshes, and freshwater pools. Part of the Broads National Park, it supports diverse wildlife and provides accessible trails.

Name: Carlton Marshes (The Broads Gateway)
Description: Carlton Marshes is a 1000-acre nature reserve managed by Suffolk Wildlife Trust. Located at the southern gateway to the Broads National Park, it features marsh, fen, and woodland habitats. The site offers a visitor centre, cafe, and accessible paths for wildlife viewing. Otters and kingfishers are present.

Name: Carmel NNR (The Turlough)
Description: Carmel National Nature Reserve, near Carmel village on Anglesey, features Wales' only turlough, a disappearing lake on limestone. It fills and empties seasonally, supporting rare plants like fen violet when dry and attracting wildfowl when full in winter.

Name: Carn Galver & Bosigran
Description: This rugged coastal area on the Penwith Peninsula features dramatic granite cliffs, ancient field systems, and prehistoric hut circles. Views of the Atlantic and Pendeen Lighthouse are prominent, exploring Cornish Mining World Heritage Site history.

Name: Carn Moor
Description: Carn Moor is a lowland heathland reserve near Bodmin, Cornwall, managed by the Cornwall Wildlife Trust. It supports diverse wildlife, including breeding birds, adders, and various invertebrates. Habitats include wet and dry heath, mire, and bog.

Name: Carnewas & Bedruthan Steps
Description: Carnewas and Bedruthan Steps presents a dramatic Cornish coastline, featuring distinctive sea stacks and sandy beaches. Visitors explore clifftop paths, discovering diverse geology and wildlife, with access to the South West Coast Path. A shop and tea-room serve visitors.

Name: Carngafallt (Upland Oakwood & Curlew)
Description: Carngafallt, a mid-Wales reserve, features ancient upland oakwood, moorland, and rush pastures. It supports breeding curlew, lapwing, and other wading birds. Red kites are common. The site is managed for wildlife, with walking trails.

Name: Carnmoney Hill (Honey Pot of Wildlife)
Description: Carnmoney Hill is a local nature reserve with ancient woodland, wildflower meadows, and open grassland. It offers panoramic views of Belfast Lough and is home to diverse wildlife, including red squirrels and long-eared owls. Managed for conservation and public access.

Name: Carr House Meadows (Species-rich Hay Meadow)
Description: Carr House Meadows is a species-rich hay meadow in the Loxley Valley, near Sheffield. It supports diverse wildflowers like ragged robin, great burnet, and betony. The site provides habitat for pollinators and invertebrates. Traditional hay cutting and grazing manage the meadow.

Name: Carr Vale
Description: Carr Vale is a Derbyshire Nature Reserve near Bolsover, a former colliery with reedbeds, ponds, and grasslands. Managed by the Trust, it supports willow tit, snipe, and water voles. Circular paths and hides are available for visitors.

Name: Carsegowan Moss
Description: Carsegowan Moss is a lowland raised bog in Dumfries & Galloway, managed by the Scottish Wildlife Trust. It supports rare plants like sundews, various insects, and bird species. Conservation efforts focus on restoring the bog's natural habitat through rewetting and controlling invasive species, enhancing its biodiversity.

Name: Carstramon Wood
Description: Carstramon Wood is a mixed woodland habitat in Dumfries and Galloway, Scotland. It features ancient oak and birch trees, bluebells in spring, and various fungi. The site is a habitat for woodland birds and red squirrels. Maintained for wildlife, it offers walking trails.

Name: Cartmel Fell (Coastal Hills)
Description: Cartmel Fell offers far-reaching views across the Lyth Valley and Morecambe Bay. This area, managed by the National Trust, includes ancient woodlands, pastures, and several historic farmsteads. Public footpaths allow access for walkers.

Name: Carvers Rocks
Description: Carvers Rocks is a Derbyshire nature reserve featuring diverse habitats: wet mire, acidic grassland, heathland, and woodland. It supports varied plants like heather, alongside birds such as tree pipit and woodcock. Invertebrates thrive in wet areas, with historic mine workings adding character.

Name: Cassiobury Park LNR
Description: Cassiobury Park Local Nature Reserve, managed by Watford Borough Council with the Wildlife Trust, offers diverse habitats including woodland, river, and grassland. It is home to various bird species and invertebrates, providing a valuable urban green space.

Name: Cassiobury Park LNR (managed section)
Description: Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust manages a section of Cassiobury Park LNR in Watford. This area encompasses ancient woodland and grassland, providing habitat for diverse wildflowers, insects, and birds. A wet woodland section attracts grey wagtail and kingfisher. Public access is available via established paths.

Name: Castle Drogo (Teign Gorge)
Description: Castle Drogo is the last castle built in England, completed in 1930. This granite structure overlooks the Teign Gorge, offering historical interiors and diverse collections. The estate features gardens, woodland walks, a shop and cafe.

Name: Castle Marshes
Description: Castle Marshes is a river flood plain reserve with wet grassland and fen habitats near Lowestoft. It supports marsh harriers, bitterns, and otters. The site is managed for nature conservation, providing crucial breeding grounds for many bird species.

Name: Castle Marshes (Waveney Valley)
Description: Castle Marshes, near Lowestoft, is a valuable mosaic of fen, grazing marsh, and scrubland beside the River Waveney. It supports diverse wildlife, including marsh harrier, water vole, and rare aquatic plants. Part of the Broads National Park.

Name: Castle Ward (Coastal Mosaic)
Description: Castle Ward Estate, in County Down, Northern Ireland, features an 18th-century mansion with contrasting Classical and Gothic architecture. Situated on Strangford Lough, its demesne includes gardens, woodlands, and coastal habitats.

Name: Caswell Wood
Description: Caswell Wood is a small ancient woodland in Wiltshire, dominated by mature ash, oak and hazel coppice. Explore the public footpath, which runs through the heart of the wood, connecting with local footpaths and bridleways.

Name: Catcott Complex (Wader & Wildfowl Haven)
Description: Catcott Complex, a key Avalon Marshes site, is managed for overwintering wildfowl and wading birds. It features grazing marsh, open water, and reedbeds. Visitors use hides to observe diverse bird species. The complex plays a crucial role in the Somerset Levels ecosystem.

Name: Catfield Fen (Partner)
Description: Catfield Fen is a nationally and internationally important wetland site within the Broads SAC. Designated as an SSSI and Ramsar site, it is arguably the UK's most species-rich wetland. The site is a private reserve and not open to the public.

Name: Catherton Common (Wild Heath & Skylarks)
Description: Catherton Common is a large lowland heathland in south-west Shropshire. It provides habitat for skylarks, red grouse, curlew, and green hairstreak butterflies. Grazing ponies maintain the heather and grassland mosaic for diverse wildlife.

Name: Cathkin Marsh
Description: Cathkin Marsh is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) in South Lanarkshire, Scotland. It features a mosaic of open water, swamp, and marshland habitats, supporting diverse wildlife including otters, water voles, and various bird species. The reserve offers walking trails for visitors to explore its wetlands.

Name: Cave Hill Country Park (Belfast's Icon)
Description: Cave Hill Country Park is an iconic green space in Belfast, Northern Ireland. It features walking trails, caves, and offers panoramic views over the city and Belfast Lough. The park is known for its biodiversity and historical significance, including Napoleon's Nose.

Name: Cawfields & the Whin Sill (Basalt Grassland)
Description: Cawfields Quarry, a Roman Wall site and Dark Sky Discovery location, sits near Haltwhistle. The Whin Sill, a prominent dolerite intrusion, forms a key geological ridge in Northumberland. Its unique basalt grasslands support specific flora, targeted for conservation by the Hadrian's Wall Wetlands project.

Name: Cawthorne Chase
Description: Cawthorne Chase is a former sand and gravel quarry, now a diverse mosaic of habitats near Cawthorne. It features wildflower meadows, ponds, and woodland, providing homes for insects and birds. Yorkshire Wildlife Trust manages the site.

Name: Cefn Cenarth (Oakwood Lichen Oasis)
Description: Cefn Cenarth is a 3-hectare ancient oak woodland in Ceredigion, Mid-Wales. It boasts internationally important bryophyte and lichen flora, including rare species like Stenocybe pullatula. The reserve supports pied flycatcher, treecreeper, dormice, otters, and bats. Radnorshire Wildlife Trust manages it to maintain ancient woodland characteristics.

Name: Cemaes Head (Pembrokeshire Coast Path)
Description: Cemaes Head is a wildlife haven on the Pembrokeshire Coast Path. It features dramatic cliffs and ancient woodlands. The site is a significant breeding ground for seabirds, including guillemots and razorbills, with seals often visible along the coast.

Name: Cemlyn (Tern Island Colony)
Description: Cemlyn is a significant shingle ridge nature reserve on Anglesey, protecting a brackish lagoon. It hosts one of the UK's most important tern colonies, with Sandwich, common, and arctic terns nesting here. The site features a wardened colony and a bird hide.

Name: Centenary Riverside (Former Steelworks Wetland)
Description: Centenary Riverside is a former steelworks site transformed into a vibrant urban wetland and nature reserve. It features diverse habitats including ponds, reedbeds, and grasslands, providing a home for wildlife and a green space for people in Rotherham.

Name: Centenary Wood
Description: Centenary Wood in Grimethorpe, Barnsley, commemorates the WWI centenary, established in 2014. This developing broadleaf woodland features extensive paths for walking, supports local wildlife, and includes memorial sculptures and a vibrant wildflower meadow for visitors.

Name: Centre for Wildlife Gardening
Description: The Centre for Wildlife Gardening in East Dulwich, London, showcases urban wildlife-friendly gardening. It features diverse habitats, including ponds, meadows, and woodland. The centre provides practical advice and hosts educational events, promoting biodiversity.

Name: Chaceley Meadow
Description: Chaceley Meadow is an ancient hay meadow next to the River Severn floodplain. It supports a diverse range of grassland flowers, including Green-winged Orchid, Pepper-saxifrage, and Corky-fruited Water-dropwort. Traditional hay cutting helps maintain its rich biodiversity.

Name: Chafer Wood
Description: Chafer Wood is an ancient woodland with diverse tree species and a rich ground flora, located in the Howardian Hills AONB. Managed by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust, it features a varied landscape including ponds and open glades, supporting a wide range of wildlife.

Name: Chafford Gorges
Description: Chafford Gorges Nature Park is a 200-acre site in Essex, featuring Lion and Grays Gorges. It supports diverse wildlife, including kestrels and kingfishers, across grasslands, woodlands, caves, and lakes. A path network allows walking, cycling, and bird watching.

Name: Challock (King’s Wood)
Description: King's Wood, near Challock in Kent, features diverse woodlands, open spaces, and ancient trees. It provides walking, cycling, and horse riding trails through a varied landscape, including a Sculpture Trail. The site is managed for both recreation and biodiversity.

Name: Chambers Farm Wood
Description: Chambers Farm Wood, Lincolnshire, combines ancient woodland and modern conifer forest. It offers diverse habitats, including ancient oak and lime, conifer plantations, and open spaces. The site provides waymarked walking trails and a butterfly garden, managed for timber, recreation, and biodiversity.

Name: Chancellor's Farm
Description: Chancellor's Farm nature reserve features flower-rich calcareous grassland, ancient hedgerows, and woodland copses, supporting diverse wildlife. It hosts rare butterflies like the Marsh Fritillary and various bird species. The site offers Mendip Hills views and is managed for conservation.

Name: Chapel Bank
Description: Chapel Bank, a former sand and gravel quarry near the River Lea, is a vital rewilding site. Managed by London Wildlife Trust, it features wetlands, grasslands, and chalk banks, supporting diverse wildlife like orchids, water voles, and kingfishers.

Name: Chapel Pit (Coastal Grassland)
Description: Chapel Pit is a former gravel pit that naturally filled with water, creating a valuable wetland habitat. Part of the Gibraltar Point complex, it supports diverse bird species including warblers and waders. Coastal grassland and scrub provide shelter and foraging for wildlife. The site is accessible via public footpaths.

Name: Chapel Six Marsh
Description: Chapel Six Marsh is a coastal marshland reserve within the Lincolnshire Coastal Country Park. It offers a mosaic of wet grassland and ponds, supporting diverse birdlife and invertebrates. The site is connected by the coastal path.

Name: Chappetts Copse
Description: Chappetts Copse is an ancient woodland, rich in wildflowers and mature trees, nestled on the northern bank of the River Hamble. The reserve features diverse habitats, including riverbank and wet grassland.

Name: Charfield Meadow
Description: Charfield Meadow is a two-hectare meadow, rich in wildflowers, with a small stream flowing through it. Located north of Charfield village, South Gloucestershire, it supports devil's-bit scabious, meadowsweet, great burnet, and butterflies like the marbled white and small skipper.

Name: Charlecote Park (Deer Park & Riverside)
Description: Charlecote Park is a Victorian country house near Wellesbourne, Warwickshire, featuring a historic deer park and landscaped gardens by the River Avon. Managed by the National Trust, it offers a tranquil setting for exploring grounds and observing diverse wildlife.

Name: Charles Sinker Fields
Description: Charles Sinker Fields is a nature reserve near Newport, Shropshire, managed by Shropshire Wildlife Trust. It comprises flower-rich meadows and marshy areas, supporting diverse plant species and associated wildlife. The site is a valuable habitat for pollinators and other invertebrates.

Name: Charley Woods
Description: Charley Woods is an ancient woodland nature reserve in Charnwood Forest, Leicestershire. It features oak, ash, and hazel trees, supporting diverse flora and fauna. Leicestershire and Rutland Wildlife Trust manages the site, offering public access.

Name: Charmouth & Westhay Cliff
Description: Charmouth and Westhay Cliff are part of the National Trust's Golden Cap Estate on the Jurassic Coast in West Dorset. This section features fossil-rich beaches and eroding cliffs, revealing significant geological history for visitors.

Name: Charnwood Lodge National Nature Reserve
Description: Charnwood Lodge is a National Nature Reserve in Leicestershire, encompassing ancient woodland and heathland habitats. It protects rare lichens, veteran trees, and a variety of wildlife. Public footpaths allow access to explore its geological and biological features.

Name: Chasewater Reservoir (managed edges)
Description: Chasewater Country Park features 90 hectares of water and 130 hectares of land. It offers watersports, walking, and a heritage railway. The park's managed edges support diverse wildlife and habitats, contributing to local conservation initiatives.

Name: Chat Moss (Landscape Scale Recovery)
Description: Chat Moss is a vast lowland raised bog within the Great Manchester Wetlands, spanning Greater Manchester and West Lancashire. It is a key focus for an extensive restoration programme, aiming to protect and expand vital peatland habitats and reconnect fragmented wetland sites.

Name: Chawton Field (River Medina IOW)
Description: Chawton Field is a wet grassland site on the River Medina flood plain on the Isle of Wight. This unimproved grassland experiences seasonal flooding, supporting wildflowers like marsh marigold and ragged robin. It is managed by volunteers.

Name: Cheddar Gorge (National Trust Side)
Description: Walk among ancient trees, see incredible views and spot wildlife from the top of Cheddar Gorge. The National Trust manages the upper parts of the Gorge, offering trails and diverse habitats.

Name: Cheddar Wood (Dormouse & Butterfly Core)
Description: Cheddar Wood is an ash, hazel, and birch woodland in the Mendip Hills Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. This Site of Special Scientific Interest supports hazel dormice and nationally significant butterfly populations. The reserve has no public access.

Name: Cheddar Wood Edge
Description: Cheddar Wood Edge is a small nature reserve on the eastern side of Cheddar Wood. It features a mosaic of woodland and limestone grassland, supporting diverse wildflowers and butterflies. The site is part of the Cheddar Complex SSSI and is managed by Somerset Wildlife Trust.

Name: Chedworth Nature Reserve
Description: Chedworth Nature Reserve in Gloucestershire is a steep-sided valley with woodland and grassland. It supports wildflowers such as fragrant orchids and cowslips. Butterflies, including marbled white, thrive alongside roe deer and badgers here.

Name: Chee Dale (The Wye Valley)
Description: Chee Dale is a spectacular limestone gorge in the Peak District, featuring the River Wye and unique flora. It is famed for its two weirs and stepping stones, providing a challenging yet rewarding walking experience along the river. The reserve supports diverse plant and insect life.

Name: Chelmorton Low (High Peak Limestone)
Description: Chelmorton Low is an excellent example of high peak limestone grassland. This nationally important habitat supports a stunning array of wildflowers, including orchids, and insects. Skylarks, curlews, and lapwings breed here. Offers panoramic views of the White Peak.

Name: Cherry Hinton Chalk Pits
Description: Cherry Hinton Chalk Pits, a Cambridgeshire SSSI managed by Wildlife BCN, features grassland, scrub, and woodland. The site supports diverse plant and insect species, and is known for rare orchids and butterfly populations.

Name: Cherryburn (Heritage Habitat)
Description: Cherryburn, in Northumberland, is the birthplace of artist Thomas Bewick. This traditional cottage and farmyard features a small museum showcasing Bewick's wood engravings, a print room, and heritage gardens. It offers insight into rural 18th-century life.

Name: Chesil Beach & Fleet Lagoon
Description: Chesil Beach, an 18-mile shingle barrier in Dorset, links Portland to the mainland. It protects the Fleet Lagoon, a brackish habitat supporting diverse wildlife. It is an SSSI and part of the Jurassic Coast.

Name: Chessel Bay (River Itchen)
Description: Chessel Bay Local Nature Reserve on the River Itchen in Southampton is a vital high-tide roost for waders and wildfowl like curlew and shelduck. Its mudflats and saltmarsh habitats support diverse marine life. Public footpaths offer access.

Name: Chettisham Meadow (Ely)
Description: Chettisham Meadow is a traditional fenland meadow near Ely, featuring a rich variety of wild flowers and grasses. It supports marsh orchid, green-winged orchid, ragged robin, and meadowsweet. The site is actively managed to maintain its biodiversity.

Name: Cheviot Summit (Upland Peat Resilience)
Description: The Cheviot Summit site is part of a large-scale peatland restoration project within Northumberland National Park. This initiative aims to improve water quality, enhance biodiversity, and increase carbon storage in the upland peatlands.

Name: Chichester Harbour National Landscape (Assorted sites)
Description: Chichester Harbour National Landscape, a large natural harbour in Southern England, is a protected Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. The Chichester Harbour Conservancy manages the landscape, focusing on conservation, recreation, and planning within this cherished coastal area.

Name: Chiddingfold Forest (SW boundary)
Description: Chiddingfold Forest is a large woodland on the Surrey/Sussex border, managed by Forestry England. It is a working forest producing timber sustainably. The site supports diverse wildlife, including wood white butterflies and nightjars. Visitors can enjoy walking, cycling, and horse riding trails.

Name: Chigborough Lakes
Description: Chigborough Lakes nature reserve is a mosaic of habitats, including open water, reedbeds, scrub, and woodland. It provides refuge for diverse wildlife, including various bird species and dragonflies. Managed for conservation, the site offers walking trails.

Name: Chigborough Lakes Nature Reserve
Description: Chigborough Lakes Nature Reserve is a former gravel quarry near Maldon, now supporting diverse wildlife. The site features various habitats, including lakes, reedbeds, and grasslands. It is managed for bird species, dragonflies, and other invertebrates.

Name: Chimney Meadows
Description: Chimney Meadows is Oxfordshire's largest nature reserve and BBOWT's flagship site, located within the River Thames floodplain near Bampton. It encompasses extensive grasslands, wet meadows, and river habitats, supporting diverse wildlife. Part of the site holds SSSI status.

Name: Chimswell Wood (Haverhill Woodland Trust)
Description: Chimswell Wood is a young broadleaf woodland, planted in 2002 near Haverhill to commemorate the Queen's Golden Jubilee. It features a pond, open space, and a network of accessible paths. The site provides a local green space for walks.

Name: Chinbrook Meadows (Reedbeds)
Description: Chinbrook Meadows is a public park in the London Borough of Lewisham. It features newly created reedbeds as part of ongoing river restoration efforts within the Ravensbourne catchment. Thames21 collaborates with the local council on environmental projects, regularly hosting volunteering events for community participation.

Name: Chinham Wood
Description: Chinham Wood, an ancient woodland in Wiltshire, is managed by BBOWT. It features mature oak, ash, and hazel coppice, supporting diverse wildlife. Spring wildflowers, including bluebells and wild garlic, provide seasonal interest for quiet walks.

Name: Chinthurst Hill
Description: Chinthurst Hill offers panoramic views towards Guildford and the North Downs, featuring ancient woodlands and a wildflower meadow. Walking trails traverse diverse habitats, supporting a variety of birds and invertebrates.

Name: Chinthurst Hill (Guildford)
Description: Chinthurst Hill, near Guildford, is an ancient woodland nature reserve and part of a registered park and garden. It features mixed woodland, hazel coppice, and open grassland habitats, offering panoramic views across the Surrey countryside with public footpaths.

Name: Chipstead Downs (LNR)
Description: Chipstead Downs Local Nature Reserve covers 75 hectares of chalk grassland and ancient woodland in Surrey. It is a Site of Special Scientific Interest, managed for protected butterflies and wildflowers, supporting diverse wildlife habitats.

Name: Chirk Castle (Estate & SSSI Wood)
Description: Chirk Castle is a medieval Welsh border castle, built for Edward I. With over 700 years of continuous habitation, the estate features ancient parkland, formal gardens, and a Site of Special Scientific Interest woodland.

Name: Chislehurst Commons (Independent)
Description: Chislehurst and St Paul’s Cray Commons span 200 acres in south-east London. Managed by Trustees under a 1888 Act of Parliament, the site features diverse habitats including woodland, grassland, and heathland. It supports varied wildlife like bats and adders. The charity relies on volunteers and donations to maintain the area for public enjoyment.

Name: Chitty's Common
Description: Chitty's Common, a remnant heathland, features dry and wet heath, acidic grassland, woodland, and scrub. It supports diverse wildlife, including woodlarks, Dartford warblers, sand lizards, and adders. The site is actively managed to preserve its habitats.

Name: Chobham Common
Description: Chobham Common, the South East's largest national nature reserve, spans 1,400 acres. It features heathland, woodland, and wetland, supporting diverse wildlife like rare birds and reptiles. The site is managed for conservation and offers public access.

Name: Chobham Common (National Nature Reserve)
Description: Chobham Common is Surrey's largest National Nature Reserve, covering 1,400 acres of ancient lowland heathland. It supports diverse wildlife, including rare birds, reptiles, and insects. A Site of Special Scientific Interest, it is managed by Surrey Wildlife Trust.

Name: Chopwell Wood (Urban Fringe Gem)
Description: Chopwell Wood is a 360-hectare woodland in Gateshead, Tyne and Wear, managed by Forestry England. It offers walking, cycling, and horse riding trails through a mix of conifers and broadleaves. The site supports diverse wildlife and provides educational and recreational opportunities.

Name: Christopher Cadbury Reserve
Description: Christopher Cadbury Reserve, one of Herefordshire Wildlife Trust's oldest sites, has been managed in partnership with the local community since 1964. It features woodland, meadows, and riverbank habitats, supporting diverse wildlife including butterflies and various bird species.

Name: Christopher Cadbury Wetland (Upton Warren)
Description: Christopher Cadbury Wetland Reserve, managed by Worcestershire Wildlife Trust, is a vital birdwatching site. Featuring lakes, shallow pools and wet grassland, it supports diverse birdlife like breeding avocets, terns, and wintering wildfowl. Hides offer excellent viewing.

Name: Chudleigh Knighton Heath
Description: Chudleigh Knighton Heath is a vital wet heathland fragment within the Bovey Basin, Devon. It supports a diverse ecosystem, including purple moor-grass, cross-leaved heath, Sphagnum mosses, and rare plants like bog-bean. The heath provides habitat for various wildlife, including nightjars and Dartford warblers.

Name: Chudleigh Knighton Heath (Heathland Haven)
Description: Chudleigh Knighton Heath is a lowland heath and mire system in Devon, a Site of Special Scientific Interest. It supports specialist plants like sundews and marsh clubmoss. The reserve provides habitat for rare birds, dragonflies, and damselflies, maintained through grazing and scrub removal.

Name: Chûn Downs
Description: Chûn Downs in West Penwith is a designated Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. This exposed site features an Iron Age hillfort and a Neolithic quoit, showcasing significant archaeological history. Heather and gorse dominate, providing habitat for rare plants and insects.

Name: Church Farm Marshes
Description: Church Farm Marshes, within the Broads National Park, features grazing marsh, reedbed, and wet grassland. This Suffolk site provides a home for avocets, lapwings, and water voles. It is managed to benefit wading birds and other wetland species.

Name: Church Wood (Blean Complex)
Description: Church Wood is a large ancient woodland in Kent, forming part of the Blean Complex. It features mature sweet chestnut coppice and oak standards, providing habitat for rare heath fritillary butterflies, various bird species, and diverse flora. Public footpaths allow access.

Name: Churchtown Farm (Community 30x30)
Description: Churchtown Farm is a working farm and nature reserve in partnership with Cornwall Council, located on the River Lynher. It features diverse habitats including saltmarsh, ancient woodland, and arable fields. The site supports a variety of wildlife and offers public access via footpaths. It forms part of the Lynher Estuary Site of Special Scientific Interest.

Name: Chyverton
Description: Chyverton House is surrounded by ancient woodlands, wetlands, and open grassland, managed for wildlife. The River Kenwyn flows through the site, which features historic elements and diverse habitats supporting various flora and fauna.

Name: Cindermead
Description: Cindermead is a tranquil nature reserve comprising ancient woodland near Bredhurst. Dominated by oak, ash, and hazel, the site supports diverse flora and fauna, including many bird species and invertebrates within its undergrowth and along a small stream.

Name: City of Trees (Greater Manchester)
Description: City of Trees plants trees across Greater Manchester, aiming for one per resident. It restores woodlands, creates new green spaces, and engages communities in environmental efforts, enhancing the region's urban and rural tree canopy.

Name: Clanger Wood (Wiltshire)
Description: Clanger Wood is an ancient woodland, once part of Selwood Forest, near Westbury, Wiltshire. It features diverse trees like oak, ash, and hazel, supporting wildlife including dormice. Public footpaths offer access for walkers.

Name: Clapton Moor
Description: Clapton Moor is a lowland wet grassland and rhine system, managed to create ideal conditions for overwintering and breeding birds. This site is part of the Gordano Valley Site of Special Scientific Interest and supports a diverse range of plant and invertebrate species.

Name: Clarborough Tunnel
Description: Clarborough Tunnel, a former railway tunnel near Retford, Nottinghamshire, is a nature reserve. It offers a unique underground habitat for a significant bat colony, including Natterer's, Daubenton's, and brown long-eared bats, managed for their conservation.

Name: Claremont Landscape Garden (Ecological Lake)
Description: Claremont Landscape Garden features a serpentine lake, grotto, and turf amphitheatre, showcasing one of the earliest surviving English landscape gardens. The historic design integrates mature trees and expansive lawns within its picturesque grounds, offering a significant example of garden evolution.

Name: Clarke's Pool Meadows
Description: Clarke's Pool Meadows is a small, quiet nature reserve near Sapperton, Gloucestershire. It features a mosaic of meadows, a stream, and ancient woodland, providing habitat for diverse wildflowers and insects, including scarce species.

Name: Clattinger Farm (The Ancient Meadow Core)
Description: Clattinger Farm, within the Cotswold Water Park, Wiltshire, is a nationally significant ancient meadow core. It is designated as a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI), supporting diverse wildflowers including the Snakes-head Fritillary.

Name: Clawthorpe Fell (Limestone Giant)
Description: Clawthorpe Fell is a large limestone grassland nature reserve in Cumbria, home to diverse wildflowers, butterflies, and birds. Grazing maintains its rich sward. A distinctive 'Limestone Giant' geoglyph is carved into the hillside.

Name: Clay Vallets Wood
Description: Clay Vallets Wood is an ancient woodland, home to uncommon plants like green-flowered helleborine and great butterfly orchid. It is part of the Herefordshire Wildlife Trust's network, managed for biodiversity and accessible via paths. The reserve protects rich woodland flora.

Name: Claybrookes Marsh
Description: Claybrookes Marsh is a small urban nature reserve in Nuneaton, Warwickshire, featuring wetland and grassland habitats. Managed for biodiversity, it supports a variety of bird species, invertebrates, and flora. The site offers accessible paths for local visitors.

Name: Clayhidon Turbary
Description: Clayhidon Turbary is a small, wet heathland and bog in East Devon. The reserve supports sphagnum mosses, cotton-grass, and other bog flora. It is an important site for invertebrates. The habitat is maintained through conservation grazing and scrub management, preserving its unique ecosystem.

Name: Clayton Vale (Medlock Valley)
Description: Clayton Vale is an urban nature reserve within Manchester's Medlock Valley. It features mature woodlands, wildflower meadows, and river habitats. The site offers walking and cycling paths, providing a vital green space for wildlife and local community recreation.

Name: Clearwell
Description: Clearwell is a small site on the north-western edge of York, supporting diverse habitats like ponds, wildflower grassland, wet grassland, and scrub. A public footpath offers wildlife views. Over 100 plant species, including common spotted orchid, thrive here.

Name: Cleaver Heath
Description: Cleaver Heath is a 13-hectare lowland heathland in Heswall, Wirral, managed by Cheshire Wildlife Trust. It features heather, gorse, and acid grassland. The site supports common lizards, green hairstreak butterflies, and various birds. Public footpaths offer access.

Name: Cleethorpes Sands (LNR)
Description: Cleethorpes Sands Local Nature Reserve encompasses diverse coastal habitats including sand dunes, saltmarsh, and intertidal flats. Located on the Humber Estuary, it supports a rich array of bird species, particularly waders and waterfowl, making it a vital site for avian conservation.

Name: Cleeve Heronry
Description: Cleeve Heronry is an important breeding site for grey herons, nesting in tall poplar trees alongside the River Yeo. A viewing platform allows visitors to observe the heronry, especially active during the spring breeding season, supporting diverse bird species.

Name: Clent Hills
Description: The Clent Hills offer far-reaching views across Worcestershire and beyond, providing a popular escape for walkers. This National Trust property features woodlands, parkland, and heathland habitats.

Name: Cley and Salthouse Marshes
Description: Cley and Salthouse Marshes is a coastal nature reserve in Norfolk, managed by Norfolk Wildlife Trust. It features reedbeds, grazing marsh, and shingle beaches, providing important habitats for diverse bird species throughout the year.

Name: Cley and Salthouse Marshes (Coastal Reserve)
Description: Cley and Salthouse Marshes is a Norfolk Wildlife Trust coastal reserve. It features a shingle bank, saline lagoons, freshwater marshes, and reedbeds. The site is important for breeding and migrating birds. It includes a visitor centre with a café, shop, and exhibition.

Name: Cley Hill
Description: Cley Hill, a prominent chalk hill in Wiltshire, offers panoramic views across the landscape. This Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) supports rare wildflowers and butterflies, and holds historical significance as a potential Iron Age hillfort.

Name: Cley Marshes
Description: Cley Marshes is a Norfolk coastal nature reserve, managed by the Norfolk Wildlife Trust. It features reedbeds, grazing marsh, and saline lagoons, providing vital habitat for many bird species. The site offers birdwatching opportunities from hides and a visitor centre. It contributes to local flood defence.

Name: Clifford Common (Riverside)
Description: Clifford Common, a Herefordshire nature reserve managed by Herefordshire Wildlife Trust, protects species-rich grassland and riverside habitats along the River Wye. It supports diverse flora and fauna, offering sanctuary for local wildlife. Public access is available for quiet nature enjoyment.

Name: Clifton Country Park (Salford)
Description: Clifton Country Park in Salford is a local nature reserve with woodlands, meadows, and lakes. Visitors enjoy walking, cycling, and horse riding trails. The park also features a children's play area, a cafe, and a local history museum at Clifton House.

Name: Clints Quarry (Industrial Archaeology)
Description: Clints Quarry, a former limestone quarry near Egremont, Cumbria, features significant industrial archaeology and unique geological formations. It is a SSSI with diverse habitats, supporting rare plants, butterflies, and bird species.

Name: Clipsham Old Quarry (Pickworth Great Wood interface)
Description: Clipsham Old Quarry, a nationally important geological site, is famed for its fossilised dinosaur footprints. This reserve features woodland and grassland, offering diverse habitats.

Name: Cliveden Estate (Woodlands & Thames Cliffs)
Description: Cliveden Estate features 375 acres of formal gardens, woodlands, and Thames River frontage in Buckinghamshire. This historic site includes a grand stately home, various plant collections, a maze, and panoramic views of the surrounding landscape.

Name: Cloatley Meadows (Braydon Forest)
Description: Cloatley Meadows is part of the Braydon Forest complex, a network of grasslands and woodlands in Wiltshire. This nature reserve features traditional hay meadows rich in wildflowers, including great burnet and pepper-saxifrage, supporting diverse insect life. Cattle grazing maintains the habitat.

Name: Close House Riverside
Description: Close House Riverside is a tranquil nature reserve situated alongside the River Tyne, offering mature woodlands and diverse plant life. It provides habitats for various bird species and features accessible paths for walks.

Name: Cloud Wood
Description: Cloud Wood is an ancient woodland nature reserve in Leicestershire, managed by Leicestershire and Rutland Wildlife Trust. It features mature oak standards, hazel coppice, and diverse ground flora, providing a habitat for numerous bird species and invertebrates.

Name: Clouts Wood (Ancient Beech & Ash)
Description: Clouts Wood is a 38-hectare ancient woodland in the North Wessex Downs. Mature beech and ash trees dominate, with diverse ground flora including bluebells and wild garlic. Managed for wildlife, the site offers public footpaths.

Name: Clovelly Coast & Gallantry Bower
Description: Clovelly Coast and Gallantry Bower features dramatic clifftop views over the Bristol Channel, part of the North Devon Coast Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. This area offers diverse coastal habitats and walking paths along a stunning stretch of the Devon coastline.

Name: Clowes Wood
Description: Clowes Wood is an ancient woodland, with a mix of broadleaf and coniferous trees located east of Sheffield. It features oak, birch, and rowan, along with larch and pine. The reserve supports varied birdlife and ground flora, offering peaceful trails for visitors.

Name: Clowes Wood (Ancient Woodland/Heath)
Description: Clowes Wood is an ancient woodland and heathland in Warwickshire, part of the historic Forest of Arden. It features diverse habitats including oak and birch trees, offering walking opportunities near Packwood House.

Name: Clowes Wood (Heathland & Bog)
Description: Clowes Wood is one of Warwickshire's last remaining heathland and bog fragments. It hosts nationally rare plants like marsh clubmoss and supports diverse invertebrates, including moths and dragonflies. The site is a designated Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI).

Name: Clumber Park (Sherwood Forest remnant)
Description: Clumber Park is a significant remnant of Sherwood Forest in Nottinghamshire, featuring extensive parkland, a serpentine lake, a walled kitchen garden, and an iconic chapel. Managed by the National Trust, it offers various walking and cycling routes amidst diverse landscapes.

Name: Clunton Coppice (Ancient Oakwood)
