Description: Clunton Coppice is an ancient oakwood with a diverse range of tree species. It features a woodland stream, veteran trees, and notable ground flora. The site supports various wildlife, including dormice and a rich insect population.

Name: Clunton Coppice (Oak Wood Birdsong)
Description: Clunton Coppice is a mature oak woodland in Shropshire, home to coppiced hazel and sessile oaks. It provides an important habitat for woodland birds and dormice. The site features a rich display of bluebells and other wildflowers in spring.

Name: Clyde Valley Woodlands NNR
Description: The Clyde Valley Woodlands NNR comprises ancient woodlands along the steep river valleys of the Clyde, Avon, and Nethan. The site is important for diverse woodland plants, birds, and fungi, and contains numerous historical features for visitors to explore.

Name: Cnwch Bank (Heather Moorland Gorge)
Description: Cnwch Bank, a steep-sided gorge near Llanwrtyd Wells, features heather moorland, wet woodland, and a fast-flowing stream. The reserve supports diverse wildlife, including otters and various bird species. Its varied habitats are managed to preserve biodiversity.

Name: Coaley Peak
Description: Coaley Peak offers panoramic views across the Severn Vale to the Malvern Hills. Part of the Cotswold scarp, this nature reserve features ancient beech woodland and diverse grasslands, supporting varied flora and fauna including rare orchids.

Name: Coalhouse Fort
Description: Coalhouse Fort is a Victorian artillery fort and nature reserve in Essex, managed by Essex Wildlife Trust. The site features coastal views and diverse wildlife, including various bird species and protected plants, preserving both military history and natural habitats.

Name: Coatham Marsh
Description: Coatham Marsh is a Local Nature Reserve wetland site near Redcar. Established in 1999, it supports diverse wildlife, including breeding birds, invertebrates, and plants. It forms part of the wider Teesmouth and Cleveland Coast SSSI.

Name: Coatham Marsh (Strategic Industrial Wetland)
Description: Coatham Marsh is a newly established strategic industrial wetland in Redcar, Teesside. It enhances biodiversity and improves water quality from adjacent industrial sites, providing a vital habitat for numerous bird species, insects, and plants. The site is a key component of local environmental regeneration efforts.

Name: Cockaynes Wood
Description: Cockaynes Wood is a mosaic of ancient woodland, grassland, and ponds near Wivenhoe, Essex. It supports diverse wildlife including dormice, great crested newts, and various bird species. The site features marked trails for visitors to explore.

Name: Cockey Down (Chalk Grassland)
Description: Cockey Down is a large Site of Special Scientific Interest near Salisbury. This ancient chalk grassland is grazed to maintain its rich biodiversity, supporting rare butterflies, orchids, and wildflowers. Panoramic views and walking trails are available.

Name: Cockles Fields
Description: Cockles Fields is a 3.75-hectare nature reserve near Chard, Somerset, managed by Somerset Wildlife Trust. It consists of two neutral grassland fields supporting wildflowers, insects, and small mammals. The site is actively managed for biodiversity.

Name: Cockshoot Broad
Description: Cockshoot Broad is a small, shallow broad in the Norfolk Broads, accessed by boat from the River Bure. Extensive reedbeds and fenland provide habitat for rare plants, insects, and birds like bitterns. Norfolk Wildlife Trust manages the site for conservation.

Name: Coed Bach (Coastal Grazing & Wood)
Description: Coed Bach, near Llanelli, Carmarthenshire, south Wales, is a small nature reserve. It features a rich mosaic of ancient woodland, coastal grazing marsh, scrub, and reedbed habitats. The site supports diverse wildlife, including various bird species.

Name: Coed Cilygroeslwyd (Limestone Woodland)
Description: Coed Cilygroeslwyd is a small, semi-natural limestone woodland SSSI on Llandudno's eastern edge. It connects the Great Orme and Little Orme, noted for its rich calcareous flora and ancient woodland indicators like small-leaved lime.

Name: Coed Garnllwyd (Vale of Glamorgan Ash Wood)
Description: Coed Garnllwyd is a 2.5-hectare ash wood in the Vale of Glamorgan, featuring mature woodland with diverse understorey and ground flora. The site supports significant birdlife and woodland plants. Managed for conservation, it provides habitat for local wildlife.

Name: Coed Maidie B. Goddard (Ceredigion Wood)
Description: Coed Maidie B. Goddard is a semi-natural ancient woodland reserve in Ceredigion. It features diverse tree species, a stream, and supports various wildlife, including important mosses and liverworts. The site offers opportunities for tranquil exploration.

Name: Coed Pendam (Upland Woodland)
Description: Coed Pendam is an ancient upland oak woodland on the Mawddach estuary, Snowdonia National Park. Rich in mosses, liverworts, and ferns, it forms part of the Pen Llyn a’r Sarnau SAC, supporting dormouse, otter, and peregrine falcon.

Name: Coed Pendithe (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Coed Pendithe is an ancient woodland nature reserve managed by North Wales Wildlife Trust. It features mature sessile oak, beech, ash, and wild cherry trees, supporting diverse woodland flowers, birds, and invertebrates.

Name: Coed Pendugwm (Ancient Sessile Oakwood)
Description: Coed Pendugwm is an ancient sessile oakwood in Montgomeryshire, featuring numerous ancient and veteran trees. It provides habitat for lesser horseshoe bats and woodland birds like redstarts and pied flycatchers. The site hosts specialist mosses, lichens, fungi, and a spring display of bluebells.

Name: Coed Trellychan (Ancient Oak & Birch)
Description: Coed Trellychan is an ancient oak and birch woodland located on the southern slopes of the Mawddach estuary in North Wales. This reserve is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI), supporting important native oak and birch trees, along with diverse bird species and invertebrates.

Name: Coed y Bedw (Ancient Pentyrch Wood)
Description: Coed y Bedw is an ancient semi-natural woodland near Pentyrch, Cardiff. It features sessile oak and downy birch, supporting a rich understorey. The reserve provides habitat for diverse plant and animal species, including badger, dormouse, and over 60 bird species.

Name: Coed y Bwl (The Wild Daffodil Wood)
Description: Coed y Bwl is an 11-hectare ancient woodland SSSI in Pembrokeshire, known as The Wild Daffodil Wood. It features a stunning spring display of wild daffodils, bluebells, and wood anemone. The woodland supports varied birdlife and rare purple hairstreak butterflies.

Name: Coed y Bwlch (Famous Wild Daffodils)
Description: Coed y Bwlch is an ancient woodland nature reserve in north Pembrokeshire, managed by the Wildlife Trust of South and West Wales. It is renowned for its early spring display of wild daffodils. The site supports diverse tree species, wildflowers, and birdlife.

Name: Coed y Cerrig NNR (Glacial Valley Wood)
Description: Coed y Cerrig National Nature Reserve protects a wet valley woodland near Abergavenny, Gwent. Trails explore diverse habitats, including ancient woodland and fen areas, important for conservation. The reserve features rich ground flora and various fungi, offering opportunities to observe seasonal changes and wildlife.

Name: Coed y Felin (Coppice & Deptford Pink)
Description: Coed y Felin is a small woodland nature reserve in Flintshire, Wales. It features ancient semi-natural woodland, a stream, and grassland areas. The reserve is notable for its population of Deptford Pink, a rare wildflower. Managed for biodiversity.

Name: Coed y Jal (Limestone Woodland)
Description: Coed y Jal is a limestone woodland nature reserve managed by North Wales Wildlife Trust. It features ancient woodlands, grassland, and a disused quarry. The site supports diverse flora, including orchids, rare plants, and various bird species and invertebrates.

Name: Coedydd Aber NNR (Abergwyngregyn)
Description: Coedydd Aber National Nature Reserve is a steep-sided valley near Abergwyngregyn, Gwynedd, featuring ancient woodland and a spectacular waterfall. It supports diverse wildlife, including bats and dormice. Walking trails offer scenic views.

Name: Coity Moor
Description: Coity Moor is a low-lying fen meadow bordering the River Severn, near Littleton-on-Severn. It supports a rich grassland flora, including ragged robin and marsh marigold. The site provides crucial habitat for protected water voles.

Name: Cokers Drift
Description: Coker's Drift is a small nature reserve alongside the River Waveney in Norfolk. It features grassland, wet woodland, and reedbeds, providing diverse habitats. The reserve supports bird species like reed buntings and sedge warblers, plus mammals such as otters.

Name: Coldmans Hill (Upland Grassland)
Description: Coldmans Hill is an upland grassland reserve on Farleton Fell, near Kirkby Lonsdale. With drystone walls, it offers views over the Lune Valley. Grazed by cattle, the site supports species-rich plants like common rock-rose, spring-sedge, early-purple orchid, and various fungi.

Name: Cole End Park
Description: Cole End Park is a 12.5-hectare site bordering the River Blythe. It features wetlands, meadows, and woodland, providing diverse habitats for local wildlife. The park is a valuable green space for the community, managed to enhance biodiversity and public enjoyment.

Name: Colekitchen Down
Description: Colekitchen Down is a sloping chalk grassland and ancient woodland site near Gomshall, Surrey. It features wildflowers, diverse butterfly species, and birds like skylarks. Panoramic views of the Tillingbourne Valley are present.

Name: Colemere (Wetland & Woodland)
Description: Colemere is a large meres and mosses site in north Shropshire, featuring open water, reedbeds, swamp, and wet woodland. It is a haven for water birds like great crested grebe and tufted duck. The site is a popular destination for walkers and nature enthusiasts.

Name: Coleton Fishacre Garden & Coast
Description: Coleton Fishacre is an Art Deco country retreat near Kingswear, Devon, built in the 1920s. Its gardens feature exotic plants, streams, and woodland, leading to a private cove. The property offers coastal walks and views across the Froward Point.

Name: College Lake
Description: College Lake is a rich wildlife haven and working farm in Buckinghamshire, featuring a large lake, grasslands, and woodlands. It offers bird hides and nature trails, attracting diverse water birds, waders, and otters. The site includes a visitor centre.

Name: Colley Hill (Managed interface)
Description: Colley Hill is a chalk grassland site on the North Downs in Surrey, offering panoramic views over the Weald. Managed by the National Trust, it features diverse flora and fauna, including rare orchids. Numerous walking trails and picnic spots are available for visitors.

Name: Colliers Wood (Greening the Grey)
Description: Colliers Wood is a 14.5-hectare nature reserve in Moorgreen, Nottinghamshire, managed by Broxtowe Borough Council. Created on former colliery land, it features woodlands, ponds, and meadows, supporting wildlife and offering public paths.

Name: Collin Park Wood
Description: Collin Park Wood is an ancient woodland, home to wildflowers like bluebells and wild daffodils in spring. It features mature trees and offers a network of paths for visitors. The site supports diverse wildlife and is managed for biodiversity.

Name: Collyweston Quarries
Description: Collyweston Quarries is a Site of Special Scientific Interest, comprising disused limestone workings. The site is notable for its rich invertebrate fauna, including several rare species. Flora includes various orchids and calcareous grassland plants.

Name: Colne Point
Description: Colne Point is an Essex Wildlife Trust nature reserve in coastal Essex. It holds SSSI, SPA, and Ramsar designations. The site features saltmarsh, shingle, mudflats, and shell banks, providing crucial habitats for breeding and overwintering birds. Access is restricted.

Name: Combe Bottom
Description: Combe Bottom is an ancient woodland and chalk grassland site in Surrey. It features diverse broadleaf trees, including beech and oak, alongside open grassy areas. The site supports various orchids and butterflies, making it a valuable habitat.

Name: Combs Wood
Description: Combs Wood is an ancient woodland, home to many species of trees including oak, ash, maple, hazel, birch, and sweet chestnut. Managed by Suffolk Wildlife Trust, it features seasonal highlights like bluebells and wild garlic.

Name: Combs Wood (Domesday Heritage)
Description: Combs Wood is an ancient woodland, once part of a much larger area mentioned in the Domesday Book. The site features mature oak, ash, hazel, and hornbeam. It supports a diverse understorey of woodland plants and various bird species.

Name: Comley Quarry (Geological Holy Place)
Description: Comley Quarry is a Shropshire SSSI, exposing Precambrian and Cambrian strata. This site is crucial for understanding early Earth life and geological history. It supports diverse flora, including various lichens and ferns within its varied habitats.

Name: Common Farm (New Acquisition)
Description: Common Farm is a newly acquired nature reserve near Crich, Derbyshire. It features grassland, hedgerows, and a woodland area, providing habitats for diverse wildlife including birds, small mammals, and insects. The site is undergoing initial surveys and management planning.

Name: Common Hill
Description: Common Hill is a 30-hectare nature reserve in Herefordshire with ancient semi-natural woodland, flower-rich unimproved grassland, and open heathland areas. It supports species like dormouse, scarce fritillary butterflies, and various birds.

Name: Compton Castle Estate
Description: Compton Castle is a medieval fortified manor house near Paignton, Devon, home to the Gilbert family for nearly 600 years. This rare, surviving example of a medieval fortified manor is nestled within a sheltered valley. Visitors explore the historic rooms and gardens.

Name: Conigre Mead (Urban Wetland)
Description: Conigre Mead is an urban wetland on the edge of Melksham, created in 1990. It features reedbeds, sedge beds, and wet grassland, attracting diverse birdlife. Boardwalks and a viewing platform provide accessible nature observation opportunities.

Name: Consall Woods (Partner)
Description: Consall Woods, part of the RSPB Coombes Valley reserve in Staffordshire, features ancient woodland, grasslands, and a stream. The site, with a history of mining, supports diverse wildlife including woodpeckers and flycatchers, offering peaceful walking trails for visitors.

Name: Contin (Pine Marten Habitat)
Description: Contin's woodlands are a confirmed pine marten habitat, evidenced by the 2022 survey. Visitors can explore various walking trails, including one providing access to the marten habitat. Facilities include a car park and accessible toilet.

Name: Conwy (Urban Wetland Gateway)
Description: RSPB Conwy is an urban wetland nature reserve on the east bank of the River Conwy. It provides vital habitats for diverse bird species throughout the year, featuring reedbeds, lagoons, and saltmarsh. Visitor facilities and educational activities are available.

Name: Cooks Field
Description: Cooks Field is a small, species-rich meadow in North Somerset, bordering Clevedon. It is home to orchids and a variety of other wildflowers. The site is managed for its biodiversity, providing a habitat for insects and small mammals.

Name: Cooks Fields (Plateau Connection)
Description: Cooks Fields is a series of four wildflower-rich meadows in Somerset, adjacent to Cheddar Wood nature reserve. Forming a crucial plateau connection, the site supports pollinators and barn owls. Actively managed since its 2013 purchase, it offers stunning views across the Axe valley.

Name: Coombe Bissett Down (Chalk Grassland Hub)
Description: Coombe Bissett Down is a chalk grassland nature reserve near Salisbury, Wiltshire. It features diverse flora, including orchids, and supports various butterfly species. Managed for biodiversity, it offers walking opportunities.

Name: Coombe Hill (Highest point in Chilterns)
Description: Coombe Hill is the highest point in the Chilterns with far-reaching views across the Aylesbury Vale. The site features the Boer War memorial and is home to a variety of chalk grassland wildflowers and butterflies. Managed by the National Trust.

Name: Coombe Hill Canal & Meadows (The Wetland Flagship)
Description: Coombe Hill Canal & Meadows is Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust's largest wetland reserve, located on the River Severn flood plain. It is a vital habitat for diverse wetland birds, including ducks and waders, especially in winter. The site features reedbeds, wet meadows, a boardwalk, and two bird hides.

Name: Coombes Valley
Description: RSPB Coombes Valley is a woodland nature reserve in a quiet Staffordshire valley. It features ancient woodlands, meadows, and streams, providing habitat for diverse wildlife, including breeding pied flycatchers and redstarts. Visitors can explore waymarked trails and observe nature.

Name: Coombes Valley & Consall Woods
Description: Coombes Valley is an RSPB reserve in Staffordshire, featuring ancient woodland and open meadows. Visitors explore waymarked trails through diverse habitats, observing wildlife such as pied flycatchers, wood warblers, and otters. The site offers a peaceful natural escape.

Name: Cooper's Hill
Description: Cooper's Hill is a nature reserve near Ampthill, Bedfordshire, featuring ancient woodland with oak, ash, and hazel. It supports diverse wildlife, known for spring flowers and fungi, providing habitat for birds and invertebrates. Managed for conservation.

Name: Cop Lane
Description: Cop Lane Ponds is a small nature reserve featuring two ponds, reedbeds, scrub, and grassland near Penwortham, Preston. Managed by the Lancashire Wildlife Trust, it provides a vital habitat for amphibians and bird species, supporting local biodiversity.

Name: Copedown Wood
Description: Copedown Wood is an ancient woodland on a steep slope above the River Trent. Its diverse canopy includes ash, elm, and oak, with hazel coppice. Spring wildflowers such as bluebells and wood anemones thrive here.

Name: Copinsay (Orkney)
Description: Copinsay is an RSPB reserve, a small island east of Orkney, known for its significant seabird colonies including puffins, guillemots, razorbills, and kittiwakes. The island is also home to a lighthouse and a diverse range of plant life.

Name: Cople Pits
Description: Cople Pits is a nature reserve near Bedford, formed from former gravel workings. It features open water, reedbeds, scrub, and grassland, supporting diverse birds, insects, and amphibians. Footpaths provide access for exploration.

Name: Copperas Wood
Description: Copperas Wood is an ancient woodland on the Shotley Peninsula. It features oak, ash, hazel, and hornbeam coppice, with a rich ground flora including bluebells and wood anemones in spring. The wood provides a habitat for various bird species.

Name: Copt Hall
Description: Copt Hall Marshes is an extensive area of coastal grazing marsh, bordering the Roach estuary in Essex. The reserve supports a range of breeding and overwintering wildfowl and waders. Ditches and ponds provide habitat for amphibians and dragonflies.

Name: Copythorne Common
Description: Copythorne Common is a New Forest nature reserve featuring open heathland with birch woodland, wet and dry heath, and acid grassland. It is home to woodlark, Dartford warbler, common lizards, and adders, alongside plants such as sundew and bog asphodel.

Name: Corehead & The Devil's Beef Tub
Description: Corehead, near Moffat, is a 600-hectare site in the Devil's Beef Tub, southern Scotland. Borders Forest Trust manages this land for native woodland restoration and peatland conservation, aiming to enhance biodiversity and ecological processes.

Name: Corfe Mullen Meadows
Description: Corfe Mullen Meadows is a wetland reserve in Dorset, featuring fen, wet grassland, and stream-side habitats. It supports diverse flora and fauna, including sedges, rushes, and orchids. The reserve is managed for biodiversity and accessible via public footpath.

Name: Cornard Mere
Description: Cornard Mere is an 11.4-hectare nature reserve in south Suffolk, featuring a rare deep-water fen. Surrounded by woodland and unimproved grassland, the site supports diverse invertebrates, plants, and bird species.

Name: Cornard Mere (Fenland)
Description: Cornard Mere is a classic example of a fenland site in Suffolk, supporting rare habitats and a wealth of wildlife including fen raft spiders and dragonflies. Spring brings marsh marigold and ragged robin. The site is actively managed to preserve its unique biodiversity.

Name: Cornmill Meadows Dragonfly Sanctuary
Description: Cornmill Meadows Dragonfly Sanctuary is a vital mosaic of wetland habitats within Lee Valley Regional Park. Over 20 species of dragonflies and damselflies thrive here. Boardwalks and trails provide access for wildlife observation, enhancing local biodiversity.

Name: Corrieshalloch Gorge NNR
Description: Corrieshalloch Gorge National Nature Reserve, near Ullapool, features a dramatic deep canyon carved by the River Droma over thousands of years. A Victorian suspension bridge offers views of the 45m Falls of Measach. Ancient woodland thrives on its steep sides.

Name: Corrimony (Black Grouse Hub)
Description: Corrimony is a reserve seven miles west of Drumnadrochit, near Loch Ness. It features native woodland, open moorland, and a loch, supporting black grouse, red kites, and red deer. A 1.5-mile trail is available for visitors.

Name: Cors Bodgynydd (Upland Mire)
Description: Cors Bodgynydd is a rare upland mire near Betws-y-Coed, encompassing diverse wetland habitats. It features blanket bog, Sphagnum mosses, and cotton grass, supporting a variety of invertebrates including rare dragonflies and damselflies.

Name: Cors Caron NNR (Tregaron)
Description: Cors Caron National Nature Reserve is a vast raised bog in Ceredigion, West Wales. This ancient landscape hosts diverse wildlife, offering accessible boardwalks and a viewing hide. The Afon Teifi river meanders through the reserve.

Name: Cors Crymlyn (Swansea Fen Giant)
Description: Cors Crymlyn is a Site of Special Scientific Interest and one of Wales's largest lowland fens. Part of the Gower National Nature Reserve, it provides a crucial habitat for various rare plants and invertebrates within its unique wetland ecosystem.

Name: Cors Ddyga (Lowland Wet Grassland)
Description: Cors Ddyga is an extensive low-lying wetland and working floodplain in Anglesey, Wales. Home to threatened wildlife like marsh harriers, bitterns, and otters. Restoration has created reedbeds, wet grassland, and fens, supporting a rich diversity of species.

Name: Cors Dyfi (The Osprey Hub)
Description: Cors Dyfi is a 55-hectare peat bog nature reserve in Powys, Wales, managed by Montgomeryshire Wildlife Trust. It hosts a successful osprey reintroduction project, with a visitor centre and hides for observing wildlife.

Name: Corsehillmuir Wood
Description: Corsehillmuir Wood is a small, ancient woodland in North Ayrshire, primarily composed of alder and birch, with pockets of oak and hazel. This site supports diverse bird species and invertebrates, offering crucial habitat connectivity within the local landscape.

Name: Cotehele Estate (Tamar Valley)
Description: Cotehele is a medieval house with terraced gardens, situated on the banks of the River Tamar in Cornwall. The estate includes a working mill, orchards, and extensive woodland walks, offering insights into Tudor life and craftsmanship.

Name: Cothill Fen
Description: Cothill Fen is a unique alkaline fen system near Oxford, part of the wider Cothill series of fens. This reserve supports a diverse range of rare plants and invertebrates, including the endangered fen violet. Water levels are crucial for its delicate ecosystem, which is managed for conservation.

Name: Coton Country Park (Cambridge PPF)
Description: Coton Country Park, 120 hectares west of Cambridge, offers footpaths and cycle routes through diverse habitats. Managed by Cambridge PPF for public access and wildlife, it features meadows, woodland, and ponds. It connects directly to the Coton footpath to Grantchester.

Name: Coton Reserve (Arable Reversion Project)
Description: Coton Reserve is a 35-hectare site near Coton village, west of Cambridge. Managed by CambridgePPF, it transforms former arable land into wildflower meadows, woodland, and wetlands. This project provides vital green space for wildlife and public access.

Name: Cotswold Commons & Beechwoods (NNR)
Description: Discover an unfinished Victorian mansion, nestled in a hidden valley. Explore miles of walking trails through ancient beech woodlands, past lakes and ponds. Spot wildlife and enjoy a tranquil landscape.

Name: Cottage Farm (Omagh - Peatland Discovery)
Description: Cottage Farm, located near Omagh, is a transforming peatland discovery site. Formerly a dairy farm, it is being restored to species-rich wet grassland and wildflower meadows, providing vital habitat. The site supports education and research.

Name: Cotterill Clough
Description: Cotterill Clough is an ancient woodland and Site of Special Scientific Interest near Manchester Airport. This diverse habitat features oak, ash, and hazel trees, wildflowers, fungi, mosses, insects, and birds. A stream supports ferns.

Name: Coulsdon Common
Description: Coulsdon Common, a 130-acre site in Coulsdon, features woodlands and open grasslands. Managed by the City of London Corporation, it provides habitats for diverse wildlife. Visitors enjoy walking and nature observation. It is a registered common.

Name: Coulsdon Common (City of London)
Description: Coulsdon Common is a 112-acre Local Nature Reserve in south London, part of the City of London Commons. It features ancient woodland, grassland, and chalk grassland, supporting diverse wildlife like roe deer and orchids. Managed for conservation and public access, it offers walking, cycling, and horse riding.

Name: Coulter's Dean (Species-rich Chalk Grassland)
Description: Coulter's Dean is a species-rich chalk grassland reserve near Buriton, Hampshire, managed by Hampshire & Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust. The site supports diverse flora, including various orchids, and is home to many butterfly species and rare insects. It features steep slopes and offers panoramic views across the South Downs.

Name: Coulters Dean
Description: Coulters Dean is a chalk grassland nature reserve on a steep north-facing slope near Buriton. It boasts diverse flora, including clustered bellflower and burnt orchid, and varied habitats like scrub and woodland. Managed by HIWWT, it supports a wide array of wildlife.

Name: Cowden Pound Pastures
Description: Cowden Pound Pastures is a mosaic of habitats in the Eden Valley, featuring wildflower meadows, ancient woodlands, ponds, and hedgerows. It provides a sanctuary for diverse wildlife including rare invertebrates and reptiles. The site is managed for conservation, maintaining its rich biodiversity.

Name: Crabtree Ponds (Urban Oasis)
Description: Crabtree Ponds, an urban oasis in Sheffield, is a former mill pond and canal feeder. This wildlife reserve supports diverse species, including waterfowl, amphibians, and invertebrates. Managed by Sheffield & Rotherham Wildlife Trust, it offers a valuable green space for the community.

Name: Crackley Wood
Description: Crackley Wood is an ancient woodland and Local Nature Reserve near Kenilworth. It features diverse habitats, including streams and marshy areas, supporting varied birdlife, invertebrates, and indicator species like wood anemone. Known for spring bluebells, the site is managed for biodiversity and public access.

Name: Cragside (Red Squirrel Stronghold)
Description: The National Trust's Cragside estate in Northumberland is home to a diverse landscape, featuring lakes, waterfalls, and a historic Victorian house. It serves as a vital red squirrel sanctuary.

Name: Craig Cerrig Gleisiad a Fan Frynych NNR
Description: Craig Cerrig Gleisiad a Fan Frynych NNR is a mosaic of habitats in the Brecon Beacons. It features an impressive glacial cwm with a lake, rocky outcrops, and moorland. The reserve supports arctic-alpine plants and various bird species.

Name: Craigellachie NNR
Description: Craigellachie NNR, near Aviemore, is an upland nature reserve in the Cairngorms National Park. It features ancient woodland, heather moorland, and a lochan. The reserve supports diverse wildlife, including peregrine falcons, ospreys, and red squirrels.

Name: Cramer Gutter (Bog & Meadow)
Description: Cramer Gutter is a diverse nature reserve featuring an unusual bog habitat alongside marshy grassland and woodlands. Key species include sundew, cotton grass, and various dragonflies and damselflies. The site supports a rich array of wildlife.

Name: Cranberry Rough
Description: Cranberry Rough is a small fen, carr, and wet woodland nature reserve in the Norfolk Broads. It supports typical fenland flora including milk parsley, marsh fern, and water soldier. Important for breeding birds like Cetti’s warbler.

Name: Cranwich Heath
Description: Cranwich Heath is a dry heathland in Norfolk, providing a vital habitat for rare plants and insects like Grayling and Small Heath butterflies. Its sandy soils support heathland birds, including Woodlark and Nightjar.

Name: Credenhill Park Wood
Description: Credenhill Park Wood is an Iron Age hill fort in Herefordshire. Managed by the Woodland Trust, this site features ancient trees, diverse wildlife, and offers extensive views across the surrounding landscape near Hereford.

Name: Creney Farm (Helman Tor Expansion)
Description: Cornwall Wildlife Trust has acquired Creney Farm, expanding the Helman Tor reserve. This project aims to create a significant rewilded landscape in central Cornwall. It restores natural processes, enhances biodiversity, and provides habitat connectivity for wildlife.

Name: Cresswell Foreshore (Coastal Ark)
Description: Cresswell Foreshore, part of Northumberland Wildlife Trust, offers diverse coastal habitats between Druridge Bay and Amble. An internationally important site, it attracts wintering wildfowl and waders, with rare plant species. Cresswell Ponds is a significant draw for birdwatchers.

Name: Cresswell Pond
Description: Cresswell Pond is a brackish lagoon behind the dunes at Cresswell, Northumberland. It is a top birdwatching site, attracting thousands of overwintering and migrating birds. The reserve supports natterjack toads; recent habitat work enhances conditions for breeding waders and amphibians.

Name: Cribb's Meadow National Nature Reserve
Description: Cribb's Meadow is a diverse unimproved neutral grassland and fen meadow, rich in wildflowers, particularly orchids. It supports a variety of insects, including butterflies, and is grazed by cattle. The reserve includes areas of wet woodland.

Name: Cricklepit Mill (Urban 30x30 Gateway)
Description: Cricklepit Mill is a wildlife haven and historic watermill in Exeter city centre. It serves as Devon Wildlife Trust's headquarters, featuring a garden, pond, and functioning waterwheel. Volunteers operate the mill, promoting urban biodiversity.

Name: Crickley Hill (Joint GWT Reserve)
Description: Crickley Hill is a prominent hilltop on the Cotswold scarp with panoramic views, extensive grasslands, and ancient woodlands. It features an Iron Age hill fort and diverse wildlife habitats, managed for conservation and public access.

Name: Crickley Hill (The Cotswold Escarpment)
Description: Crickley Hill is a prominent hilltop on the Cotswold scarp offering panoramic views. It features an Iron Age hill fort, a Neolithic causewayed enclosure, and ancient beech woodlands. The site is a popular walking destination with rich archaeological and natural heritage. Managed jointly for conservation and public access.

Name: Crimdon Dunes (Local Authority)
Description: Crimdon Dunes, a County Durham dune system designated in 2005, supports nationally and internationally important flora and fauna. It provides habitats for rare butterflies, orchids, and hosts the UK's most northerly breeding colony of little terns.

Name: Crinan Wood (Iconic Atlantic Oakwood)
Description: Crinan Wood is an ancient Atlantic oakwood on the banks of the Crinan Canal in Argyll and Bute, Scotland. It represents a prime example of this rare habitat, home to diverse flora, fauna, rare ferns, lichens, and mosses. The wood's unique ecosystem thrives in a mild, wet Atlantic-influenced climate.

Name: Croes Robert Wood (Ancient Oak & Hazel)
Description: Croes Robert Wood is a small, ancient woodland in Monmouthshire, designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI). It features mature oak and hazel coppice, supporting a diverse range of flora and fauna, including rare butterflies and fungi. The wood is managed for conservation, preserving its ecological value.

Name: Croft Castle and Parkland
Description: Croft Castle and Parkland features a medieval castle, church, and gardens. The site includes ancient oak trees, an Iron Age hill fort, and an 18th-century walled garden, offering woodland trails and views of the Herefordshire landscape.

Name: Crom Estate (Upper Lough Erne)
Description: Crom Estate, situated on Upper Lough Erne in County Fermanagh, Northern Ireland, is a 2,000-acre conservation area. It features ancient woodlands, islands, and diverse wildlife, including pine martens and otters. Visitors explore historic ruins, nature trails, and bird hides. The estate is managed by the National Trust.

Name: Cromford Canal (Water Vole & Dragonfly SSSI)
Description: Cromford Canal SSSI is a vital habitat in Derbyshire, recognised for its importance to water voles and various dragonfly species. The canal, near Whatstandwell, supports a rich aquatic environment and surrounding wetland areas. It is actively managed by the Derbyshire Wildlife Trust.

Name: Cromford Canal SSSI
Description: Cromford Canal SSSI is a linear nature reserve in Derbyshire, managed by Derbyshire Wildlife Trust. It protects the historic canal, vital for water voles, and surrounding woodlands and grasslands, supporting diverse invertebrates and birds.

Name: Crooksbury Common
Description: Crooksbury Common is a significant area of lowland heath and woodland in Surrey. It features ancient oak and pine trees, with heather and gorse covering open areas. The site is home to diverse wildlife, including reptiles and rare invertebrates. It serves as an important habitat for conservation efforts in the region.

Name: Crooksbury Hill
Description: Crooksbury Hill is a significant lowland heathland in Surrey, offering panoramic views across the Greensand Way. It features mixed woodland, acid grassland, and diverse wildlife, including reptiles and birds. A public footpath runs through the site.

Name: Croome (Lancelot 'Capability' Brown Landscape)
Description: Croome is the first landscape designed by Lancelot ‘Capability’ Brown. The estate features miles of walks, architectural follies, and the historic RAF control tower. The parkland and garden are managed by the National Trust, with Croome Court by Croome Heritage Trust.

Name: Cross Hill Quarry LNR
Description: Cross Hill Quarry LNR, Cumbria, is a former limestone quarry now a nature reserve. It features limestone grassland, woodland, and exposed rock faces. The site supports diverse flora, including orchids, and various invertebrates.

Name: Crossness Engines Trust (Nature Fringe)
Description: Crossness Pumping Station, a Grade I listed Victorian masterpiece in Abbey Wood, South East London, houses four beam engines by Sir Joseph Bazalgette. Crucial for London's 19th-century drainage, the Crossness Engines Trust conserves this historic industrial site.

Name: Crowle Moor
Description: Crowle Moor, a lowland raised mire in North Lincolnshire, is a Site of Special Scientific Interest. It features peatland habitats with bog pools and acid grassland, supporting diverse flora and fauna. Managed by Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust, public access is available.

Name: Crown Farm
Description: Crown Farm is a quiet nature reserve near Nantwich, Cheshire, managed by Cheshire Wildlife Trust. It encompasses a mosaic of wetland, meadows, and newly planted woodland, providing habitats for diverse birdlife, amphibians, and invertebrates.

Name: Croxall Lakes (Migratory Bird Waypoint)
Description: Croxall Lakes Nature Reserve is part of the National Forest, located at the confluence of the River Tame and River Trent. It features a mosaic of wetland habitats, including shallow scrapes, reedbed, open water, grassland, and woodland. The site serves as a vital migratory bird waypoint.

Name: Crymlyn Bog NNR (Swansea Fen Giant)
Description: Crymlyn Bog is one of the largest areas of lowland bog in the UK, supporting a variety of wildlife including otters and rare invertebrates. The site is a National Nature Reserve and a Ramsar site, featuring diverse habitats from open water to fen woodland.

Name: Cullaloe
Description: Cullaloe is a former sand and gravel quarry now managed as a wetland nature reserve by the Scottish Wildlife Trust. It features open water, reedbeds, and willow scrub, supporting a variety of bird species and invertebrates. Paths provide access for visitors.

Name: Culloden Wood (Ancient Wood Heritage)
Description: Culloden Wood offers walking routes through native woodland and plantations. Historically significant, it borders the famous Culloden battlefield and features ancient burial cairns. Visitors explore diverse flora and fauna here.

Name: Culpeper Community Garden
Description: Culpeper Community Garden is an award-winning Islington green space, offering individual plots and communal areas. It provides gardening opportunities for local people, run by volunteers. An oasis of peace, it is one of London's few successful community-managed open spaces.

Name: Culzean Country Park (Wild Woodland)
Description: Extensive woodlands within Culzean Country Park offer diverse habitats. Visitors explore walking trails amongst mature trees, spotting local wildlife. The park features a blend of formal gardens, rugged coastline, and natural woodland areas.

Name: Cumbernauld Glen
Description: Cumbernauld Glen is an ancient woodland reserve in North Lanarkshire, spanning 49 hectares. It is a vital green corridor, home to badgers, bats, and roe deer. Bird species like dippers and kingfishers inhabit the area, alongside diverse plant life including orchids.

Name: Cut-throat Meadow
Description: Cut-throat Meadow is a wet meadow and small wooded area beside the River Tame, near Elford, Staffordshire. It is grazed by cattle to maintain a diverse range of plant species. Key flora include Ragged Robin and various orchids.

Name: Cuthbert's Moor (High Pennines)
Description: Cuthbert's Moor is a high-altitude peatland in the North Pennines, managed by Durham Wildlife Trust. It protects significant blanket bog habitat, supporting diverse upland wildlife. The moor stores carbon and regulates water.

Name: Cwm Byddog (Ancient Pollarded Oaks)
Description: Cwm Byddog hosts a collection of ancient pollarded oaks and diverse mosses and lichens. This woodland site in the Radnorshire Hills contributes to a wider network of oak woodlands. The reserve is managed for its rich biodiversity, particularly its arboreal species.

Name: Cwm Colhuw (Llantwit Major Coast)
Description: Cwm Colhuw, also known as Llantwit Major Coast, is a nature reserve in the Vale of Glamorgan. It features a coastal strip with a pebble beach backed by cliffs, supporting various bird species and unique geology.

Name: Cwm Du Glen (Pontardawe Sanctuary)
Description: Cwm Du Glen is a woodland nature reserve near Pontardawe, Glamorgan. Managed by Welsh Wildlife Trust, it features a deep glen with a stream, ancient woodland, and diverse flora and fauna, supporting oak, ash, hazel, and various bird species.

Name: Cwm Idwal NNR (The Glacial Amphitheatre)
Description: Cwm Idwal is a glacial amphitheatre in Eryri (Snowdonia) National Park, Wales. It features the pristine Llyn Idwal and notable rock formations like the Devil's Kitchen. This National Nature Reserve is significant for its geology and rare arctic-alpine plant species.

Name: Cwm Ivy Marsh (Gower Managed Realignment)
Description: Cwm Ivy Marsh on the Gower Peninsula is a successful managed realignment scheme, transforming farmland into saltmarsh and freshwater habitats. It protects against coastal flooding, providing a haven for diverse wildlife like otters, birds, and insects.

Name: Cwm y Wydden (The Micro-Jungle)
Description: Cwm y Wydden is a small, steep-sided valley wood within Dyfnant Forest, a damp, mossy relic of ancient woodland dominated by ash and oak. The site, an SSSI, supports rare ferns, mosses, and various invertebrates, along with birds like pied flycatcher and redstart.

Name: Cwm-bythig (Radnorshire Woodland)
Description: Cwm-bythig is a 5.6-hectare nature reserve in Radnorshire, featuring ancient semi-natural sessile oak woodland. It supports a diverse ground flora including dog's mercury and bluebells, and a rich invertebrate population. The steep woodland slopes are actively managed for biodiversity.

Name: Daisy Nook Country Park (Oldham/Tameside)
Description: Daisy Nook Country Park is a 96-acre site in Oldham and Tameside, featuring woodlands, waterways, and a canal. It offers river and canal walks, fishing, a cafe, and children's play areas, serving as a popular destination for local recreation and nature activities.

Name: Dalby Forest (The Great Yorkshire Forest)
Description: Dalby Forest, in the North York Moors, offers extensive walking and cycling trails, diverse wildlife habitats, and picnic facilities. This Forestry England site provides recreational opportunities and hosts events, contributing to woodland access and nature appreciation for visitors.

Name: Dalston Eastern Curve Garden
Description: Dalston Eastern Curve Garden is a vibrant community green space in East London, built on a disused railway line. It provides a peaceful urban oasis for relaxation, hosts diverse community events, and supports educational activities, operating as a non-profit project.

Name: Dan-y-Graig (Limestone Grassland)
Description: Dan-y-Graig is a sloping bank of limestone grassland overlooking the River Usk in Newport. Managed for rich biodiversity, the site supports various wildflowers and insects, including dyer’s greenweed and greater knapweed.

Name: Danbury Commons and Blakes Wood
Description: Danbury Commons and Blakes Wood features ancient woodlands, heathland, and common land near Danbury, Essex. Visitors explore footpaths, discover diverse flora and fauna, and enjoy panoramic views.

Name: Danbury Ridge
Description: Danbury Ridge, a 147-hectare nature reserve near Danbury, Essex, encompasses ancient woodlands, wildflower meadows, and ponds. It supports diverse wildlife, including fallow deer, badgers, and various bird species. Public footpaths offer exploration.

Name: Dancersend
Description: Dancersend Nature Reserve features ancient woodland, chalk grassland, and a pond, supporting diverse wildlife including birds, butterflies, and orchids. The site provides various habitats for rare species and offers accessible walking trails for visitors to explore its natural beauty.

Name: Danemead
Description: Danemead is a nature reserve near the village of Little Berkhamsted, Hertfordshire. It features ancient woodland, wet valley bottom, and diverse habitats supporting wildlife like dormice, great spotted woodpeckers, and woodcocks. The site is part of the Ambergate SSSI, managed for biodiversity.

Name: Danes Moss (Peatland Flagship)
Description: Danes Moss is a lowland raised bog in Macclesfield, part of the wider Danes Moss Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI). The site is a rare habitat, crucial for biodiversity and carbon storage, currently undergoing extensive restoration.

Name: Daneshill Lakes
Description: Daneshill Lakes is a former gravel extraction site, now a diverse mosaic of habitats near the River Trent. It features open water, woodland, grassland, and reedbed, supporting a variety of birds, insects, and wildflowers.

Name: Daneshill Lakes (Restored Industrial Site)
Description: Daneshill Lakes is a Nottinghamshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve on a former opencast coal mine. It features diverse habitats including lakes, grassland, and woodland, supporting a variety of birds, insects, and wildflowers.

Name: Dark Peak Moorlands (Moors for the Future Partnership)
Description: Kinder Scout is a vast, high moorland plateau in the Dark Peak, Peak District National Park. Managed by the National Trust, it features unique peat bogs, dramatic gritstone landscapes, and is crucial for blanket bog restoration. It offers extensive walking routes and significant wildlife habitats.

Name: Darland Banks (Chalk Grassland)
Description: Darland Banks is a chalk grassland nature reserve near Gillingham, Kent. It features wildflowers such as wild thyme and orchids. The site supports various insects, including butterflies like the chalkhill blue. Managed by Kent Wildlife Trust, it offers views over the Medway Valley.

Name: Darnley Mausoleum and Woods
Description: Darnley Mausoleum and Woods is an ancient woodland reserve in Kent, home to the Grade I listed Darnley Mausoleum from the 1780s. Diverse trees like sweet chestnut, oak, and beech provide habitats. Paths allow exploration of wildlife, including rare invertebrates.

Name: Darsham Marshes
Description: Darsham Marshes is a Suffolk Wildlife Trust reserve, forming part of the Upper Blyth Valley. This important reedbed and grazing marsh supports wading birds like snipe and lapwing, alongside otters and water voles. It lies within the Sandlings Special Protection Area.

Name: Darsham Marshes (Water Vole Habitat)
Description: Darsham Marshes is a Suffolk Wildlife Trust reserve featuring grazing marshes, ditches, and wet woodland. It offers a vital habitat for water voles, otters, and wetland birds. A network of dykes supports diverse aquatic life, managed for rich biodiversity.

Name: Dart Valley
Description: Dart Valley Nature Reserve covers woodland, grassland, and wetland habitats along the River Dart in South Devon. It includes Bow Wood and Bickham Bridge Meadows, offering diverse flora and ancient trees. The reserve is a significant site for otters, kingfishers, and several bat species, with accessible public trails.

Name: Dart Valley (The Wildest Reserve)
Description: Dart Valley nature reserve encompasses the ancient woodlands of Sharpham and the freshwater mudflats of Bow Creek. It is managed to conserve nationally important species such as rare bats, otters, and kingfishers, along with unique estuary habitats.

Name: Dawcombe
Description: Dawcombe is a steeply sloping chalk grassland, rich in wildflowers and insects. Overlooking the M25, it forms a green island between development and arable land, managed for biodiversity. Notable species include chalkhill blue butterflies and glow worms.

Name: Dawcombe (Orchid Stronghold)
Description: Dawcombe is a steeply sloping chalk downland on the North Downs in Surrey. It is a stronghold for orchids and other chalk-loving plants. The reserve features flower-rich grassland, scrub, and woodland, supporting diverse invertebrates, including numerous butterfly species.

Name: Dawlish Warren & Exe Estuary
Description: Dawlish Warren is a sand spit and nature reserve at the River Exe mouth in Devon. The Exe Estuary is a vital wetland ecosystem, supporting overwintering birds and diverse habitats. Both sites hold designations as SSSI and NNR.

Name: Daws Hall
Description: Daws Hall is a tranquil nature reserve and education centre near Lamarsh, Essex, managed by Essex Wildlife Trust. It features diverse habitats including meadows, woodlands, and a stretch of the River Stour, supporting a variety of wildlife.

Name: Deacon Hill (Winchester)
Description: Deacon Hill, a Hampshire Wildlife Trust reserve near Winchester, features chalk grassland and ancient woodland. It supports a variety of wildflowers, butterflies, and bird species. Visitors enjoy walks and panoramic views across the landscape. The site maintains local biodiversity.

Name: Dean Wood
Description: Dean Wood is an ancient semi-natural woodland, designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) in Lancashire. It features diverse tree species, rich ground flora including bluebells, and supports various birds and mammals. The River Douglas flows through the reserve.

Name: Decoy Heath
Description: Decoy Heath is a lowland heathland and acid grassland site in Berkshire, part of the Thames Basin Heaths Special Protection Area. It supports a variety of heathland plants and insects, including rare species like grayling butterflies. Visitors can explore marked trails.

Name: Dedham Vale (Stour Valley)
Description: Flatford, located within Suffolk's Dedham Vale, is a historic hamlet celebrated for its ties to painter John Constable. Flatford Mill and Willy Lott's House, subjects of his famous art, sit by the River Stour. The National Trust manages this site, offering scenic walks and an exhibition.

Name: Dedham Vale & Stour Valley (Haverhill Headwaters)
Description: Dedham Vale and Stour Valley National Landscape, on the Essex-Suffolk border, features low-lying landscapes. The Haverhill Headwaters mark the Stour River's source, flowing through ancient woodlands, meadows, and arable land, supporting diverse habitats and species within this protected area.

Name: Deep Dale and Taddington Wood
Description: Deep Dale and Taddington Wood is a Derbyshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve featuring a stunning limestone dale and ancient woodland. It supports rare plants like dark red helleborine and rigid buckler-fern, alongside notable butterflies. The reserve showcases impressive geological formations and offers walking trails.

Name: Deepdene Terrace
Description: Deepdene Terrace is a historical landscape in Dorking, Surrey, managed by Surrey Wildlife Trust. It features ornamental gardens, woodland, and an arboretum, offering visitors a tranquil space for walks and nature observation.

Name: Deeping Lakes
Description: Deeping Lakes is a former gravel pit transformed into a nature reserve with open water, reedbeds, scrub, and grassland. It provides habitats for breeding and overwintering wildfowl, waders, and diverse plant and invertebrate species. Visitors can explore footpaths and bird hides.

Name: Deer's Leap Wood (Smethwick Oasis)
Description: Deer's Leap Wood is a small nature reserve in Smethwick, West Midlands. It features mature trees, scrub, and grassland, providing habitats for various bird species, insects, and small mammals. The site offers a peaceful green space within an urban environment.

Name: Delamere Forest (Cheshire's Largest Wood)
Description: Delamere Forest is Cheshire's largest woodland, offering diverse habitats and recreational activities. It provides walking, cycling, and adventure trails, supporting various wildlife. The forest features accessible routes for visitors.

Name: Denaby Ings
Description: Denaby Ings is an ancient wetland in the Dearne Valley, South Yorkshire. This vital wildlife refuge features reedbeds, open water, and wet grassland, attracting many bird species, including avocets and marsh harriers. Visitors can observe nature in a peaceful setting.

Name: Dene Park
Description: Dene Park is an ancient woodland reserve in Kent, featuring diverse tree species like hazel and hornbeam, and a rich ground flora including bluebells and wood anemones. The site includes a stream and pond, supporting varied wildlife habitats.

Name: Denemouth (Coastal Dene Habitat)
Description: Denemouth is a coastal dene in County Durham, with steep sides and a beach. It provides woodland and grassland habitats for wildflowers, butterflies, and seabirds. Formerly a mining and landfill site, it is now a regenerated habitat, accessed via the Durham Coastal Footpath.

Name: Dengie National Nature Reserve (managed sections)
Description: Dengie National Nature Reserve, located on the east coast of the Dengie Peninsula, protects vast mudflats, saltmarshes, and shingle beaches. It is an internationally important site, providing a vital wintering ground for Brent Geese and other wading birds.

Name: Denham Lock Wood
Description: Denham Lock Wood is a small ancient woodland on the banks of the River Colne. It features mature oaks, hornbeams, and field maples. The site supports woodpeckers, nuthatches, and various butterflies. It is adjacent to the Grand Union Canal and a railway line.

Name: Deptford Creek (Intertidal Discovery)
Description: Creekside Discovery Centre in Deptford provides environmental education about the intertidal habitat of Deptford Creek. The centre offers guided walks and educational programmes for schools and community groups, fostering connection with the unique ecosystem.

Name: Dering Wood
Description: Dering Wood is an ancient semi-natural woodland near Pluckley, Kent, covering 64 hectares. It features diverse tree species like sweet chestnut and oak, supporting a variety of wildlife. The site is actively managed for conservation and public access, preserving its ecological and historic value.

Name: Derwent Gorge and Horsleyhope Ravine
Description: Derwent Gorge and Horsleyhope Ravine comprises five distinct ancient semi-natural woodlands along the River Derwent, near Consett. The site features steep-sided ravines and diverse flora, supporting rare plants and protected species. It is a vital area for wildlife conservation.

Name: Devizes and Roundway Orchard
Description: Devizes and Roundway Orchard is a community orchard and nature reserve in Devizes, Wiltshire. Managed by Wiltshire Wildlife Trust, it features various fruit trees, wildflower meadows, and hedgerows, supporting local wildlife and providing a public green space.

Name: Dews Farm Sand Pits
Description: Dews Farm Sand Pits in Harefield is a former sand pit and landfill site, now managed as a nature reserve. It features acid grassland, heathland, woodland, and ponds, supporting diverse wildlife, including insects and breeding birds.

Name: Dick Buck's Burrows
Description: Dick Buck's Burrows is a young woodland, planted in 1999, bordering Stoke Row in Oxfordshire. The site features diverse tree species, a small pond, and offers open access for walkers to enjoy the natural environment and local wildlife. Managed by the Woodland Trust.

Name: Dickleburgh Moor
Description: Dickleburgh Moor is a fen habitat in Norfolk. It supports diverse flora including marsh orchids, bog pimpernel, and sundew. Management includes scrub clearance and water level control to maintain the wetland environment.

Name: Digby Corner (Wolds Verge)
Description: Digby Corner is a small nature reserve near Digby, Lincolnshire. It features woodland and grassland habitats, supporting diverse plant and insect life. The site, a former quarry, is now managed for conservation, forming part of the Lincolnshire Wolds Verge. Public access is available.

Name: Dilham Canal
Description: The Dilham Canal is Norfolk's only true canal, opened in 1826 for commercial traffic until 1934. Today, parts are navigable and managed by Norfolk Wildlife Trust for conservation. It is home to diverse wildlife, including otters, kingfishers, and many species of dragonfly. The site offers a peaceful environment for nature observation.

Name: Dimmel's Dale
Description: Dimmel's Dale is a semi-natural woodland in a steep-sided valley near Staunton. Part of a national wildlife corridor, it features ancient hedgerows and a stream. Rich flora like bluebells and wild garlic thrive, supporting diverse birds, rare white-clawed crayfish, and otters.

Name: Dimminsdale
Description: Dimminsdale reserve is situated within the National Forest, near Staunton Harold. It features ancient woodland, limestone grassland, and disused quarries. The site supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare plants and butterflies, with footpaths for exploration.

Name: Dinefwr (Ancient Parkwood & White Cattle)
Description: Dinefwr is a National Nature Reserve encompassing an ancient park, medieval castle, and historic house near Llandeilo. It hosts a herd of rare White Park Cattle. Visitors can explore woodlands, wetlands, and open parkland, supporting diverse wildlife. The estate offers walking trails and tranquil natural beauty.

Name: Dingle Marshes
Description: Dingle Marshes is a coastal wetland reserve in Suffolk, encompassing shingle, dunes, freshwater lagoons, reedbeds, and grazing marsh. This internationally important site supports diverse bird species, including avocet and marsh harrier, and offers habitats for otters and rare plants. It forms part of the Suffolk Coast and Heaths AONB.

Name: Dingle Marshes (Coastal Mosaic)
Description: Dingle Marshes is a coastal wetland in Suffolk, part of the Suffolk Coast National Nature Reserve. It features freshwater, brackish, and saltwater marshes, shingle, reedbeds, and wet grassland, supporting breeding birds, invertebrates, and coastal plants.

Name: Dinnington Community Woodland (Rotherham)
Description: Dinnington Community Woodland is a popular local park in Rotherham. TCV South Yorkshire manages the site for nature conservation and community benefit. It offers valuable green space for the local community and supports diverse wildlife habitats.

Name: Ditchford Lakes and Meadows
Description: Ditchford Lakes and Meadows is a nature reserve near Wellingborough, Northamptonshire, encompassing lakes, ponds, wet grassland, and ancient hedgerows. It provides a habitat for diverse wildlife, including overwintering wildfowl and many insect species. The site is actively managed.

Name: Ditchling Beacon
Description: Ditchling Beacon is the highest point in East Sussex, a nature reserve offering dramatic views across the South Downs National Park. Managed by Sussex Wildlife Trust, the site is home to diverse flora including orchids and rare insect species.

Name: Ditchling Down
Description: Ditchling Down, part of South Downs National Park, East Sussex, features chalk grassland with expansive views across the Weald and towards the coast. It is home to diverse wildlife, including rare butterflies. Walking trails and paragliding areas are present, maintained by the National Trust.

Name: Divis & Black Mountain (Belfast Super-Site)
Description: Divis and the Black Mountain is a vast upland site overlooking Belfast. It offers extensive walking trails, diverse habitats, and panoramic views across Northern Ireland. Home to varied wildlife, the site provides a rugged natural escape on the city's edge.

Name: Doe Lea (Hidden Birdwatching Gem)
Description: Doe Lea Local Nature Reserve, 14 hectares of grassland and woodland, offers accessible paths, a circular walk, benches, and a bird hide. Once an opencast coal mine, the site supports significant biodiversity, recording over 30 bird species. Weekly volunteer tasks maintain the area.

Name: Dogsthorpe Star Pit
Description: Dogsthorpe Star Pit is a disused brick clay pit, now a nature reserve. It features diverse habitats including deep water, reedbeds, grassland, and scrub. The site is notable for its rich invertebrate fauna, particularly dragonflies and beetles. It is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI).

Name: Dole Wood
Description: Dole Wood is a 40-hectare ancient woodland near Thurlby, Bourne, dating back to at least 1600. It features mature oak, ash, and hazel trees, supporting a rich ground flora like bluebells and ramsons. The site provides habitat for diverse birdlife and mammals.

Name: Dolebury Warren
Description: Dolebury Warren is an ancient hillfort on a limestone ridge, overlooking the Wrington Vale. It features flower-rich grasslands, scrub, and woodland, supporting a variety of wildlife including rare butterflies. The site offers panoramic views and archaeological interest. Public access is permitted.

Name: Dolforwyn Woods (The Herb Paris Wood)
Description: Dolforwyn Woods is a steep, west-facing ancient woodland with a stream-fed dingle at its heart. It is one of the best sites in Montgomeryshire for herb paris, a rare plant that gives the wood its alternative name. The woodland habitat supports a diverse range of plant and insect species.

Name: Dolgoch Quarry
Description: Dolgoch Quarry is a former quarry in the Shropshire Hills, managed by Shropshire Wildlife Trust. The site features exposed rock faces, spoil heaps, and acidic grassland. It supports notable plant species including moonwort and adder's-tongue fern. Lizards, newts, and diverse insect life are present.

Name: Dollar Glen
Description: Dollar Glen is a woodland glen and burn with steep slopes and diverse trees. Home to the 15th-century Castle Campbell, it features waterfalls along the fast-flowing Burn of Dollar. Footpaths offer stunning views of the Ochil Hills and access to the historic castle.

Name: Dollis Brook (River Restoration)
Description: Thames21 and partners restore Dollis Brook, a North London tributary of the River Brent. The project enhances natural river features, creates habitats, and reduces flood risk with sustainable drainage systems. Community engagement is key.

Name: Dollypers Hill
Description: Dollypers Hill is a small nature reserve in Surrey. It features ancient woodland, coppiced hazel, and a diversity of flora and fauna, including dormice and various bird species. The site offers walking trails and is managed for wildlife conservation.

Name: Dolydd Hafren (River Severn Mosaic)
Description: Dolydd Hafren, managed by Montgomeryshire Wildlife Trust, protects wet grassland and fen habitats near Llanidloes, Powys. Along the River Severn, its floodplain meadows support otters, curlews, and diverse insects within the upper Severn valley ecosystem.

Name: Dommett Wood
Description: Dommett Wood is an ancient 28-hectare woodland near Buckland St Mary, with greensand, acidic and neutral soils. It hosts nationally important ancient trees. Managed by coppicing and glade creation, it supports diverse woodland wildlife.

Name: Don Catchment Rivers Trust (Industrial Recovery)
Description: The Don Catchment Rivers Trust works to restore the rivers, streams, and wetlands across the River Don and Rother catchments. It addresses the legacy of industrialisation by improving water quality, enhancing habitats, and re-establishing fish passage for wildlife and local communities.

Name: Donna Nook (Grey Seal Breeding Colony)
Description: Donna Nook National Nature Reserve is a Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust site, renowned for its grey seal breeding colony in autumn. The coastal reserve features dunes, mudflats, and saltmarsh, providing vital habitat. Visitors can observe the seals from designated viewing areas.

Name: Donna Nook National Nature Reserve
Description: Donna Nook National Nature Reserve is a coastal site in Lincolnshire, managed by the Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust. It features sand dunes and saltmarsh, crucial habitats for migrating birds. The reserve is renowned for its large grey seal colony, particularly active during the autumn and winter pupping season.

Name: Dover's Hill (Cotswold Escarpment)
Description: Dover's Hill offers panoramic views across the Vale of Evesham, from the Malvern Hills to the Black Mountains. This National Trust site hosts the annual 'Cotswold Olimpick Games' and is a key part of the Cotswolds AONB.

Name: Downe Bank
Description: Downe Bank is a small chalk grassland and ancient woodland nature reserve, celebrated for its unique flora and fauna. Charles Darwin conducted significant research here, particularly on orchids and insectivorous plants, contributing to his evolutionary theories. The site features rich biodiversity, including various butterfly species and ancient trees, managed to preserve its ecological and historical value.

Name: Downes' Meadow
Description: Downes' Meadow is a small, species-rich grassland reserve in Herefordshire. Its flora includes green-winged orchid, betony, and sneezewort. The site is managed through grazing by cattle and regular cutting, maintaining its biodiverse habitat.

Name: Downhill Demesne (Cliff-top Grassland)
Description: Downhill Demesne features a clifftop walk with views of the Causeway Coast and Mussenden Temple. The historic estate includes gardens and woodland. The site offers exploration of its natural and architectural heritage, alongside wildlife observation opportunities within its coastal environment.

Name: Downhill Meadow
Description: Downhill Meadow, Cornwall, is a small meadow featuring an impressive early summer wildflower display, including ragged-robin and northern marsh-orchid. A small stream and mature trees create diverse wetland habitats. Managed for biodiversity, it supports local insects, birds, and mammals.

Name: Downs Banks (Midlands Wilderness)
Description: Downs Banks is a scenic area of ancient woodland and heathland in Staffordshire, offering walking trails and wildlife habitats. Managed by the National Trust, it provides a tranquil escape near Stone with varied flora and fauna.

Name: Doxey Marshes (Stafford Wetland Hub)
Description: Doxey Marshes is a nationally important wetland in Stafford, a designated Site of Special Scientific Interest. It features diverse birdlife and habitats, including wet grassland and reedbed, making it a popular area for birdwatching.

Name: Drakelow Nature Reserve
Description: Drakelow Nature Reserve, near Burton-on-Trent, is a lowland heathland and woodland site. It features acid grassland, heathland, woodland, and ponds, supporting diverse wildlife. The reserve is part of the Trent Valley Living Landscape partnership.

Name: Draycote Meadows
Description: Draycote Meadows is a site of Special Scientific Interest near Draycote Water. This traditional hay meadow supports a rich diversity of wildflowers and grasses, including green-winged orchids. The reserve is home to various insects like butterflies and grasshoppers, and birds such as skylarks.

Name: Draycott Sleights
Description: Draycott Sleights is a grassland reserve on the Mendip Hills in Somerset, designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest. It features limestone grassland and ancient woodland, supporting diverse flora and fauna, including rare butterflies and orchids. Grazing maintains its ecological value.

Name: Droitwich Canals (Reedbed Restoration)
Description: The Droitwich Canals, restored in 2010, connect the Worcester & Birmingham Canal with the River Severn. A vital wildlife corridor, it supports otters, kingfishers, and dragonflies. Ongoing conservation efforts maintain and enhance its crucial reedbed and wetland habitats.

Name: Dropping Well Farm (Heathland Link)
Description: Dropping Well Farm is a former arable field being restored to heathland, forming a vital link between The Devil's Spittleful and Hartlebury Common. It supports heathland species including grayling butterflies and woodlarks. Part of a larger conservation effort.

Name: Dropshort Marsh
Description: Dropshort Marsh is a nature reserve near Leighton Buzzard, featuring wet grassland, reedbeds, and ditches. It supports diverse wildlife, including water voles, otters, and various bird species. The site undergoes active management to maintain its habitats and biodiversity.

Name: Drovers Estate (Woodland Trails & Bats)
Description: Drovers Estate features ancient woodlands, downland, and arable fields in Sussex. It supports over 100 lichen species, rare butterflies, and several bat species like serotine and brown long-eared bats. Visitors can enjoy woodland trails and views across the Sussex Weald.

Name: Drumburgh Moss NNR
Description: Drumburgh Moss is a National Nature Reserve and important raised bog in Cumbria. It supports rare bog plants, insects like the white-faced darter dragonfly, and birds such as snipe. Habitat restoration work includes rewetting and scrub removal.

Name: Drumnaph Wood (Maghera)
Description: Drumnaph Wood is a mature native woodland in Maghera, County Londonderry, serving as a significant wildlife site. It features diverse tree species, rich ground flora, and supports various bird and mammal populations.

Name: Druridge Pools
Description: Druridge Pools, a Northumberland Wildlife Trust nature reserve, offers excellent birdwatching opportunities on the Northumberland coast. It features open water, marsh, and grassland habitats. A haven for wading birds, ducks, and geese, particularly during migration. Management focuses on supporting diverse wildlife, including invertebrates.

Name: Dry Sandford Fane (Geological Site)
Description: Dry Sandford Pit is a disused quarry exposing rocks from the Upper Greensand and Corallian series. This key geological site features significant fossil remains. The pit's edges provide habitats for diverse plants and invertebrates.

Name: Dry Sandford Pit
Description: Dry Sandford Pit, a former quarry near Abingdon, is now a nature reserve managed by BBOWT. It hosts diverse habitats like grassland and woodland, supporting wildflowers, insects, and birds. Geologically, it exposes Corallian Limestone.

Name: Dryhill
Description: Dryhill nature reserve, near Sundridge, covers acidic grassland and heathland in Kent. Elevated, it offers panoramic views. Rare plants thrive on acid soils, supporting invertebrates and reptiles. Sheep grazing helps maintain the habitat.

Name: Dubbs Moss (Species-rich Wetland)
Description: Dubbs Moss is a species-rich wetland in Cumbria, home to notable plants and insects. It is one of the Trust's oldest reserves, managed for its diverse flora and fauna, including rare sedges and dragonflies. The site features various wetland habitats.

Name: Dubwath Silver Meadows
Description: Dubwath Silver Meadows is a wetland nature reserve near Bassenthwaite Lake, managed by Cumbria Wildlife Trust. It features reedbeds, ponds, and wet grassland, providing habitat for otters, ospreys, and diverse birds. Accessible boardwalks and hides are available for visitors.

Name: Duck Gutter
Description: Duck Gutter Nature Reserve in South Yorkshire is a wet grassland and fen habitat. It supports diverse plants such as water violets and marsh orchids, and breeding wading birds like snipe and redshank. The reserve features open water, ditches, and reedbeds, managed to maintain its wetland ecosystem.

Name: Duckhole Common
Description: Duckhole Common is a small nature reserve in South Gloucestershire, featuring a mosaic of woodland, grassland, and scrub. It provides important habitats for diverse wildlife, including various insects, birds, and wildflowers. The site is managed for conservation, maintaining its ecological value.

Name: Duckmanton Railway Cutting (Geological SSSI)
Description: Duckmanton Railway Cutting is a geological SSSI near Chesterfield, showcasing significant rock sequences from the early Permian and Carboniferous periods. It provides a valuable insight into ancient environments and is used for research and education.

Name: Duckpool & Sandymouth Bay
Description: Sandymouth Bay and nearby Duckpool are National Trust sites on the North Cornwall coast. The bay features a pebble ridge, extensive sand, and rock pools at low tide. Popular for surfing and walks along the South West Coast Path. A café and facilities are available during peak seasons.

Name: Duckworth Meadows
Description: Duckworth Meadows is a hay meadow reserve in Kendal, Cumbria. It supports diverse wildflowers like ragged robin, great burnet, and meadowsweet. Managed for biodiversity, it provides habitat for insects and ground-nesting birds within its valuable grassland.

Name: Dudley No. 1 Canal (Bat Conservation)
Description: Dudley No. 1 Canal runs 10 miles through the Black Country, Stourton to Tipton, including Netherton Tunnel. It hosts a nationally important population of horseshoe bats, especially near Bumblehole, alongside notable industrial heritage.

Name: Dudley No. 2 Canal (Windmill End)
Description: The Dudley No. 2 Canal, a 2.5-mile branch, connects Parkhead Junction to Windmill End, linking with the Stourbridge Canal. It features the Netherton Tunnel and the partially collapsed Lapal Tunnel.

Name: Dudley's Path to Nature Recovery
Description: Dudley's Path to Nature Recovery is a Birmingham and Black Country Wildlife Trust project. It aims to improve and create connected nature-rich habitats across Dudley borough. The initiative involves local communities and landowners in restoring habitats and developing projects on nature reserves.

Name: Dudmaston (Comer Woods)
Description: Dudmaston Estate offers Comer Woods, a popular area for walking and cycling trails. The wider estate features an 18th-century hall, historic gardens, and extensive parkland. Visitors can explore diverse art and natural history collections.

Name: Duffield Frith (Ancient Royal Forest Remnants)
Description: Duffield Frith comprises ancient royal forest remnants across Derbyshire, historically encompassing Kedleston Estate. The area, once a vast hunting ground, now features fragmented woodlands and parkland, preserving glimpses of its medieval past. It reflects diverse land management.

Name: Dukes Wood
Description: Dukes Wood Nature Reserve, near Eakring, Nottinghamshire, spans 12 hectares. It features ancient woodland, wildflower meadows, and ponds, supporting diverse wildlife. Historically significant for early UK oil exploration, it is now managed for conservation and public access.

Name: Dukes Wood (Industrial Heritage & Oilfield)
Description: Dukes Wood Nature Reserve is an internationally important industrial heritage site near Eakring, Nottinghamshire. It preserves the UK's first commercial oilfield, operational since 1939. The site features original derricks, historical machinery, and rich woodland habitats.

Name: Dulwich Upper Wood
Description: Dulwich Upper Wood is a Local Nature Reserve in South London, one of four remnants of the Great North Wood. Managed by TCV, it features ancient woodland with oak, hornbeam, and ash, supporting diverse wildlife. The site offers a tranquil escape, promoting biodiversity.

Name: Duncliffe Wood (Dorset)
Description: Duncliffe Wood is an ancient woodland spanning over 92 hectares near Shaftesbury, Dorset. It offers diverse broadleaf habitats and open glades on a prominent hilltop. Managed by the Woodland Trust, the site provides marked trails for visitors to explore, supporting local wildlife populations and offering panoramic views.

Name: Dundon Beacon
Description: Dundon Beacon is a nature reserve near Somerton, offering views across the Somerset Levels. The site features grassland, scrub, and woodland habitats, supporting diverse wildlife, including various birds, insects, and plant species.

Name: Dundreggan Rewilding Centre
Description: Dundreggan Rewilding Centre, near Loch Ness, offers an immersive experience into rewilding. It features a tree nursery and accessible trail, showcasing efforts to restore Scotland's wild forest and biodiversity. The centre opened in 2023.

Name: Dungeness (National Nature Reserve - KWT sections)
Description: Dungeness National Nature Reserve, managed by Kent Wildlife Trust, is a vast shingle landscape important for unique flora and fauna, including rare insects and birds. It provides a diverse habitat for many species and is a significant coastal site.

Name: Dunham Massey (Ancient Deer Park)
Description: Dunham Massey is an ancient deer park, historic house, and garden located in Altrincham, Greater Manchester. It features a herd of fallow deer, a winter garden, and a grand Edwardian house. The estate offers accessible routes and seasonal attractions.

Name: Dunkeld - The Hermitage
Description: The Hermitage is a picturesque woodland garden near Dunkeld, Perthshire, featuring towering Douglas firs, scenic walks, and Ossian's Hall overlooking the Black Linn Falls. Part of Tay Forest Park, it offers accessible trails along the River Braan.

Name: Dunkery & Horner Wood (Exmoor)
Description: Dunkery & Horner Wood, a National Nature Reserve on Exmoor, is part of the Holnicote Estate. It features ancient oak woodlands, including Horner Wood, and Dunkery Beacon, Exmoor's highest point. The site supports diverse wildlife.

Name: Dunnerdale Fells (Duddon Valley)
Description: Dunnerdale Fells, within the Duddon Valley in the Lake District National Park, features ancient woodlands and high fells. The River Duddon flows through this secluded landscape, supporting diverse wildlife. The area offers various walking opportunities.

Name: Dunnet Head (Northernmost Point)
Description: Dunnet Head is mainland Great Britain's most northerly point, offering panoramic coastal views. Its cliffs and heathland host puffins, guillemots, razorbills, and kittiwakes. Marine wildlife like seals and dolphins are often seen. It is a vital seabird breeding ground.

Name: Dunollie Wood (Oban)
Description: Dunollie Wood is an ancient woodland situated on a hillside overlooking Oban Bay, offering views towards Mull and Lismore. This small, historically significant site features mature oak trees and diverse ground flora including bluebells. The Woodland Trust manages it for conservation and public access.

Name: Dunscombe Bottom (Salisbury Plain Edge)
Description: Dunscombe Bottom is a steep-sided chalk valley with a species-rich grassland in Wiltshire. The reserve is grazed by cattle to maintain its diverse flora, including orchids and rare plants. It forms part of the Salisbury Plain Special Area of Conservation.

Name: Dunsdon (Culm Grassland Flagship)
Description: Dunsdon Nature Reserve, a Devon Wildlife Trust flagship Culm grassland, lies in North Devon. This wet grassland hosts rare plants like wavy St John's-wort and marsh violet. It supports insects such as the marsh fritillary butterfly. Traditional management maintains rich biodiversity.

Name: Dunsdon National Nature Reserve
Description: Dunsdon National Nature Reserve, located in North Devon, protects a significant area of culm grassland. Managed by Devon Wildlife Trust, it provides a crucial habitat for rare butterflies, including the Marsh Fritillary, and supports diverse plant life. The reserve offers waymarked trails for visitors.

Name: Dunsford (The Wild Daffodil Valley)
Description: Dunsford Reserve is a picturesque valley on the River Teign, famed for its extensive display of wild daffodils each spring. This ancient woodland supports diverse wildlife, including many bird species and mammals, offering accessible walking trails for visitors.

Name: Dunsmore Living Landscape (Rugby to Leamington)
Description: The Dunsmore Living Landscape connects existing nature reserves, ancient woodlands, ponds, and hedgerows between Rugby and Leamington Spa. This partnership project enhances biodiversity and promotes sustainable land management across a wide area of Warwickshire.

Name: Dunstable Downs and Whipsnade Estate
Description: Dunstable Downs and Whipsnade Estate features rolling chalk grassland with extensive views over the Vale of Aylesbury. Located within the Chilterns Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty, it supports diverse wildlife and is a popular destination for walking and kite flying.

Name: Dunwich Forest (Managed Sections)
