Description: Dunwich Forest is a mixed woodland of conifer and broad-leaved trees along Suffolk's coast. Suffolk Wildlife Trust manages specific woodland and heathland sections, including Westleton Walks, within the larger forest owned by Forestry England. These areas support diverse wildlife.

Name: Dunwich Heath and Beach
Description: Dunwich Heath and Beach is a Suffolk coastal site managed by the National Trust. It features lowland heath, shingle beach, and offers diverse habitats for rare plants and birds such as woodlarks and nightjars. Visitors can explore walking trails.

Name: Dutton Park Farm
Description: Dutton Park Farm is a nature reserve near Northwich, Cheshire, featuring diverse habitats like wildflower meadows, ponds, and wetlands. It supports various wildlife, including birds and insects. Cheshire Wildlife Trust manages the site for conservation.

Name: Dyfi: Ynyslas NNR (The Nature Hub)
Description: Ynyslas National Nature Reserve, within the Dyfi Biosphere, features a diverse landscape of dunes, saltmarsh, peatland, and estuary. The Nature Hub serves as a visitor centre, providing information, refreshments, and essential facilities for exploring this significant natural area.

Name: Dyfnant Meadows (Hidden Coronation Meadows)
Description: Dyfnant Meadows, a hidden gem in Powys, features diverse wildflower meadows. This site supports various birds like meadow pipit and skylark, alongside numerous butterflies and invertebrates, offering an important habitat.

Name: Dyrham Park (Ancient Deer Park)
Description: Dyrham Park is a 17th-century baroque house, garden, and ancient deer park near Bath. The estate covers 270 acres, featuring parkland and a formal garden against the Cotswold Edge. A herd of fallow deer inhabits the park.

Name: Dyscarr Wood
Description: Dyscarr Wood is an ancient woodland in Nottinghamshire, part of the wider Sherwood Forest area. Mature oak and birch trees define the canopy, supporting diverse understory plants and wildlife. Managed as a nature reserve, it offers public footpaths.

Name: Eades Meadow (Ancient Hay Meadow)
Description: Eades Meadow is an ancient hay meadow near Upton Snodsbury, Worcestershire. It features diverse wildflowers including green-winged orchids, cowslips, and adder's-tongue fern. Managed for biodiversity, it supports various insects and ground-nesting birds. Public access is limited.

Name: Eaglehead and Bloodstone Copses
Description: Eaglehead and Bloodstone Copses nature reserve consists of ancient woodland and open glades. Mature oak, ash, and hazel trees provide diverse habitats. The site supports a variety of wildflowers and butterflies. Management focuses on conserving its biodiversity.

Name: Eakring Brail (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Eakring Brail is an ancient semi-natural woodland reserve in Nottinghamshire. Mature oak, ash, and hazel trees dominate, supporting diverse wildlife including nesting birds and wildflowers like bluebells. Public footpaths provide access through the site.

Name: Eakring Meadows
Description: Eakring Meadows is a small north Nottinghamshire reserve, established in 1993. It features rich, traditionally managed wet grassland and a small stream, providing habitat for diverse flora and fauna, including ragged robin and meadowsweet.

Name: Eames Farm (Thorney Island Wetland)
Description: Eames Farm, on Thorney Island within Chichester Harbour AONB, is managed by Chichester Harbour Conservancy and owned by Natural England. It protects saltmarsh, mudflats, and reedbeds, forming an internationally important wetland. The site supports diverse bird species including curlew and redshank, offering extensive harbour views. It is accessible via public footpath.

Name: Earl's & Pontesford Hill (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Earl's Hill and Pontesford Hill are important for ancient woodland, acid grassland, and heathland habitats. Managed by Shropshire Wildlife Trust, the site supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare butterflies and birds, offering public access.

Name: Eartham Woods (South Downs)
Description: Eartham Woods, within the South Downs National Park, offers extensive trails for walking, cycling, and horse riding. Managed by Forestry England, this ancient woodland features diverse tree species, including impressive mature beech stands, and is recognised for its rich wildlife and archaeological interest.

Name: East Blean Woods
Description: East Blean Woods is Kent's largest ancient woodland and an SSSI, featuring heathland, coppiced sweet chestnut, and oak trees. It supports diverse wildlife, including the rare heath fritillary butterfly. The site offers various paths for walking, cycling, and horse riding.

Name: East Chevington
Description: East Chevington is a Northumberland Wildlife Trust nature reserve near Amble. It features grassland, reedbeds, and open water, attracting diverse bird species. The site provides important habitats for wintering waterfowl and breeding waders, offering accessible paths for birdwatching.

Name: East Chevington (Reedbed & Goats)
Description: East Chevington is a Northumberland Wildlife Trust site, benefiting from the Green Recovery Challenge Fund. Work includes creating a new reedbed and introducing a small herd of goats to graze willow scrub, enhancing biodiversity.

Name: East Cramlington Pond
Description: East Cramlington Pond is a Northumberland Wildlife Trust nature reserve in South East Northumberland. It features a large pond, fen, and grassland habitats, supporting diverse birdlife including overwintering wildfowl. The site also hosts various invertebrates, and public access is available via designated footpaths.

Name: East Ham Nature Reserve
Description: East Ham Nature Reserve is a former graveyard transformed into a Local Nature Reserve in the London Borough of Newham. Newham Green Gym volunteers maintain diverse habitats, including woodland, wildflower meadows, and ponds, supporting local wildlife.

Name: East Riddlesden Hall (Dales Transition)
Description: East Riddlesden Hall is a 17th-century manor house with a barn, gardens, and a lake, nestled in the Aire Valley. The site includes a children's play area, cafe, and shop, offering year-round activities and events in West Yorkshire.

Name: East Ruston Common
Description: East Ruston Common is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) with a mosaic of wet acid grassland, neutral grassland, swamp, fen, tall herb fen, and woodland. It supports scarce plants, insects, amphibians, and birds. Grazing, cutting, and peat extraction have historically influenced the common.

Name: East Town Park (Haverhill Conservation)
Description: East Town Park is Haverhill's main park, featuring a children's play area, skate park, and cafe. The park holds Green Flag status and includes a nature trail and conservation area. Visitors can enjoy various walks and facilities, including WCs.

Name: East Village Waterglades (Stratford)
Description: East Village Waterglades in Stratford, London, is a vital green space within a residential area. TCV volunteers manage these wetland habitats, ensuring they remain biodiverse and accessible. The site supports various plant species and provides a home for diverse urban wildlife.

Name: East Winch Common
Description: East Winch Common is a 13-hectare Norfolk site featuring dry acid grassland, wet bog, and woodland. It supports common lizards, dragonflies, and various bird species. The site is managed for conservation and public access by the Norfolk Wildlife Trust.

Name: East Wretham Heath
Description: East Wretham Heath, a diverse Breckland reserve, features dry heathland, woodland, mire, and meres. It supports unique flora and fauna, including rare invertebrates, plants, birds, adders, and natterjack toads. Water levels in its meres fluctuate naturally. Managed by Norfolk Wildlife Trust, the site offers accessible trails.

Name: Eastbrookend Country Park (Barking/Dagenham)
Description: Eastbrookend Country Park in Dagenham spans 140 acres. It features diverse habitats including woodlands, meadows, and lakes, supporting various wildlife species. The park offers walking trails, a café, and a children's play area. It connects to The Chase Nature Reserve.

Name: Eastwood (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Eastwood is an ancient woodland in Cheshire, part of a larger wooded area. It features mature oaks and birch, with a rich ground flora including bluebells and wood anemones. Managed for biodiversity, it supports various woodland birds and invertebrates.

Name: Eathorpe Marsh
Description: Eathorpe Marsh is a small, species-rich marsh in Warwickshire. A spring-fed stream creates ideal conditions for diverse plant and animal life, including various sedges, rushes, and associated invertebrates. It is a key wetland habitat.

Name: Eaves Wood
Description: Eaves Wood is an ancient semi-natural woodland in Silverdale, featuring limestone grassland and diverse habitats. Managed by Cumbria Wildlife Trust, it supports a rich array of wildflowers, butterflies, and birds, contributing to local biodiversity.

Name: Ebbor Gorge (Mendip NNR)
Description: Ebbor Gorge is a dry limestone gorge and National Nature Reserve within the Mendip Hills National Landscape. Managed by the National Trust, it features ancient woodland, walking trails, and diverse wildlife. The site is noted for its geological formations and prehistoric archaeological interest.

Name: Ebernoe Common
Description: Ebernoe Common is an ancient woodland and grazing common in West Sussex, managed by Sussex Wildlife Trust. It features old growth trees, ponds, and open grassland, supporting diverse wildlife including bats and fungi. A National Nature Reserve and SSSI.

Name: Echo Lodge Meadows
Description: Echo Lodge Meadows, near Lockeridge in Wiltshire, is a traditionally managed grassland rich in wildflowers. This nature reserve provides a habitat for diverse insect life and is home to species like great burnet and meadow thistle.

Name: EcoPark Birmingham (Environment Centre)
Description: EcoPark Birmingham is an inner-city nature reserve and environment centre in Small Heath. This green space provides vital habitat for urban wildlife. It offers educational activities and community events for all ages, promoting biodiversity and sustainable living within Birmingham.

Name: Eday (Orkney)
Description: Eday is one of the larger North Isles of Orkney, a low-lying island with a rich natural and historical heritage. The RSPB reserve covers the north-west part of the island, including the cliffs of Red Head, a nationally important seabird colony.

Name: Eddleston Water (Flood Management Pilot)
Description: The Eddleston Water Project is a long-term initiative restoring the river's natural function, mitigating flood risk, and enhancing biodiversity in the Eddleston catchment. It uses nature-based solutions like tree planting, pond creation, and meander restoration to tackle climate change impacts.

Name: Edford Meadows
Description: Edford Meadows is a mosaic of wildflower meadows and ancient woodlands near Radstock. The reserve features several ponds and wet flushes, supporting a diverse range of plant and insect species. Key habitats include neutral grassland and broadleaved woodland.

Name: Edford Wood
Description: Edford Wood is an ancient woodland on limestone, home to mature trees like ash, oak, and field maple. Rich ground flora includes bluebells, wild garlic, and ferns. The site supports diverse insect life and bird species.

Name: Edgbaston Reservoir (Feeder & Biodiversity Site)
Description: Edgbaston Reservoir is a large urban water body in Birmingham, West Midlands. Managed by the Canal & River Trust, it serves as a vital canal feeder and a significant habitat for diverse wildlife. The reservoir provides a green oasis and recreational space within the city.

Name: Edgehills Bog
Description: Edgehills Bog is an acidic mire in the Forest of Dean, home to sphagnum mosses, cotton grass, and carnivorous plants like sundews. It supports diverse insect life, including dragonflies and damselflies. The site is managed to preserve its delicate peatland habitat.

Name: Eelscapes (The Severn Vale Recovery Project)
Description: Eelscapes is the Severn Vale Recovery Project by Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust. It focuses on European eels, aiming to improve their habitat, migration routes, and remove barriers. The project works with landowners and local communities, funded by the National Lottery Heritage Fund.

Name: Egleton Reserve (Rutland Water Lagoon Complex)
Description: Egleton Reserve is part of the Rutland Water Nature Reserve, managed by Leicestershire and Rutland Wildlife Trust. It features lagoons, woodlands, and grasslands, providing vital habitats for diverse species including breeding and wintering birds and osprey.

Name: Elan Valley (Celtic Rainforest LIFE)
Description: The Elan Valley in Mid Wales is a key site for the Celtic Rainforests LIFE project. Dŵr Cymru Welsh Water manages this estate, leading efforts to restore precious Atlantic woodlands. The area offers opportunities for both volunteering and fundraising.

Name: Elland Gravel Pits
Description: Elland Gravel Pits is a Yorkshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve. This former quarry site is now a haven for wildlife, featuring open water, reedbeds, scrub, and woodland. It supports various bird species, dragonflies, and amphibians.

Name: Ellerburn Bank
Description: Ellerburn Bank is a nature reserve within the North York Moors National Park, featuring limestone grassland, scrub, and woodland. It supports diverse wildflowers and butterflies, including green hairstreak and various orchid species.

Name: Elmdon Manor
Description: Elmdon Manor, a 2-hectare nature reserve near Solihull, features a diverse landscape of grassland, mature trees, and a pond. This Warwickshire Wildlife Trust site supports a variety of wildlife, including birds and invertebrates. Public footpaths provide access.

Name: Elstead Group of Commons
Description: Elstead Group of Commons is a significant lowland heathland site in Surrey, featuring dry and wet heath, bogs, and woodland. It supports rare species including Dartford warblers, woodlarks, and sand lizards. The area is managed to conserve its unique biodiversity.

Name: Elvaston Castle Country Park
Description: Elvaston Castle Country Park is home to Elvaston Castle, a gothic revival masterpiece set within over 200 acres of parkland and formal gardens. England's first country park, it offers nature trails, a children's play area, and hosts various events. Managed by Derbyshire County Council.

Name: Emer Bog and Baddesley Common
Description: Emer Bog and Baddesley Common Nature Reserve is a hidden gem between Romsey and Southampton. It features damp heathland, bog, wet woodland, and acid grassland. This reserve supports rare plants and animals thriving in its unique habitats, offering a significant natural area behind the motorway.

Name: Emerald Hill (Havant)
Description: Emerald Hill is a nature reserve in Havant, managed by Hampshire and Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust. It features ancient woodland, grassland, and scrub, supporting diverse wildlife, including various bird species and insects. Visitors can observe nature.

Name: Emmett Hill Meadows (The Marsh Fritillary Ark)
Description: Emmett Hill Meadows is a Wiltshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve. This wet grassland site provides a crucial habitat for the nationally rare marsh fritillary butterfly. It features wildflower-rich meadows and ancient hedgerows, supporting diverse plant and insect life.

Name: Emsworthy Mire (Dartmoor Upland Ark)
Description: Emsworthy Mire, a Devon Wildlife Trust nature reserve on Dartmoor, features mire, grassland, and scrub habitats. It supports diverse wildlife and is famed for spring bluebells. Conservation efforts restore the mire and enhance biodiversity.

Name: Epping Forest
Description: Epping Forest is London's largest open space, stretching 12 miles from East London to Epping in Essex. It is a unique remnant of an ancient forest, a Site of Special Scientific Interest, and a Special Area of Conservation, containing over 50,000 ancient trees.

Name: Epworth Turbary
Description: Epworth Turbary is a small lowland peat bog, forming the last remnant of the extensive Hatfield Chase wetland in Lincolnshire. It provides a habitat for nationally rare species, including the white-faced darter dragonfly and oblong-leaved sundew. A boardwalk offers visitor access.

Name: Erddig (Ancient Parkland Biodiversity)
Description: Erddig, near Wrexham, features over 1,200 acres of parkland, ancient woodlands, and riverside walks. This historic estate provides habitats for diverse wildlife among centuries-old trees, actively conserving its natural and historical landscape.

Name: Erewash Meadows
Description: Erewash Meadows is a wetland nature reserve in Derbyshire, a former opencast coal mine reclaimed by Derbyshire Wildlife Trust. It features wildflower meadows, ponds, and reedbeds. Managed for biodiversity, it supports diverse birds, insects, and mammals. Pathways provide access for visitors.

Name: Ernespie Wood
Description: Ernespie Wood is an ancient woodland reserve near Castle Douglas in Dumfries and Galloway. It features sessile oak and birch, with diverse ground flora. The site provides valuable habitat for birds and invertebrates within a farming landscape.

Name: Eryrys (Limestone Pavement Gem)
Description: Eryrys nature reserve protects a rare limestone pavement habitat near the village of Eryrys, Denbighshire. This small site, managed by North Wales Wildlife Trust, is part of the Clwydian Range and supports diverse plants, birds, and insects.

Name: Esher Common
Description: Esher Common is a large open common in Surrey, featuring diverse habitats including extensive heathland, ancient woodland, and acid grassland. Ponds and wet areas support varied birdlife, reptiles, and butterflies. The site is managed for conservation and public enjoyment.

Name: Eskdale Foothills (NT Grazing Projects)
Description: Eskdale, located in the western Lake District, is a valley shaped by glaciers and water, home to ancient woodlands, traditional farms, and rare wildlife. The National Trust manages land here, working with farmers on sustainable grazing and conservation projects.

Name: Eskmeals Dunes (Rare Coastal Flora)
Description: Eskmeals Dunes is a coastal dune system near Ravenglass in Cumbria, recognised for its diverse flora and fauna. It supports rare plants such as dune helleborine and marsh helleborine, alongside populations of natterjack toads. Managed by Cumbria Wildlife Trust.

Name: Etherow Country Park (Stockport)
Description: Etherow Country Park, one of England's first country parks, sits on the Peak District's edge. Established in 1968, it features extensive open woodland, two reservoirs, and the River Etherow. Visitors can enjoy fishing, sailing, and a children's play area.

Name: Everdon Stubbs (Ancient Bluebell Wood)
Description: Everdon Stubbs is an ancient woodland, known for its extensive bluebell displays in spring. The reserve features diverse mature trees and ground flora. It supports a variety of woodland birds and invertebrates.

Name: Everslade (South Warks Connectivity)
Description: Everslade is a small but important nature reserve in south Warwickshire. It features woodland, grassland, and a pond. The site provides a vital stepping stone for wildlife connectivity, managed to enhance biodiversity. Public access is available for quiet recreation.

Name: Exe Reed Beds
Description: Exe Reed Beds is a Devon Wildlife Trust reserve, a former clay pit with extensive reedbeds and open water. It provides crucial habitat for diverse bird species, including bitterns, marsh harriers, and warblers, and is a significant stop-over for migratory birds.

Name: Exeter Valley Parks (Urban Recovery Corridor)
Description: Exeter Valley Parks form a network of green spaces along river valleys through the city, providing vital habitats for wildlife and recreational areas for people. This corridor includes Ludwell, Mincinglake, Riverside, and Whitycombe Valley Parks.

Name: Eyam Edge (Lead Rake Habitat)
Description: Eyam Edge is a site of ancient lead mining on the limestone plateau above Eyam. This former lead rake is now grassland and woodland habitat, supporting species such as cowslip, common blue butterfly, and various orchids. The site is actively managed by Derbyshire Wildlife Trust.

Name: Eycott Hill (Upland Uplift)
Description: Eycott Hill Nature Reserve in Cumbria offers diverse habitats including species-rich grasslands, wetlands, and ancient woodland. It displays unique geological formations from volcanic activity and glaciations. The site is managed for biodiversity, supporting varied wildlife.

Name: Eye Brook Reservoir (SSSI Waterbird Site)
Description: Eyebrook Reservoir is a critical site for wildlife, particularly waterbirds. Designated as a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI), it supports significant populations of wildfowl and waders throughout the year. The site offers birdwatching hides and educational resources.

Name: Eyeworth Pond
Description: Eyeworth Pond is a small, quiet pond in the Roman River Valley, Essex. Situated on heavy London Clay, it retains water year-round, supporting diverse plant and insect life. Notable species include various damselflies and dragonflies.

Name: Eype Down & West Bay Coast
Description: The Golden Cap Estate includes Eype Down, cliffs, and coastal paths near West Bay, Dorset. It features the highest point on the South Coast of England, offering views across Lyme Bay towards the Isle of Portland. Managed by the National Trust.

Name: Fackenden Down (North Downs)
Description: Fackenden Down is a chalk grassland site in the Darent Valley, recognised for its diverse flora and fauna. It supports species such as the dingy skipper butterfly, burnt-tip orchid, and dormice. The site features steep slopes and open grasslands.

Name: Fairburn Ings (managed interface)
Description: Fairburn Ings, a former opencast coal mine near Castleford, West Yorkshire, is now a thriving wetland habitat. It attracts diverse birdlife, including avocets and kingfishers. The reserve features visitor trails, hides, and an accessible visitor centre for year-round nature observation.

Name: Fairfield Pit (Barton-upon-Humber)
Description: Fairfield Pit is a former chalk pit near Barton-upon-Humber, now a nature reserve. It features a mosaic of habitats including grassland, scrub, and woodland, supporting diverse insects, birds, and wildflowers. The site is managed for biodiversity.

Name: Fairham Brook
Description: Fairham Brook Nature Reserve, part of the wider Fairham Pastures site in Nottinghamshire, features mature woodland along the brook and surrounding meadows. It supports diverse bird species, invertebrates, and flora, contributing to local biodiversity.

Name: Fairham Brook Nature Reserve
Description: Fairham Brook Nature Reserve is a flood meadow and wetland site south of Nottingham. It features a meandering brook, wet woodland, grassland, and reedbeds, supporting diverse bird species and invertebrates.

Name: Fairlands Valley Park (Stevenage)
Description: Fairlands Valley Park in Stevenage, Hertfordshire, is a large recreational area featuring lakes for watersports and fishing. The park includes a splash park, adventure play areas, walking routes, and a cafe, providing diverse activities for visitors. It serves as a vital green space for both leisure and local wildlife.

Name: Fairmile Common
Description: Fairmile Common is a diverse mosaic of acid grassland, heathland, woodland, and scrub in Surrey. It provides habitats for ground-nesting birds, invertebrates, and reptiles. Managed by Surrey Wildlife Trust, it is part of a larger network of interconnected wildlife sites.

Name: Faith Marriott
Description: Faith Marriott is a small but important nature reserve on the edge of the urban area of Nottingham. It features wetlands, ponds, and grassland habitats. Managed by Nottinghamshire Wildlife Trust, it provides a habitat for various invertebrates and waterfowl.

Name: Faith Wood (Sherwood Forest Extension)
Description: Faith Wood is a small, planted ancient woodland within Sherwood Forest, near Clipstone. It features oak and birch, with a developing ground flora of bluebell and wood anemone. Woodland birds thrive here; common frogs utilise its pond area.

Name: Fal-Ruan (Seagrass Restoration Hub)
Description: Fal-Ruan is a seagrass restoration hub within Falmouth Bay, Cornwall. It spearheads efforts to re-establish vital seagrass meadows, crucial for marine biodiversity and carbon capture. This initiative enhances the health of the marine environment and supports local wildlife.

Name: Fallen-yew
Description: Fallen Yew nature reserve is a small, semi-natural ancient woodland in Cumbria, acquired in 1968. It features limestone pavement, grassland, and a diverse woodland with mature ash and sycamore, overlooking the River Kent valley. Notable plants include bluebells, primroses, and wild garlic.

Name: Falls of Clyde
Description: Falls of Clyde is a wildlife reserve and Site of Special Scientific Interest in South Lanarkshire, Scotland. It features a woodland gorge, four waterfalls, and a visitor centre. The reserve is home to various bird species and mammals. It is popular for nature walks.

Name: Falstone Forest (North Tyne Valley)
Description: Falstone Forest is a key part of Kielder Forest Park in Northumberland's North Tyne Valley. Managed by Forestry England, this extensive conifer forest provides numerous walking and cycling trails. It supports timber production, offers habitats for diverse wildlife, and welcomes visitors year-round.

Name: Fames Rough
Description: Fames Rough, managed by Surrey Wildlife Trust, features ancient woodland, chalk grassland, and scrub habitats. This biodiverse site supports rare orchids, butterflies, and various bird species, offering public access. Conservation efforts focus on enhancing its ecological value.

Name: Fancott Woods and Meadows
Description: Fancott Woods and Meadows is a diverse nature reserve in Bedfordshire. It features ancient woodland, species-rich meadows, and a stream, supporting various wildflowers, invertebrates, and birds throughout the year.

Name: Far Ings (Humber Bridge Wetlands)
Description: Far Ings National Nature Reserve, on the south bank of the Humber Estuary near the Humber Bridge, is a vital wetland site. Its reedbeds, open water, and marshland support diverse wildlife, including migratory birds like bitterns and marsh harriers. Bird hides and trails exist.

Name: Far Ings National Nature Reserve
Description: Far Ings National Nature Reserve is a significant wetland site on the Humber Estuary, near Barton-upon-Humber. It features reedbeds, open water, and grasslands, providing habitat for diverse birdlife including bitterns and marsh harriers. The reserve protects rare plants.

Name: Farlington Marshes
Description: Farlington Marshes is a coastal grazing marsh on Langstone Harbour's northern shore, a crucial site for wintering wildfowl and waders, including dark-bellied brent geese. It features reedbeds, saline lagoons, freshwater pools, and grasslands supporting diverse birds, insects, and plants. Operated by Hampshire & Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust.

Name: Farlington Marshes Nature Reserve (Solent Flagship)
Description: Farlington Marshes Nature Reserve is a coastal grazing marsh, saltmarsh, and mudflats on the Solent Way in Hampshire. An internationally important site, it supports overwintering wildfowl and waders. Habitats include reedbeds, ditches, and pools.

Name: Farm Bog
Description: Farm Bog is a semi-natural woodland reserve in Hillingdon, West London. The site features a mix of mature and young trees, a small bog, and a pond, supporting diverse aquatic and terrestrial wildlife. London Wildlife Trust manages this valuable urban green space.

Name: Farncombe Wood
Description: Farncombe Wood is an ancient woodland in Surrey, featuring mature oak trees and diverse ground flora. It is noted for seasonal displays of bluebells and other wildflowers, supporting various bird species and invertebrates. Trails offer visitor access.

Name: Farndon Willow Holt
Description: Farndon Willow Holt is a wetland nature reserve on the River Trent floodplain near Newark. It features willow holt, grassland, and ditches, supporting otters, water voles, kingfishers, and diverse insects. The site is valued for its rich wetland flora and invertebrate fauna.

Name: Farningham Wood
Description: Farningham Wood is an ancient woodland in Kent, managed by Kent Wildlife Trust. It features diverse tree species, including oak, ash, and hornbeam, and supports a variety of wildlife. Public footpaths allow access for walking and nature observation.

Name: Farthing Downs
Description: Farthing Downs, a chalk grassland and ancient woodland site, stretches 3km across Greater London and Surrey. Popular for walking and horse riding, it offers views across the North Downs and is a Site of Special Scientific Interest for its rare chalk grassland plants.

Name: Fedian (Derrygonnelly Farmland)
Description: Fedian is a 12.5-hectare nature reserve in County Fermanagh, Northern Ireland. It features species-rich wet grassland and rush pasture, with an area of deciduous woodland. The site supports notable birdlife and various wildflowers, including several orchid species.

Name: Felbrigg Hall (520-acre Great Wood)
Description: Felbrigg Hall is a 17th-century country house in Norfolk, East Anglia. Its 520-acre estate features a walled garden, lake, and the 'Great Wood', offering various waymarked walks. The estate provides a habitat for diverse local wildlife.

Name: Fell Foot (Windermere Lakeside)
Description: Fell Foot is a Victorian park on the southern shore of Windermere, offering lake access for paddleboarding and wild swimming, plus family-friendly facilities, a cafe, and boat hire. It features formal gardens and open spaces ideal for picnics and recreation.

Name: Felmersham Gravel Pits
Description: Felmersham Gravel Pits, a former gravel quarry in Bedfordshire, is a nature reserve. It features extensive open water, reedbeds, and willow scrub, supporting diverse birdlife, including overwintering wildfowl, breeding waders, dragonflies, and butterflies.

Name: Fen Bog (North York Moors)
Description: Fen Bog is a unique spring-fed fen wetland within the North York Moors National Park. It supports a rich variety of plants and insects, including globe flower and bird's-eye primrose. The site is managed by the Yorkshire Wildlife Trust.

Name: Fen Drayton Lakes
Description: Fen Drayton Lakes is a network of lakes, meadows and wetlands within the Great Ouse valley. The site is an important habitat for many bird species, including overwintering wildfowl, wading birds, and common terns. Reedbeds support warblers and bearded tits.

Name: Fencerhill Wood
Description: Fencerhill Wood is a small ancient woodland near Rennington, Northumberland, managed by the Northumberland Wildlife Trust. It features mature oak and birch trees with an understory of hazel and holly. Bluebells and wood anemones carpet the ground in spring.

Name: Fermyn Woods (Rockingham Forest)
Description: Fermyn Woods Country Park is a Green Flag award-winning park covering 135 acres in Northamptonshire, near Brigstock. It features woodland and meadows, walking trails, cycling routes, a play area, and a cafe. Managed by Fermyn Wood Trust and North Northamptonshire Council.

Name: Fidlers Wood
Description: Fidlers Wood is an ancient woodland nature reserve in Essex, managed by Essex Wildlife Trust. It features broadleaf trees like oak, hornbeam, and hazel, supporting diverse bird species. The understorey displays vibrant spring wildflowers such as bluebells. It is open for public walks.

Name: Filey Dams
Description: Filey Dams is Yorkshire Wildlife Trust's only freshwater nature reserve. The site, a former sewage works, features ponds, ditches, and wet grassland. It supports diverse wildlife, including wading birds, amphibians, and various insects.

Name: Filsham Reedbed
Description: Filsham Reedbed is a vital nature reserve located in St Leonards-on-Sea, East Sussex. It protects the county's largest remaining reedbed, providing crucial habitat for numerous bird species, including water rails and bearded tits. A boardwalk allows visitor access.

Name: Finch Foundry & Dartmoor Edge
Description: Finch Foundry in Sticklepath, Devon, is England's last working water-powered forge. It showcases traditional tool-making with water-driven hammers and grinding wheels. Visitors can explore historic workshops and learn about rural industrial heritage on the Dartmoor edge.

Name: Findhorn Hinterland (Nature30 Pilot)
Description: Findhorn Hinterland Trust manages land, woods, dunes, and wetlands near Findhorn, Moray, Scotland. It restores biodiversity and connects people with nature through education and land-based skills. The Trust promotes conservation and community engagement within the local environment.

Name: Finemere Wood
Description: Finemere Wood is an ancient woodland and Site of Special Scientific Interest near Aylesbury. It features mature oak, ash, and hazel coppice, supporting diverse butterflies, bats, and dormice. Management enhances ride edges and creates glades, promoting rich ground flora.

Name: Fineshade Wood (managed interface)
Description: Fineshade Wood offers extensive walking and cycling trails within a large ancient woodland in Northamptonshire. Features include a play area, café, and Gruffalo trail. It is home to a thriving red kite population and forms part of the wider Rockingham Forest.

Name: Fingle Woods (Dartmoor Rainforest)
Description: Fingle Woods is an ancient woodland in the Teign Valley, Dartmoor National Park, Devon. Part of a National Nature Reserve, it features diverse habitats including ancient oak and wet woodland, a river, and open heathland. Managed to restore native broadleaf trees.

Name: Fingringhoe Wick
Description: Fingringhoe Wick is a nature reserve in Essex, established by Essex Wildlife Trust. It features diverse habitats including saltmarsh, grassland, heathland, woodland, and ponds. The reserve is home to a wide array of wildlife, notably migrating birds, and overlooks the River Colne estuary.

Name: Fingringhoe Wick Nature Discovery Park
Description: Fingringhoe Wick Nature Discovery Park is Essex's first nature reserve, offering panoramic views across the Colne Estuary. It features heathland, woodland, grassland, and ponds, supporting diverse bird species like avocets and terns. The site includes a visitor centre, hides, and accessible trails.

Name: Fir and Pond Woods
Description: Fir and Pond Woods is an ancient woodland, rich in oak, hornbeam, and hazel, supporting diverse wildlife. Two ponds enhance the habitat, attracting various aquatic species, birds, and invertebrates. The site forms part of a larger ecological network within Hertfordshire.

Name: Firs Farm Wetlands
Description: Firs Farm Wetlands, in Winchmore Hill, Enfield, transformed an under-used playing field into an award-winning wetland. It provides a vital wildlife habitat, natural flood defence, and a community space. Regular volunteer days connect people with the river.

Name: Fishlake Meadows
Description: Fishlake Meadows, near Romsey, is a vital wetland reserve. It features reedbeds, fens, and wet grassland. The site supports rare invertebrates, wading birds, otters, and water voles. Accessible trails and hides are available for visitors.

Name: Fishlake Meadows (Romsey Wetlands)
Description: Fishlake Meadows is a Hampshire & Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust wetland reserve near Romsey. It features reedbeds, wet woodland, and grasslands, supporting many bird species, including bitterns and avocets. Accessible trails are open.

Name: Fiskerton Fen
Description: Fiskerton Fen is a former gravel pit, now restored to wetlands. It features open water, reedbeds, and wet grassland, providing habitat for various bird species and dragonflies. The reserve is managed for biodiversity and offers public access.

Name: Five Acres (CWT HQ)
Description: Five Acres is the headquarters of Cornwall Wildlife Trust, a small nature reserve near Truro. It features ancient woodland, grassland, and a pond, supporting various local wildlife. It is not generally open to the public, focusing on trust operations.

Name: Fivehead Arable Fields
Description: Fivehead Arable Fields is a 44-hectare nature reserve near Taunton, Somerset. It is managed for wintering birds and arable weeds through a rotation of overwintered stubbles, cultivated plots, and winter bird food crops. It supports species such as corn bunting.

Name: Flamborough Cliffs
Description: Flamborough Cliffs is a nationally important site for breeding seabirds. Thousands of kittiwakes, guillemots, razorbills, and puffins inhabit the chalk cliffs during the breeding season. Diverse wildflowers and grasses grow on the cliff tops and in grasslands.

Name: Flanders Moss NNR
Description: Flanders Moss is a raised bog, a rare habitat in Europe. It provides a home for a wide range of plants and animals. Boardwalks and a viewing tower allow visitors to explore the moss and observe wildlife. It is an internationally important site for nature conservation.

Name: Flatford (Dedham Vale)
Description: Flatford, within Dedham Vale AONB, is famous for painter John Constable. Visitors explore 18th-century Flatford Mill, Willy Lott's House, and Bridge Cottage by the River Stour. Picturesque walks offer views of the iconic landscape.

Name: Fleecefaulds Meadow
Description: Fleecefaulds Meadow, an Angus grassland, is managed by Scottish Wildlife Trust. It supports diverse wildflowers and grasses, providing vital habitat for pollinators and invertebrates within the local ecosystem.

Name: Fletchwood Meadows
Description: Fletchwood Meadows is a quiet nature reserve on the edge of the New Forest. It features unimproved grassland, mature hedges, a small stream, and a woodland edge. Wildlife includes roe deer, grass snakes, and various bird species such as skylarks.

Name: Flexford
Description: Flexford Nature Reserve is a mosaic of habitats, including ancient woodland, meadows, wetlands, managed for biodiversity near Chandler's Ford, Hampshire. It provides a refuge for various wildlife, including butterflies, birds, and amphibians.

Name: Flitwick Moor
Description: Flitwick Moor is a nationally important wetland in Bedfordshire, hosting one of the largest valley mires. Habitats include wet woodland, fen, and acid grassland. Diverse flora and fauna, including rare species, thrive. Boardwalks and trails provide visitor access.

Name: Flitwick Wood
Description: Flitwick Wood is an ancient woodland, hosting bluebells, primroses, and diverse tree species. It features varied topography, with a stream flowing through the reserve. Visitors can observe various bird species and other wildlife in a tranquil setting.

Name: Florence Court (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Florence Court in County Fermanagh, Northern Ireland, is a National Trust property featuring a large 18th-century house and demesne. The estate includes a walled garden, parkland, and ancient woodlands with walking trails. It hosts diverse flora and fauna, offering insights into historic landscape management.

Name: Fobbing Marsh
Description: Fobbing Marsh is a traditional grazing marsh in Essex, featuring reedbeds, ditches, and wet grassland habitats. It supports significant populations of water voles and overwintering wildfowl. The reserve plays an important role in the local flood defence system along the Thames Estuary.

Name: Folkestone Warren (Coastal Landslip Habitat)
Description: Folkestone Warren is a geological SSSI within the Kent Downs AONB, managed by White Cliffs Countryside Partnership. This significant coastal landslip habitat features cliffs, boulder fields, and rare species, attracting walkers and naturalists. It is home to unique invertebrates, plants, and lichens.

Name: Folly Farm
Description: Folly Farm is a 250-acre nature reserve in the Chew Valley, managed by Avon Wildlife Trust. It features ancient woodland, species-rich meadows, and ponds, providing habitats for dormice, badgers, and barn owls. The site offers walking trails and educational activities.

Name: Fontmell Down (Chalk Downland)
Description: Fontmell Down is a significant chalk downland in Dorset, offering steep slopes and views across the Blackmore Vale. It is rich in wildflowers and butterflies during summer, and home to various bird species. Managed for conservation, it represents an important grassland habitat.

Name: Fordham Woods
Description: Fordham Woods is a small, semi-natural ancient woodland in Cambridgeshire. Managed for its ancient character and biodiversity, it features bluebells, primroses, and wild garlic. Visitors can enjoy peaceful walks.

Name: Foreland Point & Countisbury
Description: Foreland Point & Countisbury is a wildlife-rich headland on Exmoor National Park’s coast. Visitors can explore dramatic cliffs, enjoy coastal walks, and observe diverse flora and fauna. The area offers stunning views across the Bristol Channel.

Name: Formonthills (Urban/Fringe Forestry)
Description: Formonthills features a diverse woodland, integrating mature and younger trees with open grasslands. A network of paths provides public access. The site offers views over the Firth of Forth and is actively managed for wildlife conservation and recreation.

Name: Forsinard Flows
Description: Forsinard Flows is a vast blanket bog in the north of Scotland, home to rare plants, insects, and breeding birds like red-throated divers and golden plovers. It features trails, an observation tower, and a 'flows lookout' viewing point.

Name: Forth River (Ecological Corridor)
Description: The Forth River Ecological Corridor is a West Belfast regeneration project. It enhances natural habitats, promotes biodiversity, and develops a community pathway along the river. Environmental initiatives offer training and volunteering for local residents.

Name: Forty Hall Estate (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Forty Hall Estate in Enfield, London, features significant ancient woodland alongside a medieval fishing lake and organic farm. The estate offers a crucial green space, supporting various habitats and biodiversity within the borough. It is managed by the local council.

Name: Forvie NNR (Shifting Sands)
Description: Forvie NNR features dynamic shifting sands and a significant dune system along the Scottish coast. It hosts Britain's largest eider duck breeding colony and an internationally important grey seal colony. The reserve is home to various seabirds and provides a wild coastal landscape.

Name: Fosterdown (Caterham)
Description: Fosterdown is a beautiful chalk grassland nature reserve overlooking the M25 and London. A variety of flora and fauna thrive here, including rare orchids and butterflies. Managed primarily for its biodiversity, the site offers public access paths and views over the valley. It is a vital green space within the North Downs.

Name: Foulmere Fen
Description: Holme Dunes NWT Reserve spans 124 hectares of dunes, saltmarsh, woodland, and beach in Norfolk. It is an internationally important bird migration watch-point, supporting natterjack toads and diverse flora. A visitor centre is present.

Name: Foulshaw Moss (Ospreys & Peat)
Description: Foulshaw Moss is a restored lowland raised bog in Cumbria, home to breeding ospreys, adders, and dragonflies. A boardwalk allows access to view diverse wildlife and the ongoing peatland restoration.

Name: Fountainbleau and Ladypark
Description: Fountainbleau and Ladypark is a small urban wildlife reserve in Perth, Tayside. It features a diverse woodland habitat, a burn, and open grassland. The reserve supports a variety of bird species and invertebrates, providing a green space for both nature and local community enjoyment.

Name: Fountains Abbey & Studley Royal (Water Garden & Deer Park)
Description: Fountains Abbey and Studley Royal is a World Heritage Site in North Yorkshire. It features the ruins of a Cistercian monastery, an 18th-century water garden, and a medieval deer park. Over 800 acres of diverse landscape for visitors to explore.

Name: Fowlsheugh (Seabird Cliffs)
Description: Fowlsheugh is a clifftop nature reserve south of Stonehaven, Aberdeenshire. It supports thousands of breeding seabirds including kittiwakes, guillemots, razorbills, and puffins. Fulmars and shags also nest here, offering spectacular cliff views.

Name: Fox Corner
Description: Fox Corner is a small broadleaved woodland reserve in Cornwall, featuring ancient hedgebanks and a stream. It supports diverse wildlife, including various birds, small mammals, and insects. The site is actively managed by Cornwall Wildlife Trust for biodiversity enhancement.

Name: Fox Fritillary Meadow
Description: Fox Fritillary Meadow, a species-rich grassland in Suffolk, is famous for its abundant display of snake's-head fritillaries each spring. Managed for wildlife, it provides a vital habitat for various insects and plants, integral to local biodiversity.

Name: Fox Hagg (Heathland & Bilberry)
Description: Fox Hagg is a mosaic of habitats, including dry heathland, acid grassland, and bilberry. This site supports diverse insect life, particularly butterflies like the Small Heath and Grayling. It offers expansive views over the surrounding Loxley Valley.

Name: Foxburrow
Description: Foxburrow is a 95-hectare nature reserve near Woodbridge, Suffolk. Managed by Suffolk Wildlife Trust, it features reverting arable land, woodland, meadows, and ponds. The site supports diverse wildlife like barn owls, offering educational activities and public access.

Name: Foxburrow Farm (Rewilding Project)
Description: Foxburrow Farm near Melton, Suffolk, is a former arable farm managed by Suffolk Wildlife Trust. It demonstrates rewilding techniques, converting fields to grassland and scrub, and creating ponds for wildlife habitats. The site features various trails and a pond-dipping platform for visitors.

Name: Foxburrow Wood
Description: Foxburrow Wood is an ancient woodland nature reserve managed by Kent Wildlife Trust, near Maidstone. It features coppiced sweet chestnut and oak, supporting diverse fungi, mosses, and lichens. The site provides a habitat for various bird species and invertebrates, with seasonal wildflowers.

Name: Foxcote Reservoir
Description: Foxcote Reservoir is a small, shallow reservoir and an important nature reserve on the Buckinghamshire/Northamptonshire border. It attracts overwintering wildfowl and migrating waders. Surrounding banks feature scrub, ancient hedgerows, and wildflower grassland.

Name: Foxearth Meadows (Flagship Dragonfly Wetland)
Description: Foxearth Meadows is a 6.8-hectare nature reserve in north Essex, owned and managed by A Rocha UK. It features wildflower-rich grasslands, woodlands, hedgerows, and a restored river and pond system. The site is recognised for its importance to dragonflies and various other wildlife species.

Name: Foxhill Bank LNR
Description: Foxhill Bank Local Nature Reserve is a woodland and wetland site near Oswaldtwistle, Lancashire. It features mature trees, reedbeds, and open water, supporting diverse wildlife including kingfishers and dippers. The reserve provides important habitats for birds, amphibians, and insects.

Name: Foxholes
Description: Foxholes is an ancient woodland nature reserve managed by BBOWT, located east of Great Rissington. The site features rich woodland wildflowers in spring, including bluebells and orchids. Butterflies, such as white admiral and silver-washed fritillary, are common in summer.

Name: Foxley Wood
Description: Foxley Wood is the largest ancient woodland in Norfolk, managed by Norfolk Wildlife Trust. It features diverse flora, including spring flowers like bluebells and wild garlic, and supports various bird and insect species. Public access is via marked footpaths throughout the year.

Name: Framlingham Mere
Description: Framlingham Mere is a wetland nature reserve south-east of Framlingham town. It features fen, reedbed, wet woodland, and unimproved grassland. The site supports diverse bird species, invertebrates, and wildflowers. Public access is via a circular trail.

Name: Fransham Common
Description: Fransham Common is a small nature reserve in Norfolk, managed by the Norfolk Wildlife Trust. It comprises wet woodland and fen, important for various plant species including marsh marigold and ragged robin. The site supports diverse insect life and is a habitat for birds such as willow warbler. Access is via a public footpath.

Name: Fraser Down (Silver-spotted Skipper site)
Description: Fraser Down is a chalk grassland and ancient woodland reserve on the North Downs Way in Surrey. It provides habitat for the rare silver-spotted skipper butterfly and offers panoramic views across the Surrey Weald. The site management focuses on maintaining chalk grassland features, supporting diverse flora and insect life.

Name: Frays Farm Meadows
Description: Frays Farm Meadows, a Hillingdon nature reserve in West London, features hay meadows and riverside habitat along the River Frays. It supports diverse wetland plants, invertebrates, birds, and water voles. The site is managed for biodiversity conservation.

Name: Frays Island and Mabey's Meadow
Description: Frays Island and Mabey's Meadow form nature reserves along the Frays River, a branch of the River Colne near Uxbridge. These sites support diverse wildlife, including grey wagtails, kingfishers, and various dragonflies. Mature trees like crack willow thrive alongside meadow wildflowers such as meadowsweet and yellow iris.

Name: Freemans Pasture
Description: Freemans Pasture is a small lowland meadow between Burnley and Padiham, bordering the River Calder. It features species-rich grassland, including plants like ragged robin and meadow cranesbill. The site is actively managed for biodiversity.

Name: Freiston Shore
Description: Freiston Shore is an RSPB nature reserve on the Lincolnshire coast. It comprises lagoons, saltmarsh, and intertidal mudflats, providing crucial habitats for wading birds like avocets and redshanks, and supporting migratory species. Visitors can explore walking trails and use bird hides.

Name: Frensham Common (Managed interface)
Description: Frensham Common is a large Surrey heathland with woodland, bogs, and two ponds, designated a SSSI and part of a SPA. It supports rare plants, insects, and birds like Dartford warblers. The site offers opportunities for walking, cycling, and birdwatching.

Name: Frensham Little Pond (Medieval Fish Pond & Heath)
Description: Frensham Little Pond, a medieval fish pond and heathland in Surrey, provides a vital habitat for diverse wildlife. Accessible paths lead through woodland and open heath, offering opportunities for walking and nature observation. The site is managed for conservation.

Name: Freshfield Dune Heath
Description: Freshfield Dune Heath is a rare habitat on the Sefton Coast SSSI in Merseyside. It supports unique flora and fauna, including natterjack toads, sand lizards, and rare insects. The site features open heathland, developing woodland, and provides crucial habitat.

Name: Friar's Crag (Derwent Water Shoreline)
Description: Friar's Crag is a prominent rocky promontory on the eastern shore of Derwent Water, near Keswick. It provides panoramic views across the lake towards Cat Bells and the Borrowdale valley. The site is a popular walking destination within the Lake District National Park.

Name: Friar's Goose (Fenland)
Description: Wicken Fen, Britain's oldest nature reserve, expands into areas like Friar's Goose in the Cambridgeshire Fens. It's a crucial wetland habitat supporting diverse wildlife. The National Trust actively works to restore and enlarge this vital landscape.

Name: Frieze Bridge (River Soar)
Description: Frieze Bridge is a small wetland nature reserve situated alongside the River Soar, between Stanford on Soar and Sutton Bonington. It comprises a flood meadow, two scrapes, and a section of riverbank. The site attracts diverse waterfowl, waders, and other wildlife.

Name: Friskney Decoy Wood
Description: Friskney Decoy Wood is an ancient wet woodland nature reserve in Lincolnshire, managed by the Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust. It protects mature trees, ditches, and supports diverse birdlife and invertebrates. The site provides walking opportunities through its natural habitats.

Name: Friston Forest
Description: Friston Forest in the South Downs National Park, East Sussex, is Sussex's largest ancient woodland. It offers trails for walking, cycling, and horse riding. The forest includes a visitor centre, picnic area, play trail, and supports diverse wildlife.

Name: Frith Wood
Description: Frith Wood is a mature ancient semi-natural woodland, predominantly oak, on a steep scarp slope on the Cotswold plateau. This tranquil nature reserve offers walking paths through diverse trees and flora, supporting various bird species, invertebrates, and mammals.

Name: Fronwen Wood (Semi-natural Ancient Wood)
Description: Fronwen Wood, an ancient semi-natural woodland in Powys, Wales, overlooks the River Ithon. It features mature oak, birch, and diverse ground flora including Bluebells. The site supports nesting birds, invertebrates, and mammals. Radnorshire Wildlife Trust manages it.

Name: Fryent Country Park (Brent)
Description: Fryent Country Park in Kingsbury, North West London, covers 254 acres. This Local Nature Reserve and Site of Importance for Nature Conservation features farmland, ancient woodland, and offers extensive views across London.

Name: Fulbourn Fen
Description: Fulbourn Fen is a rare chalk stream fen, fed by springs and supporting diverse wildlife. It features wet grassland, reedbeds, and chalk stream habitats, home to water voles, kingfishers, and various invertebrates. The reserve is actively managed.

Name: Gailey Reservoirs (Staffs Birdwatching)
Description: Gailey Reservoirs are two linked bodies of water by the Shropshire Union Canal near Gailey. The site is a popular destination for birdwatchers, known for its significant populations of winter wildfowl, gulls, and various wader species.

Name: Gait Barrows NNR (Limestone Pavement)
Description: Gait Barrows National Nature Reserve protects extensive limestone pavement, ancient woodland, and mires in Lancashire. It supports rare plants and insects, including the high brown fritillary butterfly. Management focuses on grazing and woodland restoration.

Name: Galley and Warden Hills
Description: Galley and Warden Hills is a nature reserve near Luton, covering chalk grassland and ancient woodland. Managed by The Wildlife Trust BCN, it features rare plants, butterflies, and panoramic views.

Name: Gallows Bridge Farm
Description: Gallows Bridge Farm is a nature reserve near Milton Keynes, featuring floodplain meadows, hedges, and ditches. The site supports various wildflowers, insects, and bird species, managed to enhance local biodiversity and provide natural habitats.

Name: Gamlingay Wood
Description: Gamlingay Wood is an ancient woodland in Cambridgeshire. It is managed for biodiversity, featuring mature trees, open glades, and wetland areas. The site supports diverse flora and fauna, including purple hairstreak butterflies and woodland birds.

Name: Gang Mine
Description: Gang Mine is a nature reserve near Matlock Bath, Derbyshire. It features unique geology, supporting rare plants like Spring Sandwort and Leadwort on lead-rich soils. The site also supports diverse invertebrates, including over 20 species of butterfly.

Name: Gannow Meadow (Unimproved Hay Meadow)
Description: Gannow Meadow, near Llangollen, is an unimproved hay meadow. Rich in wildflowers, it supports species like great burnet, yellow rattle, and devil's-bit scabious. The site also includes a small broadleaved woodland and wetland, managed by North Wales Wildlife Trust.

Name: Gaps Hill
Description: Gaps Hill is an ancient woodland, formerly part of the Cannock Chase deer park. It features mature oak, ash, and hazel trees, with walking paths throughout. The site supports diverse wildlife, offering a tranquil natural environment for visitors.

Name: Garbutt Wood
Description: Garbutt Wood Nature Reserve is an ancient woodland remnant located in a steep valley in North Yorkshire. It features diverse habitats, waymarked trails, and supports various bird species and wildflowers. Managed by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust.

Name: GB Gruffy (The Lead-Rich Calaminarian)
Description: GB Gruffy is a unique calaminarian grassland and heathland in the Mendip Hills, rich in lead and zinc. This Scheduled Monument supports a distinct flora, including rare spring sandwort and thrift, thriving on the mineral-rich soils. Panoramic views across the Chew Valley are visible from the site, which also features historical mining relics.

Name: Geilston Garden & Wood (Partner site)
Description: Geilston Garden is a tranquil riverside garden in Argyll, West Scotland, featuring an ancient walled garden, a herbaceous border, and a large kitchen garden.

Name: Gelli-hir Wood (Large Gower Woodland)
Description: Gelli-hir Wood is a large woodland reserve on the Gower Peninsula, featuring ancient sessile oak, beech, and birch trees. It is known for its diverse ground flora, including bluebells, wood anemones, and wild garlic, vibrant in spring. A stream runs through the site.

Name: Gelt Woods (Ancient Woodland Ravine)
Description: Gelt Woods is an ancient woodland ravine in Cumbria, known for its diverse flora and fauna. The River Gelt carves through the sandstone, creating unique habitats. The reserve supports ancient trees, wildflowers, and various bird species.

Name: George Henry Wood (Recent Woodland Creation)
Description: George Henry Wood is a recently established woodland in Leicestershire, featuring over 13,000 trees planted by Leicestershire and Rutland Wildlife Trust. The site, developed from former arable land, aims to enhance biodiversity and connect existing woodlands. It offers a new habitat for local wildlife.

Name: Gernon Bushes
Description: Gernon Bushes is an ancient woodland reserve and Site of Special Scientific Interest within Epping Forest, Essex. It features hornbeam, oak, and ash trees, supporting dormice, badgers, and diverse birdlife. Historic wood banks are present.

Name: Gernon Bushes (Ancient Hornbeam Pollards)
Description: Gernon Bushes is an ancient woodland, home to some of Europe's oldest hornbeam pollards, estimated at up to 1,000 years old. These veteran trees support a rich ecosystem of fungi, mosses, lichens, and invertebrates. The reserve is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI).

Name: Giant's Causeway & Causeway Coast NNR
Description: The Giant's Causeway is a UNESCO World Heritage Site on Northern Ireland's Antrim coast. It features around 40,000 interlocking basalt columns, formed by ancient volcanic activity. A national nature reserve, it offers dramatic coastal landscapes.

Name: Gibraltar Point (Flagship Coastal NNR)
Description: Gibraltar Point National Nature Reserve is a dynamic, 1,100-acre coastal site in Lincolnshire, extending three miles to The Wash. It features dunes, saltmarsh, and intertidal flats, supporting internationally important bird populations. A bird observatory and visitor centre are onsite.

Name: Gibraltar Point National Nature Reserve
Description: Gibraltar Point National Nature Reserve is a dynamic Lincolnshire coastline, extending three miles south from Skegness to The Wash. It features diverse habitats including sand-dunes, saltmarsh, and freshwater marsh. Managed by Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust, it is a significant site for migratory birds, serving as a key bird observatory.

Name: Gibside (Nature Recovery Trail)
Description: Gibside, a National Trust property near Rowlands Gill, Tyne and Wear, features an 18th-century landscape garden, a chapel, and parkland. The Nature Recovery Trail highlights ongoing conservation work, protecting diverse habitats and wildlife within this historic Georgian estate.

Name: Gibson Mill (Sustainability Flagship)
Description: Gibson Mill, nestled within Hardcastle Crags near Hebden Bridge, is a fully off-grid 19th-century cotton mill. It generates its own electricity via hydroelectricity and solar panels, processes its own waste, and provides visitor facilities. The site demonstrates sustainable living principles.

Name: Gight Wood
Description: Gight Wood is an ancient woodland reserve near Methlick, Aberdeenshire. It features oak, birch, and alder trees, supporting diverse fungi and lichens. The wood provides habitat for roe deer, red squirrels, and various bird species. Visitors can explore walking trails.

Name: Gilling Down
Description: Gilling Down is a species-rich grassland nature reserve in north east Somerset. This site features wildflowers such as cowslips and pyramidal orchids, supporting diverse insect life, including marbled white and small blue butterflies. The reserve provides significant habitat.

Name: Gillingham Royal Forest (Heritage & Nature)
Description: Gillingham Royal Forest is an historic lowland landscape in North Dorset. It features ancient woodlands, veteran trees, and historic field systems, remnants of its past as a royal hunting forest. The area supports diverse wildlife.

Name: Gisburn Forest (Bowland Forest)
Description: Gisburn Forest and Stocks reservoir, located in the Forest of Bowland AONB, is a diverse site in north east Lancashire. It offers mountain biking, walking trails, horse riding, and orienteering, and is a notable habitat for red squirrels.

Name: Glapthorn Cow Pasture (Black Hairstreak Stronghold)
Description: Glapthorn Cow Pasture is a 16-hectare nature reserve in Northamptonshire, significant for its ancient ash and elm woodland. It is a vital stronghold for the rare Black Hairstreak butterfly, which feeds on the blackthorn. The reserve also supports diverse flora and fauna, including various bird species and wildflowers.

Name: Glapthorn Cow Pastures
Description: Glapthorn Cow Pastures is a valuable grassland site, home to species like green-winged orchids and marsh helleborine. Managed for biodiversity, it supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare invertebrates. It serves as a significant botanical interest site in Northamptonshire.

Name: Glaslyn (The Upland Wilderness)
Description: Glaslyn is a high-altitude nature reserve in Powys, Mid Wales. It features wet heath, acid grassland, and mountain streams, supporting wading birds like curlew and snipe. The site is part of the Cambrian Mountains. Grazing helps maintain its diverse habitats.

Name: Glasshouse Copse
Description: Glasshouse Copse is an ancient semi-natural woodland near Henley-in-Arden, Warwickshire. It features mature oak, ash, and hazel, with a rich ground flora including bluebells and wood anemones. It provides habitat for diverse birds and invertebrates.

Name: Glasswater Wood (Crossgar)
Description: Glasswater Wood is a young broadleaf woodland planted in 2000. It covers 43 acres near Crossgar in County Down, Northern Ireland. The site offers a circular path and is popular with local walkers.

Name: Glazebrook & Flax Moss (Carbon Project)
Description: Glazebrook and Flax Moss are carbon projects near Wigan, forming part of the Great Manchester Wetlands. Restoration work, including re-wetting and tree planting, helps lock carbon in peat, improves biodiversity, and reduces flood risk in the area.

Name: Glen Finglas (The Great Trossachs NNR)
Description: Glen Finglas is a Highland estate within Loch Lomond & The Trossachs National Park, part of the Great Trossachs Forest National Nature Reserve. It features ancient woodlands, heathland, and open ground, managed for conservation and public access. The site protects significant habitats and species.

Name: Glen Moss
Description: Glen Moss is a nationally important lowland raised bog near Kilmacolm, Renfrewshire, one of the best surviving examples in the UK. It supports diverse peat-forming plants, dragonflies, damselflies, and various bird species. Conservation efforts focus on restoring its natural hydrological conditions.

Name: Glen Roy NNR (Parallel Roads)
Description: Glen Roy National Nature Reserve in the Scottish Highlands is renowned for its 'Parallel Roads', a unique geological feature. These three distinct shorelines were formed by an ancient ice-dammed loch, providing a clear record of glacial lake levels and processes.

Name: Glenarm (Ancient Woodland & River)
Description: Glenarm Nature Reserve protects ancient woodland and a salmon river near Glenarm village. The site features steep slopes with oak and ash trees. Spring sees carpets of bluebells and wild garlic. Red squirrels and otters are present, reflecting its biodiversity.

Name: Glenborrodale (Sunart)
Description: Glenborrodale RSPB reserve lies on the Ardnamurchan peninsula, featuring ancient oak woodlands and coastal habitats. The site overlooks Loch Sunart and supports golden eagles, white-tailed eagles, red deer, and otters. Walking trails are accessible.

Name: Glenbrook Wetlands
Description: Glenbrook Wetlands is a new wetland in Glenbrook Park, Enfield, created by Thames21 as part of the "Restoring Enfield's Rivers" project. It involved de-culverting and re-naturalising the Glenbrook, enhancing local amenity and providing flood attenuation benefits.

Name: Glencairn (River Restoration Hub)
Description: Glencairn River Restoration Hub is situated within Glencairn Park, West Belfast. It focuses on ecological improvements to Glencairn Stream, enhancing biodiversity and water quality. The initiative involves habitat restoration and community engagement.

Name: Glencoe NNR
Description: Glencoe is a stunning glacial glen and National Nature Reserve in the Scottish Highlands. Managed by the National Trust for Scotland, it is a popular destination for hikers and climbers, offering diverse trails, wildlife, and a rich history.

Name: Glencoyne Park (NT Nature-Friendly Farming)
Description: Glencoyne Park, near Ullswater in the Lake District, showcases nature-friendly farming practices. It demonstrates how food production and nature recovery thrive, protecting fell and valley bottom habitats while supporting local wildlife.

Name: Glendun (Traditional Hill Farm)
Description: Glendun is a traditional hill farm in the Glens of Antrim. Acquired by Ulster Wildlife, it aims to demonstrate the benefits of farming for nature. The reserve features peatlands, species-rich grasslands, and woodlands, supporting diverse wildlife.

Name: Glendurgan Wooded Valley
Description: Glendurgan Garden, a National Trust property in Cornwall, features a renowned cherry laurel maze, giant rhubarb plants, and a scenic three-valley descent to Durgan beach on the Helford River. It showcases diverse sub-tropical flora.

Name: Glenfeshie Estate (Wildland Ltd Core)
Description: Glenfeshie Estate spans over 44,000 acres in the Scottish Highlands, actively engaged in ecological restoration and rewilding. It restores native woodlands and supports diverse wildlife populations through ongoing conservation and research efforts.

Name: Glentress (Tweed Valley Hub)
Description: Glentress, near Peebles, is a 7stanes mountain biking centre within Tweed Valley Forest Park. It offers walking trails, a cafe, bike hire, and Go Ape. This site provides diverse outdoor activities for visitors.

Name: Glory Wood and Devil's Den
Description: Glory Wood and Devil's Den is an ancient woodland and heathland site near Godalming, Surrey. It features diverse tree species, acid grassland, and supports various birds, insects, and mammals. The site provides important habitats.

Name: Gobions Wood
Description: Gobions Wood is an ancient woodland nature reserve near Potters Bar, Hertfordshire. It features mature trees, diverse flora, and a pond, providing habitat for woodland birds and invertebrates. Public footpaths offer access.

Name: Goblin Combe
Description: Goblin Combe is a steep-sided limestone valley in North Somerset, managed by Avon Wildlife Trust. This 120-hectare reserve features ancient woodland and grassland, home to diverse flora including rare orchids. It offers important habitats for wildlife and walking trails.

Name: Goddards (Arts & Crafts Wildlife Garden)
Description: Goddards in York is a Grade I listed Arts and Crafts garden, originally designed by George Dillistone for the Terry family. It features herbaceous borders, a tennis court, a pond, and fruit trees, maintained with wildlife in mind. The garden supports local biodiversity.

Name: Godmanchester
Description: Godmanchester Nature Reserve, near Huntingdon, Cambridgeshire, offers wet woodland, reedbed, and species-rich grassland habitats. Managed by Wildlife Trust BCN, it supports diverse bird species, insects, and flora, providing valuable wetland wildlife habitat for conservation.

Name: Godmanchester Nature Reserve
Description: Godmanchester Nature Reserve features lakes, grassland, woodland, and an orchard, supporting diverse wildlife. The site is a former gravel pit, now managed for conservation with accessible paths, bird hides, and educational opportunities. Habitats include reedbeds, wet grassland, and ancient hedgerows.

Name: Godolphin Estate & Tregonning Hill
Description: Godolphin Estate features an ancient house, garden, and woodlands in Cornwall. Visitors can explore the historic estate, river walks, and Godolphin Hill. Tregonning Hill, accessible from the estate, reveals evidence of its mining heritage.

Name: Godrevy (Seal Colony Coast)
Description: Dramatic headland with stunning views across St Ives Bay. Popular with walkers, surfers, and wildlife watchers, particularly for the grey seal colony at Mutton Cove.

Name: Gogar Wood (Edinburgh Fringe)
Description: Gogar Wood is a young, developing woodland on the western outskirts of Edinburgh. It features a range of native broadleaf species. Pathways allow for exploration and wildlife observation. The site is managed for biodiversity and public access.

Name: Gogarth (The Great Orme Cliff)
Description: Gogarth nature reserve protects the Great Orme's western and northern cliffs. It features limestone grassland, maritime heath, and cliff ledges, providing a vital breeding ground for guillemots, razorbills, and kittiwakes. Choughs and peregrine falcons are also here.

Name: Golden Cap Estate (Highest Point on South Coast)
Description: Golden Cap Estate is the highest point on England's south coast, offering expansive views of Lyme Bay and the Jurassic Coast. The estate features woodlands, cliffs, and beaches, managed for nature conservation and public access through diverse footpaths.

Name: Golitha Falls (Lichen & Moss Sanctuary)
Description: Golitha Falls is an ancient woodland in Cornwall, England, featuring the River Fowey cascading through a steep valley. It is renowned for its diverse mosses, ferns, and lichens, thriving in the humid microclimate. The site is a National Nature Reserve, supporting significant biodiversity.

Name: Golitha Falls (Temperate Rainforest)
Description: Golitha Falls is an ancient woodland nature reserve in Cornwall, South West England, featuring a series of cascades along the River Fowey. This site is a temperate rainforest, supporting a rich diversity of mosses, lichens, and ferns.

Name: Gordano Valley
Description: Gordano Valley National Nature Reserve is a unique mosaic of wetlands, woodlands, and grasslands in North Somerset. It supports diverse wildlife, including otters, dormice, and various bird species. The reserve features rare habitats like fen and wet woodland, contributing significantly to local biodiversity.

Name: Gors Maen Llwyd (The Heather Giant)
Description: Gors Maen Llwyd is a lowland raised bog in Conwy, North Wales, designated as a Site of Special Scientific Interest. It features rare bog plants like sphagnum moss and sundew, and supports diverse insect life, including dragonflies. The reserve provides a valuable habitat for various bird species.

Name: Gors Maen Llwyd (Upland Peat Expansion)
Description: Gors Maen Llwyd is an upland peat bog and wet heath habitat, home to rare plants like cranberry and bog rosemary. It supports diverse wildlife, including curlew and polecat, with its diverse flora and mosaic of habitats. The reserve is near Rhosesmor.

Name: Gorse Farm (Marshy Grassland Pocket)
Description: Gorse Farm, a small marshy grassland pocket, lies west of Abergele in Conwy. It features a pond and ditches, supporting diverse wildlife. The reserve hosts plants like marsh pennywort and ragged robin, attracting dragonflies and damselflies.

Name: Gorthanydd (Upland Heath)
Description: Gorthanydd is an upland heathland nature reserve on Pen Llŷn, acquired in 2013. It supports choughs, lizards, and adders, alongside purple moor-grass, heather, gorse, and wet heath plants. The site is managed for its diverse wildlife habitats.

Name: Goscote Reedbed (Wyrley & Essington)
Description: Goscote Reedbed, a former landfill site along the Wyrley & Essington Canal in Walsall, West Midlands, has been transformed into a thriving wildlife haven. It features newly created reedbeds and wetland habitats, alongside a specific kingfisher nesting bank, enhancing biodiversity.

Name: Gosforth Nature Reserve (Partner)
Description: Gosforth Nature Reserve is Northumberland's oldest nature reserve, managed by the Natural History Society of Northumbria. It offers diverse habitats, including wetlands and woodlands, supporting various wildlife. Access is restricted to members of the NHSN and Northumberland Wildlife Trust.

Name: Gosling Sike (Nature Hub)
Description: Gosling Sike, near Carlisle, is Cumbria Wildlife Trust's nature hub and a small nature reserve. It offers diverse habitats, including wildflower meadows, wetlands, and woodlands, supporting local wildlife. The site hosts conservation activities and educational programmes.

Name: Gowbarrow Park (Ullswater Restoration)
Description: Gowbarrow Park, part of the Ullswater landscape, offers extensive views across the lake and features ancient woodlands. The National Trust manages this Lake District site, focusing on conservation to protect its diverse wildlife and enhance its natural beauty.

Name: Gowy Meadows
Description: Gowy Meadows is a 165-hectare wetland nature reserve in Cheshire, managed by Cheshire Wildlife Trust. It features meadows, woodland, and ponds along the River Gowy, supporting diverse wildlife. Conservation efforts focus on habitat restoration.

Name: Grace Dieu Wood (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Grace Dieu Wood is an ancient semi-natural woodland in Leicestershire, a remnant of the historic Charnwood Forest. It features mature oak and ash, a diverse understorey, and a stream valley, supporting a rich ground flora and woodland birds.

Name: Graeme Hendrey Wood
Description: Graeme Hendrey Wood is a small ancient woodland on the edge of the River Wey in Surrey. It features mature trees, bluebells in spring, and various bird species. The site is managed for biodiversity.

Name: Graffham Common
Description: Graffham Common is a lowland heathland and woodland site in the South Downs National Park. Managed by Sussex Wildlife Trust, it features heather, gorse, and native woodland. The site supports rare species including nightjar, woodlark, adders, and sand lizards.

Name: Grafham Water
Description: Grafham Water is a large reservoir and a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI), covering 806 hectares. It features extensive reedbeds, woodlands, and grasslands, providing habitat for diverse wildlife including overwintering birds and otters. The site includes nature reserves and hides for birdwatching.

Name: Graig Wood (Mawddach Estuary Wood)
Description: Graig Wood is a steep ancient oak woodland overlooking the Mawddach Estuary in Wales. It hosts a rich ground flora, including bluebells and wood anemone, and supports diverse bird species like pied flycatchers and redstarts. Otters and dormice are also present.

Name: Graig Wyllt (The High Wild)
Description: Graig Wyllt (The High Wild) is a 24-hectare nature reserve in the Conwy Valley. It features acidic oak woodland, species-rich grassland, and heathland, supporting various birds, reptiles, and invertebrates. Traditional management includes grazing with Welsh Mountain Ponies.

Name: Grange Barn (Coggeshall Meadows)
Description: Grange Barn is a 13th-century Cistercian monastic barn near Coggeshall, Essex, managed by the National Trust. It preserves medieval timber architecture and serves as a site for events and exploration within the wider Coggeshall Meadows.

Name: Grange Farm
Description: Grange Farm is an RSPB nature reserve near Honington, Suffolk, actively undergoing restoration from former arable land. It features a mix of newly created grasslands, wetlands, and hedgerows. The reserve provides vital habitats for a variety of bird species and local wildlife, focusing on ecological recovery.

Name: Grange Wood
Description: Grange Wood is a small ancient woodland and a Site of Nature Conservation Interest (SNCI). It hosts rare woodland plants and insects. Managed for nature conservation, the reserve features mature trees and diverse ground flora. Paths allow exploration.

Name: Grange-le-Sallows
Description: Grange-le-Sallows is a vital heathland remnant in Nottinghamshire, showcasing acid grassland, heathland, and woodland. It hosts birch, oak, foxglove, heather, and birds like nightjar and woodlark. Managed by Nottinghamshire Wildlife Trust, it is one of two such sites in the county.

Name: Grangelands and Kimble Warren
Description: Grangelands and Kimble Warren is a Buckinghamshire nature reserve. It features ancient woodlands, chalk grasslands, and panoramic views. The site supports rare plants, butterflies, and bird species. Managed by BBOWT, it conserves biodiversity and provides public access for walking.

Name: Granville (Urban Nature Reserve)
Description: Granville Urban Nature Reserve covers 60 hectares of former industrial land near Telford, Shropshire. It features wildflower meadows, woodlands, and ponds, supporting diverse wildlife. Managed for biodiversity, it offers accessible walking trails.

Name: Grass Wood (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Grass Wood is an ancient woodland, one of the largest in the Yorkshire Dales National Park. Situated on a steep limestone hillside above the River Wharfe near Grassington, it offers a haven for birds, bats, and invertebrates. Spring brings a carpet of wildflowers.

Name: Gravel Hole
Description: Gravel Hole, near Stockton-on-Tees, is a wet grassland nature reserve managed by Tees Valley Wildlife Trust. It provides a habitat for diverse wildlife, including breeding waders. Habitat management focuses on grazing and water level control.

Name: Graveney Marshes (Bird Sanctuary)
Description: Oare Marshes near Faversham is an internationally important bird sanctuary, managed by Kent Wildlife Trust. This reserve features saltmarsh, lagoons, and reedbeds, providing crucial habitats for breeding and wintering birds within the Kent Swale National Nature Reserve.

Name: Great Breach Wood
Description: Great Breach Wood is a 60-hectare Site of Special Scientific Interest in Somerset. The ancient woodland features diverse tree species, coppiced hazel, and supports bats, dormice, and numerous butterflies. Public footpaths allow access through this important wildlife habitat.

Name: Great Casterton Road Banks
