Description: Great Casterton Road Banks is a roadside verge nature reserve in Rutland, important for its array of wildflowers and grasses. The reserve supports species such as Dyer's greenweed, greater knapweed, and bee orchids. Managed for biodiversity.

Name: Great Cornard Country Park
Description: Great Cornard Country Park is located along the River Stour Valley, forming part of the Stour Valley Path. It features meadows, woodland, and ponds, providing habitats for diverse wildlife and offering recreational space for visitors.

Name: Great Hangman & Little Hangman
Description: Great Hangman and Little Hangman are prominent coastal hills in North Devon. Great Hangman, at 318m, is England's highest sea cliff. Little Hangman is 218m. Both are part of the South West Coast Path, offering dramatic views of the Bristol Channel.

Name: Great Hockham (Thetford Forest)
Description: Great Hockham is a tranquil woodland site within Thetford Forest, offering diverse habitats for wildlife. It features waymarked trails for walking and cycling, providing a peaceful environment for visitors to explore the former arable land now covered in trees.

Name: Great Langdale - Blea Tarn (Upland Ecosystem)
Description: Great Langdale, a glacial valley in the Lake District, showcases dramatic fells and the serene Blea Tarn. This upland ecosystem supports diverse wildlife and offers tranquil landscapes. National Trust actively manages its conservation.

Name: Great Manchester Wetlands (NIA)
Description: Great Manchester Wetlands is a 46 sq km Nature Improvement Area across Salford, Wigan, and Warrington. It comprises peatlands, wetlands, and woodlands. Projects restore habitats, enhance biodiversity, provide natural flood management, and connect communities.

Name: Great Merrible Wood
Description: Great Merrible Wood is a mature ancient semi-natural woodland, predominantly oak, ash, and field maple. Its ground flora includes bluebell, primrose, wood anemone, and wild garlic, supporting diverse woodland birds and mammals.

Name: Great Oakley Meadow
Description: Great Oakley Meadow is a 2.3-hectare nature reserve, part of the Great Oakley estate. It features traditional hay meadows, woodlands, and a pond, supporting diverse wildflowers. Managed via traditional hay-cutting and autumn cattle grazing.

Name: Great Wood (Ancient Braydon Heart)
Description: Great Wood, managed by Wiltshire Wildlife Trust, is a 20-hectare ancient woodland in Wiltshire, England. It forms part of the Braydon Forest and features diverse habitats, including mature broadleaf trees, wetlands, and open glades. The site is important for biodiversity and public access.

Name: Great Wood (Ancient Forest Core)
Description: Great Wood is a 27-hectare ancient woodland in Wiltshire, dominated by broadleaved trees such as oak and hazel. It features rich ground flora like bluebells and wood anemones, supporting diverse bird and invertebrate species. A stream, Wetty Bottom, traverses the site.

Name: Great Wood (Bray)
Description: Great Wood, an ancient woodland nature reserve in Bray, Berkshire, is managed by BBOWT. Mature oak, beech, and hornbeam trees thrive here. The site is known for its spring bluebells and diverse woodland birds, offering accessible walking trails.

Name: Great Wood (Quantocks)
Description: Great Wood, an ancient woodland and conifer plantation on the Quantock Hills, features mature oak, beech, and sweet chestnut. Diverse flora and fauna thrive here. Trails for walkers and horse riders offer scenic views across the Bristol Channel.

Name: Great Wood Borrowdale (Atlantic Rainforest)
Description: Great Wood, an ancient oak woodland in Borrowdale, forms part of a rare Atlantic woodland habitat in the Lake District. Situated near Derwent Water, it is a significant area for conservation, supporting diverse flora and fauna. The site offers various walking trails.

Name: Great Yarmouth North Denes
Description: Great Yarmouth North Denes is a significant coastal nature reserve just north of Great Yarmouth. It features a shingle beach and sand dunes, providing a vital habitat for ground-nesting birds such as little terns. The site is a key breeding area and offers views of diverse coastal flora and fauna. It is managed for wildlife conservation.

Name: Greatstone Dunes (Littlestone-on-Sea)
Description: Greatstone Dunes is a Site of Special Scientific Interest in Kent, known for its unique habitat. It features mobile and fixed dunes, dune slacks, shingle, and saltmarsh. The site supports many rare plants and invertebrates, including the nationally rare sandhill rustic moth.

Name: Green Down
Description: Green Down is a flower-rich limestone grassland reserve on the Mendip Hills, Somerset. A Site of Special Scientific Interest, it hosts over a million pyramidal orchids and supports the large blue butterfly. Management involves cattle grazing and conservation volunteers.

Name: Green Farm (Monkwood Connectivity)
Description: Monkwood is an ancient oak and hazel coppice woodland in Worcestershire, forming part of the Monkwood Green Farm SSSI. It is nationally important for its moth species, including the aspen hawk-moth, and supports birds like hawfinch. Wild service-tree and lily-of-the-valley grow here.

Name: Green Lane Wood
Description: Green Lane Wood, an ancient woodland in Cambridgeshire, is managed by the Woodland Trust. The site features mature trees, diverse flora, and a network of paths for visitors, providing a habitat for local wildlife and offering a tranquil setting.

Name: Green Lane Woodland Complex (Biss Wood)
Description: Biss Wood is a 38-hectare woodland complex near Trowbridge, Wiltshire. It features ancient woodland, coppiced areas, and open glades, supporting diverse wildlife. The site offers public access via footpaths, managed by Wiltshire Wildlife Trust for conservation.

Name: Green Park - Meadow Restoration Areas
Description: Green Park's meadow restoration areas enhance biodiversity in central London. Located near The Mall and Constitution Hill, these designated zones support pollinators and various wildlife, creating diverse habitats within the park's landscape.

Name: Greena Moor
Description: Greena Moor is a Cornwall Wildlife Trust nature reserve featuring wet grassland, a rare habitat supporting diverse wildlife. It hosts otters, marsh fritillary butterflies, dormice, barn owls, cuckoos, and reed buntings. The reserve offers accessible walks year-round.

Name: Greenfields (Species-rich Grassland)
Description: Greenfields nature reserve is a species-rich grassland in Oswestry. This small reserve has a diverse range of plant species and supports pollinators. It is managed by Shropshire Wildlife Trust, with grazing and hay cuts maintaining its biodiversity.

Name: Greenham and Crookham Commons
Description: Greenham and Crookham Commons is a vast, open landscape of heathland, grassland and woodland in Berkshire. It is a nationally important site for wildlife, supporting nightjars, woodlarks, and sand lizards. The area offers diverse habitats.

Name: Greenlee Lough NNR (The Floating Bog)
Description: Greenlee Lough NNR is Northumberland's only natural lake, featuring a unique floating bog ecosystem. It forms part of the Hadrian's Wall Wetlands Landscape Recovery Project, which aims to restore and enhance wetland habitats across 5,475 hectares for biodiversity and water quality.

Name: Greenway Estate & River Dart Woods
Description: Greenway is a historic estate on the River Dart in Devon, once home to author Agatha Christie. The property features a house, gardens, and woodlands. Visitors can explore the house interiors, gardens with plant collections, and riverside walks.

Name: Greenwich Park (Deer Park & Acid Grassland)
Description: Greenwich Park is a historic Royal Park in London, part of the Greenwich Maritime World Heritage Site. It features the Prime Meridian Line, Royal Observatory, and panoramic views of the city. The park includes a deer park, acid grassland, ancient trees, and flower gardens.

Name: Greenwich Peninsula Ecology Park
Description: Greenwich Peninsula Ecology Park is a two-acre nature reserve in London, a designated Site of Importance for Nature Conservation. It features diverse habitats, ponds, and reedbeds, supporting a rich variety of wildlife. TCV manages the site, offering environmental education and community events.

Name: Greno Woods (Ancient Woodland & Heath)
Description: Greno Woods near Sheffield is an ancient woodland and heathland. It features oak, birch, and rowan trees, with diverse flora like bilberry and ling heather. Wildlife includes roe deer, badger, and many bird species. A Local Wildlife Site, it offers walking and cycling.

Name: Grey Hope Bay
Description: Greyhope Bay is an award-winning community-led marine experience at the mouth of Aberdeen Harbour. It offers views of bottlenose dolphins, harbour porpoise, grey seals, and various seabirds. The site features a repurposed shipping container visitor centre and cafe.

Name: Grey Mare's Tail (Nature Reserve)
Description: Grey Mare's Tail is a dramatic 60m waterfall in the Moffat Water Valley, offering a challenging upland walk to a tranquil loch above. The nature reserve is home to peregrine falcons, ospreys during summer, and a population of wild mountain goats.

Name: Greyfield Wood
Description: Greyfield Wood is a 30-acre ancient woodland in the Chew Valley, near Hallatrow, Bath and North East Somerset. It features diverse tree species, wildflowers, and wildlife. Managed for biodiversity and public access, it offers walking trails.

Name: Greylake (RSPB Wetland Bridge)
Description: Greylake is an RSPB nature reserve situated on the Somerset Levels. The site features an accessible hide and boardwalk, offering views of wetland birds such as lapwings, snipe, and various duck species. It provides habitat for diverse wildlife, with seasonal highlights including breeding waders and wintering wildfowl.

Name: Greystones Farm
Description: Greystones Farm is a 66-hectare nature reserve in Bourton-on-the-Water, Gloucestershire. It features wildflower meadows, the River Eye, and Iron Age archaeology. The site protects wildlife, including rare native plants and aquatic species, offering visitor walks and learning experiences.

Name: Greystones Farm (Cotswold Edge)
Description: Greystones Farm Nature Reserve, near Bourton-on-the-Water, is a working farm and Scheduled Monument managed by Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust. It protects rare native breeds like Cotswold Lion sheep, ancient archaeological features, and wildflower meadows. Visitors explore trails, a discovery barn, and a café.

Name: Greywell Moors
Description: Greywell Moors is a tranquil wetland nature reserve in Hampshire, supporting diverse wildlife including rare species. A mosaic of habitats features chalk stream, fen, and wet woodland. It is part of the Basingstoke Canal.

Name: Greywell Moors (Chalk Spring Mire)
Description: Greywell Moors, a Hampshire SSSI, is a significant chalk spring fed mire system supporting rare invertebrates, blunt-flowered rush, and early marsh orchids. Otters, water voles, and kingfishers inhabit this important wetland, managed through grazing.

Name: Griffin Wood (St Helens)
Description: Griffin Wood is a young woodland with a mix of broadleaved trees and pines. It features meandering paths and a community orchard, encouraging biodiversity and local engagement within St Helens. Managed by The Mersey Forest, it offers green space for recreation and nature observation.

Name: Grime’s Graves (Nature Area)
Description: Grime’s Graves is a unique Neolithic flint mine site, a Scheduled Monument managed by Norfolk Wildlife Trust. It features diverse chalk grassland and woodland, supporting rare plants like the military orchid and various invertebrate species, important for its archaeology and biodiversity.

Name: Grimston Warren (Heathland Restoration)
Description: Grimston Warren is a large heathland, mire, and woodland complex near King's Lynn, Norfolk. Part of Roydon Common, it supports rare plants, invertebrates, and reptiles across diverse habitats. Conservation efforts focus on heathland restoration.

Name: Grimwith Reservoir (Upland Habitat)
Description: Grimwith Reservoir, a large upland reservoir in the Yorkshire Dales National Park near Pateley Bridge, supplies drinking water. It features extensive peat bogs, heather moorland, and grasslands, supporting diverse wildlife and critical habitats for wading birds.

Name: Grindon Moss (Solway Mossland)
Description: Grindon Moss is an internationally important raised bog and a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI). Part of the Solway Mosses Special Area of Conservation, it features peatland with sphagnum mosses and bog-specific flora.

Name: Gringley Carr (Idle Washlands)
Description: Gringley Carr is the northern section of Idle Valley Nature Reserve, a large wetland site north of Retford. It covers arable farmland on peaty soil, drained by ditches, alongside a 2km section of the River Idle.

Name: Grizedale Forest (Sculpture & Wildlife)
Description: Grizedale Forest, located in the Lake District, features an extensive network of walking and cycling trails through woodlands. It is home to a unique collection of outdoor sculptures and provides habitats for diverse wildlife, including red deer. A visitor centre offers amenities.

Name: Grogley Wood
Description: Grogley Wood is a semi-ancient woodland site near Bodmin, Cornwall. The site consists of broadleaf and conifer areas, with a rich ground flora and diverse birdlife. Public footpaths offer walking routes through the wood.

Name: Groton Wood
Description: Groton Wood is a 55-hectare ancient woodland in Suffolk, managed by Suffolk Wildlife Trust. It features diverse habitats like oak, hornbeam, and hazel coppice, supporting dormice, nightingales, and many insects. Public footpaths allow access near Hadleigh.

Name: Groton Wood (Small-leaved Lime)
Description: Groton Wood is a large ancient woodland in Suffolk's Brett Valley, dating to the 16th century. It is one of only two Suffolk sites where small-leaved lime is dominant. Varied geology supports diverse flora, managed through coppicing and ride maintenance.

Name: Grove Park (Historic Wood Pasture)
Description: Grove Park is a historic wood pasture near Warwick, managed by Warwickshire Wildlife Trust. It protects ancient oak pollards and diverse habitats, including grassland and wetlands. The site supports a rich array of wildlife, from veteran trees and fungi to insects.

Name: Grovelands Park (Biodiversity Lake)
Description: Grovelands Park is a Grade II* listed Green Flag park in Enfield, London. It features extensive woodlands, a formal garden, and a large lake. The lake has undergone enhancements to boost its biodiversity, supporting local wildlife.

Name: Grubbins Wood
Description: Grubbins Wood is an ancient semi-natural woodland near Arnside, Cumbria, dominated by mature oaks. A path leads to the estuary shore. It forms part of the Arnside and Silverdale Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty.

Name: Guisborough Forest (Cleveland Way)
Description: Guisborough Forest is a popular woodland site managed by Forestry England, located on the edge of the North York Moors National Park. It provides trails for walking, cycling, and horse riding, along with a visitor centre and children's play areas for recreational activities.

Name: Gullivers Farm
Description: Gullivers Farm is a 54-acre nature reserve in Dorset, linking ancient woodlands to the River Stour. It features species-rich grassland, ancient woodland, and hedgerows. Conservation efforts include grazing and traditional hay cutting to maintain habitats.

Name: Gun Moor & Gun Moor Meadow (Upland Connectivity)
Description: Gun Moor & Gun Moor Meadow protects blanket bog, wet heath, and acidic grassland in the Peak District uplands. It supports red grouse, curlew, hen harrier, and adder. Active management includes habitat restoration and invasive species control.

Name: Gunby Hall Estate (Wolds Edge Farming for Nature)
Description: Gunby Hall Estate near Spilsby, Lincolnshire, is a Victorian country house with gardens and parkland managed by the National Trust. It focuses on 'Wolds Edge Farming for Nature' to encourage wildlife and biodiversity across the landscape. Visitors can explore the house, formal gardens, and wider estate.

Name: Gunners Park and Shoebury Ranges
Description: Gunners Park and Shoebury Ranges is a coastal mosaic of grasslands, wetlands, scrub, and woodland in Essex. It is a vital green space for wildlife, bordering the Thames Estuary, and is important for breeding birds and overwintering wildfowl. The site includes a Local Nature Reserve and part of the Shoeburyness SSSI.

Name: Gunnislake Wood
Description: Gunnislake Wood is an ancient semi-natural woodland in Cornwall, nestled on the banks of the River Tamar. It features mature oaks, sessile oaks, and sycamores, with a rich ground flora of bluebells and wild garlic. The site offers tranquil riverside walks within an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty.

Name: Gunpowder Park
Description: Gunpowder Park, a Green Flag site in Lee Valley Regional Park, was transformed from a former testing ground. It offers 100 acres of meadows and woods, providing habitats for diverse wildlife. The park features trails for walking and cycling.

Name: Gunton Meadow (Lowestoft)
Description: Gunton Meadow is a small nature reserve near Lowestoft, Suffolk, featuring a freshwater ditch, marshland, and damp meadows. It provides habitat for water voles, dragonflies, and various bird species, offering a peaceful green space for local residents.

Name: Gunton Warren
Description: Gunton Warren is a coastal reserve in north Suffolk, featuring dune, shingle, and grassland habitats. It supports nesting birds like little terns and ringed plovers, rare plants, and various insects. The site is a key stopover for migrating birds.

Name: Gunton Warren (Coastal Heath)
Description: Gunton Warren is a valuable coastal reserve in Lowestoft, featuring coastal heathland, grassland, and cliff habitats. It supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare invertebrates and birds, contributing to local biodiversity and coastal defence.

Name: Gunwalloe & Church Cove
Description: Gunwalloe and Church Cove in Cornwall is a coastal site managed by the National Trust. It features sandy beaches, a historic church, and access to the South West Coast Path. The area is known for shipwrecks and natural beauty.

Name: Gutteridge Wood and Meadows
Description: Gutteridge Wood and Meadows, located in Hillingdon, West London, features diverse woodland, grassland, and wetland habitats. It supports varied wildlife including amphibians, insects, and birds. The site is managed for biodiversity and public access.

Name: Gwenffrwd-Dinas (Bluebell & Pied Flycatcher)
Description: Ancient oak woodlands in the Tywi valley, Carmarthenshire, Wales. A Special Area of Conservation, home to pied flycatchers, redstarts, and wood warblers. Bluebells carpet the valley floor. Features rocky river stretches and waterfalls, managed by RSPB.

Name: Hackhurst and White Downs
Description: Hackhurst and White Downs is a 105-hectare nature reserve in Surrey, featuring ancient woodland, grassland, and chalk downland. The site supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare orchids, butterflies, and dormice. Managed for conservation, it offers public access via footpaths and a section of the North Downs Way.

Name: Hackhurst Downs (Abinger Hammer)
Description: Hackhurst Downs is a chalk grassland nature reserve near Abinger Hammer, Surrey, managed by Surrey Wildlife Trust. It supports diverse flora including orchids and wildflowers, attracting various insects and butterflies. The site offers walking trails and panoramic views across the Surrey Hills.

Name: Hackpen Hill
Description: Hackpen Hill is a large, flower-rich chalk grassland site in the North Wessex Downs Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. It features a prominent Iron Age hill fort. The reserve supports diverse flora and fauna, including orchids and rare butterflies.

Name: Hadfield Moors (Dark Peak interface)
Description: Hadfield Moors is a 400-hectare nature reserve in the Dark Peak, featuring extensive blanket bog and wet heath. It supports species like red grouse, golden plover, and curlew. The site is crucial for carbon storage and water quality.

Name: Hadfield Topps
Description: Hadfield Topps is a small, species-rich grassland located near Hadfield in Derbyshire. It features varied wildflowers including common spotted orchid and cowslip, attracting diverse insect life. The site provides a habitat for small mammals and birds. Maintained for biodiversity, it supports local wildlife populations.

Name: Hadrian's Wall & Housesteads Fort
Description: Housesteads Roman Fort, on Hadrian's Wall, is Britain's most complete Roman fort. Visitors explore barracks, a hospital, granaries, and latrines, discovering Roman soldier life on the Empire's edge with stunning views across the wall.

Name: Hadrian's Wall Wetlands (11,000-acre Recovery)
Description: Hadrian's Wall Wetlands is an 11,000-acre Landscape Recovery Project. It restores and enhances peatlands and wetlands across the Hadrian's Wall area in Northumberland, aiming to improve biodiversity, water quality, and carbon storage within the national park.

Name: Hagbourne Copse (Swindon Pocket Wood)
Description: Hagbourne Copse, also known as Swindon Pocket Wood, is a small woodland in East Swindon. Managed by Wiltshire Wildlife Trust, it features diverse trees like oak, ash, and hazel. It supports various bird species and invertebrates, providing a valuable green space.

Name: Hagg Lane (Idle Washlands)
Description: Hagg Lane Idle Washlands is a 45-hectare nature reserve acquired in 2021, situated alongside the River Idle north of Retford. It forms part of the Idle Valley's nationally important network of floodplain wetlands, including an SSSI and SPA. The site is a nationally important breeding ground for waders and wintering wildfowl. No public access is currently permitted.

Name: Hainault Forest
Description: Hainault Forest spans 335 acres in northeast London, featuring ancient woodland, grasslands, a lake, and a zoo. Visitors can explore various walking trails, engage with wildlife, and enjoy open spaces for recreation. The site is a significant green lung.

Name: Hainault Forest (managed sections)
Description: Hainault Forest features ancient woodlands, rolling pasture, lakes, and rare habitats. It offers walking and cycling routes, a children's play area, and bird watching opportunities. This significant green space is located in northeast London.

Name: Halbullock Moor
Description: Halbullock Moor is a nature reserve near Scorrier in Cornwall. It features a mosaic of wet and dry heathland, bog, and grassland habitats. It supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare plant species and various invertebrates. The site offers important conservation value for lowland heath.

Name: Haldon Forest Park (Butterfly Conservation)
Description: Haldon Forest Park, near Exeter, provides varied outdoor activities including walking, running, and cycling trails for all abilities. It features scenic views and diverse woodlands managed for recreation and conservation. The site offers a Discovery Trail and challenging mountain bike routes.

Name: Hales and Shadwell Woods (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Hales Wood is an ancient woodland in Essex with diverse tree species including hornbeam, oak, and hazel. Rich ground flora supports various wildlife. Traditional coppicing manages the reserve, which provides walking trails.

Name: Halesworth Millennium Green (River Restoration)
Description: Halesworth Millennium Green is a 50-acre nature reserve on the River Blyth flood plain in Suffolk, offering diverse habitats from meadows to woodlands. The site provides walking trails and focuses on conservation, with ongoing river restoration work.

Name: Halt-a-marsh (Morfa Mawddach)
Description: Morfa Mawddach National Nature Reserve is a vast area of saltmarsh and sand dunes on the Mawddach estuary in Gwynedd, North West Wales. Located opposite Barmouth, it supports diverse birdlife like oystercatchers and curlews, alongside various coastal plant species.

Name: Halwill Junction
Description: Halwill Junction is a former railway junction now featuring woodland, wetlands and grassland, providing diverse habitats. The reserve is known for its dragonflies, damselflies and rare marsh fritillary butterflies. Walking trails offer visitor access.

Name: Ham Hill (Chalk Scarp)
Description: Ham Hill is a chalk downland site on a steep scarp slope overlooking the Vale of Pewsey. Its chalk grassland, rich in wildflowers like orchids and bellflowers, supports diverse insects including chalkhill and adonis blue butterflies. Yellowhammer and corn bunting are present.

Name: Ham House and Garden (Wildflower Meadows)
Description: Ham House is a 17th-century house by the River Thames in Richmond, London. Its formal gardens feature an orangery, kitchen garden, and ancient trees. Re-created 17th-century wilderness areas and wildflower meadows also exist within the estate.

Name: Ham Marshes (Faversham)
Description: Oare Marshes, near Faversham, features wet grasslands, reedbeds, saltmarsh, and mudflats. It is a vital habitat for migratory and breeding wading birds and wildfowl, offering a crucial stopping-off point for many species.

Name: Ham Street Woods
Description: Ham Street Woods is an ancient woodland near Ashford, Kent. The site features diverse habitats, including oak and hornbeam stands, supporting varied flora and fauna. It is managed by Kent Wildlife Trust for conservation and public access.

Name: Ham Wall (RSPB Avalon Core)
Description: Ham Wall is a Somerset wetland nature reserve managed by the RSPB. It features extensive reedbeds, open water, and wet woodland, providing habitat for diverse wildlife including bitterns, marsh harriers, and otters. Visitors can explore a network of trails and viewing hides.

Name: Hambleton Wood (Rutland Water Ancient Wood)
Description: Hambleton Woods forms part of the Anglian Water Birdwatching Centre on Rutland Water's north shore. This ancient woodland features diverse tree species and supports various wildlife. Visitors explore marked trails, observing deer, birds, and insects within this significant natural habitat.

Name: Hamford Water (National Nature Reserve)
Description: Hamford Water is an internationally important wetland, encompassing creeks, saltmarsh, and mudflats. It supports significant populations of overwintering and breeding birds, including avocets and dark-bellied brent geese. This estuarine site is also home to seals and diverse marine life.

Name: Hammond's Field (Unimproved Grassland)
Description: Hammond's Field is a gently sloping unimproved grassland in High Green, Sheffield. It supports a diverse range of wildflowers, including common spotted orchid and yellow rattle. The site offers a vital green space for both local residents and wildlife within the village.

Name: Hampstead Heath
Description: Hampstead Heath is a large, ancient parkland in north London, offering diverse habitats, natural swimming ponds, and panoramic city views. It features extensive woodlands, meadows, and sports facilities, managed for public access and conservation.

Name: Hampton Meadow (Lugg Valley)
Description: Hampton Meadow is a traditional hay meadow on the River Lugg flood plain in Herefordshire. It hosts plants such as great burnet and ragged robin. Insect life includes orange-tip butterflies, while reed bunting and kingfisher are present. Herefordshire Wildlife Trust manages the site.

Name: Hampton Wood & Meadow
Description: Hampton Wood & Meadow combines ancient woodland and wildflower-rich grassland in Warwickshire. The reserve supports dormice, butterflies, and diverse fungi. Managed through coppicing and grazing, it aims to enhance biodiversity. Public footpaths offer access.

Name: Hams Hall Environmental Centre (Tame Valley Hub)
Description: Hams Hall Environmental Centre is the Tame Valley Hub for the Tame Valley Wetlands, a significant landscape partnership. It hosts events and meetings, connecting visitors to the wetlands and serving as a key base for volunteers and partners within the scheme.

Name: Hams Hall Environmental Studies Centre
Description: Hams Hall Environmental Studies Centre is currently closed, not accepting bookings or visits. Previously, the site near Lea Marston offered educational opportunities to explore diverse habitats including woodlands, meadows, and ponds, fostering connections with local wildlife.

Name: Hamsterley Forest (County Durham's Largest)
Description: Hamsterley Forest covers 2,000 hectares, making it County Durham's largest woodland. It features diverse walking, cycling, and horse riding trails, alongside play areas and a cafe. The forest offers year-round opportunities for wildlife observation and outdoor activities.

Name: Hanbury Hall (Parkland & Gardens)
Description: Hanbury Hall is a William and Mary style country house in Worcestershire, cared for by the National Trust. It features a formal Baroque garden, extensive parkland, and an orchard, reflecting 18th-century design and rural life.

Name: Handa Island
Description: Handa Island, off Sutherland, is a Site of Special Scientific Interest and Special Protection Area. It hosts globally important seabird colonies including razorbills, guillemots, and kittiwakes. The Scottish Wildlife Trust manages the island.

Name: Hannah's Meadow (Upland Hay Meadow)
Description: Hannah’s Meadow is a rare upland hay meadow, home to a variety of wildflowers and grasses. Located in the North Pennines, the site is managed by Durham Wildlife Trust to preserve its biodiversity and traditional farming methods. It is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI).

Name: Hanningfield Reservoir
Description: Hanningfield Reservoir is a large Essex Wildlife Trust site in Essex. It features a vast expanse of water, ancient woodland, and farmland. The site offers footpaths, bird hides, a visitor centre, and education facilities, supporting diverse wildlife.

Name: Hanningfield Reservoir Nature Discovery Park
Description: Hanningfield Reservoir Nature Discovery Park, an Essex Wildlife Trust site, features ancient woodland, meadows, and reservoir margins. Visitors explore bird hides, nature trails, and a visitor centre. It supports diverse wildlife, including key bird species, within a significant Essex conservation site.

Name: Hardcastle Crags (Temperate Rainforest)
Description: Hardcastle Crags features ancient woodlands and a rare temperate rainforest. The 19th-century Gibson Mill is water-powered. Miles of footpaths wind through steep valleys, home to diverse wildlife and over 100 species of mosses and liverworts.

Name: Harden Quarry (Bronze Age/Biodiversity Site)
Description: Harden Quarry is a former Bronze Age quarry and biodiversity hotspot located within Northumberland National Park, part of the Hadrian's Wall World Heritage Site. Nature recovery efforts are underway, involving local partners and volunteers for conservation tasks.

Name: Hardington Moor
Description: Hardington Moor is a nature reserve near Hardington Mandeville, Somerset. It is managed by Somerset Wildlife Trust. The site comprises wildflower meadows and ancient hedgerows, supporting diverse insect life, including marbled white butterflies. It features accessible footpaths.

Name: Hardwick Dene & Elm Tree Wood
Description: Hardwick Dene and Elm Tree Wood is a local nature reserve in Stockton-on-Tees, featuring ancient woodland, grassland, and the flowing Elm Tree Beck. The site supports diverse wildlife, including various bird species and invertebrates, offering a natural habitat within an urban setting.

Name: Hardwick Hall (Doe Lea River Valley Restoration)
Description: Hardwick Hall, a National Trust property, is undergoing significant restoration within the Doe Lea River Valley. Work focuses on improving river health, water quality, and biodiversity. Efforts aim to restore natural river flow and create new habitats for a diverse range of wildlife.

Name: Hardwick Wood
Description: Hardwick Wood is an ancient woodland in Cambridgeshire, featuring mature oak, ash, and field maple. Managed by Wildlife Trust BCN, it supports diverse flora and fauna, including bluebells and woodland birds. Visitors can explore the site via established trails.

Name: Hare Hill (Woodland Garden & Habitat)
Description: Hare Hill is a tranquil, wooded garden and parkland on the Cheshire/Greater Manchester border. It features a walled garden, renowned rhododendrons, and azaleas. Visitors can explore various paths, diverse plant collections, and a lake. The site is managed by the National Trust.

Name: Haresfield Beacon & Standish Wood
Description: Haresfield Beacon and Standish Wood offers expansive views across the Severn Vale towards the Brecon Beacons. This Cotswold escarpment features ancient woodland, limestone grassland, and Iron Age hillfort remnants. It provides habitats for diverse wildlife, including wildflowers and butterflies.

Name: Harland Mount
Description: Harland Mount is a small, mature woodland nature reserve in Woodthorpe, York, managed by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust. It provides a vital urban green space, featuring a mix of native trees and a stream, supporting local wildlife and offering footpaths for visitors.

Name: Harridge Woods
Description: Harridge Woods is a diverse ancient woodland site near Frome in Somerset, managed by Somerset Wildlife Trust. It features oak, ash, and hazel trees with rich ground flora including bluebells and wild garlic. The site supports various bird species and dormice.

Name: Harrogate Ringway
Description: Harrogate Ringway Nature Reserve is a green corridor through urban Harrogate, North Yorkshire, comprising three distinct sites: Oakdale, Bilton, and Nidd Gorge. It offers diverse habitats for local wildlife, including birds and insects, with accessible paths.

Name: Hartington Meadows
Description: Hartington Meadows is a flower-rich hay meadow in the Upper Dove Valley, Derbyshire. It supports a diverse range of wildflowers, including globeflower and great burnet, providing vital habitat for insects and birds. The site is managed to enhance its biodiversity.

Name: Hartlepool Submerged Forest (Geological Rarity)
Description: The Hartlepool Submerged Forest is a rare geological site on the Co Durham coastline. Dating back 7000 years, this ancient forest becomes visible only during very low tides. It offers a unique glimpse into prehistoric landscapes.

Name: Hartslock
Description: Hartslock is a steep chalk grassland slope overlooking the River Thames near Goring. It is home to a significant population of monkey orchids and a rare hybrid orchid swarm. The reserve also supports rare juniper and features ancient woodland, managed for biodiversity.

Name: Harwood Forest (Simonside Edge)
Description: Harwood Forest, north of Rothbury, is a vast expanse of commercial conifers and ancient woodland. It offers numerous walking and cycling trails, providing scenic views across the Northumberland countryside.

Name: Haskayne Cutting (Rare Dragonflies)
Description: Haskayne Cutting is a linear wildlife site near the Leeds and Liverpool Canal in Lancashire. It features reed-fringed ponds, wet meadows, and scrub, known for rare dragonflies like the black darter. It is part of a former railway line and managed by the Lancashire Wildlife Trust.

Name: Hat Gate (Dismantled Railway Corridor)
Description: Hat Gate is a 0.7-hectare nature reserve near Chippenham, Wiltshire, forming part of a dismantled railway corridor. It features diverse habitats including damp grassland, scrub, and woodland. The site supports various insects, wildflowers, and bird species, contributing to local biodiversity.

Name: Hatchmere (Beaver Project)
Description: Hatchmere is Cheshire's sole meres and mosses National Nature Reserve, located within Delamere Forest. It hosts the Cheshire Beaver Project, a five-year reintroduction trial launched in 2020. Beavers enhance the site's rich wetland habitats, boosting biodiversity and improving water quality.

Name: Hatfield Forest (NT Managed NNR)
Description: Hatfield Forest, a National Nature Reserve in Essex, is England's only remaining intact Royal hunting forest. It features ancient trees, woodlands, grasslands, and a lake, demonstrating a landscape shaped by centuries of traditional forest management.

Name: Hatfield Forest (Partner)
Description: Hatfield Forest is a unique example of a nearly complete medieval hunting forest. Managed in partnership by Essex Wildlife Trust and the National Trust, it features ancient trees, open glades, and a large lake, providing diverse habitats for wildlife. Visitors can explore woodlands and grasslands.

Name: Hatton Meadows
Description: Hatton Meadows is a species-rich grassland in Lincolnshire, featuring traditional ridge and furrow patterns. It supports diverse wildflowers, including common spotted-orchid and meadow saffron. Butterflies like gatekeeper and small skipper are present. The Trust manages the site to maintain biodiversity and prevent scrub encroachment.

Name: Haughmond Hill
Description: Haughmond Hill offers views over Shrewsbury, the Shropshire Hills, and the Cheshire Plain. It features waymarked trails for walking and cycling through mixed woodland. Visitors can find picnic areas, a cafe, and a play area. The site is managed by Forestry England.

Name: Hauxley (The 2026 Visitor Flagship)
Description: Hauxley Wildlife Discovery Centre, managed by Northumberland Wildlife Trust, offers diverse habitats including ponds, meadows, and reedbeds. It supports various bird species, insects, and mammals, with hides and trails for observation.

Name: Hawkchurch Common
Description: Hawkchurch Common is a nature reserve near Axminster, East Devon. It features wet heathland, damp grassland, and ancient broadleaved woodland. The site supports diverse flora, including purple moor-grass, and birds like Dartford warblers. Ponies graze to maintain its habitats.

Name: Hawkes Wood
Description: Hawkes Wood is a diverse woodland reserve nestled in the Lynher Valley. Ancient oak, ash, and hazel trees provide habitat for Dormice, woodpeckers, and buzzards. The river attracts kingfishers and otters. Paths allow exploration.

Name: Hawthorn Dene
Description: Hawthorn Dene is an ancient woodland and coastal dene near Seaham, County Durham, featuring a magnesian limestone gorge, cliffs, and a beach. It supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare plants and woodland birds. Part of the Durham Heritage Coast, it provides walking trails.

Name: Haxey Turbary
Description: Haxey Turbary is a small, wet grassland and fen site in the Humberhead Levels. This SSSI is a rare remnant of the Isle of Axholme's original lowland peatlands. It supports species-rich grassland, ditches, and ponds.

Name: Haydon Hill
Description: Haydon Hill is a 27-hectare nature reserve in Dorset, featuring ancient woodland, grassland, and scrub. Managed for its diverse habitats, it supports a range of wildflowers, insects, and bird species. Visitors can explore marked trails.

Name: Hayley Wood (Ancient Coppice)
Description: Hayley Wood is an ancient woodland, traditionally managed on a 12-year coppice rotation. It boasts significant wildlife, particularly notable for its rich array of wildflowers like oxlip and a diverse population of butterflies and moths.

Name: Haysden Country Park
Description: Haysden Country Park, near Tonbridge, is a Local Nature Reserve. It features varied habitats, including lakes, a river, and woodlands. The park supports diverse wildlife and offers recreational activities such as walking and watersports. It is popular for its natural beauty and accessible paths.

Name: Hazelborough Wood (Northamptonshire/Bucks border)
Description: Hazelborough Wood is a mixed woodland near Silverstone, straddling the Northamptonshire and Buckinghamshire border. Managed by Forestry England, it offers walking and cycling trails through diverse habitats including ancient woodland and newer plantations. The site provides a natural environment for wildlife and public recreation.

Name: Hazlewood Marshes (Tidal Transformation)
Description: Hazlewood Marshes is a coastal grazing marsh on the River Alde in Suffolk, undergoing tidal transformation. This project creates new intertidal habitats, enhancing biodiversity for wading birds and offering natural flood protection.

Name: Headley Gravel Pit
Description: Headley Gravel Pit, a former quarry, is now a thriving wetland habitat. It features open water, reedbeds, and willow scrub, supporting diverse birdlife, amphibians, and invertebrates. Footpaths allow visitors to observe the rich wildlife.

Name: Headley Heath (High Ridge Heath)
Description: Headley Heath is a rare heathland and woodland site in the Surrey Hills National Landscape, South East England. This Site of Special Scientific Interest supports diverse habitats, including rare birds and reptiles. It is popular with walkers, cyclists, and horse riders.

Name: Headley Heath (High Weald Mosaic)
Description: Headley Heath is a large expanse of heathland, woodland, and chalk downland in Surrey. It offers varied habitats for wildlife and panoramic views across the landscape. The site features a diverse network of walking trails for visitors to explore.

Name: Headstone Manor Park (Daylighting)
Description: Headstone Manor Park in Harrow, London, features a river restoration project that daylighted 150m of the Yeading Brook. A new meandering channel replaces a culvert, improving water quality, habitats, and reducing flood risk, enhancing the park's environment.

Name: Heartwood Forest
Description: Heartwood Forest near St Albans, Hertfordshire, is England's largest new native forest, spanning 858 acres. Managed by the Woodland Trust, it features extensive woodlands, meadows, and orchards, offering walking trails and diverse wildlife habitats.

Name: Heather Underhill
Description: Heather Underhill is a 13-hectare woodland, part of the National Forest in Leicestershire. It features diverse native broadleaf and conifer trees, offering wildlife habitat. The site provides public access for walking and connects to a wider path network. It is actively managed.

Name: Heaton Park (Biodiversity Recovery Areas)
Description: Heaton Park is a municipal park in north Manchester, England, covering over 600 acres. The largest park in Greater Manchester, it features woodlands, grasslands, and wetlands. Biodiversity recovery areas are actively managed to enhance ecological value for diverse wildlife.

Name: Heber's Ghyll (Ilkley - local partnership)
Description: Heber's Ghyll is a beautiful woodland area and stream with waterfalls located near Ilkley, West Yorkshire. It offers scenic walks through ancient woodland and is managed by the National Trust, providing a tranquil spot for nature observation.

Name: Heddon Valley (Atlantic Rainforest)
Description: Heddon Valley in North Devon is a deep, wooded valley leading to a rugged coastline, part of an Atlantic 'rainforest' ecosystem. The River Heddon flows through ancient woodlands, home to diverse wildlife, before meeting the sea at a shingle beach.

Name: Hedgecourt
Description: Hedgecourt Lake, a Surrey SSSI, is a key wetland. It features a shallow lake, fen, swamp, and wet woodland, supporting diverse wildlife. Notable species include greater tussock-sedge, royal fern, various dragonflies, and the scarce hazel dormouse.

Name: Hedley Stringer Wood
Description: Hedley Stringer Wood is a young woodland near the village of Wilsden, Bradford, planted in 2000. It offers open vistas, a small stream, and a network of paths suitable for dog walking and family visits. The site enhances local biodiversity and provides green space.

Name: Hedleyhope Fell
Description: Hedleyhope Fell is a 136-hectare nature reserve in County Durham, managed by Durham Wildlife Trust. It features wet and dry heath, mire, and acid grassland habitats. The site supports breeding birds such as snipe and curlew, along with diverse invertebrates. Conservation work focuses on habitat restoration.

Name: Heigham Holmes (Hidden Island)
Description: Heigham Holmes is a secluded island in the Norfolk Broads, only accessible by boat on the River Thurne. It serves as a vital wildlife haven, home to otters and the rare fen raft spider. Visits are by pre-booked boat trip only.

Name: Helford River (Seagrass Regeneration)
Description: The Helford Seagrass Project regenerates seagrass meadows in Cornwall's Helford River. Pioneering methods involve volunteers collecting 10,000 seeds from healthy beds, planting them in biodegradable bags, and deploying them in the river. This vital project supports marine biodiversity and carbon capture, with growth ongoing.

Name: Hell Hole (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Hell Hole is an ancient woodland in Warwickshire, known for its spring display of bluebells, primroses, and anemones. Mature oak and ash trees dominate the canopy, supporting a diverse range of woodland birds and insects. Managed for conservation, it offers quiet walking paths.

Name: Hellenge Hill
Description: Hellenge Hill is an unimproved ancient grassland, designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest. This reserve supports diverse wildflowers, including several orchid species, and attracts various butterflies. It offers panoramic views of the Chew Valley.

Name: Hem Heath Woods (Urban Oasis Restoration)
Description: Hem Heath Woods is a 54-hectare mixed woodland on Stoke-on-Trent's southern fringe. It features mature oak and birch with a diverse understorey, supporting nuthatches and woodpeckers. The site, an ancient woodland, is currently undergoing an urban oasis restoration project.

Name: Hemsted Forest
Description: Hemsted Forest is a working woodland near Benenden, Kent, offering walking, cycling, and horse riding trails. It features mature conifer and broadleaf trees, and is managed by Forestry England. The site provides a natural habitat for various wildlife.

Name: Hen Reedbeds (Blyth Valley)
Description: Hen Reedbeds is a Suffolk Wildlife Trust nature reserve in the Blyth Valley, Suffolk. This freshwater site within the Blyth estuary features a mosaic of reedbed, fen, dykes, and pools. It provides a crucial stronghold for bitterns, marsh harriers, bearded tits, avocets, and wintering waders.

Name: Hendover Coppice (Ancient Woodland Restoration)
Description: Hendover Coppice is an ancient woodland site with a rich history of coppicing, now undergoing restoration. It features diverse tree species, wildflowers, and a variety of wildlife. The reserve offers walking trails through managed woodland habitats.

Name: Henllys (Urban Woodland)
Description: Henllys Urban Woodland is a community-planted woodland and wetland nature reserve near Cwmbran, Torfaen. Managed by Gwent Wildlife Trust, it features native trees, ponds, and marshland, supporting diverse wildlife. The site offers a valuable green space for local residents.

Name: Herrington Hill
Description: Herrington Hill, a grassland SSSI in Tyne and Wear, features magnesian limestone supporting diverse flora including blue moor-grass and orchids. It provides habitat for invertebrates and small mammals, actively managed for biodiversity.

Name: Hertford Heath
Description: Hertford Heath, a nature reserve near Hertford, Hertfordshire, encompasses ancient woodland, acid grassland, and ponds. It supports a range of wildlife, including reptiles and diverse insect species. Hertfordshire and Middlesex Wildlife Trust manages the site.

Name: Hertford Heath (Acid Heathland)
Description: Hertford Heath is a 60-hectare nature reserve in Hertfordshire, featuring rare acid heathland and mature broadleaved woodland. It supports silver birch, oak, Scots pine, heather, and gorse. The site provides habitat for nightjars, woodlarks, and reptiles like adders.

Name: Hesleden Dene
Description: Hesleden Dene is a Local Wildlife Site in County Durham. This wooded valley features ancient oak, ash, and hazel remnants, with diverse ground flora. A stream supports invertebrates, and birds like nuthatch are present. Public access paths are available.

Name: Hetchell Wood
Description: Hetchell Wood is a mosaic of ancient woodland, grassland, and exposed rock outcrops. This Yorkshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve provides diverse habitats for flora and fauna. Notable features include rare plants and geological formations, with public access via a network of footpaths.

Name: Hethel Old Thorn
Description: Hethel Old Thorn nature reserve protects a truly ancient tree, estimated to be 500 years old. This rare Midland hawthorn is the largest in Norfolk and a significant historical landmark. The site features various wildlife habitats, including grassland and scrub, supporting diverse insect and bird life.

Name: Hethel Old Thorn (Smallest NWT Reserve)
Description: Hethel Old Thorn is Norfolk Wildlife Trust's smallest reserve, protecting a single ancient hawthorn tree. This designated Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) showcases a unique ecosystem, supporting diverse lichen and insect communities around the ancient tree near Hethel.

Name: Hewitts Chalk Bank
Description: Hewitts Chalk Bank is a small chalk grassland nature reserve located on the North Downs in Kent. Managed by Kent Wildlife Trust, it supports a diverse range of wildflowers and butterflies. The site was acquired in 1968, preserving a valuable habitat.

Name: Hexhamshire Common (Northumberland Plateau)
Description: Hexhamshire Common, part of the Northumberland uplands, is a significant peatland area within the Great North Bog initiative. This landscape-scale restoration project aims for environmental recovery across Northern England.

Name: Hexton Chalk Pit
Description: Hexton Chalk Pit is a former chalk quarry, now a Site of Special Scientific Interest in north Hertfordshire. The reserve features chalk grassland and mature woodland, supporting diverse flora and fauna, including rare orchids, butterflies, and bird species.

Name: Hexton Chalk Pit (Rare Butterflies)
Description: Hexton Chalk Pit is a small, species-rich grassland reserve with steep, south-facing slopes, creating a warm microclimate. It supports diverse chalk grassland flowers and insects, including many butterfly species, such as the rare Duke of Burgundy.

Name: Heyford Meadow
Description: Heyford Meadow is a species-rich floodplain meadow by the River Cherwell, Oxfordshire. It features colourful wildflowers such as meadowsweet and great burnet, supporting diverse insects, ground-nesting birds, and water voles.

Name: Heysham Coast (Bay Ecosystem)
Description: Heysham Coast offers varied coastal habitats including saltmarsh, sand dunes, and grassland, important for diverse birdlife. Visitors can explore historic St Patrick's Chapel ruins and enjoy scenic views across Morecambe Bay, a significant natural environment.

Name: Heysham Moss
Description: Heysham Moss is a lowland raised bog and SSSI. It supports diverse flora, including sphagnum mosses, cotton grass, and heather. The site provides a habitat for various invertebrates, dragonflies, and birds like snipe, and is actively managed.

Name: Heysham Nature Reserve
Description: Heysham Nature Reserve is a wildlife haven built on the site of a former power station. Habitats include reedbed, open water, and woodland, supporting birds and insects. Visitors use accessible paths and hides.

Name: Hickling Broad and Marshes
Description: Hickling Broad is the largest expanse of open water in the Broads National Park. It is a vital haven for rare wildlife, including common crane and swallowtail butterflies. The reserve features reedbeds, marshes, and open water habitats.

Name: Hickling Broad and Marshes (NNR)
Description: Hickling Broad is the largest of the Norfolk Broads, an extensive area of open water, reedbed, and fen. A key Norfolk Wildlife Trust reserve, it hosts rare species including common crane, swallowtail butterfly, and bittern. It is a Ramsar, SAC, and SSSI site.

Name: Hicks Lodge (The National Forest Cycle Centre)
Description: Hicks Lodge, within The National Forest, offers family-friendly cycling trails for all abilities, a balance bike track, and walking routes. The site features a cafe and bike hire, with accessible routes around a former opencast coal mine. It is managed by Forestry England.

Name: High Clear Down (Steep Chalk Downland)
Description: High Clear Down is a steep chalk downland in Wiltshire, home to ancient earthworks and a Neolithic long barrow. This Site of Special Scientific Interest is grazed by cattle to maintain diverse chalk grassland, supporting wildflowers and butterflies.

Name: High Close (Arboretum & Estate)
Description: High Close Estate and Arboretum, near Loughrigg Fell, Lake District, features historic gardens with mature trees, rhododendrons, and azaleas. It offers views across Langdale Valley and includes diverse plant collections and walking paths for visitors.

Name: High Elms Country Park
Description: High Elms Country Park, near Orpington, features ancient woodlands, wildflower meadows, and chalk grassland. It supports diverse wildlife. Managed by London Borough of Bromley, Kent Wildlife Trust also manages a part.

Name: High Halks
Description: High Halks is a Kent Wildlife Trust nature reserve in Kent, featuring a mosaic of woodland, grassland, and ponds. Acquired in 1993, the site supports diverse wildlife, including dragonflies and various bird species, across its planted broadleaf woodland and wet grassland habitats.

Name: High Leas Farm (Upland Hay Meadows)
Description: High Leas Farm is a nature reserve in Cumbria, featuring traditionally managed upland hay meadows rich in wildflowers and diverse grasses. These habitats support various invertebrates and ground-nesting birds, playing a role in local biodiversity conservation.

Name: High Peak Estate (Landscape Scale Recovery)
Description: Kinder Scout, an iconic plateau in the Peak District, is subject to extensive peatland restoration and rewilding. This work improves biodiversity, water quality, and carbon sequestration. The National Trust manages public access and conservation efforts.

Name: High Wood (River Eden Ancient Wood)
Description: High Wood is an ancient woodland nature reserve on the River Eden banks in Cumbria. It features mature trees, diverse ground flora like bluebells, and supports wildlife, including otters. Public footpaths provide access for visitors to explore its natural beauty.

Name: High Wood and Meadow
Description: High Wood and Meadow is an ancient woodland and species-rich meadow located in north Bedfordshire. Dominated by oak, ash, and field maple, it supports dormice and woodland birds. The meadow provides a habitat for various wildflowers and butterflies.

Name: High Woods Country Park
Description: High Woods Country Park, a Green Flag award winner in Colchester, features woodland, grassland, lake, and marsh habitats. Owned by Colchester City Council, it provides crucial space for local wildlife and community recreation. Essex Wildlife Trust manages adjacent areas.

Name: High Woods Country Park (Colchester)
Description: High Woods Country Park in Colchester is a Green Flag Award winner. It offers 170 acres of woodland, open spaces, a lake, and a visitor centre. The park provides various walking routes, wildlife habitats, and a children's play area for residents.

Name: Higham Ferrers Riverside
Description: Higham Ferrers Riverside is a flood plain meadow and riverbank site along the River Nene. Managed by Wildlife BCN, it supports wildflowers and grasses, providing habitat for insects and birds. Public footpaths offer access for nature observation.

Name: Higher Kiln Quarry (Bat Cave Stronghold)
Description: Higher Kiln Quarry, near Torquay, is a disused limestone quarry and nature reserve in South Devon. It is a nationally important bat roost, primarily for Greater Horseshoe bats. The site features flower-rich limestone grassland, supporting diverse butterflies.

Name: Higher Moors (Porth Hellick Wetland)
Description: Higher Moors is the largest wetland on St Mary's, Isles of Scilly, featuring reedbed, marsh, and open water habitats. This reserve supports resident and migratory birds, dragonflies, and rare plants. It includes Porth Hellick Pool and Lower Moors, managed for biodiversity.

Name: Highgate Common (Lowland Heathland)
Description: Highgate Common is a nationally important lowland heathland in South Staffordshire, managed by Staffordshire Wildlife Trust. The site features diverse habitats including acid grassland, woodland, and ponds, supporting adders, slow worms, and many invertebrates.

Name: Highgate Wood
Description: Highgate Wood is a 28-hectare ancient woodland in north London, managed as a public open space. It features mature trees, diverse wildlife, and facilities including a playground, cafe, and sports pitches. The site is popular for recreation and nature observation.

Name: Hill Court Farm (Wetland Recovery)
Description: Hill Court Farm, near Worcester, is a 95-hectare site undergoing significant wetland habitat restoration. Formerly a dairy farm, it now supports diverse wildlife, including curlews and otters, through a mosaic of ponds, wetlands, and woodlands.

Name: Hill Hook LNR (Sutton Coldfield)
Description: Hill Hook Local Nature Reserve is a former Victorian recreation ground in Sutton Coldfield. Managed by Birmingham and Black Country Wildlife Trust, it offers diverse habitats including grassland, woodland, and a lake, supporting local wildlife.

Name: Hill of White Hamars
Description: Hill of White Hamars is a varied nature reserve featuring lowland heath, scrub, grassland, and woodland. It provides habitats for juniper, various heathers, and a diverse range of birds and invertebrates. The site supports significant biodiversity within its landscape.

Name: Hill Park
Description: Hill Park is a tranquil grassland reserve near Leatherhead, known for its chalk grassland and diverse wildflowers. It supports various insect species, including butterflies and bees. The site offers views over the Mole Valley and is actively managed for biodiversity.

Name: Hilton Gravel Pits
Description: Hilton Gravel Pits is a diverse nature reserve in Derbyshire, formerly an aggregate quarry. It features open water, reedbeds, grassland, and woodland, supporting diverse bird species, invertebrates, and plant life.

Name: Hindhead Commons and the Devil's Punch Bowl
Description: Hindhead Commons and the Devil's Punch Bowl is a large area of heathland and woodland in Surrey, managed by the National Trust. It offers dramatic views, walking trails, and significant natural heritage. This site is known for its diverse wildlife and ongoing conservation.

Name: Hindhead Commons and the Devil's Punch Bowl (Surrey Hills)
Description: Hindhead Commons and the Devil's Punch Bowl features extensive heathland and woodland in the Surrey Hills, offering dramatic views. The former A3 road route is now reclaimed by nature after tunnel construction. This National Trust site is managed for conservation, supporting diverse wildlife including rare bird species and reptiles.

Name: Hinksey Heights
Description: Hinksey Heights is a diverse nature reserve in Oxford, featuring unique limestone grassland and ancient woodlands. It provides vital habitats for rare plants, butterflies, and birds, offering significant biodiversity. Visitors enjoy extensive views across the city.

Name: Hobbyhorse Wood
Description: Hobbyhorse Wood is an ancient woodland nature reserve near Ware, Hertfordshire, managed by Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust. It features diverse tree species and a rich ground flora, with notable bluebell displays in spring. The site supports various bird species.

Name: Hockenhull Platts
Description: Hockenhull Platts is a nature reserve in Cheshire, renowned for its medieval packhorse bridge and rich biodiversity. The site features various habitats, including woodlands, meadows, and the River Gowy, supporting diverse flora and fauna. It provides public access for nature observation and walks.

Name: Hockley Meadows
Description: Hockley Meadows is a diverse wildlife haven along the River Itchen in Hampshire. It features wet grassland, reedbed, scrub, and woodland. Otters and water voles frequent the river. Managed for wildlife, it offers a mosaic of habitats.

Name: Hockley Meadows (Itchen Valley)
Description: Hockley Meadows is a floodplain meadow reserve near Winchester, Hampshire, featuring a chalk stream section of the River Itchen. The site supports diverse plant life, water voles, and wading birds. Grazing cattle maintain the species-rich grasslands.

Name: Hod Hill (Iron Age Hillfort)
Description: Hod Hill is a dramatic Iron Age hillfort with visible Roman fort remains. It offers panoramic views across the Dorset countryside, supporting rare butterflies and wildflowers. The site is a significant archaeological and natural heritage area managed by the National Trust.

Name: Hoe Grange Quarry (Butterfly Specialist Site)
Description: Hoe Grange Quarry is a Derbyshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve near Longcliffe. This former limestone quarry features species-rich grassland, supporting diverse wildflowers and insects. The site is noted for its significant butterfly populations.

Name: Hoe Road Meadow
Description: Hoe Road Meadow is a species-rich SSSI in Bishop's Waltham. The meadow supports grassland fungi and wildflowers, maintained by traditional hay-cutting and grazing. Diverse habitats support various insects and birds.

Name: Hoe Rough and Beetley Meadows
Description: Hoe Rough and Beetley Meadows form a nature reserve in Norfolk, featuring a mosaic of woodland, grassland, heathland, and fen. Part of a SSSI along the River Wensum, it supports diverse invertebrates, rare plants like marsh orchids, and offers public footpaths.

Name: Hog's Back (Guildford to Seale)
Description: The Hog's Back nature reserve stretches across the North Downs, between Guildford and Seale. It features ancient woodland, grassland, and scrub, supporting diverse wildlife. The site is managed for biodiversity, offering walking trails and scenic views.

Name: Holborough Marshes
Description: Holborough Marshes is a Kent Wildlife Trust nature reserve located on the River Medway. It features diverse habitats including reedbeds, ditches, and wet grassland, supporting wildlife such as water voles, otters, kingfishers, and various dragonfly species.

Name: Holburn Moss
Description: Holburn Moss, a raised mire west of Lowick, supports diverse bog plants like sphagnum mosses, cotton-grass, and heather. It provides habitat for various bird species, showcasing a unique peatland ecosystem in Northumberland.

Name: Holcroft Moss
Description: Holcroft Moss is a Cheshire Wildlife Trust reserve and a Site of Special Scientific Interest. This raised bog habitat supports diverse Sphagnum mosses, cotton grass, and cranberries. It hosts various invertebrates, including dragonflies and damselflies.

Name: Holiday's Hill
Description: Holiday's Hill is a small nature reserve near Lyme Regis, featuring ancient woodland, neutral grassland, and unimproved acid grassland. It supports a variety of flora and fauna, including rare plant species and diverse invertebrates.

Name: Holland Haven (managed sections)
Description: Holland Haven is a coastal nature reserve in Essex, featuring saltmarsh, reedbeds, and grasslands. It provides essential habitats for a variety of birds, including bearded tits and marsh harriers, alongside diverse insect populations. The site offers accessible walking routes and birdwatching opportunities.

Name: Hollinhurst Wood
Description: Hollinhurst Wood is a semi-ancient woodland with an oak, ash, and hazel canopy. Bluebells carpet the ground in spring. Located near Barnsley, it supports diverse bird species and wildflowers. The site features a meandering stream and is accessible via public footpaths.

Name: Holly Hill
Description: Holly Hill is a Kent Wildlife Trust nature reserve near Meopham. It features ancient woodland with coppiced hazel, mature oak, and sweet chestnut trees. The site supports diverse wildlife, including various bird species and invertebrates, providing a habitat for woodland flora.

Name: Hollybank Wood (Newtownabbey)
Description: Hollybank Wood is a small but diverse woodland on the outskirts of Belfast, Northern Ireland. It features mature trees, open grassland, and walking paths, providing a valuable green space for local wildlife and community recreation.

Name: Holme Dunes
Description: Holme Dunes National Nature Reserve is a dynamic coastal landscape in Norfolk, featuring sand dunes, saltmarsh, and pine woodlands. It serves as a crucial stop-over for migratory birds and supports a colony of natterjack toads. Public access includes bird hides and walking trails.

Name: Holme Dunes (National Nature Reserve)
Description: Holme Dunes National Nature Reserve is a dynamic coastal landscape featuring sand dunes, saltmarsh, pine woodland, and freshwater pools. It hosts a diverse range of wildlife, including migrating birds, natterjack toads, and rare plants. The reserve protects important habitats and offers bird hides.

Name: Holme Fen
Description: Holme Fen is Great Britain's lowest point and Cambridgeshire's largest original fenland. It features a Silver Birch woodland and diverse fen habitats, including reedbeds and open water. The reserve supports rare invertebrates and birds.

Name: Holme Fen (NNR)
Description: Holme Fen is a National Nature Reserve in Cambridgeshire and a key part of the Great Fen project. It holds the lowest point in Britain and features diverse habitats including silver birch woodland and fenland. Distinctive 'bog oaks' are present.

Name: Holme Pierrepont (Skylarks Nature Reserve)
Description: Skylarks Nature Reserve is part of the Holme Pierrepont National Watersports Centre, owned by Nottinghamshire County Council. The site features woodland, grassland, wetland, and open water, attracting diverse bird life. It is popular with walkers, runners, and cyclists, offering good public access.

Name: Holmsley Gravel Pit
Description: Holmsley Gravel Pit is a former quarry in the New Forest. It features ponds, wet and dry heathland, acid grassland, and woodland, supporting diverse wildlife including dragonflies, damselflies, birds, and reptiles.

Name: Holt Fish Pass (River Severn)
Description: Holt Lock & Weir is the highest point fish can reach on the River Severn. The fish pass, part of the Unlocking the Severn project, helps species like the Twaite Shad bypass the weir. The site includes an observation window for visitors to view migrating fish. Parking is available nearby.

Name: Holt Lowes
Description: Holt Lowes is a diverse heathland, grassland, and woodland site in Norfolk. It supports a range of wildlife, including nightjars, woodlarks, and adders. The site features various habitats, providing important biodiversity.

Name: Holway Woods (Dorset Wildlife Trust)
Description: Holway Woods is an ancient woodland with a mix of oak, ash, hazel, and field maple. Seasonal wildflowers carpet the ground, including bluebells in spring. The reserve features a stream and diverse birdlife. Public footpaths allow access for visitors.

Name: Holwell Nature Reserve (Former Ironstone Diggings)
Description: Holwell Nature Reserve is a former ironstone quarry near Melton Mowbray, managed by Leicestershire and Rutland Wildlife Trust. It features diverse habitats including woodland, grassland, and wetland, supporting a wide range of flora and fauna, such as marsh orchids, butterflies, and amphibians. It is a Local Wildlife Site.

Name: Holyfield Marsh
Description: Holyfield Marsh, part of Lee Valley Regional Park in Essex, is an internationally important wetland. It provides a vital habitat for diverse bird populations, including waterfowl and waders, offering year-round birdwatching.

Name: Holywell Bay & Kelsey Head
Description: Holywell Bay offers golden sands, grassy dunes, and the iconic Gull Rocks on the North Cornwall coast. Part of a larger landscape including Kelsey Head, it provides coastal walks, wildlife spotting, and rock pool exploration for visitors.

Name: Holywell Pond
Description: Holywell Pond is a key Northumberland nature reserve, noted for its diverse birdlife. A large freshwater pond, surrounded by reedbeds and grassland, provides essential habitat for many species. Visitors can observe various waterfowl from hides.

Name: Homefield Wood
Description: Homefield Wood is a mixed woodland nature reserve near Marlow. It features ancient woodland characteristics and supports diverse flora and fauna, including dormice and various orchids. Traditional coppicing is used to maintain biodiversity and create habitats for rare butterflies.

Name: Honeygar
Description: Honeygar is a 145-acre nature reserve on the Somerset Levels, undergoing restoration from arable land to wetland habitats. It supports bird species like lapwing and curlew, plus many invertebrates, forming part of a larger Living Landscape area.

Name: Honeygar Research Hub (Lowland Peat Center)
Description: Honeygar Research Hub is a Somerset Wildlife Trust site dedicated to lowland peatland research. It hosts collaborative projects monitoring peatland restoration, carbon sequestration, and biodiversity. It supports sustainable land management in the Somerset Levels and Moors.

Name: Honeymoor Common
Description: Honeymoor Common, a Herefordshire nature reserve, features unimproved acid grassland, heathland, and marshy areas. The Batch Brook flows through the site, supporting diverse flora, invertebrates, and bird species.

Name: Honeypot Wood (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Honeypot Wood, an ancient woodland in Norfolk, is managed by Norfolk Wildlife Trust. It supports diverse wildlife, including woodpeckers and nuthatches. In spring, bluebells and wood anemones carpet the floor. Archaeological finds include prehistoric flint tools.

Name: Hook Common and Bartley Heath
Description: Hook Common and Bartley Heath is a large nature reserve in northeast Hampshire. It features diverse habitats including dry and wet heathland, acid grassland, woodland, and ponds. The reserve supports wildlife such as dragonflies, common lizards, woodlarks, and Dartford warblers. It is managed for conservation.

Name: Hook Mountain (North Hants Heath)
Description: Hook Mountain, a Site of Special Scientific Interest, features extensive heathland, acid grassland, and woodland. Part of the Basingstoke heathlands, it supports rare ground-nesting birds, reptiles like sand lizards, and diverse invertebrates. Popular for walking and cycling.

Name: Hook Norton Cutting (Geological & Ironstone)
Description: Hook Norton Cutting is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) and a regionally important geological site. It features Lower Jurassic Marlstone rock, rich in ironstone fossils and minerals. The reserve includes limestone grassland, home to diverse flora and insects. Managed by BBOWT for geological and ecological interest.

Name: Hookheath Meadows
Description: Hookheath Meadows is a small wildlife haven bordering the River Blackwater. It comprises two traditionally managed hay meadows. The site hosts diverse wildflowers like ragged robin and great burnet, along with various grasses. It supports a range of insects, including butterflies and dragonflies.

Name: Hookheath Meadows Nature Reserve
Description: Hookheath Meadows is a diverse mosaic of wildflower meadows, ancient woodland, and hedgerows along the River Blackwater. It provides a habitat for various plants, insects, and birds. The site is managed for conservation and biodiversity.

Name: Hope Valley (Rainforest Restoration)
Description: Hope Valley, near Minsterley, Shropshire, is a steep-sided wooded valley with ancient semi-natural woodland. It hosts wildflowers, fungi, and mosses, and is classed as a 'rainforest' due to its lush, damp conditions. This site is a Site of Special Scientific Interest.

Name: Hopland's Wood
Description: Hopland's Wood is a small ancient woodland on boulder clay, part of a larger plantation. It features mature ash and oak, with field maple, hazel and wild cherry. Bluebells bloom in spring, along with early purple orchid and greater butterfly-orchid.

Name: Hopton Fen
Description: Hopton Fen is a mosaic of wet woodland, reedbed, and species-rich unimproved grassland in the Waveney Valley. It provides habitat for diverse wildlife, including otters, water voles, and various invertebrates. Springs create flush habitats.

Name: Hornchurch Country Park
Description: Hornchurch Country Park is a Green Flag park in Havering, London, on a former WWII airfield. It offers diverse habitats like grasslands, woodlands, and ponds, traversed by the River Ingrebourne, providing wildlife haven and recreation.

Name: Horsey Mere & Marshes
Description: Horsey Mere and its marshes are a key part of the Norfolk Broads. The site offers panoramic views and provides habitat for diverse wildlife, including many bird species. Visitors explore the historic Horsey Windpump and its scenic walking trails.

Name: Horsey Windpump (The Broads)
Description: Horsey Windpump stands on the Horsey Estate in the Broads National Park, Norfolk. This iconic National Trust windpump offers panoramic views across the mere and surrounding marshes, a haven for wildlife. Visitors can climb to the top for expansive views.

Name: Hosehill Lake
Description: Hosehill Lake is a former gravel pit, now a nature reserve west of Reading. It offers open water, reedbeds, and woodland, supporting overwintering wildfowl and migrating birds. Footpaths provide access for wildlife observation and quiet recreation.

Name: Hosehill Lake (Bird Sanctuary)
Description: Hosehill Lake is a former gravel pit near Theale, West Berkshire, now a diverse nature reserve. It features a large lake, reedbeds, and woodlands, providing a vital habitat for numerous bird species throughout the year. Visitors can enjoy walking trails and bird hides.

Name: Hoselaw Loch and Din Moss
Description: Hoselaw Loch and Din Moss is a Scottish Borders reserve with an ancient loch and peatland habitat. It is a Site of Special Scientific Interest. The site supports otters, whooper swans, overwintering wildfowl, and diverse plant species. Habitats include fen, marsh, and woodland.

Name: Hothfield Heathlands (Last Post-Glacial Heath)
Description: Hothfield Heathlands is Kent's last remaining lowland heath, a rare habitat supporting bog plants, heather, and gorse. Rare insects thrive here, alongside birds like willow warbler and nightjar. The reserve features ponds and boardwalks.

Name: Houghton Forest (Chalk Downland Edge)
Description: Houghton Forest lies on the edge of the South Downs National Park in West Sussex. It features varied woodlands, open chalk grassland, and panoramic views across the Arun Valley. This site provides diverse habitats and is popular for walking.

Name: Houghton Meadows
Description: Houghton Meadows is a fenland remnant in the Ouse Valley, bordering the River Great Ouse. Designated a SSSI, it features diverse wildflowers like marsh-marigold and meadow-rue, supporting varied insect life. The reserve provides a mosaic of fen, wet grassland, and pond habitats.

Name: Hounslow Heath (Green Gym)
Description: Hounslow Heath Green Gym is a TCV project in West London. It meets weekly, offering conservation activities like scrub removal, path clearing, and pond maintenance. Tasks vary seasonally, contributing to the management of Hounslow Heath. No experience is required.

Name: Hoveringham Pastures (Trent Valley)
Description: Hoveringham Pastures is a mosaic of grasslands, wetlands, and open water habitats located in the Trent Valley, Nottinghamshire. The reserve supports diverse birdlife, including migratory species, and a range of invertebrates. It is managed to enhance biodiversity and maintain ecological balance.

Name: How Hill (Broads Authority)
Description: How Hill National Nature Reserve offers diverse habitats within the Norfolk Broads, including wetlands and woodlands. Visitors can explore nature trails, the gardens of How Hill House, and a boardwalk through sedge and rush beds. Electric Eel boat trips provide unique water-based tours.

Name: Howe Ridding (Wood Pasture)
Description: Howe Ridding is a small wood pasture nature reserve in Cumbria. Ancient oak and ash trees stand within semi-natural grassland, supporting diverse invertebrates, fungi, and bird species. Grazing cattle manage the sward.

Name: Howell Hill
Description: Howell Hill, a Surrey Wildlife Trust reserve near Ewell, features varied habitats including grassland, woodland, and a pond. It supports diverse wildlife, such as birds and amphibians. Management includes coppicing and scrub clearance for biodiversity.

Name: Howell Hill (Ewell Chalk)
Description: Howell Hill is a small, steep chalk grassland site with a mosaic of scrub, woodland and grassland, including areas of both ancient woodland and regenerating secondary woodland. Wildflowers like pyramidal orchid thrive.

Name: Howlands Marsh (Colne Estuary)
Description: Howlands Marsh is a small coastal grazing marsh beside the Colne Estuary. Dominated by rush and sedge, the reserve hosts a diverse range of wading birds and wildfowl, especially during winter months. Public access is limited to a small track along the seawall, offering views across the marsh.

Name: Howsham Mill (Island)
Description: Howsham Mill Island Nature Reserve is a wooded island in the River Derwent near Malton, North Yorkshire. It provides habitat for otters, kingfishers, and various invertebrates. The island is part of a larger SSSI and is managed for biodiversity, though it is not publicly accessible.

Name: Huckerby's Meadows
Description: Huckerby's Meadows is a valuable wet meadow, rich in wildlife and supporting over 140 plant species. Situated on the floodplain of the River Lea, it features sedges, grasses, and wildflowers like cuckooflower and ragged-robin. Managed for biodiversity.

Name: Hucking Estate
Description: Hucking Estate offers a mosaic of ancient woodland, grassland, orchards, and arable land in the Kent Downs Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. This wildlife haven supports varied flora and fauna, including dormice and many bird species. Walking trails are present.

Name: Hucking Estate (Partnership)
Description: Hucking Estate is a large woodland and open space mosaic in Kent, South East England. It features ancient woodland, recently planted areas, and meadows, providing diverse habitats for wildlife. The site offers numerous walking trails and is part of a landscape restoration project.

Name: Huish Moor
Description: Huish Moor, a wet grassland and rush pasture in Somerset, supports rare plants like Cornish moneywort and tubular water-dropwort. The site provides crucial breeding habitat for waders including curlew, lapwing, and snipe. Management enhances biodiversity and hydrological conditions.

Name: Humberhead Peatlands NNR (Thorne & Hatfield Moors)
Description: Humberhead Peatlands National Nature Reserve is England's largest lowland raised bog, encompassing Thorne, Hatfield, and Crowle Moors across 2,800 hectares. It is a Special Area of Conservation, vital for its unique peatland habitats and rare wildlife. Restoration efforts are ongoing.

Name: Humberhead Peatlands NNR (Thorne & Hatfield)
