Description: Humberhead Peatlands NNR, near Doncaster, South Yorkshire, is a major lowland raised bog remnant, combining Thorne and Hatfield Moors. It supports rare plants and invertebrates. Natural England manages the site, providing vital wildlife habitat. Volunteering is available.

Name: Humphrey Head (Coastal Limestone)
Description: Humphrey Head is a coastal limestone outcrop on the Cartmel Peninsula, providing spectacular views over Morecambe Bay. This Cumbria Wildlife Trust nature reserve features limestone grassland, woodland, scrub, and cliffs, supporting unique plants and diverse wildlife.

Name: Hunsdon and Eastwick Meads
Description: Hunsdon and Eastwick Meads is a Site of Special Scientific Interest in Hertfordshire. This traditional riverside grazing meadow supports diverse flora, including various grasses, sedges, and wildflowers. The site is important for insects, amphibians, and birds.

Name: Hunter's Baulk
Description: Hunter's Baulk is a small, species-rich grassland nature reserve in Warwickshire. It features a medieval field boundary baulk, creating diverse habitats for wildflowers such as cowslips and ox-eye daisies, alongside various insect species. The reserve offers valuable insight into traditional agricultural landscapes.

Name: Hunter's Hill (Douglas Valley)
Description: Hunter's Hill is a woodland reserve overlooking the Douglas Valley near Parbold. Mixed oak and birch woodlands support diverse wildlife along a network of paths. The site also features the remains of a Victorian folly, offering historical interest to visitors.

Name: Hunter's Wood
Description: Hunter's Wood is a 3.7-hectare ancient woodland, primarily composed of mature oak, birch, and rowan trees. The reserve features a mix of broadleaf and coniferous trees, with bluebells in spring. Tawny owls, treecreepers, dragonflies, and damselflies are present.

Name: Hustyn Wood
Description: Hustyn Wood is a mixed woodland in Cornwall, featuring ancient trees and a diverse habitat for wildlife. It offers walking trails through broadleaf and conifer sections, providing a peaceful natural escape. The site contributes to local biodiversity.

Name: Hutchison's Meadow
Description: Hutchison’s Meadow is a charming wet meadow and woodland, located in the village of Great Glemham, near Saxmundham. The reserve spans 1.25 hectares, offering a mosaic of diverse habitats that support a wide range of wildlife.

Name: Huttoft Bank Pit
Description: Huttoft Bank Pit is a former sand and gravel quarry now supporting diverse aquatic and terrestrial wildlife. It features several ponds, fenland, and grasslands, providing habitats for various bird species, invertebrates, and plant life, including orchids.

Name: Huttoft Marsh (Coastal Grazing)
Description: Huttoft Marsh is a coastal grazing marsh and a Site of Special Scientific Interest in Lincolnshire. It features freshwater scrapes, ditches, and reedbeds, supporting breeding and wintering birds like avocets and lapwings. It is part of the Lincolnshire Coastal Country Park.

Name: Hutton Roof Crags (Limestone Pavement)
Description: Hutton Roof Crags, near Kirkby Lonsdale, is a nationally important mosaic of limestone grassland and ancient woodland. Its extensive limestone pavement provides a unique habitat for specialised plants and diverse invertebrates. Mixed broadleaf woodland also flourishes.

Name: Hyde Park (The Dell & Serpentine Wildlife)
Description: Hyde Park in central London features The Dell, a picturesque rock garden, and the Serpentine lake, which supports diverse wildlife. It is one of the capital's eight Royal Parks, offering both natural habitats and recreational spaces for visitors.

Name: Hythe Spartina Marsh (Southampton Water)
Description: Hythe Spartina Marsh is a saltmarsh habitat on the western shore of Southampton Water, important for wading birds and migratory species. It features spartina grass, tidal creeks, and mudflats, supporting diverse invertebrates and fish. The site is a significant feeding and roosting ground.

Name: Ickenham Marsh
Description: Ickenham Marsh is a remnant of ancient wet woodland and marshland habitat along the River Pinn. Managed to protect its rich biodiversity, it supports various plant and animal species, including mature alder and willow trees, and diverse invertebrates.

Name: Ickworth Estate (Nature Recovery & Lownde Wood)
Description: Ickworth Estate is an 1,800-acre Suffolk property featuring a neoclassical mansion, Italianate garden, and ancient deer park. The estate actively manages nature recovery projects across its parkland and Lownde Wood, enhancing diverse habitats for wildlife. Visitors can explore extensive walks.

Name: Ickworth Estate (Parkland & Ancient Wood)
Description: Ickworth Estate, a National Trust property in Suffolk, encompasses an 1,800-acre parkland with ancient woodlands, a historic Italianate Palace, and gardens. It features the grand rotunda and was once home to the Hervey family. The estate offers various walks and opportunities to observe wildlife.

Name: Ide Hill (Greensand Ridge)
Description: Ide Hill offers panoramic views over the Weald of Kent, featuring ancient woodlands and open commons along the Greensand Ridge. Visitors can follow waymarked trails through diverse habitats. The site is managed for conservation, providing opportunities for walking and enjoying nature.

Name: Idle Valley Nature Reserve
Description: Idle Valley Nature Reserve, a 400-hectare wetland in the East Midlands, features lakes, reeds, and grasslands along the River Idle. It supports diverse bird species like overwintering wildfowl and migrating waders, plus otters and various invertebrates.

Name: Idle Valley Nature Reserve (North Notts Wetlands)
Description: Idle Valley Nature Reserve, near Retford, is one of the East Midlands' largest wetland sites, spanning 450 hectares. The reserve features lakes, wetlands, and woodland habitats, supporting a diverse array of wildlife, including numerous bird species year-round.

Name: Iffley Meadows
Description: Iffley Meadows is a floodplain meadow in Oxfordshire, renowned for its snakeshead fritillaries. Located next to the River Thames, it offers a habitat for various invertebrates and waterfowl. Managed for wildlife conservation, it provides an important green space.

Name: Iffley Meadows (Snake's-head Fritillary)
Description: Iffley Meadows is a riverside site in Oxford, famous for its display of Snakes-head Fritillaries in April. It is a floodplain meadow on the River Thames with various wildlife habitats including hedgerows, ditches, and the river itself.

Name: Ilam Park & Dovedale (White Peak Limestone)
Description: Ilam Park, a 160-acre riverside park, features a 17th-century hall and designed landscape near Dovedale. It offers walking routes along the River Manifold and serves as a gateway to the White Peak, known for its distinctive limestone geology.

Name: Ilfracombe & Torrs Park
Description: A dramatic coastal landscape near Ilfracombe, North Devon, offering rugged cliffs, panoramic views across the Bristol Channel, and ancient woodlands. Popular for walking, the site features maintained paths, including parts of the South West Coast Path. Managed for nature and public access.

Name: Inchnadamph NNR (Bone Caves)
Description: Inchnadamph NNR in the Scottish Highlands is a landscape of limestone hills, caves, and gorges. Its famous Bone Caves contain unique remains of arctic animals from the last Ice Age, including lynx and polar bear. The reserve supports ancient woodlands and diverse plant life.

Name: Inchree (Waterfall & Wood)
Description: Inchree, near Fort William, features a spectacular multi-tiered waterfall and accessible woodland trails for walking and cycling. Visitors enjoy nature and wildlife watching opportunities within the Scottish Highlands.

Name: Ingrebourne Valley
Description: Ingrebourne Valley is a diverse nature reserve in East London, featuring grasslands, woodlands, marshes, and the River Ingrebourne. It supports various wildlife, including water voles, kingfishers, and rare invertebrates, with accessible nature trails.

Name: Ingrebourne Valley (Havering)
Description: Ingrebourne Valley, a nature reserve in Havering, offers diverse habitats including woodlands, grasslands, and wetlands. Part of Ingrebourne Marshes SSSI, it runs south from Upminster to Hornchurch, supporting various wildlife species and providing public recreation.

Name: Inkpen Common (Heathy Plateau)
Description: Inkpen Common is a heathland site on a high plateau, featuring heather, gorse, and acid grassland. It supports Dartford warblers, woodlarks, and nightjars. The site includes ancient woodland and ponds, providing diverse habitats.

Name: Inkpen Crocus Field
Description: Inkpen Crocus Field is a small, unique grassland site in West Berkshire, famous for its annual display of wild crocuses. It is managed to conserve this rare flowering spectacle, attracting visitors in late winter, and supports diverse flora.

Name: Inner Forth (Landscape Project)
Description: The Inner Forth Wetlands are a significant landscape project in Central Scotland, focused on restoring and connecting vital habitats for migratory birds and local wildlife. It encompasses a network of nature reserves and coastal areas.

Name: Insh Marshes (River Spey)
Description: Insh Marshes is one of Europe's most important wetlands, a vast flood plain alongside the River Spey. Thousands of migratory birds stop here seasonally, and many species of waders, ducks, and geese breed in summer. The reserve supports diverse wildlife, including otters and ospreys. Accessible trails and hides offer viewing opportunities.

Name: Invereshie and Inshriach NNR
Description: Invereshie and Inshriach NNR spans a vast Highland landscape between Strathspey and Speyside. It features extensive Caledonian pinewoods, open moorland, montane habitats, and the River Feshie. This reserve supports rare species including capercaillie, Scottish crossbill, red squirrel, and wildcat.

Name: Inverewe Garden & Estate (Wild Side)
Description: Inverewe Garden & Estate is a historic botanical garden in Wester Ross, Scotland, established in the 19th century. It showcases diverse plant collections from around the world, benefiting from the Gulf Stream. The estate includes woodlands, a walled garden, and offers various walking routes.

Name: Iona (Coast & Grazing)
Description: Iona, an island off Mull, offers sandy beaches, rocky coves, and significant natural heritage. Its traditional crofting lands support diverse wildlife, including corncrakes. It is a historic place of pilgrimage and peace.

Name: Iping and Stedham Commons
Description: Iping and Stedham Commons in West Sussex protect extensive areas of dry and wet lowland heathland, mire, acid grassland, and woodland. The site supports six native reptile species, Dartford warblers, woodlarks, and nightjars. Public footpaths allow access to this diverse habitat.

Name: Iping and Stedham Commons (Lowland Heath)
Description: Iping and Stedham Commons comprise lowland heathland, woodland, mire, and wet grassland habitats. A Site of Special Scientific Interest and Special Area of Conservation on the Greensand ridge, it supports rare species including sand lizards, smooth snakes, and nightjars.

Name: Ipstones Edge (Swineholes Wood & Black Heath)
Description: Ipstones Edge includes Swineholes Wood, an ancient semi-natural woodland, and Black Heath, an acid grassland and heathland. The reserve supports diverse flora like heather and bilberry, along with various bird species and invertebrates.

Name: Iron Latch
Description: Iron Latch is a mature woodland reserve near Layer Marney in Essex, featuring oak, ash, and hazel trees. Ancient woodland flora thrives on the floor, providing a habitat for diverse bird species. Public footpaths allow access.

Name: Irthlingborough Lakes and Meadows
Description: Irthlingborough Lakes and Meadows is a nature reserve in Northamptonshire, part of the Nene Wetlands. It features diverse wetland habitats, including open water, reedbeds, and wildflower meadows, supporting a rich array of bird, insect, and plant life.

Name: Isabella Plantation (Richmond Park)
Description: Isabella Plantation is a 40-acre ornamental woodland garden located within Richmond Park in London. It is renowned for its evergreen azaleas, rhododendrons, and other acid-loving plants. The garden features diverse seasonal displays, streams, and ponds, providing a habitat for native wildlife.

Name: Isleworth Ait (River Crane Project)
Description: Isleworth Ait is a wooded island in the River Thames near Isleworth, West London, close to where the River Crane meets the Thames. The Conservation Volunteers (TCV) supports habitat management work in the wider River Crane Project area to enhance local biodiversity and natural habitats.

Name: Iver Nature Centre
Description: Iver Nature Centre in Buckinghamshire features diverse habitats including ponds, meadows, and a community orchard. It serves as a local hub for environmental activities and outdoor learning, supporting wildlife thriving in the area.

Name: Jackson’s Bank
Description: Jackson’s Bank is a community woodland near Swadlincote within the National Forest, featuring mixed broadleaf trees and a network of footpaths. The site offers opportunities for local residents and visitors to explore nature and enjoy woodland walks, supporting local biodiversity.

Name: Jackson's Coppice & Marsh
Description: Jackson's Coppice and Marsh is a nature reserve in Staffordshire, featuring ancient woodlands and wetlands. This site supports a diverse range of plant and animal species, including various invertebrates and amphibians. It offers quiet enjoyment of nature.

Name: Jan Hobbs
Description: Jan Hobbs is a small woodland nature reserve located in the Somerset Levels, managed by the Somerset Wildlife Trust. It protects mature oak and ash trees, providing essential habitat for a variety of birds, insects, and wildflowers throughout the seasons.

Name: Jeffry Bog
Description: Jeffry Bog is a rare lowland raised mire in Yorkshire. This nature reserve supports diverse peat-forming bog plants, including sphagnum mosses, cotton grass, and heather. It provides a vital habitat for various insects, such as dragonflies and damselflies, contributing to regional biodiversity.

Name: Jeskyns Community Woodland
Description: Jeskyns Community Woodland spans 360 acres near Cobham, Kent, featuring new and established woodlands, meadows, and ponds. Owned by Forestry Commission and managed by Kent Wildlife Trust, it offers trails, play areas, and a cafe. This diverse site provides habitats for varied wildlife.

Name: Joe's Pond
Description: Joe's Pond is a former ballast pond and unique man-made wetland in County Durham. It supports rare plants, various dragonflies, and amphibians. A surfaced path and boardwalk provide accessible views of the pond and its diverse wildlife.

Name: Johnsons Meadows
Description: Johnson's Meadows is a small hay meadow in Essex, purchased by the Trust in 1968. It supports flowering plants like ox-eye daisy and great burnet. Wetter areas host meadowsweet and purple loosestrife. The meadow is managed with a single hay cut each summer, followed by grazing.

Name: Johnston Terrace Garden
Description: Johnston Terrace Garden is a small, sloping urban wildlife garden at the foot of Edinburgh Castle, managed by the Scottish Wildlife Trust. It offers a vital green space for city dwellers and supports diverse plant and animal life.

Name: Jones's Mill (Fenland Sanctuary)
Description: Jones's Mill is a fenland sanctuary near Pewsey, Wiltshire, managed by Wiltshire Wildlife Trust. It features reedbeds, wet woodland, and wet grassland. The reserve supports rare plants and invertebrates, including marsh orchids and water voles. Boardwalk access is available.

Name: Jones's Mill (River Avon Fen)
Description: Jones's Mill is a Wiltshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve on the River Avon floodplain. This site protects a rare fen habitat, crucial for diverse wetland flora and fauna. Boardwalks allow access to observe species adapted to the wet conditions, including various rare plants and invertebrates.

Name: Jones's Rough (Limestone Woodland)
Description: Jones's Rough is a species-rich limestone woodland on a steep slope in Shropshire. Green hellebore, stinking hellebore, and purple-flowered honesty thrive. The site supports various butterflies like the brown hairstreak. Management includes coppicing and glade creation.

Name: Jupiter Urban Wildlife Centre
Description: Jupiter Urban Wildlife Centre is an urban nature reserve in Grangemouth, Falkirk. It features a mosaic of woodland, ponds, and grassland habitats within an industrial setting. The centre offers environmental education and community engagement, including a wildlife garden and interpretation displays, supporting local biodiversity.

Name: Keal Carr
Description: Keal Carr is a small wetland nature reserve near Keal Cotes, Lincolnshire, managed by the Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust. It provides crucial habitat for a diverse array of wildlife, including bird species and amphibians, within its reedbeds and wet woodlands.

Name: Kearney Village & Coast (Ards Peninsula)
Description: Kearney Village, Ards Peninsula, Northern Ireland, is a National Trust site. It preserves a traditional Ulster fishing community. Whitewashed cottages, a harbour, coastal paths, wildlife, and sea views.

Name: Kedington Meadow (Community Conservation)
Description: Kedington Meadow is a species-rich grassland near Kedington in Suffolk, managed for its wildflowers and insects. Traditional hay cutting and grazing maintain this vital habitat. The reserve offers an important green space for local wildlife.

Name: Kedleston Hall (Parkland & Serpentine Lake)
Description: Kedleston Hall, an 18th-century mansion near Derby, sits amidst historic parkland. Designed by Robert Adam and Matthew Brettingham, the estate features a large serpentine lake and ancient trees, supporting diverse wildlife.

Name: Kelham Bridge
Description: Kelham Bridge Nature Reserve is a small wet grassland site near Bottesford, Leicestershire. It features unimproved wet grassland, marsh, and open water, supporting various wading birds like curlew and snipe, and diverse wildfowl such as teal. The reserve is notable for its array of plant species adapted to damp conditions, providing a crucial habitat within the local landscape.

Name: Kelham Heronry
Description: Kelham Heronry, a Site of Special Scientific Interest by the River Trent, hosts one of the UK's largest heron colonies. It provides a vital breeding ground for grey herons and little egrets within diverse wetland habitats, including wet woodland.

Name: Kelsick Moss
Description: Kelsick Moss is a raised bog nature reserve near Sedbergh, Cumbria. It features diverse mosses, cotton grass, and heather, supporting dragonflies, damselflies, and various bird species. The site is a significant example of a lowland raised bog ecosystem.

Name: Kemsing Down
Description: Kemsing Down is an ancient chalk grassland and woodland site near Sevenoaks in Kent. It features diverse flora, including orchids and rare wildflowers, supporting numerous insect species. The reserve offers panoramic views across the valley.

Name: Kemsing Down (Chalk Escarpment)
Description: Kemsing Down is a chalk escarpment and woodland site, forming part of the Kent Downs Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. The reserve provides habitats for wildflowers, butterflies, and diverse insect life, offering expansive views across the Weald. Kent Wildlife Trust manages the area for conservation.

Name: Kemyel Crease
Description: Kemyel Crease is a coastal nature reserve near Lamorna, West Cornwall. It features wooded valleys and ancient semi-natural woodland, sloping down to the sea. The site protects diverse flora and fauna, including rare lichens and various bird species. It offers walking trails.

Name: Ken-Dee Marshes
Description: Ken-Dee Marshes is an RSPB nature reserve in Dumfries and Galloway, Scotland. It features a mosaic of wetlands, woodlands, and grasslands, supporting ospreys, red kites, and other diverse wildlife. The site offers trails and hides for visitors.

Name: Kenilworth Common
Description: Kenilworth Common features diverse habitats including ancient woodland, grassland, and a pond within Kenilworth. It provides crucial habitat for various wildlife species, from wildflowers to amphibians, and is maintained for both biodiversity and public enjoyment.

Name: Kenley Common
Description: Kenley Common is a 55-hectare site in Croydon, South London. It features chalk grassland, ancient woodland, and neutral grassland, offering extensive views and supporting diverse wildlife. The common includes elements of the historic Kenley Airfield.

Name: Kenley Common (City of London)
Description: Kenley Common is a 55-hectare chalk grassland and ancient woodland in Surrey, near London. A Site of Special Scientific Interest, it hosts diverse wildlife. The common offers popular walking routes and scenic views over the Caterham valley.

Name: Kennall Vale
Description: Kennall Vale is a former gunpowder works nestled in a steep wooded valley. The site features the remains of old mill buildings, granite leats, and the River Kennall. Ancient woodland covers the valley sides, supporting diverse flora and fauna. The area is popular for walks.

Name: Kennall Vale (Forestry/TWT Joint)
Description: Kennall Vale, a former gunpowder works in Cornwall, is now a nature reserve with ancient woodlands, historic ruins, and the River Kennall. The site offers walking trails, wildlife observation, and exploration of industrial heritage.

Name: Kensington Gardens - The Long Water (Bird Sanctuary)
Description: The Long Water in Kensington Gardens, shared with Hyde Park, serves as a significant bird sanctuary. It provides a haven for diverse waterfowl species including mute swans, greylag geese, and tufted ducks, supporting local wildlife populations.

Name: Kensington Gardens (Wilderness & Allotment)
Description: Kensington Gardens is a 265-acre Royal Park in London. It includes the Wildflower Meadow, offering key habitats for local wildlife. The park provides public access to diverse landscapes, including formal gardens, monuments, and open spaces for relaxation.

Name: Kersal Dale (Irwell Valley)
Description: Kersal Dale is a local nature reserve in Salford, part of the Irwell Valley. It features diverse habitats including woodlands, grasslands, and wetlands, supporting a variety of wildlife. The site is popular for walks and nature observation.

Name: Kesters Docking (Habitat Mosaic)
Description: Kesters Docking is a former arable field south of Engine Farm. It is currently being restored to wet grassland with shallow scrapes providing good feeding and breeding habitat for wading birds. These scrapes hold water all year round.

Name: Ketford Banks
Description: Ketford Banks is a Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust reserve featuring woodland, species-rich grassland, and traditional orchard trees alongside the River Leadon. Grazing maintains diverse habitats for orchids, birds, and invertebrates.

Name: Ketton Quarry
Description: Ketton Quarry is a 66-hectare former limestone quarry and active cement works. It is now a mosaic of woodland, grassland, scrub, and ponds, providing diverse habitats for wildlife. The site features rich flora including orchids, butterflies, and amphibians, managed for biodiversity.

Name: Kielder Water & Forest Park
Description: Kielder Water & Forest Park, Northumberland, hosts northern Europe's largest man-made lake and England's biggest forest. This 250 sq mile park offers walking, cycling, water sports, and vital habitats for red squirrels and ospreys. It is famed for its dark skies.

Name: Kielderhead (High Upland Recovery)
Description: Kielderhead, a remote upland area in Northumberland, is central to significant habitat restoration efforts within Kielder Forest. The High Upland Recovery project focuses on rewilding, peatland restoration, and natural regeneration, aiming to enhance biodiversity and ecological resilience.

Name: Kielderhead NNR (The High Wild)
Description: Kielderhead NNR is a vast nature reserve in Northumberland, covering blanket bog and heather moorland. It provides a habitat for rare birds including hen harrier, merlin, and black grouse. The site is vital for carbon storage and water regulation.

Name: Killaloo Wood (Sperrin Edge)
Description: Killaloo Wood is a mature broadleaved woodland near Derry. It features a circular path, offering views of the Sperrin Mountains and the River Faughan. Species include oak, ash, and hazel, with a diverse ground flora.

Name: Killerton Estate (Massive Tree Planting Site)
Description: Killerton Estate in Devon features an 18th-century house, extensive garden, and parkland. It encompasses woodlands and is an active site for large-scale tree planting and conservation efforts, supporting local biodiversity.

Name: Killiecrankie (Wooded Gorge)
Description: Killiecrankie is a dramatic wooded gorge in Perthshire, Scotland, famed for spectacular autumnal colours. It features the 'Soldier's Leap' viewpoint over the River Garry, offering diverse walking trails and habitats for wildlife.

Name: Killington Reservoir (Waterbirds)
Description: Killington Reservoir is a large waterbody significant for wintering wildfowl and migrating waders in Cumbria. Its surrounding habitats include reedbeds and woodland, attracting various bird species. Ospreys and terns are observed here, making it a key site for waterbird conservation and observation.

Name: Kilminning Coast
Description: Kilminning Coast is a rocky shoreline and coastal grassland in Fife, Scotland. It features seabirds like fulmars and kittiwakes nesting on cliffs, alongside wildflowers such as thrift and sea campion. The area supports a variety of marine life.

Name: Kilminorth Woods
Description: Kilminorth Woods, an ancient woodland by the West Looe River in Cornwall, is a designated SSSI. It hosts diverse flora like wild garlic and bluebells, alongside fauna such as bats and otters. Well-maintained walking trails provide access to the Looe Valley Line.

Name: Kilmory (Knapdale Beaver Interface)
Description: Kilmory is a key site within Knapdale, famous for the Scottish Beaver Trial. It features walking trails offering views of beaver activity and their impact on the landscape. The visitor infrastructure includes an accessible hide, car park, and information panels detailing the species' reintroduction.

Name: Kiln Wood
Description: Kiln Wood is an ancient woodland reserve located south of Roughway, near Plaxtol, Kent. It features oak, hornbeam, and hazel coppice with diverse ground flora including bluebells and wood anemones. The site is managed by Kent Wildlife Trust.

Name: Kilnhurst Ings (River Don Washlands)
Description: Kilnhurst Ings is a Yorkshire Wildlife Trust site near the River Don. This wetland habitat supports various bird species, including lapwings and snipe, particularly during winter. The site features scrapes, ponds, and reedbeds, contributing to its biodiversity. It is a key area for local wildlife conservation efforts.

Name: Kilvrough Manor Woods (Gower Limestone)
Description: Kilvrough Manor Woods is an ancient woodland SSSI on the Gower Peninsula, notable for its broadleaf trees and rich ground flora, including orchids. The limestone features create a unique habitat supporting diverse wildlife. It is actively managed for biodiversity.

Name: Kimberley Meadow
Description: Kimberley Meadow is a 2.5-hectare site in Nottinghamshire. It features unimproved acid grassland and ancient hedgerows. The reserve supports a diverse array of insects, including butterflies, and various plant species such as tormentil.

Name: Kinclaven Bluebell Wood
Description: Kinclaven Bluebell Wood, Perthshire, Scotland, is an ancient woodland known for its stunning bluebell displays each spring. It features native trees like oak and birch, supporting diverse wildlife. Managed by the Woodland Trust, the site offers walking routes.

Name: Kinder Scout (High Peak Estate)
Description: Kinder Scout is a moorland plateau and the highest point in the Peak District, managed by the National Trust. Famous for the 1932 Mass Trespass, it offers challenging walking trails across peat bogs and gritstone edges. The estate focuses on landscape conservation and rewilding efforts.

Name: Kinder Scout (National Nature Reserve - Peat Restoration)
Description: Kinder Scout, a high plateau in the Peak District, is a National Nature Reserve. The National Trust manages the site, undertaking extensive peat restoration to enhance biodiversity and carbon sequestration. The area is accessible for walking and nature observation.

Name: King Barrow Quarry
Description: King Barrow Quarry is a Site of Special Scientific Interest on Portland, managed by Dorset Wildlife Trust. This former quarry provides a crucial haven for wildlife including peregrine falcons, small blue butterflies, and Portland sea-lavender. Open limestone areas support unique plant habitats.

Name: King's Castle Wood (The Wells Gateway)
Description: King's Castle Wood is an ancient woodland near Wells, Somerset. It features mature oak, ash, and hazel, supporting diverse flora like bluebells and wild garlic. Formerly a medieval hunting park, the site has varied terrain, including steep slopes. Management enhances biodiversity.

Name: King's Meadow
Description: King's Meadow is a flood meadow and wet grassland on the River Trent near Radcliffe-on-Trent. It supports diverse wildflowers, including ragged robin and marsh marigold. The site offers a refuge for wading birds and amphibians.

Name: King's Meadow (Nottingham City)
Description: King's Meadow in Nottingham City is a lowland wet grassland and scrub habitat near the River Trent. Managed by Nottinghamshire Wildlife Trust, it supports various bird species, insects, and flora. The site features a footpath network for visitors. It forms part of a wider green corridor within the city.

Name: King's Meads
Description: King's Meads is a diverse nature reserve near Hertford, featuring open water, reedbeds, and wet grassland habitats. It supports a rich array of wildlife, including otters, kingfishers, and various wildfowl, offering trails for walking and birdwatching.

Name: King's Meads (Large Water Meadow)
Description: King's Meads is a large area of floodplain grassland in Hertfordshire, featuring ditches, ponds, and extensive river frontage. It supports diverse bird species, invertebrates, and a range of flora, managed by Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust for conservation.

Name: King's Wood (Corby - Ancient Woodland)
Description: King's Wood is a 119-hectare ancient woodland site near Corby, Northamptonshire. It features diverse habitats including mature oak, ash, and hazel coppice, supporting a range of wildlife. The wood is managed for conservation.

Name: King's Wood and Rammamere Heath
Description: King's Wood and Rammamere Heath in Bedfordshire is an ancient woodland and heathland complex. It features diverse habitats including oak, birch, hornbeam woodland, acid grassland, and wet heath. The reserve supports dormice, nightingales, and rare fungi. Conservation manages the site.

Name: Kings Wood (Mendips)
Description: Kings Wood, near Winscombe, is an ancient woodland, once part of the Mendip Royal Forest. It features a diverse range of tree species, including oak, ash, and hazel, supporting varied wildlife. Footpaths allow public access for walking and nature observation.

Name: Kingsbury Meadow
Description: Kingsbury Meadow is a wet grassland nature reserve in North Warwickshire. It features a diverse range of wildflowers, including various orchid species and wetland plants. The site supports a variety of invertebrates and bird life, managed for biodiversity by Warwickshire Wildlife Trust.

Name: Kingsthorpe Meadow
Description: Kingsthorpe Meadow is a small, species-rich floodplain grassland in Northamptonshire, designated as a SSSI. Bordering the River Nene, it features diverse flora, including green-winged orchids. Traditional hay cutting and grazing practices maintain its ecological value.

Name: Kingston Lacy Estate (Dorset Heathland/Parkland)
Description: Kingston Lacy is an elegant country mansion set in 8,500 acres of estate. The property features formal gardens, parkland, and a diverse heathland habitat. The estate is home to significant biodiversity, including rare plants and wildlife.

Name: Kinnelhead
Description: Kinnelhead is a 1300-hectare reserve in the Lowther Hills, Dumfries and Galloway. It encompasses upland blanket bog, wet heath, and acid grassland. The site supports hen harriers, merlin, and golden plover. Conservation efforts restore natural hydrological processes and enhance biodiversity.

Name: Kintail & Morvich (Wild Land)
Description: Kintail, on Scotland's west coast, encompasses dramatic mountains, lochs, and glens, including the Five Sisters of Kintail. This wild land offers challenging walking routes and diverse wildlife. Morvich provides a ranger centre and accessible trails, supporting conservation efforts.

Name: Kintbury Holt
Description: Kintbury Holt is a woodland nature reserve in Berkshire, featuring ancient oak trees and diverse wildlife. It is home to dormice, bats, and various bird species, alongside a rich understorey of plants. The reserve offers walking trails, allowing visitors to explore its habitats. It is managed for conservation.

Name: Kinver Edge & the Rock Houses
Description: Kinver Edge is an escarpment of Triassic sandstone. The Rock Houses are dwellings carved into the sandstone cliff, inhabited until the 1960s. The site offers views across Worcestershire and the Black Country. Woodland and heathland are present.

Name: Kiplingcotes Chalk Pit
Description: Kiplingcotes Chalk Pit is a nature reserve in the Yorkshire Wolds. This disused chalk quarry has flower-rich grassland on chalky soils, supporting diverse wildflowers and insects. Notable for orchids and butterflies, it is a key habitat managed for biodiversity.

Name: Kippax Meadows
Description: Kippax Meadows Nature Reserve is a former agricultural site in West Yorkshire. It features species-rich grassland, ponds, and hedgerows, supporting diverse wildlife. The site is actively managed for nature conservation by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust.

Name: Kirkby Moor
Description: Kirkby Moor is a 26-hectare Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve. Located near Kirkby on Bain, it preserves a remnant of fenland with dry acid grassland and wet woodland. The site supports diverse wildflowers and insects, including nationally rare species like the Lincolnshire feather-moss.

Name: Kirkstall Valley (Urban Leeds)
Description: Kirkstall Valley Nature Reserve is a former industrial site between the River Aire and the disused railway line in west Leeds. It provides valuable habitat for a range of wildlife, including various bird species and amphibians. The site features woodland, wetlands, and open grassland.

Name: Kitt's Grave
Description: Kitt's Grave Nature Reserve in Hampshire is a chalk grassland site with coppiced hazel, mature oak, and diverse wildflowers. It provides habitat for numerous insects and birds. A pond supports amphibians and aquatic invertebrates. The reserve offers peaceful exploration.

Name: Kitty\'s Orchard (Heritage Fruit Recovery)
Description: Kitty's Orchard in Monmouthshire preserves rare local apple varieties. Maintained for wildlife and heritage, it provides habitat and a genetic resource. It encourages fruit recovery and traditional orchard management for biodiversity.

Name: Kiveton Community Woodland (Former Pit Site)
Description: Kiveton Community Woodland, a former colliery, now provides varied woodland, ponds, meadows, and paths near Kiveton Park, Sheffield. TCV South Yorkshire manages the site, offering regular volunteering opportunities for habitat creation and woodland management.

Name: Knapdale (Nature30 Pilot - Rainforest)
Description: Knapdale Forest, in Argyll, Scotland, hosts a pioneering beaver reintroduction project. Visitors can explore ancient woodlands, temperate rainforest remnants, and lochside trails. The area is rich in biodiversity, offering numerous walking opportunities through diverse habitats.

Name: Knapdale Forest (Beaver Reintroduction)
Description: Knapdale Forest, a National Nature Reserve in Argyll, Scotland, hosts the Scottish Beaver Reintroduction Project. The site's diverse woodlands and lochs allow visitors to explore trails and observe beaver activity and environmental impact.

Name: Knapp Lane (The 2026 Landscape Bridge)
Description: The 2026 Landscape Bridge at Knapp Lane, west Cheltenham, provides a new underpass connecting the Golden Valley. It links habitats for wildlife and people, addresses the ecological emergency, and features a new wetland area by the River Chelt.

Name: Knettishall Heath
Description: Knettishall Heath offers 430 acres of lowland heath, ancient woodland, and riverside meadows in Suffolk. It supports rare species like nightjar and woodlark. Designated as a Site of Special Scientific Interest, the reserve provides varied habitats for wildlife.

Name: Knettishall Heath Nature Reserve
Description: Knettishall Heath is a 430-acre Suffolk Wildlife Trust nature reserve in the Little Ouse valley. It protects varied habitats including heathland, woodland, and meadows, supporting diverse wildlife. The site features walking trails.

Name: Knightlow Hill (30x30 Step Change Site)
Description: Knightlow Hill, located in Warwickshire, forms part of the Dunsmore Living Landscape. Warwickshire Wildlife Trust manages this site, focusing on habitat restoration, creation, and connection across the wider landscape, enhancing local biodiversity and ecological resilience.

Name: Knighton Down
Description: Knighton Down is a remote chalk downland site on the Hampshire Downs. The reserve offers extensive views towards Wiltshire and the Isle of Wight. It features diverse flora, including cowslip, devil's-bit scabious, and early purple orchid. Numerous butterfly species thrive here.

Name: Knightshayes (Woodland & Parkland)
Description: Knightshayes features a Victorian country house and extensive gardens with rare plants. Visitors can explore parkland, ancient woodland, and scenic walks. The estate offers views over the Exe Valley, conserving diverse habitats and historic landscapes in Devon.

Name: Knipe Moor (Limestone Plateau)
Description: Knipe Moor is a small limestone plateau rising from the Lyth Valley's agricultural land. Its short grassland boasts lime-loving plants, including orchids. Notable butterflies include dingy and grizzled skippers. Woodland areas feature ancient oak and hazel coppice. The site is managed for biodiversity.

Name: Knockshinnoch Lagoons
Description: Knockshinnoch Lagoons is a mosaic of habitats, including open water, reedbed, and wet grassland. Formed from former coal workings, it supports wetland birds, invertebrates, and amphibians. The site also features woodlands, serving as an important wildlife haven.

Name: Knotbury Common (South Pennine Moors Link)
Description: Knotbury Common is an upland site of acid grassland and blanket bog, part of the South Pennine Moors SSSI. It features wet and dry heath, purple moor-grass, and cotton-grass. The site supports diverse invertebrates, reptiles, and birds like curlew and snipe.

Name: Knotts Farm
Description: Knotts Farm is an Essex nature reserve with diverse habitats, including arable land, woodland, hedgerows, and grasslands. It connects with Priory Farm, forming a larger natural area. The site focuses on restoring natural processes and improving biodiversity for local wildlife.

Name: Knowle Hill
Description: Knowle Hill is a small woodland nature reserve near Rugby, Warwickshire. It features an ancient semi-natural woodland with mature ash and oak trees, a stream, and diverse ground flora. Wildlife includes woodpeckers, nuthatches, and various insects.

Name: Kymin (East of England boundary)
Description: Shaw's Corner, the former home of playwright George Bernard Shaw, preserves his personal possessions and original interiors in Hertfordshire. Visitors explore the house, study, and gardens, experiencing the literary legacy within this National Trust property.

Name: Kynance Cove (Serpentine Cliffs)
Description: Kynance Cove is a stunning cove on the Lizard Peninsula, famous for its turquoise waters, white sand, and unique serpentine rock formations. Managed by the National Trust, it features an iconic tidal island, Asparagus Island, and offers dramatic coastal walks.

Name: L-Moor Shepreth
Description: L-Moor Shepreth is a fenland reserve near Cambridge, managed by the Wildlife Trust BCN. It features reedbeds, wet grassland, and open water, providing habitat for marsh harriers and bearded tits. Restoration efforts focus on water level control and invasive species removal.

Name: Lackford Lakes
Description: Lackford Lakes is a Suffolk wildlife oasis in the Lark Valley. This network of islands, lakes, and reedbeds provides habitat for diverse bird species, including kingfishers, and otters. Visitors enjoy hides and accessible paths. Suffolk Wildlife Trust manages the reserve.

Name: Lacock Abbey & Riverside Woods
Description: Lacock Abbey, a former nunnery, features medieval architecture and a Georgian country house. It is the home of photography pioneer William Henry Fox Talbot, with a museum and medieval cloister. Riverside woods and grounds surround the historic Abbey.

Name: Lady Lee Quarry
Description: Lady Lee Quarry is a former limestone quarry north of Newark, managed by Nottinghamshire Wildlife Trust. It features unique limestone grassland habitats and a small pond, supporting diverse plant and insect life.

Name: Lady’s Belt
Description: Lady’s Belt is a small, quiet nature reserve in south-west Norfolk, important for its woodland, especially old oak pollards and veteran trees. It supports a variety of birds and invertebrates. Access is via a narrow track and parking is limited.

Name: Lady's Mead
Description: Lady's Mead is a small, species-rich meadow adjacent to the River Stour, near Blandford Forum. It features various wildflowers and grasses, supporting diverse insect and bird life. The site is managed by Dorset Wildlife Trust.

Name: Lady's Wood (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Lady's Wood is an ancient woodland in Cambridgeshire, managed by Wildlife Trust BCN. It features mature oak, ash, and field maple trees. A diverse ground flora includes bluebells and primroses, providing habitat for various bird species.

Name: Lady's Wood (Oldest Reserve & Dormice)
Description: Lady's Wood is Devon Wildlife Trust's oldest nature reserve, established in 1962 near Newton Poppleford. This woodland and traditional orchard protects dormice and various other woodland species. A public footpath provides access.

Name: Ladywalk Nature Reserve (Tame Valley)
Description: Ladywalk Nature Reserve is a wildlife haven situated in the Tame Valley, near Birmingham, and managed by Warwickshire Wildlife Trust. It encompasses diverse habitats including wetlands, pools, and reedbeds, supporting a wide array of bird species. The reserve offers walking routes, allowing visitors to observe wildlife year-round.

Name: Ladywalk Reserve (Tame Valley)
Description: Ladywalk Reserve, a 70-hectare nature reserve in Warwickshire's Tame Valley, is managed by the West Midland Bird Club. It features diverse habitats like pools, marshland, and reedbeds, supporting a variety of bird species. Hides offer viewing access.

Name: Ladywell Fields (River Ravensbourne)
Description: Ladywell Fields features over 1000m of river restoration work on the Ravensbourne, including naturalisation and new backwater channels. This increases biodiversity by creating habitats for fish, invertebrates, and plants. Volunteers maintain the river and its banks.

Name: Lake View Park (River Sherbourne Restoration)
Description: Lake View Park, Coventry, is a site for River Sherbourne restoration. Previously culverted river sections are transformed into natural habitats, improving water quality and biodiversity. Efforts include re-meandering and tree planting, benefiting local wildlife and residents.

Name: Lamb's Pool
Description: Lamb's Pool is a small, shallow, clay-lined pool and damp meadow in Oxfordshire. It supports diverse wildlife, including dragonflies and marsh plants. It is home to nationally rare flora like small fleabane. The site showcases success in pond restoration.

Name: Lambert's Castle & Coney's Castle
Description: Lambert's Castle and Coney's Castle are Iron Age hill forts near Marshwood Vale in Dorset. Lambert's offers panoramic views of Lyme Bay. Coney's is a Scheduled Monument. Managed by the National Trust, the site provides walking and nature enjoyment.

Name: Lancaut (Wye Gorge & Peregrine Crag)
Description: Lancaut is a nature reserve in the Lower Wye Gorge, managed by Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust. Ancient woodland, limestone cliffs, and grassland support diverse wildlife, including peregrine falcons. The site offers views of the Wye River and contains remnants of a medieval church.

Name: Lancot Meadow
Description: Lancot Meadow is a unimproved grassland site, rich in wildflowers. Managed for biodiversity, it supports various insects and is a valuable habitat, showcasing nature conservation efforts. Part of the Wildlife Trust BCN.

Name: Landford Bog (New Forest Heath)
Description: Landford Bog is a valley mire on the northern edge of the New Forest, a Site of Special Scientific Interest and Special Area of Conservation. It features wet acid heath, valley mire, ancient woodland, supporting rare plants and insects.

Name: Landpark Wood
Description: Landpark Wood is an ancient woodland in Cambridgeshire, once part of the Royal Forest of Huntingdonshire. It features mature oak, ash, maple, and hazel coppice. The wood supports diverse wildlife, with bluebells and primroses in spring.

Name: Lang Craigs (Kilpatrick Hills)
Description: Lang Craigs is a Woodland Trust site within the Kilpatrick Hills, featuring ancient oakwoods, grassland, and newly planted areas. It offers a network of paths with views over the Clyde and Dumbarton Rock, providing accessible walks for wildlife enthusiasts.

Name: Langdale Forest (North York Moors)
Description: Langdale Forest, near Pickering, is a working forest on the edge of the North York Moors National Park. It offers marked walking and cycling routes suitable for various abilities. The forest actively produces timber, contributing to local industry and woodland management.

Name: Langdon Nature Discovery Park
Description: Langdon Nature Discovery Park features ancient woodlands, wildflower meadows, and lakes, offering expansive views across the Thames Estuary. The park provides various trails for birdwatching and wildlife exploration, serving as a vital conservation site in Essex.

Name: Langford Lakes (Wetland Flagship)
Description: Langford Lakes is a wetland nature reserve on the River Wylye in Wiltshire. A former gravel works, it features lakes, ponds, meadows, and woodland. It provides crucial habitat for diverse birds, including wildfowl and waders, with accessible paths and hides.

Name: Langholme Wood
Description: Langholme Wood is an ancient woodland reserve near Market Rasen. It is managed to maintain a diverse understorey, supporting wood anemone, bluebell, and yellow archangel. Mature trees include oak, ash, and hazel, providing habitat for woodland birds and insects.

Name: Lanhydrock Parkland & Woods
Description: Lanhydrock is a grand Victorian country house, gardens, and wooded estate in Cornwall. The gardens feature colourful parterres and borders. Extensive parkland offers walking and cycling trails through ancient woodlands and the River Fowey valley.

Name: Lanhydrock Woods (National Trust partnership)
Description: Lanhydrock Woods, a Woodland Trust site, is situated in the Glynn Valley near Bodmin, Cornwall. The woods feature diverse native trees like oak, ash, and hazel, offering varied habitats for wildlife. Visitors can explore walking trails along the River Fowey.

Name: Larches
Description: Larches is a small woodland nature reserve near Tunbridge Wells, Kent, managed by Kent Wildlife Trust. The site features a mix of broadleaf and conifer trees, including ancient oaks, and diverse ground flora like bluebells. A stream flows through the reserve, supporting local wildlife.

Name: Lardon Chase
Description: Lardon Chase is a chalk grassland nature reserve near Streatley, Berkshire, offering expansive views over the Thames Valley. The site supports a rich diversity of wildflowers, including orchids, and numerous insect species like butterflies and moths.

Name: Lashford Lane Fen
Description: Lashford Lane Fen is a diverse nature reserve on peaty soils over Gault Clay. It features a rich array of wildflowers, sedges, mosses, and supports diverse insect life, including dragonflies and marsh orchids. Managed for biodiversity.

Name: Lassington Wood
Description: Lassington Wood is a young deciduous woodland near Highnam, Gloucestershire, established on former farmland in 1999. It features broadleaved species, a wildflower meadow, and a small pond, supporting diverse wildlife. Public access is via a network of paths.

Name: Latterbarrow
Description: Latterbarrow is an ancient semi-natural woodland nature reserve in the Lake District National Park. It features lime kilns and supports great spotted woodpeckers, nuthatches, badgers, and deer. Spring brings bluebells, wood anemones, and wild garlic to the site.

Name: Latterheards (Mixed Woodland)
Description: Latterheards is a mixed woodland of oak, ash and birch with a hazel understorey. The reserve is managed for its flora and fauna, including bluebells, dog's mercury, badgers, roe deer and a variety of bird species. Located near Kirkoswald, Cumbria, it offers a tranquil habitat for wildlife.

Name: Lattersey (Whittlesey)
Description: Lattersey is an internationally important fenland reserve, supporting diverse invertebrate and bird species. Once worked for peat, the site now features reedbeds, fen, and wet grassland habitats. It is managed to conserve its unique biodiversity.

Name: Lattersey Field
Description: Lattersey Field is a 1.25 hectare site near Whittlesey, managed by Wildlife BCN. It is a mosaic of grassland, scrub, and ditches, providing habitat for various species including harvest mice, reptiles, and birds. It serves as a valuable ecological area.

Name: Launde Big Wood (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Launde Big Wood is an ancient woodland, home to diverse tree species including oak, ash, and field maple. The reserve features open glades and a mosaic of habitats supporting various wildflowers and invertebrates. It is managed for biodiversity.

Name: Launde Woods
Description: Launde Woods Nature Reserve is a significant ancient woodland in Leicestershire and Rutland. Comprising Launde Park Wood and Launde Big Wood, it features bluebells, diverse bird species, and butterflies. Part of a SSSI, the site includes woodland, grassland, and Launde Brook, actively managed for biodiversity.

Name: Laurie Lee Wood
Description: Laurie Lee Wood is a nature reserve in the Slad Valley, near Stroud, Gloucestershire. It features mixed woodland, grassland, and an orchard, supporting diverse wildlife like dormice and various birds. Public footpaths offer valley views.

Name: Lavender Pond Nature Park
Description: Lavender Pond Nature Park, part of Russia Dock Woodlands in Rotherhithe, Southwark, is a peaceful oasis, formerly a working dock. It features woodland, meadows, and ponds, providing vital habitats for diverse wildlife. Conservation tasks and wildlife surveys occur regularly.

Name: Lavenham (Suffolk Woodlands)
Description: Lavenham Woodland, near Lavenham village, provides walking, cycling, and horse riding trails. Managed by Forestry England, it forms part of the Suffolk Coast and Heaths AONB, offering varied routes and diverse wildlife habitats for visitors to explore.

Name: Lavenham Guildhall (Countryside Walks)
Description: Lavenham Guildhall, a 1530 timber-framed building, showcases the village's rich history, including its wool trade prominence, its uses as a prison and workhouse, and local heritage exhibitions. The site also provides access to various countryside walks in and around Lavenham.

Name: Lavernock Point (Migratory Waypoint)
Description: Lavernock Point is a coastal nature reserve in Wales, known as a significant migratory waypoint for birds crossing the Bristol Channel. The site features dramatic cliffs, rocky shores, and areas of woodland. It is also a popular location for fossil hunting. The reserve is managed for biodiversity.

Name: Lawn Wood
Description: Lawn Wood is an ancient woodland in Lincolnshire, managed by Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust. It features mature ash and oak trees, a rich ground flora including bluebells, primroses, and dog's mercury. The site supports diverse bird species, and a stream flows through, enhancing local biodiversity.

Name: Lawrence Weston Moor
Description: Lawrence Weston Moor is a significant wetland in north-west Bristol, featuring fen and wet grassland. It provides habitat for diverse plant life, numerous insects including the shrill carder bee, and supports water voles and various bird species.

Name: Lawthorn Wood
Description: Lawthorn Wood is an ancient woodland, covering 3.8 hectares north-east of Irvine. It features broadleaf trees such as oak, ash, birch, and hazel. The site supports various bird species and wildflowers.

Name: Lea & Paget's Wood (Bluebell Stronghold)
Description: Lea & Paget's Wood is an ancient woodland in Herefordshire, celebrated for its spring bluebells. It features mature oak, ash, and hazel coppice, supporting diverse woodland life. A stream flows through the site, managed by Herefordshire Wildlife Trust.

Name: Lea Meadows
Description: Lea Meadows is a species-rich hay meadow on the River Soar floodplain, south of Leicester, Leicestershire. Grazed by cattle, it hosts diverse wetland flora, including sedges, wild angelica, and great burnet. It also supports significant insect populations.

Name: Lea Wood
Description: Lea Wood is an ancient woodland of oak, ash, and birch, set on a steep hillside overlooking the River Derwent in Derbyshire. Part of a World Heritage Site, it features diverse habitats including heathland, ponds, and marsh, supporting various wildlife.

Name: Leam Valley (Urban Nature Reserve)
Description: Leam Valley Local Nature Reserve protects a mosaic of urban habitats. Reedbed, grassland, and woodland support diverse wildlife within Leamington Spa, including water voles, kingfishers, and rare insects.

Name: Leat Stream
Description: Leat Stream Nature Reserve is a small woodland with a chalk stream habitat. It is located near Storrington, West Sussex. The reserve supports a variety of wildlife, including brown trout and water voles. Managed to enhance biodiversity.

Name: Ledsham Bank
Description: Ledsham Bank is a wetland nature reserve near Fairburn Ings, managed by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust. It features reedbeds, ponds, and wet grassland, attracting diverse bird species, invertebrates, and water voles.

Name: Ledston Luck
Description: Ledston Luck Nature Reserve is located on a former colliery site in West Yorkshire. It features grassland, scrub, and woodland habitats, supporting a variety of wildlife, including various bird species and invertebrates. The site is actively managed for nature conservation.

Name: Lee Navigation Reedbeds
Description: Lee Navigation Reedbeds, near Tottenham Hale, are artificial floating reedbeds created to improve water quality and increase biodiversity. They provide crucial habitats for wildlife in an urban waterway setting. This project enhances ecological resilience.

Name: Legbourne Wood
Description: Legbourne Wood is a mixed woodland nature reserve south of Louth. It features ancient semi-natural woodland, conifer plantation, and species-rich grassland. Key features include bluebells in spring, veteran trees, and a pond. It supports diverse bird species and invertebrates.

Name: Leigh Woods (Forestry England Section)
Description: Leigh Woods is a National Nature Reserve near Bristol, managed by Forestry England. It features ancient woodland, walking and cycling trails, and views of the Avon Gorge. The site is home to diverse wildlife and rare species, designated for its ecological significance.

Name: Leith Hill
Description: Leith Hill is Surrey's highest point, home to Leith Hill Tower, offering panoramic views across the South East. Managed by the National Trust, visitors explore ancient woodlands and footpaths surrounding this popular beauty spot, located within the Surrey Hills AONB.

Name: Leith Hill (Highest Point in South East England)
Description: Leith Hill is the highest point in South East England, offering panoramic views across the Surrey Hills and South Downs. Visitors explore woodlands, walk trails, and climb Leith Hill Tower. The National Trust manages this prominent natural landscape.

Name: Lemsford Springs (Chalk Stream Lagoons)
Description: Lemsford Springs is a unique chalk stream wetland, home to diverse wildlife, including breeding waders like snipe and redshank. Spring-fed lagoons provide a crucial overwintering habitat for birds, supporting significant populations of wigeon and teal.

Name: Letchmire Pastures
Description: Letchmire Pastures, near Allerton Bywater, West Yorkshire, is a nature reserve reclaiming land from a former opencast mine and landfill. It now boasts diverse habitats like wet woodland, wetland, and grassland, supporting dragonflies, damselflies, birds, and varied plant life.

Name: Letcombe Valley
Description: Letcombe Valley is a chalk stream valley with ancient woodlands, wildflower meadows, and chalk grassland. Letcombe Brook flows through the reserve, supporting water voles and kingfishers. This diverse habitat is home to various invertebrates and other wildlife in Oxfordshire.

Name: Letham Moss
Description: Letham Moss is a lowland raised bog in Fife, managed to restore its peat-forming habitat. Visitors can observe diverse plant and insect life, including dragonflies and mosses. A boardwalk provides access across the bog. It supports a unique ecosystem threatened by historical drainage.

Name: Letwell
Description: Letwell Nature Reserve is a small wetland site in South Yorkshire, featuring a main pond, marshy grassland, and wet woodland. It supports diverse wildlife, including various bird species, amphibians, and dragonflies, providing important habitats.

Name: Levant Mine & the Tin Coast
Description: Levant Mine, a Cornish beam engine and a site of a Victorian mining disaster, is part of the Cornish Mining World Heritage Site. Located on the Tin Coast, it offers underground tours and insights into Cornwall's rich mining heritage.

Name: Levin Down (South Downs Chalk & Heath)
Description: Levin Down is a 42-acre chalk downland nature reserve within the South Downs National Park. It supports rare chalk grassland flora like round-headed rampion and attracts butterflies such as adonis blue. Grazing by cattle and ponies maintains its diverse habitat. Raptors are present.

Name: Levington Lagoon (Orwell Estuary)
Description: Levington Lagoon is a brackish lagoon on the River Orwell, near Levington in Suffolk. This important site provides feeding and roosting grounds for migratory and overwintering birds, including avocets, ringed plovers, and dunlins, across its saltmarsh, mudflats, and reedbeds.

Name: Lexden Gathering Grounds
Description: Lexden Gathering Grounds is a former sewage works in Colchester, Essex. Now a nature reserve, it features diverse wetlands, grasslands, and scrub. The site supports various bird species, dragonflies, and damselflies, with accessible paths for visitors.

Name: Leyburn Old Glebe
Description: Leyburn Old Glebe is a mosaic of meadows and woodland in North Yorkshire, bordered by a drystone wall. It supports wildflowers like oxeye daisy and common spotted orchid, along with diverse bird species and insects. Located near Leyburn.

Name: Leythorne Meadow
Description: Leythorne Meadow is a tranquil green space near Chichester, West Sussex, managed by Sussex Wildlife Trust. It connects to other local wildlife sites, providing a haven for diverse species. The meadow features a mosaic of habitats, including grassland and scrub, supporting wildflowers and insects.

Name: Leyton Jubilee Park
Description: Leyton Jubilee Park hosts the Waltham Forest Green Gym, offering weekly conservation tasks. Activities include tree and shrub maintenance, gardening, habitat management, and litter picking, suitable for all abilities.

Name: Lightwater Country Park (Heathland sections)
Description: Lightwater Country Park, owned by Surrey Heath Borough Council, encompasses heathland, woodland, and grassland. Its heathland sections, part of the Thames Basin Heaths Special Protection Area, protect ground-nesting birds. The site is open to the public.

Name: Ligoniel Dams & Park (Urban Oasis)
Description: Ligoniel Dams and Park is an urban oasis in north Belfast, managed by the Belfast Hills Partnership. Two man-made dams, fed by the Ligoneil River, support waterbirds and amphibians. Walking paths explore former printworks and mill race remains.

Name: Lince Wood
Description: Lince Wood is a small, ancient woodland reserve in the Stour valley. It features mature oak, ash, and hornbeam trees, with a rich ground flora including bluebells and wild garlic in spring. The woodland supports various bird species and invertebrates.

Name: Lincomb Fish Pass (River Severn)
Description: Lincomb Fish Pass, near Stourport-on-Severn on the River Severn, aids migrating fish like salmon and eels in bypassing Lincomb Weir. Part of the Unlocking the Severn project, it uses a Larinier super active baffle design for easier upstream passage.

Name: Lindean Loch (Swan Sanctuary)
Description: Lindean Loch is a shallow loch in a natural hollow, surrounded by sedge and reed swamp, with open water. It supports diverse bird life, including wildfowl and waders, acting as an important breeding ground. The site is a Site of Special Scientific Interest, with limited visitor access.

Name: Lindisfarne (Holy Island) Castle & Coast
Description: Lindisfarne Castle, a 16th-century fortress, stands on Holy Island, a tidal island off the Northumberland coast. Architect Sir Edwin Lutyens transformed it into a holiday home in the early 20th century. The island also features a National Nature Reserve and historic priory.

Name: Lineover Wood (Cotswold Escarpment)
Description: Lineover Wood is an ancient woodland on the Cotswold scarp, featuring mature beech and ash trees. It is home to diverse wildlife, including various bird species and dormice. The site offers walking trails and scenic views over the surrounding countryside.

Name: Lings Wood (Northampton Heath/Woodland)
Description: Lings Wood is a mixed woodland and heathland nature reserve in Northampton, featuring mature oak, ash, maple, and hazel alongside open grassland. The site supports diverse wildlife, including various bird species and invertebrates, providing a valuable urban green space for local communities.

Name: Linhope Spout (Temperate Rainforest Pilot)
Description: Linhope Spout is a waterfall located within Northumberland National Park, North East England. It currently hosts a temperate rainforest pilot project. This initiative focuses on restoring and expanding vital rainforest habitats around the spout and the adjacent Bizzle Burn.

Name: Linhouse Glen
Description: Linhouse Glen is a small woodland reserve in West Lothian, managed by the Scottish Wildlife Trust. The Linhouse Water flows through a sandstone gorge, creating waterfalls and pools. Ancient semi-natural woodland supports diverse fungi and mosses. A circular path offers walks.

Name: Linn of Tummel
Description: The Linn of Tummel is a stunning natural waterfall and gorge located where the rivers Tummel and Garry meet in Perthshire. Ancient woodlands and rich wildlife surround tranquil pools and cascades. Historic bridges cross the river, offering scenic viewpoints.

Name: Linton Lane
Description: Linton Lane Meadows is a Northumberland nature reserve comprising varied grasslands, wet pastures, and developing woodland. The River Coquet forms its eastern boundary, supporting diverse birds, insects, amphibians, and small mammals.

Name: Linwood
Description: Linwood Nature Reserve, nestled within the New Forest, features diverse habitats including ancient woodlands, heathland, and mires. It supports various bird species alongside rare plants and insects. Managed by Hampshire & Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust, the reserve offers tranquil walking trails, providing crucial refuge for ponies and wildlife.

Name: Linwood Warren
Description: Linwood Warren is an ancient heathland with areas of acid grassland, heather, and young birch and pine woodland. It supports rare species like nightjar and woodlark, with adders also present. The site is a Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve.

Name: Little Baddow Heath
Description: Little Baddow Heath is an ancient heathland and woodland nature reserve in Essex. It features acid grassland, heather, and mature trees, supporting diverse wildlife including butterflies and woodland birds. Public footpaths allow access to explore the various habitats.

Name: Little Dartmouth & Coast
Description: Little Dartmouth offers dramatic clifftop walks with views of Dartmouth Castle, Start Bay, and the coastline. It features diverse habitats, including woodland and coastal paths, supporting varied wildlife. This section of the South West Coast Path is popular for walkers.

Name: Little Haven (Daws Heath Corridor)
Description: Little Haven is part of the Daws Heath Corridor in south east Essex, a network of woodlands and heathland. Designated as a SSSI, it supports diverse habitats including acid grassland, heathland, and ancient woodland. Rare species like dormice and nightingales thrive here.

Name: Little Marises Meadow
Description: Little Marises Meadow is a small, south-facing traditional hay meadow near Bromyard, Herefordshire. Its unimproved neutral grassland features orchids and knapweed, supporting diverse insect life. Cattle graze the meadow in autumn.

Name: Little Waltham Meadows
Description: Little Waltham Meadows is a wet grassland nature reserve in Essex, managed by Essex Wildlife Trust. It supports diverse flora, including water forget-me-not and ragged robin, and is home to various bird species. The site features the River Chelmer and offers a circular walk.

Name: Little Whinny
Description: Little Whinny is a 1.25-hectare lowland meadow and ancient woodland site in Middlesbrough. It connects urban green spaces as part of the Middlesbrough Green Network. The reserve supports wildflowers, grassland, and mature trees, providing habitat for insects and birds.

Name: Little Woolden Moss (Carbon Sequestration Hub)
Description: Little Woolden Moss is a regenerating lowland raised peat bog in Greater Manchester. Once drained for commercial peat extraction, the Lancashire Wildlife Trust is restoring the site. This improves biodiversity and sequesters carbon, supporting sphagnum mosses and specialist bog plants.

Name: Little Woolden Moss (Yorkshire Link)
Description: Little Woolden Moss is a lowland raised bog in Greater Manchester under active restoration. It provides habitat for white-faced darter dragonflies, curlews, and sundews. Re-wetting efforts restore the peatland ecosystem and its vital biodiversity.

Name: Littlebeck Wood
Description: Littlebeck Wood is an ancient woodland nature reserve near Whitby, North Yorkshire. It features a steep valley, waterfalls, and diverse wildlife, including pied flycatcher and wood warbler. The rich ground flora includes bluebell and wild garlic, especially vibrant in spring. Managed by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust.

Name: Littleworth Park (Old Colliery)
Description: Littleworth Park, near Barnsley, South Yorkshire, is a nature reserve on a former colliery site. It features grasslands, woodlands, and wetlands, supporting diverse wildlife like birds, amphibians, and invertebrates. Accessible paths allow visitors to explore the site.

Name: Littley Garth
Description: Littley Garth is a small nature reserve in Kent, featuring ancient woodland, meadows, and a pond. It supports a variety of wildlife, including amphibians, reptiles, and diverse plant species. Public footpaths allow access to observe its natural habitats.

Name: Lizard Point & Bass Point
Description: Lizard Point, the UK's most southerly mainland point, and Bass Point are dramatic headlands on Cornwall's coast. The area features rugged cliffs, rare flora, and diverse marine wildlife, including choughs. Visitors explore coves and walk scenic coastal paths.

Name: Llanbwchllyn (Mesotrophic Lake Hub)
Description: Llanbwchllyn is a rare mesotrophic lake in Wales, with an extensive range of aquatic plants, including nationally rare species. The surrounding habitat includes wet woodland, rush pasture, and damp grassland, supporting diverse birds, otters, and invertebrates.

Name: Llandinam Gravels (The Shifting River)
Description: Llandinam Gravels is a dynamic riverine nature reserve on the River Severn near Llandinam. It features constantly shifting gravel beds and sandbanks, creating unique habitats for specialised wildlife. The site supports significant bird species and diverse invertebrates adapted to its ever-changing river environment.

Name: Llandrindod Wells Lake (Urban Wetland)
Description: Llandrindod Wells Lake Local Nature Reserve features a large lake and diverse surrounding habitats. Managed by Radnorshire Wildlife Trust and Powys County Council, this urban wetland supports many bird species throughout the year.

Name: Llanerchaeron (The Self-Sufficient Estate)
Description: Llanerchaeron is a Georgian villa, farm, and walled garden near Aberaeron, Ceredigion, West Wales. This largely unaltered 18th-century estate features traditional Welsh livestock and showcases historic self-sufficiency. It offers a glimpse into rural life.

Name: Llanrhidian Saltmarsh (Gower Blue Carbon)
Description: Llanrhidian Saltmarsh, located on the Gower Peninsula, is a significant Welsh saltmarsh habitat. The Welsh Wildlife Trust manages and protects this area, focusing on its restoration for crucial carbon sequestration and to support local biodiversity.

Name: Llanymynech Rocks (Limestone Cliffs)
Description: Llanymynech Rocks is a former limestone quarry and nature reserve. Its dramatic cliffs and flower-rich grasslands support rare plants and diverse insects, including butterflies. The site offers panoramic views across the Shropshire Plain and into Wales.

Name: Llanymynech Rocks (The Limestone Giant)
Description: Llanymynech Rocks, a former limestone quarry, is a Montgomeryshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve. It features diverse habitats including grassland, woodland, and cliffs, supporting rare plants and insects. Walking trails are accessible.

Name: Lloyd Park (Waltham Forest Green Gym)
Description: Waltham Forest Green Gym meets weekly in Lloyd Park, London. Volunteers improve local green spaces through practical conservation tasks. Activities include planting, path maintenance, and habitat creation, providing free outdoor exercise for health.

Name: Llyn Brenig (Osprey & Heather Moor)
Description: Gors Maen Llwyd Nature Reserve is a lowland raised bog in Denbighshire, supporting cotton grass, sphagnum moss, sundews, and bog rosemary. It provides habitat for the large heath butterfly, near Llyn Brenig.

Name: Llyn Coed y Dinas (Former Quarry Restoration)
Description: Llyn Coed y Dinas is a 40-acre nature reserve near Welshpool, on the River Severn floodplain. This former quarry now features a diverse mosaic of pools, scrapes, reedbeds, and wildflower meadows, supporting a wide range of birds, invertebrates, and flora.

Name: Llyn Coed-y-Dinas (The Industrial Oasis)
Description: Llyn Coed-y-Dinas is a wetland reserve near Welshpool, at the confluence of the Rivers Severn and Vyrnwy. Formerly a quarry, it now supports diverse wetland birds. The site features woodlands, wetlands, an orchard, a visitor centre, and a bird hide. Accessible paths are available.

Name: Llyn Fach (UK's Southernmost Montane Lake)
Description: Llyn Fach is a stunning glacial lake on Mynydd Du, within Brecon Beacons National Park. It is the southernmost montane lake in the UK, surrounded by moorland and home to unique aquatic flora and fauna. The site supports important bird species.

Name: Llyn Tegid (Bala Lake Node)
Description: Llyn Tegid (Bala Lake) is Wales' largest natural lake, found within Snowdonia National Park, Gwynedd. This glacial lake is a Special Area of Conservation, known for its clear waters and rare gwyniad fish, supporting diverse habitats.

Name: Llyn Tegid NNR (Bala Lake)
Description: Llyn Tegid (Bala Lake) is Wales’ largest natural lake. A National Nature Reserve (NNR) protecting rare wildlife like the gwyniad fish, it is popular for water sports, walking, and its scenic railway.

Name: Llynclys Common
Description: Llynclys Common is a Shropshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve. It comprises limestone grassland, scrub, and woodland, offering panoramic views across the Shropshire plain. A Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI), it supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare orchids.

Name: Loch Ardinning
Description: Loch Ardinning is a wetland reserve located near Strathblane, north of Glasgow. It features open water, fen, and woodland habitats, supporting diverse wildlife including otters, roe deer, and various bird species. Public access is available via a network of footpaths.

Name: Loch Arkaig Pine Forest
Description: Loch Arkaig Pine Forest is a surviving fragment of ancient Caledonian Forest in the Scottish Highlands. The site features ancient Scots pine, hosting diverse wildlife including red squirrels, pine martens, and ospreys. Woodland Trust and Arkaig Community Forest manage the area for conservation.

Name: Loch Fleet
Description: Loch Fleet is a large, shallow, tidal basin and National Nature Reserve in Sutherland, Scotland. The site features saltmarsh, dunes, coastal heath, and pine woods. It provides habitat for wading birds, wildfowl, otters, seals, and ospreys.

Name: Loch Garten (Nature Centre)
Description: Loch Garten Nature Centre is within RSPB Abernethy, a significant Caledonian pinewood in the Cairngorms National Park. It is renowned for returning ospreys, red squirrels, capercaillie, and crested tits, offering visitor viewing facilities.

Name: Loch Gruinart (Islay)
Description: Loch Gruinart is an RSPB nature reserve on Islay, known for its diverse birdlife including wintering barnacle geese. The reserve features wetlands, woodlands, and farmland habitats, offering hides for wildlife viewing and marked walking trails.

Name: Loch Leven NNR (Migratory Hub)
Description: Loch Leven NNR, a freshwater loch in Perth and Kinross, Scotland, is a vital wintering ground and breeding site for wildfowl and birds. It serves as a crucial migratory stopover for many bird populations. NatureScot manages this important wetland.

Name: Loch Lomond (Nature Hub)
Description: RSPB Loch Lomond reserve protects vital habitats on Scotland's largest freshwater loch. It supports diverse wildlife, including geese, ducks, and waders. Visitors can explore trails, hides, and learn about conservation at the Nature Hub.

Name: Loch of Kinnordy
Description: Loch of Kinnordy is a shallow freshwater loch and fen wetland in Strathmore, Scotland. It supports breeding and wintering wildfowl and waders like osprey, pintail, and whooper swans. The fen and reedbeds provide habitat for rare plants and insects.

Name: Loch of Lintrathen
Description: Loch of Lintrathen is a large freshwater loch in Angus, Scotland, surrounded by farmland and conifer plantations. It supports a significant wildfowl community, especially in winter. The loch is designated as a SSSI and a Special Protection Area.

Name: Loch of Spiggie (Shetland)
Description: Loch of Spiggie is a large freshwater loch in south-west Mainland Shetland. It is important for wintering wildfowl, including wigeon, teal, mallard, pintail, tufted duck, pochard, and whooper swans. Surrounding habitats feature reedbeds and fen areas. The site is notable for great northern divers and Slavonian grebes, managed by RSPB.

Name: Loch of Strathbeg
Description: Loch of Strathbeg is Scotland's largest coastal wetland, situated in Aberdeenshire. It serves as a crucial wintering ground for thousands of migrating wildfowl, including geese, swans, and ducks. The reserve features diverse habitats like reeds, dunes, and farmland.

Name: Loch of the Lowes
Description: Loch of the Lowes is a Site of Special Scientific Interest, managed by the Scottish Wildlife Trust. The reserve supports otters, beavers, and osprey, with a visitor centre and hides available. A ranger service operates on site.

Name: Loch of Wineide
Description: Loch of Wineide is an important lowland loch site on the island of Yell, Shetland. A rich mosaic of habitats includes both acidic and alkaline fen, grassland, and open water. It supports nationally important breeding bird populations and rare aquatic plants.

Name: Loch Ruthven (Slavonian Grebes)
Description: Loch Ruthven is a remote, tranquil upland freshwater loch in the Scottish Highlands. It hosts over a quarter of the UK's Slavonian grebe population, which breed here each spring before moving to sea for winter. The site is an important habitat for these birds.

Name: Lochwinnoch
Description: RSPB Lochwinnoch is a nature reserve in West Scotland, situated on the shores of Castle Semple Loch. It features open water, reedbed, and wet woodland habitats, supporting diverse wildlife. Visitors can use nature trails, bird hides, and a visitor centre.

Name: Lockerbrook (High Peak Plateau)
Description: Lockerbrook Outdoor Centre, located in the High Peak area of the Dark Peak, provides an outdoor learning facility for young people. Situated on a remote hillside, it offers educational experiences and accommodations, fostering personal development through engagement with the natural environment within the Peak District National Park.

Name: Lodge Park & Sherborne Park Estate
Description: Lodge Park, a 17th-century deer course grandstand, stands within Sherborne Park Estate, a significant conservation landscape. This estate features historic parkland and diverse wildlife habitats. Visitors explore the grandstand and enjoy walks through the wider estate, observing nature and architectural heritage.

Name: Lolly Moor
Description: Lolly Moor is a mosaic of habitats in the Norfolk Broads, including acid grassland, fen, and wet woodland. The reserve supports rare plants and insects, managed for biodiversity, and is a haven for wildlife. It protects diverse ecosystems.

Name: Long Craig Island
Description: Long Craig Island, an islet in the Firth of Forth, is a Scottish Wildlife Trust reserve. It provides a vital breeding ground for various seabirds, including cormorants, shags, and kittiwakes. The island is accessible by boat, offering significant ecological interest.

Name: Long Deans (Hemel Hempstead)
Description: Long Deans is a nature reserve near Hemel Hempstead, managed by Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust. It comprises chalk grassland slopes, ancient woodland, and former arable land being restored to wildflower meadows.
