The High Fell Project aims to restore natural processes and improve biodiversity across the 1,500-hectare site, characterised by peatlands, blanket bogs, and upland grassland habitats.

Name: Skiddaw Forest Nature Recovery (Landscape Scale)
Description: The Skiddaw Forest project restores natural processes and biodiversity across 12 sq km in the Lake District National Park. It focuses on peatland and native woodland restoration, improving habitat for key species, reducing flood risk, and capturing carbon.

Name: Skinflats Marsh
Description: Skinflats Marsh is a coastal wetland, part of the Inner Forth SSSI, providing vital habitats for wintering and breeding wildfowl and wading birds. Features saltmarsh and reedbed areas, supporting otters and the rare sea aster mining bee.

Name: Skip Garden (King's Cross)
Description: The Skip Garden in King's Cross is a community food growing garden, kitchen, and educational space. It uses repurposed skips as raised beds to cultivate vegetables, herbs, and fruits. The garden hosts workshops on food growing, composting, and ecology, powered by renewable energy.

Name: Skippers Island
Description: Skippers Island is a 55-hectare remote island nature reserve, situated in Hamford Water near Walton-on-the-Naze. It is an important haven for birds and other wildlife, including large numbers of brent geese and grey seals. The site features saltmarsh, shingle, and mudflats.

Name: Skippers Island (Restricted Access/Boat Only)
Description: Skippers Island is a remote, boat-access-only nature reserve in the Walton Backwaters, managed by Essex Wildlife Trust. This SSSI features diverse habitats including saltmarsh, shingle, and grasslands, supporting important populations of breeding terns and wintering waders.

Name: Skipwith Common NNR (Lowland Heath)
Description: Skipwith Common NNR is a significant lowland heathland in North Yorkshire, encompassing 285 hectares. It hosts rare flora and fauna, including notable insect and bird species. This National Nature Reserve is a prime example of the Vale of York's diminishing heathland and is actively managed for conservation.

Name: Skokholm Island (The Bird Observatory)
Description: Skokholm Island, off the Pembrokeshire coast, is an internationally important seabird island. It hosts breeding populations of Manx Shearwater, Puffin, Storm Petrel, and Guillemot. A Bird Observatory monitors wildlife and conducts research.

Name: Skomer Island (Global Seabird Stronghold)
Description: Skomer Island is a National Nature Reserve off the Pembrokeshire coast, renowned for its globally significant seabird colonies. It hosts numerous puffins, Manx shearwaters, and guillemots. Access is via a boat trip from Martin's Haven, with visitor numbers limited to protect wildlife.

Name: Skylarks Nature Reserve
Description: Skylarks Nature Reserve, near Holme Pierrepont, features wet grassland, wetland scrapes, reedbeds, scrub, and wet woodland. It supports breeding lapwing and redshank. Winter sees ducks, geese, and swans. Water voles inhabit its ditches.

Name: Sladden Wood
Description: Sladden Wood is an ancient woodland with a mixture of hornbeam and sweet chestnut coppice. The site supports a diverse ground flora, including bluebells and wood anemones, and provides a habitat for various bird species.

Name: Sliabh Beagh (Trans-border Blanket Bog)
Description: Sliabh Beagh is a trans-border blanket bog in Counties Tyrone and Fermanagh, Northern Ireland. A Special Area of Conservation, it supports diverse bog plant communities, including Hen Harrier. Restoration focuses on hydrological improvements and promoting peat formation.

Name: Slievenacloy "Super-Reserve" (Belfast Hills)
Description: Slievenacloy Nature Reserve in the Belfast Hills features wet heath, grassland, and blanket bog habitats. It supports diverse wildlife, including breeding waders like snipe and curlew, and various insects. Restoration efforts enhance biodiversity.

Name: Slindon Estate (Ancient Woodland & Downland)
Description: Slindon Estate is a 1,400-hectare site in West Sussex, featuring ancient woodland, downland, farmland, and parkland on the South Downs. It supports diverse wildlife, including rare butterflies. Miles of trails are available for walkers and cyclists.

Name: Slindon Estate (Woodland & Nature Recovery)
Description: Slindon Estate, West Sussex, features ancient woodlands, open parkland, and traditional downland, supporting diverse wildlife and archaeological sites. Visitors enjoy walking and cycling. Nature recovery projects are actively pursued here.

Name: Smallbrook Meadows (Chalk River Mosaic)
Description: Smallbrook Meadows is a riverside reserve near Warminster, Wiltshire. It features wet meadows, hedgerows, and the River Wylye, supporting diverse plant life and wildlife including kingfishers and otters. It is part of the Chalk River Mosaic project.

Name: Smallbrook Meadows (Urban Wetland)
Description: Smallbrook Meadows is a 13-hectare urban wetland reserve near Warminster, Wiltshire, managed by Wiltshire Wildlife Trust. It features wildflower meadows, wet woodland, ponds, and the River Were, attracting kingfishers, buzzards, otters, and water voles. Popular for birdwatching and quiet escapes.

Name: Smallburgh Fen
Description: Smallburgh Fen is a Norfolk Wildlife Trust reserve, a Site of Special Scientific Interest protecting a unique transition mire habitat. It supports rare plants and insects, and is home to Norfolk's only colony of the swallowtail butterfly.

Name: Smardale Gill NNR
Description: Smardale Gill NNR features a dismantled railway path and a dramatic viaduct traversing the River Scandal valley. It encompasses ancient woodlands, species-rich limestone grasslands, and river habitats. This reserve supports a diverse array of flora and fauna, offering scenic walking routes.

Name: Smardale Nature Reserve
Description: Smardale Nature Reserve follows a former railway line through a deep valley, featuring an impressive viaduct. It supports a variety of wildlife, including red squirrels, native woodland birds, and rare butterflies.

Name: Smart's & Bullswater Commons
Description: Smart's and Bullswater Commons cover 56 hectares of heathland. The site features woodland, acid grassland, and mire habitats. It supports all six native British reptile species, including sand lizards and smooth snakes. Nightjars and Dartford warblers breed here.

Name: Snailbeach (Lead Mine Heritage & Bats)
Description: Snailbeach Lead Mine, in Shropshire, is a former industrial site now managed as a nature reserve. It is a nationally important hibernation site for several bat species, including Natterer's and Daubenton's bats. The site preserves significant industrial heritage within its woodlands.

Name: Snape Marshes
Description: Snape Marshes is a Suffolk Wildlife Trust nature reserve protecting diverse habitats including reedbeds, saltmarsh, and intertidal mudflats. It supports various bird species, particularly waders and wildfowl, and is an SSSI.

Name: Snape Marshes (Alde Estuary)
Description: Snape Marshes is a coastal grazing marsh and tidal reedbed on the River Alde. This Suffolk Wildlife Trust reserve supports wintering wildfowl, waders like avocets and curlews, and diverse invertebrates and flora. Habitats include saltmarsh and reedbed.

Name: Snapes Point (Salcombe Estuary Views)
Description: Snapes Point offers walking trails with views of Salcombe Estuary, a dog-friendly site managed for wildlife. Visitors can observe various bird species and explore the South Devon Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty.

Name: Snelsmore Common
Description: Snelsmore Common is a large area of heathland, woodland, and mire near Newbury. It is home to diverse wildlife, including Dartford warblers, woodlarks, and adders. Managed to protect its habitats, the site offers public access and walking trails.

Name: Snelsmore Common (Heathland)
Description: Snelsmore Common is a diverse heathland nature reserve near Newbury, Berkshire. It encompasses acid grassland, wet and dry heathland, and woodland habitats. The site supports rare birds like nightjars and woodlarks, alongside various invertebrates. Public access is maintained through footpaths.

Name: Snipe Dales
Description: Snipe Dales is an unspoilt inland valley in the Lincolnshire Wolds, an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. It features wet valleys, woodlands, and permanent pasture, providing a habitat for diverse wildlife. The site offers walking routes.

Name: Snipe Dales (Country Park & Nature Reserve)
Description: Snipe Dales Country Park and Nature Reserve is located in the Lincolnshire Wolds, an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. It features diverse habitats including woodland, heathland, and acid grassland. The site offers walking trails through varied terrain, providing opportunities to observe local wildlife.

Name: Snitterfield Bushes (Ancient Forest of Arden)
Description: Snitterfield Bushes is a relic of the ancient Forest of Arden, featuring mature oak and ash standards over dense hawthorn scrub. This woodland supports diverse birds, invertebrates, and wildflowers. It is a prime example of secondary woodland in Warwickshire.

Name: Snowdonia - Carneddau & Glyderau (NT)
Description: The Carneddau and Glyderau ranges, within Eryri (Snowdonia) in North Wales, offer rugged mountain landscapes. These high peaks, including Y Garn, provide wild terrain and support significant biodiversity. The National Trust manages substantial parts of these areas.

Name: Snows Farm
Description: Snows Farm is a 33-hectare grassland nature reserve in the Painswick Valley, leased by Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust. It features flower-rich pastures supporting diverse butterflies, offering panoramic views across the valley. Conservation focuses on restoring traditional grassland habitats.

Name: Snows Farm (Dillay Valley Mosaic)
Description: Snows Farm, a Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust reserve in Dillay Valley, conserves unimproved limestone grassland. It hosts over 150 flowering plants, notably rare orchids. Scarce invertebrates and woodland dormice, managed by rare breed cattle grazing.

Name: Soar Mill Cove
Description: Soar Mill Cove is a secluded sandy beach on the South Devon coast, within the Bolt Head to Bolt Tail area. Accessible only on foot, it lies amidst dramatic cliffs and is home to diverse wildflowers and birdlife. It forms part of the South Devon Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty.

Name: Soham Meadow
Description: Soham Meadow, east of Ely, is a species-rich fen meadow. Traditionally a hay meadow, it floods in winter, then in spring supports fen violet, marsh marigold, meadow rue, ragged robin, and great burnet. The site attracts diverse invertebrates, including dragonflies and damselflies from Soham Lode.

Name: Solent Seascape Project (Marine Restoration)
Description: The Solent Seascape Project restores vital marine habitats across the Solent, focusing on seagrass meadows and oyster reefs. The initiative enhances biodiversity, improves water quality, and involves scientific research and active restoration.

Name: Solsbury Hill (Bath Grassland)
Description: Solsbury Hill is an Iron Age hillfort near Batheaston, overlooking Bath. It is a Site of Special Scientific Interest, known for its limestone grassland and diverse wildlife. The hill offers panoramic views of the Avon Valley.

Name: Sot's Hole
Description: Sot's Hole is a small, semi-natural ancient woodland and grassland nature reserve in Warwickshire. It sits on a limestone escarpment overlooking the River Avon, managed to maintain a rich diversity of flora and fauna.

Name: Sotby Meadows
Description: Sotby Meadows is a 32-hectare nature reserve near Wragby, managed by the Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust. It comprises species-rich damp and dry grassland habitats. The site supports diverse wildflowers and insects, with grazing used to maintain the habitat structure.

Name: Sourlie Wood
Description: Sourlie Wood is an ancient woodland and wetland site near Irvine, North Ayrshire. It features mature oak, birch, and alder trees, with a rich ground flora including bluebells and ramsons. The site supports various bird species and offers accessible paths.

Name: Souter Lighthouse & The Leas (Coastal Grassland)
Description: Souter Lighthouse, established 1871, was the first purpose-built for alternating electric current globally. Its red and white striped tower stands on a dramatic coastline between Tyne and Wear. The adjacent Leas features 3 miles of magnesian limestone cliffs and coastal grasslands, managed for wildlife and public access.

Name: South Efford Marsh
Description: South Efford Marsh is a tidal marshland at the mouth of the River Avon, South Devon. It is a haven for overwintering and passage wildfowl and waders, including avocet and curlew. The site offers footpaths and bird hides for observation.

Name: South Hill
Description: South Hill features unimproved calcareous grassland, scrub, and a traditional orchard with old apple, plum, and pear trees. Managed by grazing, the site supports diverse butterflies, insects, and plant species including orchids. Views extend across the Chew Valley.

Name: South House Pavement (Limestone)
Description: South House Pavement is a species-rich limestone grassland and pavement site near Grassington in the Yorkshire Dales. It features rare plants like blue moor-grass and rigid brome. The reserve is managed to preserve its biodiversity and geological features, offering botanical interest.

Name: South Milton Sands
Description: South Milton Sands is a picturesque sandy beach on the South Devon coast, featuring an iconic rock arch and clear waters. Managed by the National Trust, the site offers coastal walks, rock pooling, and diverse marine life. It is dog-friendly all year.

Name: South Pennines Park (Community-Led 30x30)
Description: The South Pennines Park is a Community Interest Company managing the South Pennines landscape. This area, between Greater Manchester, Lancashire, and West Yorkshire, features distinctive upland scenery, rich industrial heritage, and significant wildlife, shaped by centuries of human and natural influence.

Name: South Solway Mosses NNR
Description: South Solway Mosses NNR in Cumbria protects extensive lowland raised bog habitats like Wedholme Flow. This internationally important peatland ecosystem supports specialist wildlife. Managed by Natural England, it is a Ramsar site and SAC.

Name: South Stack (The 2026 Exclusion Zone)
Description: South Stack Cliffs RSPB reserve offers dramatic cliff-top views across the Irish Sea. It hosts nesting seabirds, including guillemots, razorbills, and puffins, alongside choughs and rare lizard orchids. Ellin's Tower provides wildlife observation.

Name: South Swale Nature Reserve
Description: South Swale is a coastal nature reserve on the Isle of Sheppey, Kent, featuring grazing marsh, reedbeds, and tidal mudflats. It supports significant bird populations, including waders and wildfowl. Managed by Kent Wildlife Trust for wildlife conservation.

Name: South Walney (Grey Seal Colony)
Description: South Walney Nature Reserve, near Barrow-in-Furness, Cumbria, hosts the county's only grey seal colony, visible year-round. On Walney Island, it supports diverse birdlife, including eider ducks. Public access is restricted in sensitive areas to protect wildlife.

Name: South Walsham Broad
Description: South Walsham Broad is a freshwater lake in the Norfolk Broads, split into two main sections. Known for its tranquil waters, it supports diverse birdlife and aquatic plants. The area is popular for boating and offers scenic walks.

Name: Southerham Farm (South Downs Grassland)
Description: Southerham Farm is a chalk grassland nature reserve located near Lewes, East Sussex. It features species-rich habitats, supporting a diverse array of wildflowers and insects. The site is actively managed by Sussex Wildlife Trust for biodiversity conservation, primarily through traditional grazing practices.

Name: Southerscales
Description: Southerscales Nature Reserve, a nationally important limestone pavement, lies beneath Ingleborough in the Yorkshire Dales. Designated as an SSSI and SAC, its deep grikes shelter a rich variety of wildflowers, ferns, and mosses.

Name: Southfield Farm Marsh
Description: Southfield Farm Marsh is a small wet grassland nature reserve near Bourne. It supports breeding waders such as lapwing and redshank. The site also attracts wintering wildfowl and many plant species. The reserve is managed for biodiversity.

Name: Southmoor
Description: Southmoor Nature Reserve is a small wetland mosaic south of Havant, Hampshire. It features ditches, ponds, reedbeds, and grassland habitats. The site supports various bird species, including snipe and water rail, alongside dragonflies and rare plants.

Name: Southmoor Nature Reserve (Langstone Harbour)
Description: Southmoor Nature Reserve, part of Langstone Harbour, features diverse habitats including saltmarsh, grazing marsh, and intertidal mudflats. This coastal site supports a variety of bird species, especially waders and wildfowl, and is managed to enhance biodiversity.

Name: Southrepps Common
Description: Southrepps Common is a Norfolk Wildlife Trust reserve featuring diverse habitats like wet woodland, fen, reedbed, and acidic grassland. It supports a variety of plant and animal species, including rare invertebrates and wildflowers. It is a Site of Special Scientific Interest.

Name: Southrepps Common (Valley Fen)
Description: Southrepps Common is a rare valley fen SSSI in Norfolk, featuring wet woodland, fen meadow, and scrub. It supports diverse plant and invertebrate species, including uncommon orchids, dragonflies, and butterflies. Managed by Norfolk Wildlife Trust, it offers boardwalks for visitors.

Name: Southwell Potwell Dyke Grasslands
Description: Southwell Potwell Dyke Grasslands is a 2-hectare species-rich grassland on the south-east edge of Southwell, Nottinghamshire. It represents one of the last examples of traditionally managed neutral grassland in the county, featuring a diverse range of plant species and significant ecological value.

Name: Southwick Coast
Description: Southwick Coast is a rich mosaic of habitats in Dumfries and Galloway. It features saltmarsh, shingle banks, woodland, and coastal grassland. The site supports a variety of wildlife, including migrating birds, natterjack toads, and otters. It is managed for biodiversity conservation.

Name: Sow Dale (Wolds Valley)
Description: Sow Dale is a dry valley nature reserve in the Lincolnshire Wolds, an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. It features wildflower-rich grassland with cowslips and bee orchids. Diverse insect life, including dingy skipper butterflies, thrives. Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust manages the site.

Name: Spalford Warren
Description: Spalford Warren, a Nottinghamshire nature reserve, features an active sand quarry, open sand, acidic grassland, and woodland. It supports diverse invertebrates, including solitary bees and wasps. Heathland flora thrives. The site holds significant geological and conservation value.

Name: Spalford Warren (Inland Dunes)
Description: Spalford Warren is an inland sand dune habitat in Nottinghamshire, featuring diverse plant life including rare mosses and lichens. The reserve supports various invertebrates, birds, and reptiles, acting as an important site for ecological succession and conservation efforts.

Name: Sparham Pools (Gravel Pit Restoration)
Description: Sparham Pools is a 36-hectare nature reserve near Lyng, Norfolk, comprising former gravel pits. It supports various water birds, woodlands, scrub, and grassland, providing habitat for diverse wildlife. The site is important for overwintering and breeding birds.

Name: Speke Hall (Coastal Woodland & Estate)
Description: Speke Hall is a rare Tudor manor house located near the River Mersey in Liverpool. The estate features ancient woodland, a garden, and a home farm. Visitors explore the preserved wattle and daub mansion.

Name: Spencer Road Wetlands
Description: Spencer Road Wetlands is a nature reserve in south London, created on former railway land. It features woodlands, wildflower meadows, and ponds. The site supports diverse wildlife including amphibians, insects, and birds, offering a valuable urban green space.

Name: Spendluffe Meadows
Description: Spendluffe Meadows is a former school playing field near Alford, transformed into a wildlife haven. The site features flower-rich grassland, a small orchard, and a pond, supporting diverse invertebrates and amphibians.

Name: Spey Bay
Description: Spey Bay is a coastal reserve at the mouth of the River Spey, featuring shingle beaches and rich marine life. It is a prime location for viewing bottlenose dolphins, ospreys, and other seabirds. The site is managed for wildlife conservation and offers a visitor centre.

Name: Spinnies, Aberogwen (Coastal Gateway)
Description: Spinnies Aberogwen, a coastal gateway, offers diverse habitats managed for wildlife. A bird hide overlooks the Ogwen estuary, attracting various wildfowl and waders. The reserve includes saltmarsh, grassland, and reedbed, providing important habitats for birds, insects, and flora.

Name: Spong Wood
Description: Spong Wood is an ancient woodland in Kent, managed by Kent Wildlife Trust. It features diverse tree species, including oak and hornbeam, supporting various wildlife. The site is notable for its seasonal wildflowers and offers walking trails.

Name: Spring Park
Description: Spring Park is a mature woodland in the London Borough of Bromley, managed by the City of London Corporation. It features ancient oak trees and diverse species, providing essential wildlife habitats. Public footpaths offer opportunities for walking and enjoying the natural environment.

Name: Springdale Farm (Wildflower Flagship)
Description: Springdale Farm is a 42-hectare nature reserve near Usk, Gwent, managed by Gwent Wildlife Trust. This flagship site features a mosaic of wildflower meadows, ancient woodland, and ponds. Grazing maintains species-rich grasslands, supporting diverse wildlife. Visitors can explore waymarked trails with panoramic views.

Name: Springhill (Ancient Parkland & Wood)
Description: Springhill, a 17th-century plantation house, sits within ancient parkland and woodland in County Londonderry. The estate features mature trees, open lawns, and waymarked walking trails. The site offers insights into the region's heritage.

Name: Springwell Reedbed
Description: Springwell Reedbed, near Rickmansworth, is a 10-hectare nature reserve. It protects reedbed, marsh, wet woodland, and grassland, supporting various warblers and dragonflies. Public access is limited to a short track. Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust manage the site.

Name: Sprotbrough Flash
Description: Sprotbrough Flash nature reserve features open water, woodland, scrub, and grassland habitats. It supports diverse wildlife, including various bird species, butterflies, and dragonflies. Winter sees wildfowl arrivals, and summer brings a range of insects to the site.

Name: Spurn National Nature Reserve
Description: Spurn National Nature Reserve is a dynamic three-mile-long sand and shingle spit located in the Humber Estuary, Yorkshire. This unique landscape provides vital habitats for migratory birds, rare plants, and insects. It features mudflats, saltmarsh, and dunes.

Name: Spurn National Nature Reserve (The Anchor)
Description: Spurn National Nature Reserve is a dynamic sand and shingle spit in East Yorkshire, managed by the Yorkshire Wildlife Trust. It is a crucial migratory bird hotspot, offering unique coastal habitats and a distinctive landscape for wildlife.

Name: Spyway (Limestone Grassland & Orchids)
Description: Spyway is a limestone grassland near Langton Matravers on the Isle of Purbeck, Dorset. It supports diverse wildflowers, including orchids, and various butterflies. The site offers expansive views of the Jurassic Coast and is managed for its wildlife.

Name: St Abb's Head NNR (Seabird Colony)
Description: St Abb’s Head is a designated National Nature Reserve, supporting one of mainland Scotland’s largest seabird colonies. Puffins, guillemots, razorbills, and kittiwakes nest on the 90m cliffs. The site features a lighthouse and nature reserve centre, offering panoramic coastal views and rich biodiversity.

Name: St Agnes & Gugh (Rat-Free Pioneer)
Description: St Agnes and Gugh, two islands in the Isles of Scilly, were declared rat-free in 2016 following a successful eradication project. This initiative led to a dramatic increase in breeding Manx shearwater and storm petrel populations.

Name: St Agnes Head & Wheal Coates
Description: St Agnes Head, Cornwall, offers dramatic cliffs, coastal heathland, and significant mining heritage. Wheal Coates, a historic tin mine, stands prominently on the clifftops. The site offers striking views and extensive walks along the South West Coast Path.

Name: St Aldhelm's Head & Worth Matravers
Description: Spyway is a former stone quarry on Dorset's Isle of Purbeck, forming part of the Jurassic Coast World Heritage Site. It offers panoramic views towards St Aldhelm's Head and the English Channel, featuring ancient strip lynchets and wildflower meadows.

Name: St Audries Wood
Description: St Audries Wood is a small, semi-natural ancient woodland within the Quantock Hills Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. It offers coastal views over the Bristol Channel, featuring a circular walk. The site is an important habitat for dormice and diverse wildflowers.

Name: St Austell Bay (Blue Carbon Node)
Description: St Austell Bay, a vital marine area in Cornwall, is a focus for the 'Tor to Shore' project. This initiative by Cornwall Wildlife Trust works to restore and protect marine habitats, including seagrass beds. It aims to enhance biodiversity and promote blue carbon sequestration, with significant community involvement.

Name: St Catherine's Hill
Description: St Catherine's Hill is a chalk downland nature reserve near Winchester, featuring an Iron Age hill fort, maze, and ancient chapel site. It supports diverse chalk grassland flowers, rare butterflies like the brown hairstreak, and birds. Managed in partnership by Hampshire & Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust.

Name: St Catherine's Hill (Winchester Chalk Grassland)
Description: St Catherine's Hill is a chalk downland nature reserve near Winchester. Known for its varied butterflies and wildflowers, the site features an Iron Age hill fort, a Mizmaze, and provides panoramic views of the Itchen Valley and city. Managed for its rich biodiversity.

Name: St Clair's Meadow
Description: St Clair's Meadow is a grassland nature reserve situated on the banks of the River Meon near Soberton in Hampshire. It encompasses species-rich chalk grassland, traditional hay meadows, and diverse riverine habitats. Managed for biodiversity, it supports a variety of wildflowers and insects.

Name: St Cyrus NNR (Volcanic Grasslands)
Description: St Cyrus NNR is a stunning coastal reserve in Scotland, featuring cliffs, dunes, and beach, backed by important volcanic grasslands. It supports diverse wildlife, including nesting seabirds, rare wildflowers, and marine life.

Name: St Ebba's Beadnell (Coastal Link)
Description: St Ebba's Beadnell (Coastal Link) is a specific section of the Northumberland Coast, managed by the National Trust. This area provides public access to diverse coastal landscapes, offering scenic views and opportunities for walking and exploring the natural environment near Beadnell village.

Name: St Erth Pits
Description: St Erth Pits is a disused quarry site revealing a Pliocene shell deposit, offering significant geological and palaeontological interest. The reserve features wet woodland, marshland, and open water, supporting various wildlife.

Name: St Helen's (Atlantic Sentinel Node)
Description: St Helen's is a small rocky tidal island within the Isles of Scilly, an important breeding ground for seabirds like storm petrels and puffins. It hosts diverse plant species adapted to its harsh coastal environment and is managed for biodiversity.

Name: St James's Park - Duck Island
Description: Duck Island, within St James's Park, serves as a significant sanctuary for diverse waterfowl species, including pelicans. This central London location provides a vital habitat for various birds, fostering biodiversity in the urban environment.

Name: St James's Park (Duck Island & Lake)
Description: St James’s Park is in Westminster, central London. Duck Island is at the eastern end of St James's Park lake, home to various wildfowl. The lake supports diverse bird species, providing a vital urban wildlife habitat, managed by a dedicated wildlife officer.

Name: St Kilda NNR (World Heritage Site)
Description: St Kilda is a remote archipelago 40 miles west of North Uist, within the Outer Hebrides. It comprises volcanic islands and sea stacks, hosting vast seabird colonies including puffins and gannets. Evacuated in 1930, the islands are a dual World Heritage Site, managed for its natural and cultural heritage.

Name: St Lawrence Field
Description: St Lawrence Field is a sloping chalk grassland and ancient woodland near Ventnor, Isle of Wight. It supports diverse flora, including orchid species, and provides habitat for insects and red squirrels. The reserve offers views over St Lawrence and the English Channel.

Name: St Lawrence Undercliff Woods
Description: St Lawrence Undercliff Woods is a fascinating mosaic of habitats, including woodlands, scrub, and grassland, all formed on a massive landslip. It's home to a variety of wildlife, notably dormice, bats, and rare insects.

Name: St Lawrence Undercliff Woods (Isle of Wight)
Description: St Lawrence Undercliff Woods is an ancient woodland on the Isle of Wight's south coast. It features diverse tree species like sycamore and ash, with a rich understorey. Known for spring wildflowers, woodland birds, and historic terracing.

Name: St Martin's (Chapel Down Heath)
Description: St Martin's, part of Chapel Down Heath on the Isle of Wight, showcases a mosaic of grassland, heathland, wetlands, and sea cliffs. This diverse habitat supports various wildlife, including marsh fritillary butterflies, birds, and rare plants.

Name: St Mary’s Glen
Description: St Mary’s Glen is a small woodland reserve in the Scottish Borders, important for its ancient oak and ash trees. The glen supports various woodland birds, including redstarts and wood warblers. The river Teviot runs through the reserve, adding to its ecological value and habitat diversity.

Name: St Michael's Mount (Island Conservation)
Description: St Michael's Mount is a tidal island and castle in Mount's Bay, Cornwall. Managed by the National Trust and the St Aubyn family, it offers historic exploration of its castle, terraced gardens, and island village.

Name: St. Clair’s Meadow (River Meon)
Description: St Clair's Meadow is a traditional flood meadow alongside the River Meon, supporting diverse riverside and wetland wildlife, including a small alder carr woodland. The site offers views of local flora and fauna, characteristic of a chalk river habitat.

Name: Stackpole (Coastal Mosaic & Otters)
Description: Stackpole Estate in Pembrokeshire, Wales, features a diverse landscape of golden beaches, woodlands, and lakes. This National Trust property is home to abundant wildlife, including otters, and offers various walking trails. Volunteers support conservation efforts.

Name: Staffhurst Wood (Limpsfield)
Description: Staffhurst Wood is ancient woodland in the Surrey Weald near Limpsfield. It features diverse tree species, a variety of ground flora including bluebells and wood anemones, and a pond that supports amphibians. The site is managed for biodiversity.

Name: Staffordshire & Worcestershire Canal (Green Flag Site)
Description: The Staffordshire & Worcestershire Canal is a 30-mile Green Flag site connecting Stourport-on-Severn to the River Trent. Designed by James Brindley, it flows through picturesque landscapes, rich heritage, and diverse wildlife habitats in Staffordshire and Worcestershire.

Name: Stainsby Wood
Description: Stainsby Wood is a remnant ancient woodland in Middlesbrough, featuring a diverse mix of tree species like oak, ash, and hazel. It supports a rich understorey and various wildlife, serving as a valuable green space for local communities.

Name: Stanborough Reedmarsh
Description: Stanborough Reedmarsh is a nature reserve in Welwyn Garden City, managed by Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust. It features reedbeds, scrub, and wet woodland, providing habitats for birds, insects, and amphibians. A boardwalk offers access to observe wildlife.

Name: Stanford Warren
Description: Stanford Warren is a woodland reserve near Cattawade, on the River Stour. It features impressive spring bluebell displays, primroses, and wood anemones. The woodland, mainly oak, chestnut, and hornbeam, supports badgers, foxes, and diverse birds. Fungi are also prominent.

Name: Stanford Warren (Large Reedbed)
Description: Stanford Warren is a large reedbed, created during bypass construction, managed for breeding wetland birds. It is an important site for species like Reed Warblers, Grasshopper Warblers, Cetti's Warblers, Bearded Tits, Water Rails, and regularly visited by Bittern and Marsh Harrier. No public facilities are present.

Name: Stanground Newt Ponds
Description: Stanground Newt Ponds in Peterborough, established 2005, features six ponds specifically for great crested newts, a protected species. The site includes grassland and scrub, providing diverse habitats. Volunteers manage the reserve.

Name: Stanground Wash (Peterborough)
Description: Stanground Wash is a 70.8-hectare biological Site of Special Scientific Interest east of Farcet, Cambridgeshire. Part of the Lower Nene Valley SPA and Ramsar site, this wet grassland with shallow pools supports breeding waders and wintering wildfowl.

Name: Stanmore Marsh (Harrow)
Description: Stanmore Marsh is a 4.6-hectare Local Nature Reserve in Harrow, North London. It features a unique spring-fed mire, reedbeds, grassland, and woodland, forming the headwaters of the River Crane. The site is popular for walking, picnics, and includes a children's play area.

Name: Stanmore Marsh Wetlands
Description: Stanmore Marsh Wetlands, located in Harrow, is a thriving wetland habitat and Local Nature Reserve. The site has undergone significant transformation, with ongoing efforts to clear invasive species, plant native flora, and enhance public access through boardwalks. It supports diverse biodiversity.

Name: Stanton's Pit
Description: Stanton's Pit is a former gravel pit now a nature reserve near Holkham, Lincolnshire. It features open water, reedbeds, and damp grassland, supporting diverse bird species, amphibians, and insects.

Name: Stanwick Lakes (managed interface)
Description: Stanwick Lakes is a 750-acre nature reserve and park in the Nene Valley, Northamptonshire. It features extensive walking and cycling paths, lakes for water sports, and play areas. The site supports diverse wildlife and offers educational programs.

Name: Staunton Quarry
Description: Staunton Quarry, a former limestone quarry, is now a nature reserve with diverse grassland, scrub, and woodland habitats. It supports species like common spotted orchids and twayblade. The site is managed for wildlife conservation.

Name: Stave Hill Ecological Park
Description: Stave Hill Ecological Park is a 5.2-acre nature reserve in Rotherhithe, South East London. It features diverse habitats including woodlands, grasslands, and ponds. The park includes a re-created volcanic landscape and is managed by TCV.

Name: Staveley Woodlands (Coppice Restoration)
Description: Staveley Woodlands, near Staveley, Cumbria, features ancient woodland, wet woodland, and meadows. Managed by Cumbria Wildlife Trust, it supports diverse flora and fauna. Ongoing coppice restoration enhances biodiversity and improves habitats for local wildlife.

Name: Steep Holm Island
Description: Steep Holm Island is a rugged limestone island in the Bristol Channel, west of Weston-super-Mare. A designated SSSI and nature reserve, it hosts seabird colonies, rare plants, and historical remains. Avon Wildlife Trust manages the island to protect its unique wildlife and heritage.

Name: Stenhouse Wood
Description: Stenhouse Wood is a small, ancient woodland reserve near West Calder, managed by the Scottish Wildlife Trust. It features a diverse mix of broadleaf trees like oak, ash, and elm, supporting rich ground flora, including bluebells. The site provides valuable habitat for birds and invertebrates.

Name: Stiffkey Marshes (Saltmarsh Core)
Description: Stiffkey Marshes is a National Trust site on the North Norfolk coast. It comprises saltmarsh, mudflats, and sand dunes, supporting diverse birdlife and unique coastal flora. The area is a designated Site of Special Scientific Interest, managed for conservation.

Name: Stiffkey Saltmarshes
Description: Stiffkey Saltmarshes are part of the Blakeney National Nature Reserve, a vast expanse of unspoilt saltmarshes and mudflats on the North Norfolk coast. Managed by the National Trust. A haven for diverse wildlife, including avocets and Brent geese. Visitors explore via public footpaths.

Name: Stirley (Community Farm)
Description: Stirley Community Farm, managed by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust, is a working farm and nature reserve near Huddersfield. It demonstrates sustainable farming practices and offers educational programmes. The site features meadows, woodlands, and ponds, supporting local wildlife.

Name: Stockbridge Down
Description: Stockbridge Down offers expansive views over the Test Valley in Hampshire. This chalk grassland is home to wildflowers, butterflies, and ancient barrows. Visitors can explore walking trails and observe local wildlife. The site is managed for conservation.

Name: Stockbridge Down (Chalk Downland & Iron Age Fort)
Description: Stockbridge Down is an Iron Age hillfort and chalk downland in Hampshire. It offers expansive views across the Test Valley, with habitat for rare butterflies and wildflowers. Remains of the hillfort's ramparts are visible.

Name: Stocker's Lake
Description: Stocker's Lake is a gravel pit lake and nature reserve in Hertfordshire, managed by Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust. It features open water, reedbeds, and diverse woodland, supporting overwintering wildfowl and migrating birds. The site offers accessible trails and bird hides.

Name: Stocker's Lake (Colne Valley)
Description: Stocker's Lake is a large lake forming part of the Colne Valley Wetlands Site of Special Scientific Interest. Managed by Hertfordshire Wildlife Trust, it features reedbeds, marsh, and wet woodland. The site supports significant bird populations, including overwintering wildfowl and breeding birds, alongside otters.

Name: Stocking Springs Wood
Description: Stocking Springs Wood, a Local Wildlife Site, features ancient woodland with oak and hornbeam standards. Its diverse understorey supports seasonal wildflowers like bluebells and wood anemone, providing habitat for various bird species.

Name: Stocksmoor Common
Description: Stocksmoor Common is a small nature reserve near Stocksmoor village in Kirklees. It features damp grassland, scrub, and woodland. The site is notable for its rich invertebrate life, including various butterfly species and offers walking paths.

Name: Stockton Wood
Description: Stockton Wood is ancient woodland, managed by Warwickshire Wildlife Trust. It features oak, ash, and hazel trees, providing a habitat for diverse wildlife, including various bird species and wildflowers. The site offers peaceful walks throughout the year.

Name: Stodmarsh (National Nature Reserve - Stour Valley)
Description: Stodmarsh is a National Nature Reserve in the Stour Valley, Kent. It features a mosaic of reedbeds, open water, and wet grassland, supporting diverse birdlife including marsh harriers and bearded tits. It is home to many invertebrates and rare plants.

Name: Stodmarsh National Nature Reserve
Description: Stodmarsh National Nature Reserve is a large area of reedbeds, marshes, and waterways near Canterbury, Kent. It is an important site for migratory birds and diverse aquatic wildlife, offering hides for birdwatching and accessible trails.

Name: Stoke Common
Description: Stoke Common is a 100-hectare Site of Special Scientific Interest and Special Area of Conservation in Buckinghamshire. It protects rare lowland heathland, part of the Chiltern commons, with diverse habitats. The City of London Corporation manages this biodiversity site.

Name: Stoke Ferry Fen
Description: Stoke Ferry Fen protects species-rich fen meadowland in Norfolk. It provides important habitat for rare plants like marsh-marigold and bog pimpernel, and insects including dragonflies, damselflies, and butterflies. This small reserve conserves a valuable wetland ecosystem.

Name: Stoke Point & Revelstoke Drive
Description: Stoke Point and Revelstoke Drive offer dramatic coastal views and clifftop paths in South Devon. This stretch, part of the South West Coast Path, is home to seabirds and rare plants. Visitors can enjoy a four-mile drive and explore various walking routes from the free car park.

Name: Stoke Wood (Oxfordshire)
Description: Stoke Wood in Oxfordshire is a diverse woodland on the edge of the Chiltern Hills. It offers gentle walking trails. Wildlife includes muntjac deer and birds. Historically part of Bernwood Forest, mature oak and beech trees are present.

Name: Stoke Wood End Quarter
Description: Stoke Wood End Quarter is an ancient woodland in Northamptonshire, part of a larger wood. It contains diverse tree species like oak and ash, with an undergrowth of bluebells and primroses. The site provides a habitat for various birds and insects.

Name: Stonebarrow Hill (Jurassic Coast Wildlife)
Description: Stonebarrow Hill, managed by the National Trust, is a prominent point on the Jurassic Coast in Dorset. It offers panoramic views and features heathland and grassland habitats supporting diverse wildlife. An accessible circular route is present.

Name: Stonefolds (Nature Recovery Ranger Site)
Description: Stonefolds is a nature recovery site near Hadrian's Wall in Northumberland National Park. It focuses on restoring peatlands, woodlands, and floodplains. The site contributes to broader nature recovery efforts within the park, aiming to meet national net zero targets.

Name: Stonehill Down
Description: Stonehill Down is a 12-hectare nature reserve in Dorset, featuring species-rich chalk grassland. It supports various butterflies like the Adonis blue and chalkhill blue, alongside a range of orchids. The site offers panoramic views of the surrounding countryside.

Name: Stonepit Plantation
Description: Stonepit Plantation is a woodland and scrub site on former gravel pits. It features diverse trees like oak, ash, and sycamore, supporting a rich ground flora. Various bird species, including great spotted woodpecker, are present. Paths provide public access.

Name: Stonesby Quarry
Description: Stonesby Quarry is a disused limestone quarry, now a nature reserve in Leicestershire. Managed by LRWT, it features diverse grassland habitats supporting wildflowers, insects, and birds. The site offers public access for walks.

Name: Stoneycliffe Wood
Description: Stoneycliffe Wood is a mixed woodland of oak, ash, birch, and sycamore. It supports diverse woodland birds, mammals, and ancient flora. Public paths allow visitors to explore the site, which also features hazel, hawthorn, and rowan.

Name: Storton's Pits
Description: Storton’s Pits is a former gravel extraction site, now a nature reserve located near Peterborough. It features lakes, reedbeds, and grassland, supporting diverse waterfowl, invertebrates, and rare plants. The site is managed for wildlife conservation.

Name: Storton's Pits (Northampton Urban Wetland)
Description: Storton's Pits is a former gravel quarry by the River Nene, now a wetland nature reserve in Northampton. It features open water, reedbeds, and varied habitats supporting diverse wildlife, including breeding birds and rare plants. Footpaths allow access.

Name: Stoughton Moor (managed interface)
Description: Stoughton Moor, managed by the National Trust, is an area near Dunstable Downs in Bedfordshire. It is part of the Chilterns landscape, contributing to local biodiversity. The Trust conserves its natural features, providing habitats for wildlife and opportunities for walking.

Name: Stourbridge Canal (Fens Pools Connectivity)
Description: The Stourbridge Canal connects the Staffordshire & Worcestershire Canal at Stourton with the Dudley No 1 Canal at Brierley Hill. It forms part of the Stourport Ring and features the Fens Pools nature reserves. The canal is 5.5 miles long.

Name: Stourhead Estate (Ancient Parkland Restoration)
Description: Stourhead Estate, a 2,650-acre property in Wiltshire, features a Palladian house, world-famous landscape garden, and ancient woodlands. The site is at the source of the River Stour and undergoes active parkland restoration efforts.

Name: Stourhead Estate (Wiltshire Parkland)
Description: Stourhead Estate, a 1,072-hectare property in Wiltshire, features an 18th-century landscape garden, a Palladian mansion, woodlands, and farmland. Key attractions include a Pantheon, Temple of Flora, and Grotto. The estate sits at the source of the River Stour.

Name: Stover Canal
Description: Stover Canal is a historic transport waterway bordered by wet woodland, fen, and unimproved grassland. The reserve supports diverse wetland plants, invertebrates, and breeding birds, offering a tranquil environment for wildlife. It is part of a wider nature reserve managed for biodiversity and conservation.

Name: Stowford Moor (Orchid & Wet Grassland)
Description: Stowford Moor, a Devon Wildlife Trust reserve near Bude, features wet and culm grassland habitats. It hosts diverse orchid populations, including Heath Spotted and Greater Butterfly-orchid, providing crucial wetland and grassland ecosystems.

Name: Strangford Lough (Marine & Coastal Hub)
Description: Strangford Lough, Northern Ireland, is a large tidal inlet managed by the National Trust. It supports diverse marine and coastal habitats, including mudflats, salt marshes, and islands. A vital overwintering site for brent geese and home to common seals, it is a protected area.

Name: Stratford-upon-Avon Canal (Orchard Project)
Description: The Stratford-upon-Avon Canal links Stratford to the Worcester and Birmingham Canal. Its 13.5 miles include 54 locks and unique cast-iron aqueducts, winding through rural Warwickshire landscapes. It offers a popular route for boating and towpath walks.

Name: Strawberry Banks
Description: Strawberry Banks is a 1.2-hectare chalk grassland nature reserve in Kent. Managed by Kent Wildlife Trust, it protects a rare habitat supporting diverse wildflowers, butterflies, and insects. The site features steep slopes, offering important refuge for local wildlife.

Name: Strawberry Hill
Description: Strawberry Hill is a small area of acid grassland, marsh, and scrubland located near Godmanchester in Cambridgeshire. It is home to various invertebrates and birds, including marbled white butterflies and green woodpeckers. The site is managed for its biodiversity.

Name: Strawberry Hill Heath
Description: Strawberry Hill Heath is a small remnant of heathland and grassland in Nottinghamshire. It supports a mix of acid grassland, heather, and birch scrub, providing habitat for various insects and birds. The site is a local wildlife site.

Name: Strawberry Hill Heath (Sherwood Habitat)
Description: Strawberry Hill Heath is an important remnant of the historic Sherwood Forest, featuring a mosaic of acid grassland, heathland, and woodland. The site supports notable species like nightjar, woodlark, and dingy skipper butterfly.

Name: Street Heath
Description: Street Heath is a lowland raised bog in Somerset, managed by Somerset Wildlife Trust. It supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare bog plants and invertebrates. Boardwalks provide access for visitors.

Name: Strensall Common (Heathland)
Description: Strensall Common is a Site of Special Scientific Interest, one of Yorkshire's last lowland heathland fragments. Managed by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust, it supports nationally rare plants and animals, including the emperor moth and raft spider. Parts of the common are also used by the Ministry of Defence.

Name: Stringer's Common
Description: Stringer's Common in Surrey, near Worplesdon, combines heathland, woodland, and acid grassland. A remnant of a larger historic heathland, it supports species such as woodlark, tree pipit, and Dartford warbler. Management includes grazing and scrub removal.

Name: Studland Bay (Purbeck Heaths Super NNR)
Description: Studland Bay offers a 4-mile stretch of golden sand and heathland in Dorset, managed by the National Trust. It supports diverse wildlife, including all six native reptile species, and is a popular destination for nature walks, water sports, and family beach visits.

Name: Sturts East
Description: Sturts East is a 3.7-hectare nature reserve in Herefordshire, featuring unimproved grassland, wetland, and woodland. It supports diverse wildflowers, insects, and bird species. Managed by grazing, the reserve maintains rich biodiversity.

Name: Sturts North
Description: Sturts North is a wet meadow and fen habitat near Leominster, Herefordshire. The reserve features wetland plants, sedges, rushes, and supports diverse insects and birds. It is managed to enhance biodiversity and maintain its unique waterlogged environment.

Name: Sturts North & South
Description: Sturts North & South are two Herefordshire meadows near Garway, managed by Herefordshire Wildlife Trust. The sites feature unimproved neutral grassland and mature hedgerows. They support diverse flora, including green-winged orchids, and provide habitat for invertebrates.

Name: Sturts South
Description: Sturts South is a small Herefordshire nature reserve featuring undisturbed flood meadow grasslands alongside the River Lugg. It supports diverse wildflowers and insects within its rich, wet habitats. This site is managed for its botanical interest.

Name: Suffolk Sandlings (Woods)
Description: The Suffolk Sandlings is a fragmented landscape of heathland and woodland along the coast from Ipswich to Southwold. It includes sites like Rendlesham Forest, Dunwich Forest, Snape Wood, and Hazlewood Common, offering diverse habitats and walking trails.

Name: Sumburgh Head (Shetland)
Description: Sumburgh Head is a nature reserve and lighthouse at the southern tip of mainland Shetland. It is a key breeding site for seabirds including puffins, guillemots, razorbills, and kittiwakes. The site offers panoramic views and an accessible visitor centre.

Name: Summer Leys
Description: Summer Leys is a mature Northamptonshire nature reserve, a former gravel pit. It features open water, reedbeds, grasslands, and wet woodlands. Supporting diverse wildlife, especially birds, it is an SSSI and popular for birdwatching.

Name: Summer Leys (Flagship Nene Valley Wetland)
Description: Summer Leys is a prominent wetland nature reserve in the Nene Valley, Northamptonshire. It features open water, wet grassland, and scrub, supporting diverse bird species, including overwintering wildfowl and breeding waders. Several hides are available for birdwatching.

Name: Summerseat Nature Reserve
Description: Summerseat Nature Reserve is a restored former railway goods yard now functioning as a vital woodland and wetland habitat. Situated near the River Irwell, it features ponds, wildflower meadows, and mature trees, supporting diverse local wildlife. Visitors can enjoy quiet accessible trails.

Name: Sunnybank (Urban Nature Park)
Description: Sunnybank is an urban nature park in north Sheffield, featuring ancient woodland, meadows, and a community orchard. Managed by Sheffield & Rotherham Wildlife Trust, it provides a valuable green space for wildlife and local residents. Bluebells carpet Longley Wood in spring.

Name: Surfleet Lows
Description: Surfleet Lows is a remnant of wet grassland, a once common habitat in the Fens. Ditches and open water areas support varied aquatic invertebrates. The reserve is home to significant populations of water voles. Managed by Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust.

Name: Sutcliffe Park (Greenwich)
Description: Sutcliffe Park, located in Greenwich, is a significant greenspace incorporating a re-naturalised section of the River Ravensbourne. It forms a key part of flood alleviation and biodiversity enhancement projects, encouraging natural habitats.

Name: Sutcliffe Park (Quaggy)
Description: Sutcliffe Park is a vital green space in the borough of Greenwich, South East London, featuring the River Quaggy, which was naturalised in 2005. Thames21 engages local communities here, focusing on habitat improvement and river health.

Name: Sutton & Hollesley Heaths (Sandlings)
Description: Sutton & Hollesley Heaths, a significant Sandlings heathland in Suffolk, features fragmented heathland, acid grassland, and woodland. It provides habitat for rare species including nightjar, woodlark, and silver-studded blue butterfly. Habitats are actively managed.

Name: Sutton Fen
Description: Sutton Fen, a Norfolk Wildlife Trust reserve, protects diverse fenland habitats in Norfolk. It supports rare plants, insects like the Swallowtail butterfly, and other nationally significant species. Visitors can observe its unique biodiversity and ongoing conservation efforts.

Name: Sutton Hoo (Heathland & Estate)
Description: Sutton Hoo in Suffolk features Anglo-Saxon royal burial mounds, including the ship burial of an Anglo-Saxon king. The estate comprises woodland and heathland. An exhibition hall displays replica treasures and artefacts. The landscape offers various walking routes.

Name: Sutton Hoo (Heathland & River Deben)
Description: Sutton Hoo is a globally significant Anglo-Saxon royal burial ground in Suffolk. The site features extensive heathland and woodland, offering walks with views over the River Deben. Visitors can explore the ancient burial mounds and learn about its rich history.

Name: Sutton's Fen
Description: Sutton's Fen, a north-east Essex site, is a relic of ancient fenland and marsh pasture. Fed by spring water, it supports diverse sedges, rushes, and grasses, attracting dragonflies, damselflies, and butterflies. Over 100 plant species thrive, including greater tussock-sedge and southern marsh-orchid. Roe deer and water voles inhabit the reserve.

Name: Swanpond Copse
Description: Swanpond Copse is an ancient woodland nature reserve near Petersfield. Managed by Hampshire and Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust, it features mature oaks, hazel coppice, and woodland flowers like bluebells. The site provides habitats for birds and small mammals.

Name: Swanvale
Description: Swanvale Nature Reserve in Falmouth, Cornwall, features a wooded valley and a freshwater lake. Managed by Cornwall Wildlife Trust, it supports diverse wildlife, including otters and many bird species. Public footpaths allow access for nature observation.

Name: Swanwick Lakes
Description: Swanwick Lakes Nature Reserve, managed by Hampshire & Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust, features diverse habitats including woodland, meadows, and lakes. This former clay pit supports a variety of wildlife and provides nature trails for visitors near Southampton.

Name: Swanwick Lakes Nature Reserve (Restored Clay Pits)
Description: Swanwick Lakes Nature Reserve is a 38-hectare site near Fareham, Hampshire, featuring two lakes, woodlands, and grasslands. Formerly a clay pit, it now supports diverse wildlife including dragonflies, bats, and bird species. This restored habitat provides a valuable ecological resource.

Name: Sweeney Fen (Marsh Orchid Sanctuary)
Description: Sweeney Fen is a wetland site near Oswestry, Shropshire. It features diverse habitats including marshy grassland, reedbed, and wet woodland. The reserve supports a wide array of wildlife, notably various orchid species, insects, amphibians, and birds.

Name: Sweeney Fen (Marsh Orchid Site)
Description: Sweeney Fen is a nature reserve near Oswestry, Shropshire, managed by Shropshire Wildlife Trust. It comprises fen and marshy grassland, supporting marsh orchids, ragged robin, and meadowsweet. A pond on the site attracts various damselflies and dragonflies.

Name: Sweet Briar Marshes
Description: Sweet Briar Marshes is an urban wildlife haven in Norwich. It comprises 90 acres of wetlands and woodlands along the River Wensum. The site supports a diverse range of species, including water voles and otters, with significant wetland restoration efforts underway.

Name: Sweet Briar Marshes (Norwich City)
Description: Sweet Briar Marshes is a 90-acre Norfolk Wildlife Trust reserve in Norwich. It protects fen and wet woodland, providing habitat for water vole and kingfisher. As the city's largest remaining wild space, it is managed for conservation and public access.

Name: Sweetings Meadow
Description: Sweetings Meadow is a small, species-rich grassland in Essex, formerly part of a larger area. It supports diverse wildflowers and insects, including various butterfly species. It is best visited in late spring and summer for its vibrant flora and fauna.

Name: Swell Wood
Description: Swell Wood is an ancient woodland and Coronation Meadow in Somerset. It hosts a significant heronry, numerous bat species, and many ancient trees. The reserve is managed for biodiversity and accessible via public footpaths.

Name: Swettenham Meadows (Dane Valley)
Description: Swettenham Meadows is a flood-plain meadow adjacent to the River Dane, managed to support diverse grassland species. It features a rich array of wildflowers and provides habitats for various invertebrates and ground-nesting birds. The site is a valuable ecological area within the Dane Valley.

Name: Swettenham Valley
Description: Swettenham Valley, a Cheshire Wildlife Trust reserve, features varied habitats including woodland, meadows, and wetlands along the River Dane. It supports diverse wildlife like otters and kingfishers. The site offers walking trails and educational opportunities for visitors.

Name: Swift Valley
Description: Swift Valley Nature Reserve lies on the northern edge of Rugby, following the River Swift. The site features grassland, wetland, and woodland habitats, supporting diverse wildlife. A network of paths and boardwalks allows year-round exploration.

Name: Swift Valley Nature Reserve
Description: Swift Valley Nature Reserve in Rugby, Warwickshire, features diverse habitats including wetlands, grasslands, and woodlands. The River Swift meanders through, creating a haven for wildfowl and wading birds. Grazing cattle manage the grasslands, fostering wildflowers and insects for visitors to observe.

Name: Swift's Hill
Description: Swift's Hill Nature Reserve is an important example of limestone grassland, located near Slad. It supports diverse flora including cowslips, wild thyme, and orchids. The site offers panoramic views across the Slad Valley. Management includes grazing to maintain habitat diversity for wildflowers and insects.

Name: Swift's Hill (Cotswold Grassland)
Description: Swift's Hill is a steep, west-facing slope of Cotswold grassland, rich in wildflowers and insects. Designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI), it forms part of the Cotswolds Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. The reserve supports rare flora including pasqueflower and orchids.

Name: Swift's Hill (Slad Valley Limestone Scarp)
Description: Swift's Hill is a limestone grassland reserve in the Slad Valley. It protects diverse flora, including fragrant and bee orchids. The site supports rare insects like the Duke of Burgundy butterfly, marbled white, and fritillary species.

Name: Swineshead Wood
Description: Ancient woodland and former parkland trees on clay soil near Swineshead. This site features a rich mix of ash, oak, and field maple, supporting diverse flora and fauna. Maintained by the Woodland Trust for public access and wildlife.

Name: Swinley Forest (SW boundary)
Description: Swinley Forest, a Crown Estate woodland in Berkshire, is managed by Forestry England. It offers extensive trails for walking, cycling, and horse riding, and is known for its mountain biking routes. The forest spans 2,600 acres.

Name: Swinn Wood
Description: Swinn Wood is an ancient woodland in Lincolnshire, with records dating back to Domesday. It features a diverse mix of tree species including oak, ash, and hazel. Bluebells carpet the woodland floor in spring, attracting various insect life.

Name: Swithland Wood (Ancient Woodland & Slate)
Description: Swithland Wood is a designated Site of Special Scientific Interest in Leicestershire, East Midlands. This ancient woodland features historic slate quarries and diverse ecosystems. Owned and managed by Bradgate Park Trust, it offers significant habitats for wildlife and is popular for walks.

Name: Syderstone Common
Description: Syderstone Common is a 55-hectare nature reserve with diverse habitats, including heathland, woodland, and a unique geological 'pingos' landscape. It supports rare flora and fauna, offering important conservation value in Norfolk.

Name: Syderstone Common (Natterjack Toad Site)
Description: Syderstone Common is a rare heathland habitat in Norfolk, managed by the Norfolk Wildlife Trust. It supports unique species like natterjack toads, sand lizards, and various rare plants. The site features open sandy areas and dry heathland, maintained through conservation grazing.

Name: Sydlings Copse
Description: Sydlings Copse, near Headington, Oxford, is a small ancient woodland and nature reserve. It features diverse habitats including wildflower meadows, ponds, and fen. Known for its rich flora and fauna, including rare butterflies and orchids, it offers walking trails.

Name: Sydlings Copse (Ancient Wood & Fen)
Description: Sydlings Copse is a diverse nature reserve featuring ancient woodland, calcareous grassland, and fen habitats. This site supports a rich array of flora and fauna, including rare orchids, butterflies, and invertebrates. It offers accessible paths for visitors to explore its varied ecosystems.

Name: Sywell Country Park (Reservoir habitat)
Description: Sywell Country Park is a Green Flag Award winning site in Northamptonshire. Built around a former Victorian reservoir, it provides habitat for diverse wildlife, including overwintering wildfowl. The park offers walking trails, bird hides, a play area, and a cafe for visitors.

Name: Tadnoll Heath
Description: Tadnoll and Winfrith Heath is a large nature reserve near Dorchester, Dorset, encompassing dry and wet heathland, bog, and ancient woodland. It supports rare species like the Dartford warbler and sand lizard, and all six native British reptiles. The site holds SSSI and SAC designations.

Name: Taf Fechan (Limestone Gorge)
Description: Taf Fechan is a woodland reserve in the Brecon Beacons National Park, featuring a limestone gorge and a section of the river. A Site of Special Scientific Interest, it supports diverse flora and bird species such as dipper. Managed by Welsh Wildlife Trust.

Name: Tailby Meadow
Description: Tailby Meadow is a small grassland reserve in Great Oxendon, Northamptonshire, managed by Wildlife BCN. The meadow features a rich diversity of wildflowers and grasses, supporting various insect species. It is a tranquil spot for nature observation. Seasonal management includes hay cutting.

Name: Tailby Meadow (River Ise)
Description: Tailby Meadow is a species-rich grassland situated next to the River Ise in Northamptonshire. The site supports diverse wildflowers like ragged robin and great burnet, attracting many insects. Managed for conservation, it provides a vital habitat for local wildlife.

Name: Tailend Moss
Description: Tailend Moss is a lowland raised bog, an extremely rare and threatened habitat in Scotland. The reserve supports diverse bog plants including cotton grasses, cranberries, and sundews. It serves as a significant site for dragonflies and damselflies, particularly during July and August.

Name: Talybont Reservoir (Catchment Partnership)
Description: Talybont Reservoir is a significant water body within the Usk catchment, central to conservation efforts. It supports various wildlife habitats and is managed with partners. Welsh Wildlife Trust collaborates with Dŵr Cymru Welsh Water on catchment partnership initiatives here, focusing on nature-based solutions.

Name: Tame Valley Canal (Green Corridor)
Description: The Tame Valley Canal is an 8.5-mile green corridor in Birmingham, connecting to the national canal network. It traverses industrial landscapes, offering walking and cycling routes, and is a significant wildlife haven within an urban setting.

Name: Tame Valley Wetlands (Landscape Partnership)
Description: The Tame Valley Wetlands scheme restores and recreates habitats along the River Tame, spanning 100 square kilometres across North Warwickshire and South Staffordshire. It focuses on nature conservation, heritage protection, and community engagement, enhancing the local environment for wildlife and people.

Name: Tandle Hill Country Park (Oldham)
Description: Tandle Hill Country Park in Oldham covers 110 acres of woodland and grassland, offering panoramic views across the Manchester Plain and Pennine Hills. The park features a children's play area, cafe, and picnic spots.

Name: Tardebigge Reservoir (SSSI)
Description: Tardebigge Reservoir is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) in Worcestershire, England. It serves as a feeder reservoir for the Worcester and Birmingham Canal, supporting diverse wetland habitats and birdlife. The area offers opportunities for walking and wildlife observation.

Name: Target Heath
Description: Target Heath is a small lowland heathland in the Brecks, bordering Norfolk and Suffolk. It supports rare plants and insects thriving in acidic conditions. This reserve is one of the UK's rarest habitats and features various paths.

Name: Tarr Steps (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Tarr Steps features an ancient clapper bridge crossing the River Barle, set within Exmoor's Tarr Steps woodland. The National Trust cares for surrounding ancient woodlands and farmland, offering diverse wildlife habitats and scenic riverside walks.

Name: Tarras Valley Nature Reserve (Langholm Initiative)
Description: The Tarras Valley Nature Reserve spans 10,500 acres in the South of Scotland, near Langholm. This community-owned reserve focuses on ecological restoration and rewilding initiatives, benefiting both wildlife and local people. It was acquired by the community in 2021.

Name: Tas Valley Woods
Description: Tas Valley Woods comprises a mosaic of woodlands, meadows, and wetlands situated along the River Tas in Norfolk. It provides habitat for diverse wildlife, including otters, kingfishers, and various bird species, with public walking trails available for visitors.

Name: Tasker’s Meadow
Description: Tasker's Meadow is a traditionally managed meadow adjacent to the River Leam, supporting over 80 species of wildflowers and grasses. It provides a habitat for rare orchids and is maintained through regular grazing.

Name: Tattershall Castle (Fenland Interface)
Description: Tattershall Castle is a significant red-brick castle built for Ralph Cromwell in the 15th century. It stands in Lincolnshire, featuring a tower with multiple floors and grand fireplaces. Visitors can explore its architecture and history, set within grounds near the Fenland.

Name: Teal Lough NNR (Lowland Raised Bog)
Description: Teal Lough NNR is a lowland raised bog in County Londonderry, Northern Ireland. It is valued for its unique biodiversity and significant peatland features, representing a crucial conservation area for this rare habitat.

Name: Tealham & Tadham Moors
Description: Tealham & Tadham Moors is a Somerset Wildlife Trust nature reserve. This large area of lowland wet grassland, ditches and rhynes within the Somerset Levels and Moors supports diverse plant and insect life. The site holds nationally important populations of wetland birds, with traditional grazing maintaining valuable habitats.

Name: Tealham and Tadham Moors (Peatland Buffer)
Description: Tealham and Tadham Moors is a Site of Special Scientific Interest within the Avalon Marshes. Adjacent to Honeygar Farm, it forms a crucial peatland buffer zone, supporting diverse wetland habitats and species. The area is part of a significant restoration project by Somerset Wildlife Trust.

Name: Teän (Biodiversity Archipelago Node)
Description: Teän is a low-lying, uninhabited island in the Isles of Scilly, managed by the Isles of Scilly Wildlife Trust. It supports a nationally important breeding colony of Lesser Black-backed Gulls and holds SSSI designation. The island is leased from the Duchy of Cornwall.

Name: Tees-Swale: Naturally Connected (River Link)
Description: Tees-Swale: Naturally Connected is a landscape initiative in the North Pennines AONB. It focuses on restoring and connecting habitats, including peatlands and river systems across the Tees and Swale catchments, enhancing biodiversity and water quality.

Name: Teifi Marshes (Wetland & Visitor Hub)
Description: Teifi Marshes Nature Reserve, near Cardigan, is the flagship site of the Wildlife Trust of South and West Wales. It provides access to diverse wetlands, reedbeds, and woodlands along the River Teifi. Visitors explore walking trails and spot otters, kingfishers, and rare butterflies.

Name: Teigngrace Meadow
Description: Teigngrace Meadow is a wet grassland nature reserve beside the River Teign, near Newton Abbot, Devon. It features fen, rush pasture and swamp, supporting water vole, otter, and various insects. Managed for biodiversity.

Name: Teignmouth & Shaldon Coast
Description: The Teignmouth & Shaldon Coast in South Devon offers coastal walks with views of the Teign Estuary and historic ports. Diverse habitats include saltmarsh and cliffs. Visitors can explore local beaches and learn about the area's natural history.

Name: Temple Balsall
Description: Temple Balsall is a small nature reserve in the Warwickshire countryside, encompassing a hay meadow and woodland. It supports a diverse range of wildflowers, including common spotted orchid, and provides habitat for various insects and birds. The meadow is managed with a traditional hay cut to encourage biodiversity, maintaining its ecological value.

Name: Temple Grafton (Limestone Grassland)
Description: Temple Grafton is a small, species-rich limestone grassland reserve near Alcester, Warwickshire. It supports diverse wildflowers including common twayblade, lady's bedstraw, and orchids. The site is managed for biodiversity, providing habitat for butterflies and grasshoppers.

Name: Ten Acre Wood and Meadows
Description: Ten Acre Wood and Meadows is an urban nature reserve in Bromley. It features wildflower meadows, ancient woodland, and hedgerows, supporting diverse birds and invertebrates. This vital green space offers habitats for local wildlife and serves the community.

Name: Tentsmuir Point NNR (Sand Dunes)
Description: Tentsmuir Point NNR, on the North East Fife coast, features sand dunes, woodlands, and intertidal habitats. This dynamic landscape is home to seals, migratory birds, and diverse plant life. Visitors can enjoy walks and observe wildlife.

Name: Terrace Meadows SNCI (Sherborne)
Description: Terrace Meadows SNCI, a traditional hay meadow in Sherborne, is managed to protect and enhance its rich biodiversity. This Site of Nature Conservation Interest provides important habitat for wildflowers, grasses, and invertebrates. It offers public open space for local residents.

Name: Testwood Lakes
Description: Testwood Lakes is a nature reserve near Totton, Hampshire, created from former gravel pits. It features diverse habitats including lakes, woodlands, and grasslands, supporting a variety of bird species and invertebrates.

Name: Testwood Lakes Nature Reserve
Description: Testwood Lakes Nature Reserve is a significant area for wildlife and people, managed in partnership with Hampshire & Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust. It features two large lakes, woodlands, and meadows, providing diverse habitats and walking trails for visitors.

Name: Tetney Blow Wells
Description: Tetney Blow Wells is a unique geological site near Grimsby, Lincolnshire, known for its freshwater springs. These springs emerge through chalk, creating distinctive ponds and reedbeds. The reserve supports a variety of aquatic invertebrates and plant species. It forms part of the Humber Estuary SSSI and Ramsar site, crucial for its ecological value.

Name: Teversal Pastures
Description: Teversal Pastures is a species-rich grassland site in Nottinghamshire, featuring diverse meadows, scrub, and woodland. It supports a variety of wildflowers, grasses, insects, and birds. The site is managed for conservation and public enjoyment.

Name: Teversal Pastures (SSSI Grassland)
Description: Teversal Pastures is a valuable species-rich neutral grassland SSSI on a south-facing magnesian limestone slope. Managed under a Higher Level Stewardship scheme, grazing has been reintroduced. The site supports wildflowers such as cowslip and common spotted orchid, attracting butterflies including dingy skipper.

Name: Teversal Trail & Pleasley Viaduct
Description: Teversal Trail and Pleasley Viaduct is a 50-hectare wildlife haven. This green corridor connects Nottinghamshire and Derbyshire green spaces, forming part of a 100-mile network of trails on a disused railway line. It is popular with walkers, cyclists, and horse riders.

Name: Tewin Orchard and Hopkyns Wood
Description: Tewin Orchard is an ancient Hertfordshire orchard, established around 1880, featuring over 100 fruit trees, including rare varieties. Adjacent Hopkyns Wood supports diverse flora and fauna. This site is a valuable habitat for dormice and various bird species.

Name: Tewinbury
Description: Tewinbury Nature Reserve, managed by Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust, encompasses diverse habitats including ancient woodland, grassland, and wetlands. It supports badgers, muntjac deer, and numerous bird species. Visitors can explore walking trails.

Name: Tewinbury (River Mimram SSSI)
Description: Tewinbury nature reserve spans 12 hectares along the River Mimram, a rare chalk stream. It features woodlands, wildflower meadows, and fen habitats, supporting diverse wildlife. The site is a Site of Special Scientific Interest, managed for conservation.

Name: Thames Barrier Park (Wildflower Areas)
Description: Thames Barrier Park in East London offers landscaped gardens, a rose garden, and wildflower areas providing important urban habitats. It features fountains, a children's play area, and views of the iconic Thames Barrier.

Name: Thames Chase Community Forest
Description: Thames Chase Community Forest spans east London and south Essex. This community forest features woodlands, parks, and open spaces, offering a range of recreational activities. The forest centre provides information, a shop, and a café.

Name: Thames Road Wetland
Description: Thames Road Wetland is a constructed wetland in Crayford, Bexley, filtering surface water before it enters the River Cray. Managed by Thames21, it improves water quality and provides vital habitat for local wildlife. The site helps mitigate pollution impacts on the river system.

Name: Thameside Nature Discovery Park
Description: Thameside Nature Discovery Park, a former landfill site in Thurrock, is a wildlife haven offering a 3-mile sea wall walk with Thames Estuary views. Its diverse habitats support various birds and insects. The park includes a visitor centre and café.

Name: Thatcham Reedbeds
Description: Thatcham Reedbeds is a Berkshire nature reserve featuring extensive reedbeds, fen, wet grassland, and open water. It hosts diverse bird species, including bitterns, marsh harriers, and Cetti's warblers, plus various invertebrates and plants.

Name: The Argory (River Blackwater)
Description: The Argory is a neo-classical house and 320-acre wooded riverside estate in County Armagh, Northern Ireland. It features an original gas-lighting system, stable yard, and a scenic garden overlooking the River Blackwater.

Name: The Avenue Country Park (Wingerworth)
Description: The Avenue Country Park in Wingerworth is a former coking works transformed into a diverse wildlife haven. It features ponds, grasslands, woodlands, and a river corridor, supporting varied species. The park provides walking trails and green space for nature observation and public enjoyment.

Name: The Avenue Washlands
Description: The Avenue Washlands is a new nature reserve near Chesterfield, featuring extensive wetlands and grasslands on a former opencast coal mine. It supports diverse wildlife, including various bird species and amphibians. Part of the Trans Pennine Trail, it provides public access for walks and nature observation.

Name: The Carbon Landscape (Heritage Fund Project)
Description: The Carbon Landscape is a partnership programme restoring and reconnecting wetlands, mosslands, and woodlands across Wigan, Salford, and Warrington. It improves biodiversity, captures carbon, and connects communities with nature in former industrial landscapes across 43 square miles.

Name: The Carrifran Wildwood (Rewilding Gold Standard)
Description: The Carrifran Wildwood is a rewilding project in the Southern Uplands of Scotland, managed by Borders Forest Trust. Since 2000, over 600,000 native trees have been planted, restoring woodland and associated habitats. The site focuses on ecological restoration and biodiversity.

Name: The Combe
Description: The Combe is a sloping ancient oak woodland near Whitchurch, North Shropshire, managed by Shropshire Wildlife Trust. It features numerous streams, ponds, and is a significant site for ancient woodland plants, providing an ideal habitat for dormice.

Name: The Compound (Urban Wild)
Description: The Compound (Urban Wild) is an urban nature reserve in Sheffield, managed by Sheffield & Rotherham Wildlife Trust. This transformed industrial site provides a vital biodiverse habitat for wildlife and a green space for community engagement and education.

Name: The Crumbles (Eastbourne Coastal)
Description: The Crumbles, near Eastbourne, is a unique shingle beach and vegetated ridge formed by coastal processes. Located in East Sussex, it supports a distinctive range of coastal plants and invertebrates, part of the Eastbourne Coastal Local Nature Reserve.

Name: The Dark Peak (Moors & Bogs)
Description: The Dark Peak is a rugged moorland landscape within the Peak District, managed by the National Trust. It features extensive peat bogs and heather, vital for carbon storage and flood management. Conservation efforts focus on restoring these habitats, supporting wildlife like mountain hares.

Name: The Deneway
Description: The Deneway is a nature reserve located in Brighton and Hove, East Sussex. It comprises woodland, chalk grassland, and a pond, supporting diverse local wildlife. Managed by Sussex Wildlife Trust, it offers a tranquil green space.

Name: The Denny (Severn Estuary Island)
Description: The Denny is a small, uninhabited island in the Severn Estuary, near Chepstow. Composed of Old Red Sandstone, it provides an important roosting site for various bird species, particularly gulls and waders. Designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest.

Name: The Devil's Spittleful (Heathland Core)
