Description: The Devil's Spittleful is a remnant heathland core near Kidderminster, Worcestershire. It encompasses diverse habitats including dry heath, acid grassland, and woodland edges. This reserve supports reptiles, rare insects, and ground-nesting birds. Public footpaths provide access.

Name: The Doward
Description: The Doward features ancient woodlands, grassland, and limestone rock faces near Symonds Yat in Herefordshire. It is home to diverse wildlife, including horseshoe bats and dormice. The reserve provides crucial habitats for rare plants and insects, alongside panoramic views of the River Wye.

Name: The Farne Islands NNR
Description: The Farne Islands NNR, off the Northumberland coast, hosts tens of thousands of seabirds, including puffins, and a large grey seal colony. Visitors take boat trips to observe wildlife and the historic lighthouse. Managed by the National Trust.

Name: The Fine Foundation Wild Chesil Centre
Description: The Fine Foundation Wild Chesil Centre sits on Chesil Beach causeway, near Portland, Dorset. It offers insights into the Fleet Lagoon and Chesil Beach, a unique 18-mile shingle barrier. The centre provides educational exhibits on local wildlife, geology, and coastal processes.

Name: The Flit
Description: The Flit is a woodland and wetland reserve in Herefordshire, featuring ancient oak and alder woods, ponds, and streams. It supports diverse wildlife including otters, dormice, and various bird species. The reserve offers walking trails.

Name: The Flow Country (UNESCO Site Hub)
Description: Forsinard Flows, a major RSPB reserve in Caithness and Sutherland, forms part of the Flow Country UNESCO candidate site. This vast blanket bog is a vital European wilderness, supporting unique wildlife like waders. Its peatlands store significant carbon.

Name: The Garrison (St Mary's 30x30 Gateway)
Description: The Garrison on St Mary's, Isles of Scilly, is a 20-hectare site protecting coastal heathland and maritime grassland. It supports rare plants, diverse birds including shags, and historic military defences. Walking trails offer panoramic views.

Name: The Great Canal Orchard (Wolverhampton Hub)
Description: The Great Canal Orchard in Wolverhampton is a community green space managed by the Canal & River Trust. It features various fruit trees, providing fresh produce for local communities and hosting events. The site is situated along the canal network.

Name: The Great Fen
Description: The Great Fen is a vast landscape-scale conservation project in Cambridgeshire, connecting Woodwalton and Holme Fens. It aims to restore 3,700 hectares of wetland habitat, enhancing biodiversity and creating new spaces for wildlife. The site offers trails and hides for visitors.

Name: The Great North Bog (92% of England's Upland Peat)
Description: The Great North Bog protects 92% of England's upland peat, spanning 7000 sq km across Northern England. This initiative restores degraded peatlands, vital for carbon storage, biodiversity, and water quality.

Name: The Great North Bog (Cumbria/Lancashire Link)
Description: The Great North Bog is a vast peatland restoration initiative covering 95% of England's upland peat. This section specifically details work within Cumbria and Lancashire, involving multiple protected landscapes. The North Pennines AONB Partnership leads efforts to restore and protect vital peatland habitats.

Name: The Green Park (Wildflower Meadows)
Description: The Green Park covers 40 acres of mature grassland and trees in central London. Located between Buckingham Palace and Piccadilly, it provides a tranquil, open space popular for relaxation. Part of the Royal Parks, it is a significant green lung for the city.

Name: The Growing Space (Wincanton Community Wildlife)
Description: The Growing Space is a community garden in Wincanton, Somerset, offering a welcoming environment for local people. It provides opportunities to learn gardening skills, connect with nature, and improve wellbeing. Produce is utilised by the Balsam Centre cafe and distributed within the local community.

Name: The Gwent Levels (Resilient Network)
Description: The Gwent Levels is an internationally important wetland in Wales. Gwent Wildlife Trust works to create a resilient network by restoring ponds and re-connecting vital habitats, enhancing biodiversity and increasing climate change resilience.

Name: The Hollies (Ancient Holly Trees)
Description: The Hollies is a small, semi-natural woodland reserve near Pulverbatch, Shropshire, dominated by ancient holly trees, some over 200 years old. It features oak, birch, and rowan, alongside diverse ground flora, managed for biodiversity.

Name: The Hook (Lady Bay)
Description: The Hook Local Nature Reserve, in Lady Bay, West Bridgford, Nottinghamshire, is a strip of land between the Grantham Canal and River Trent. It features grassland, scrub, and mature trees, supporting diverse wildlife and offering accessible paths.

Name: The Hudnalls (Atlantic Rainforest Fragment)
Description: The Hudnalls is an ancient woodland reserve within the Wye Valley Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty, Gloucestershire, representing a vital fragment of the Atlantic rainforest. It is globally significant for its rich lichen, moss, and fern communities, supporting diverse woodland birds and wildlife. The site's varied terrain slopes towards the River Wye.

Name: The Knapp & Papermill (Rainforest Stream)
Description: A beautiful and varied reserve in the Leigh Valley with abundant wildlife. Ancient woodland, traditional orchards, unimproved meadows, a rainforest stream and the River Teme. Woodland birds, otters, kingfishers, and a rich invertebrate fauna thrive here.

Name: The Larches
Description: The Larches is a small deciduous woodland in Sevenoaks, Kent. It features mature oaks and sweet chestnut, with a dense understorey of hazel and hornbeam. The reserve supports ancient woodland flora like bluebells and wood anemone. Its mosaic of habitats provides a home for various bird species.

Name: The Lines Way (Urban Greenway)
Description: The Lines Way is an urban greenway in West Yorkshire, following a disused railway line between Garforth, Allerton Bywater, and Fairburn Ings. Managed by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust, it provides a vital wildlife corridor with diverse habitats.

Name: The Lizard Wildlife Watchpoint
Description: The Lizard Wildlife Watchpoint offers panoramic views of the coast and sea from mainland Britain's southernmost tip. It is a prime location for observing marine life and diverse bird species.

Name: The Lofts
Description: Lofts, managed by Essex Wildlife Trust, is a nature reserve in Essex. It features ancient woodland, unimproved grassland, and a pond, supporting diverse wildlife including dormice, great crested newts, and various woodland birds.

Name: The Loons and Loch of Banks (Orkney)
Description: The Loons and Loch of Banks is a significant wetland on the Orkney mainland. It features wet grassland, reedbeds, and open water, providing habitat for breeding waders and wintering ducks and geese. It is the largest remaining wetland of its type in Orkney.

Name: The Marston Hill Reserve
Description: Marston Hill Reserve features ancient woodland, unimproved grassland, and wetland areas. The woodland contains oak, ash, and hazel. Species-rich grassland supports green-winged orchid and cowslip. Wetland areas host ragged robin and marsh marigold.

Name: The Mens
Description: The Mens is an ancient woodland nature reserve in West Sussex. It features historic beech trees, clearings and ancient wood banks. Managed by Sussex Wildlife Trust, it is a site of scientific interest, providing habitat for diverse wildlife.

Name: The Mens (Large Ancient Woodland)
Description: The Mens is a large ancient woodland in West Sussex, a Site of Special Scientific Interest. It hosts diverse fungi, lichens, and invertebrates due to its undisturbed nature. Much of the woodland remains unmanaged, fostering a natural habitat.

Name: The Mere (Ellesmere)
Description: The Mere (Ellesmere) is a glacial lake in Shropshire, forming part of the Shropshire Meres and Mosses Ramsar site. It comprises open water, reedbeds, and woodland habitats, supporting diverse wildlife. The site is popular for walks and wildlife observation.

Name: The Mersey Forest
Description: The Mersey Forest is a large-scale environmental regeneration initiative covering Merseyside and North Cheshire. It involves planting new woodlands and managing existing green spaces to improve the local environment for communities and wildlife.

Name: The Mill Pond (South Warks Wetland)
Description: The Mill Pond is a small wetland reserve located near the River Avon, south of Warwick. It features sedge, rush, and reed communities within a wet grassland setting. The site provides a habitat for breeding birds, including little ringed plover and gadwall, and is home to various dragonflies and damselflies.

Name: The Naze
Description: The Naze, a coastal Essex site, is a Site of Special Scientific Interest due to its geology, revealing London Clay and Crag formations where fossils are found. It is important for migrating birds and features varied habitats including scrub and beach.

Name: The Naze (Walton-on-the-Naze)
Description: The Naze in Walton-on-the-Naze, Essex, is a significant geological and wildlife site. Its eroding cliffs, sandy beaches, and mudflats support diverse marine and bird life. Essex Wildlife Trust manages the area for conservation.

Name: The North Pennines National Landscape (Peatland Heart)
Description: The North Pennines National Landscape is home to 20% of England's blanket bog. Its Peatland Programme, celebrating 20 years, restores degraded peatlands across the region. This internationally recognised conservation work has been ongoing for 30 years.

Name: The Parks (Dulas Brook)
Description: The Parks encompasses two fields alongside the Dulas Brook. The site is managed for diverse habitats, including wildflower meadows, wet grassland, and scrub, supporting various wildlife species. Public access is available for visitors to enjoy.

Name: The Pennings (Eye)
Description: The Pennings is a wet grassland site bordering the River Dove in Suffolk. Historically managed as hay meadow, it features reedbed, fen, and wet woodland. The site supports rich plant and invertebrate life.

Name: The Pightle
Description: The Pightle is a small community-owned nature reserve near the River Wensum in Norfolk. This wildflower meadow is home to rare plant species including great burnet, meadow rue, and saw-wort.

Name: The Pools
Description: The Pools is a wetland nature reserve in Norfolk, transformed from a former sand and gravel quarry. This site features shallow pools, extensive reedbeds, and areas of willow scrub, supporting diverse bird species, invertebrates, and native flora. Boardwalks and hides are present.

Name: The Regent's Park (Wildlife Garden & West Pond)
Description: Regent's Park hosts a dedicated Wildlife Garden, fostering diverse flora and fauna within London. The park also features the West Pond, an important habitat for aquatic life and birds, contributing to urban biodiversity. It serves as a green sanctuary.

Name: The Riddy (River Ivel)
Description: The Riddy nature reserve in Bedfordshire protects a section of the River Ivel floodplain. It features wet grassland, reedbed, and wet woodland habitats, supporting diverse wildlife including otters, water voles, and various bird species. Public access is available.

Name: The Rifle Range (Heathland Buffer)
Description: The Rifle Range, a nature reserve in Worcestershire, buffers Hartlebury Common SSSI. Once a military site, it now hosts heathland and acid grassland, supporting common lizards, green tiger beetles, and heathland plants. Management includes grazing and scrub removal.

Name: The Roaches (Blanket Bog Restoration)
Description: The Roaches is a prominent gritstone ridge in the Peak District, featuring expansive blanket bog. Staffordshire Wildlife Trust actively restores degraded peatland here, enhancing biodiversity and improving carbon storage. It provides vital habitats for upland birds and plants.

Name: The Roman Road & Fleam Dyke (Chalk Grassland)
Description: The Roman Road and Fleam Dyke are two Scheduled Monuments in Cambridgeshire, spanning over 20km. These sites represent significant chalk grassland habitats. The Friends of the Roman Road and Fleam Dyke conserve, protect, and enhance their character, beauty, and scientific interest.

Name: The Rough
Description: The Rough is a nature reserve in Devon, featuring ancient woodland with mature oak, ash, and hazel trees. A small stream runs through the site, supporting diverse flora and fauna, including various bird species. The reserve is open to visitors.

Name: The Seek (South Witham)
Description: The Seek is a 2.5-hectare nature reserve located on the Lincolnshire/Leicestershire border, acquired by LRWT in 1999. It comprises mature woodland with oak, ash, and hazel, and a hay meadow. The site supports diverse flora including green-winged orchids, and wildlife such as buzzards and badgers.

Name: The Sill: National Landscape Discovery Centre
Description: The Sill is an award-winning visitor centre and youth hostel, providing a gateway to Northumberland National Park and Hadrian's Wall. It offers exhibitions, learning spaces, and a cafe, promoting understanding and enjoyment of the landscape.

Name: The Stepping Stones Project (Shropshire Hills)
Description: The Stepping Stones Project works to restore habitats and reintroduce species like water voles in Carding Mill Valley, Shropshire. It is a National Trust collaboration with Shropshire Wildlife Trust and the AONB Partnership, focusing on river health and creating connected wildlife sites.

Name: The Stiperstones (Upland Heathland Core)
Description: The Stiperstones is a spectacular landscape of heather-clad moorland and jagged quartzite ridges in south Shropshire. It is home to rare plants like cloudberry and sundew, and birds such as red grouse. A Site of Special Scientific Interest and National Nature Reserve.

Name: The Swale (National Nature Reserve - managed sections)
Description: The South Swale National Nature Reserve, managed by Kent Wildlife Trust, protects extensive coastal habitats including saltmarsh, mudflats, and shingle ridges. Located between Faversham and Whitstable in Kent, it is a vital site for numerous overwintering and migrating bird species, forming part of a wider Special Protection Area.

Name: The Vyne Estate
Description: The Vyne Estate, a former Tudor powerhouse in Hampshire, features a historic chapel, extensive gardens, and a tranquil lake. Its wider estate includes wetlands and woodlands, supporting diverse wildlife. Seasonal events and family activities are regularly hosted.

Name: The Warren
Description: The Warren is a nature reserve in south east London, managed by London Wildlife Trust. It protects ancient semi-natural woodland, home to mature oak and hazel, with diverse ground flora including bluebells and wood anemones.

Name: The Wash National Nature Reserve (England's Largest)
Description: The Wash National Nature Reserve is England's largest, covering intertidal sand, mudflats, and saltmarsh. It is a vital feeding ground for hundreds of thousands of birds, supporting important populations and England's largest seal haul-out.

Name: The White Cliffs of Dover
Description: Iconic landmark in Kent offering dramatic views across the English Channel. The site features rare wildflowers and butterflies, with walking trails along the cliff tops. It is managed for conservation and public access.

Name: The White Rocks
Description: White Rocks is a species-rich woodland and grassland nature reserve near Woolhope, Herefordshire. The lime-rich soils support diverse flora, including orchids and wildflowers. Mature trees and open glades provide habitats for various insects and birds.

Name: The Wig
Description: The Wig is a small East Norfolk nature reserve, featuring extensive rushy pasture that supports various wetland plants. Dykes provide vital habitats for dragonfly larvae. Formerly grazing marshes, the site is now managed solely for wildlife.

Name: The Wilderness
Description: The Wilderness is a nature reserve near Ellesmere in north Shropshire, featuring wetland and alder woodland. A small stream flows through the site. It supports diverse wildlife, including willow tit and kingfisher, and various plants. Shropshire Wildlife Trust manages the reserve for conservation.

Name: The Wilderness (Urban Nature Park)
Description: The Wilderness is a small urban nature reserve within Milford Haven, Pembrokeshire, Wales. It features woodland, grassland, and wetland habitats, supporting diverse wildlife, including otters and kingfishers. Visitors can explore a network of paths.

Name: The Wood (Ancient Radnorshire Wood)
Description: The Wood is a small, ancient woodland covering 2.4 hectares near Presteigne. It features a diverse tree population, including sessile oak, ash, wych elm, and field maple. The reserve supports a rich ground flora, with bluebells, wood anemones, and early purple orchids. The site is part of the Radnorshire Wildlife Trust’s network of nature reserves.

Name: The Workhouse, Southwell (Heritage Orchard & Meadow)
Description: The Workhouse, Southwell, is a preserved Victorian workhouse built in 1824. It offers insight into 19th-century poverty and social welfare history through original architecture. A heritage orchard and meadow showcase traditional local flora and fruit varieties.

Name: The Wou (Border Peat Discovery Site)
Description: The Wou, a 20-hectare Border Peat Discovery Site within Northumberland National Park, undergoes extensive peatland restoration. This site is part of a wider partnership project led by the Northumberland National Park Authority, working with the Border Uplands Peat Partnership to restore vital peatlands in the Border Uplands.

Name: The Wou (Border Peat Recovery)
Description: The Wou, a 70-hectare degraded peatland in Northumberland's Border Mires SSSI, undergoes restoration by Northumberland Wildlife Trust. They block drains and rewet peat, promoting moss recovery. This enhances carbon storage and wildlife habitat.

Name: The Wrekin & The Ercall (Ancient Woodland)
Description: The Ercall is an ancient woodland forming part of the Wrekin Forest, known for its significant geological features dating back 550 million years. The site supports diverse wildlife, including various bird species within its coppiced oak, birch, and hazel habitats.

Name: Thelnetham Fen
Description: Thelnetham Fen is a Suffolk Wildlife Trust nature reserve featuring fenland, wet grassland, scrub, and woodland habitats. It supports rare plants, invertebrates, and birds like snipe and lapwing, contributing to local biodiversity.

Name: Thetford Warren
Description: Thetford Warren is a Site of Special Scientific Interest in Breckland, Norfolk. It features typical Breckland grassland on sandy soils, supporting rare plants and invertebrates. The reserve also contains the ruins of a medieval warren lodge, a Scheduled Monument built in the early 15th century.

Name: Thirlmere (Managed Forest & Catchment)
Description: Thirlmere is an active working forest in the Lake District National Park. It serves as a vital catchment for United Utilities, providing water and hydro-electric power. The area offers various footpaths and cycle trails for recreation while focusing on conservation and wildlife.

Name: Thompson Common
Description: Thompson Common is a 154-hectare Norfolk Wildlife Trust reserve in Breckland, featuring internationally important pingo ponds, a unique ice-age landscape. It supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare aquatic invertebrates and Great Crested Newts.

Name: Thompson Common (Glacial Pingo Pools)
Description: Thompson Common, a Norfolk SSSI near Watton, features over 400 glacial pingo ponds, a unique landscape from the last ice age. It supports diverse wildlife, including rare plants and invertebrates. The site is managed for conservation.

Name: Thorley Wash
Description: Thorley Wash is a diverse wetland site next to the River Stort in East Hertfordshire. It features wet grassland, reedbed and supports water vole, otter, and various birds. Rare-breed cattle maintain valuable habitats.

Name: Thorley Wash (Stort Valley)
Description: Thorley Wash is a fenland nature reserve in the Stort Valley, featuring wet grassland, marsh, reedbeds, and open water. This Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) supports diverse plants, insects, and birds. Key species include dragonflies, damselflies, water voles, and various marshland birds, highlighting its importance for fenland habitats.

Name: Thornbury Park
Description: Thornbury Park hosts a TCV Green Gym every Tuesday morning. Volunteers participate in practical conservation tasks, improving the local environment. It offers opportunities to learn new skills and meet people.

Name: Thorndon
Description: Thorndon encompasses ancient woodland, open parkland, and extensive conifer plantations in Essex. It supports diverse wildlife, including deer and various bird species. Visitors can explore a network of trails and engage with nature.

Name: Thorndon Nature Discovery Centre
Description: Thorndon Nature Discovery Centre, located within Thorndon Park North in Essex, provides public access to ancient woodland, heathland, and open parkland habitats. The centre features a Gruffalo Trail, a gift shop, and a café. It supports diverse wildlife and offers opportunities for walking and birdwatching.

Name: Thorney Island (Coastal Habitats)
Description: Thorney Island, a Site of Special Scientific Interest in Chichester Harbour, features extensive mudflats, saltmarshes, and shingle beaches. These support migratory birds and marine life. Public access is limited to a coastal path. The site is managed to conserve its unique ecosystems.

Name: Thornham Marshes
Description: Thornham Marshes, part of Holme Dunes National Nature Reserve, lies near Holme-next-the-Sea in Norfolk. This coastal area features extensive saltmarsh, intertidal mudflats, and freshwater pools, providing a crucial habitat for various bird species, especially during migration.

Name: Thornthwaite Forest (Bassenthwaite)
Description: Thornthwaite Forest, near Keswick, offers walking and cycling trails with views of Bassenthwaite Lake and surrounding fells within the Lake District National Park. Managed by Forestry England, it provides routes for various abilities and supports a diverse range of local wildlife.

Name: Thornton Glen
Description: Thornton Glen is a small woodland reserve in East Lothian, east of Innerwick. Owned by the Scottish Wildlife Trust, it features a tributary of the Thornton Burn with a small waterfall. Diverse plant life, including ferns, mosses, and ancient woodland indicators, thrives.

Name: Thornton Wood
Description: Thornton Wood is a small, ancient woodland in Thornton Hough, owned by Wirral Wildlife. The site features good ground flora, including Ramsons and Bluebells, and supports a diverse range of birds. It is accessible to the public during organised work parties and specific events.

Name: Thorpe Marshes (Urban Broadland)
Description: Thorpe Marshes is Norfolk Wildlife Trust's only urban reserve, located on the edge of Norwich. It features grazing marsh, fens, open water, and wet woodland. The site supports reed and sedge warblers, marsh harriers, and various dragonfly species.

Name: Thorpe Wood
Description: Thorpe Wood is a maturing woodland in the Tees Valley, planted in 1999 on former arable land. It features over 15 native tree species, a wetland area, and a wildflower meadow. The reserve provides valuable habitats, supporting diverse wildlife including dragonflies, damselflies, and bird species.

Name: Thorpe Wood (Peterborough)
Description: Thorpe Wood is a small area of ancient semi-natural woodland to the west of Peterborough, characterised by mature oak, ash, and field maple. The reserve supports a variety of woodland birds and insects. It is managed by Wildlife BCN.

Name: Thorswood (Limestone Flora Capital)
Description: Thorswood, part of the Weaver Hills, is a limestone grassland reserve in Staffordshire. It features rich flora including orchids and rare plants like rigid brome. The site is managed by Staffordshire Wildlife Trust.

Name: Threave Estate (Nature Recovery)
Description: Threave Estate, near Castle Douglas, focuses on nature recovery across diverse habitats. The site supports significant populations of bats, otters, and ospreys. Conservation efforts involve wetland restoration and native woodland expansion.

Name: Three Groves Wood
Description: Three Groves Wood is an ancient semi-natural woodland in Gloucestershire's Golden Valley. It features mature oak and ash, with wild garlic and bluebells in spring. The reserve supports local wildlife and offers peaceful walks. Management focuses on conserving its biodiversity.

Name: Threecorner Grove
Description: Threecorner Grove is a small woodland nature reserve in Enfield. It is managed to maintain its mature trees and understory, providing a habitat for woodland birds and invertebrates. The site is a valuable urban green space.

Name: Thrunton Wood
Description: Thrunton Wood offers walking and mountain biking trails amidst coniferous forest in Northumberland. Visitors can find challenging routes and enjoy views of the Cheviot Hills and the Northumberland coastline.

Name: Thundry Meadows
Description: Thundry Meadows is a traditional wet grassland, bordered by woodland and the River Wey. It supports a diverse range of plant and animal species, including wading birds and various invertebrates. The site is a valuable habitat for biodiversity conservation in Surrey.

Name: Thundry Meadows (Wealden Heaths NNR)
Description: Thundry Meadows, near Elstead, Surrey, is a wetland nature reserve and part of the Wealden Heaths NNR. Habitats include wet meadow, alder carr, and reedbed. The River Wey flows through the site, supporting water voles, otters, and diverse wetland birds.

Name: Thurlby Fen Slipe (Wetland Corridor)
Description: Thurlby Fen Slipe is a wetland corridor in Lincolnshire, managed by the Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust. It comprises wet grassland, ditches, and reedbeds, forming a crucial link for wildlife between larger fenland areas. The site supports diverse invertebrates, wetland birds, and rare plants.

Name: Thurrock Thameside
Description: Thurrock Thameside Nature Park is a 120-acre former landfill site transformed into a thriving wildlife haven with habitats including grassland, woodland, and a large lake. It offers extensive views of the Thames Estuary and provides walking and cycling trails.

Name: Tiddesley Wood (Ancient Woodland)
Description: Tiddesley Wood is an ancient woodland in Worcestershire, managed by Worcestershire Wildlife Trust. It features diverse habitats including oak and hazel coppice, glades, and a pond. It is known for its spring bluebells and wildflowers, supporting various bird species and invertebrates.

Name: Tile Wood (Daws Heath Corridor)
Description: Tile Wood is an ancient woodland, once part of the Great Forest of Essex, featuring mature oak, hornbeam, and hazel. It provides habitat for woodpeckers and dormice and connects with other woodlands, forming the Daws Heath Corridor, vital for local wildlife.

Name: Tilton Cutting (Geological SSSI)
Description: Tilton Cutting, a Geological SSSI in Leicestershire, exposes Jurassic rocks including the Marlstone Rock and Tilton Formations. Key for understanding East Midlands geology, it is rich in fossils such as ammonites and belemnites. The site also supports significant calcareous grassland flora and various butterflies.

Name: Tilton Railway Cutting
Description: Tilton Railway Cutting is a disused railway line now a nature reserve in Leicestershire. It features species-rich grassland, woodland, and scrub habitats. The site supports diverse flora, including several orchid species, and numerous insect types, particularly butterflies.

Name: Tintagel Coast & Barras Nose
Description: Tintagel Coast and Barras Nose feature a rugged coastline surrounding Tintagel Castle in North Cornwall. This exposed headland supports various seabirds and coastal flora. The South West Coast Path traverses the area, offering expansive Atlantic views amidst a landscape rich in Arthurian legend.

Name: Tiptree Heath
Description: Tiptree Heath is a living relic, the last fragment of a once extensive heathland. Dominated by heather and gorse, it provides habitat for uncommon wildlife. Nationally important for its invertebrates, including rare bees, wasps, and spiders.

Name: Tiptree Heath (Largest Lowland Heath in Essex)
Description: Tiptree Heath is Essex's largest lowland heath, featuring heather, gorse, and acid grassland. It provides habitat for rare plants, insects, and birds, including nightjars and adders. The site is managed to preserve its distinct heathland ecosystem.

Name: Titchmarsh
Description: Titchmarsh Nature Reserve is a complex of former gravel pits and grasslands in Northamptonshire. It is an important inland wetland site, known for breeding and wintering wildfowl and waders. Notable species include Gadwall, Shoveler, and Bitterns. Diverse invertebrate and plant life are supported.

Name: Titchmarsh Nature Reserve (Nene Valley)
Description: Titchmarsh Nature Reserve offers diverse wetland habitats within the Nene Valley, bordering the River Nene. The site includes lakes, reedbeds, meadows, and scrub, supporting many bird species, otters, and invertebrates. Public access is available via trails and bird hides.

Name: Titchwell Marsh (managed interface)
Description: Holme Dunes National Nature Reserve, on Norfolk's north-west coast, features diverse habitats including dunes, saltmarsh, and freshwater pools. It supports many bird species, including migrants, and various insects. This reserve is a key site for coastal conservation.

Name: Titford Pools (Black Country Green Lung)
Description: Titford Pools are large reservoirs in the Black Country, serving the Titford Canal and wider Birmingham New Main Line Canal network. A wildlife haven, the pools offer popular activities like walking, fishing, and boating, providing a vital green space for the local community.

Name: Titley Pool
Description: Titley Pool is a Site of Special Scientific Interest and a designated Ramsar site due to its important wintering bird populations. The pool is fed by the River Arrow and surrounded by ancient woodlands and meadows.

Name: Titley Pool (Glacial Lake)
Description: Titley Pool is a glacial lake in north Herefordshire, supporting diverse aquatic plants and invertebrates. Surrounded by woodland, fen, and grassland, the site is notable for its birdlife and rare plants.

Name: Tocil Wood (Ancient Bluebell Wood)
Description: Tocil Wood is an ancient woodland with a carpet of bluebells in spring. This Warwickshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve provides a habitat for various bird species, including buzzards and tawny owls, and supports diverse flora.

Name: Tollesbury Wick
Description: Tollesbury Wick is a coastal nature reserve in Essex. It encompasses saltmarsh, mudflats, and grazing marsh, supporting diverse bird species and marine life. The reserve is actively managed by Essex Wildlife Trust for biodiversity conservation and coastal defence.

Name: Tollesbury Wick Marshes (Ancient Grazing Marsh)
Description: Tollesbury Wick Marshes is a 240-hectare coastal grazing marsh, saltmarsh, and mudflat reserve in Essex. It supports diverse wildlife, including wintering wildfowl and breeding waders. Management involves cattle grazing and water level maintenance.

Name: Tollymore Forest Park (Riparian)
Description: Tollymore Forest Park, near Newcastle, County Down, is Northern Ireland's first state forest park. Established in 1955, it covers 630 hectares at the Mourne Mountains' base. The Shimna River flows through it, known for its arboretum and historic follies.

Name: Tonford Wood
Description: Tonford Wood is a Kent Wildlife Trust reserve north of Ashford, Kent. This deciduous woodland features oak, ash, and hazel trees, with hornbeam coppice. Diverse ground flora includes dog's mercury and wood anemone. The Great Stour river forms its western boundary.

Name: Top Lodge (Fineshade Visitor Hub)
Description: Top Lodge is the visitor hub for Fineshade Wood. It offers a café, toilets, and accessible paths for walking, cycling, and horse riding. Play areas and Go Ape treetop adventure are also available.

Name: Tophill Low NNR (Water & Wildlife)
Description: Tophill Low NNR, managed by Yorkshire Water near Watton, East Yorkshire, is a Site of Special Scientific Interest. It features diverse wetlands, woodlands, and two reservoirs. This popular reserve offers excellent birdwatching opportunities.

Name: Tortoiseshell Woods
Description: Tortoiseshell Woods is a small, semi-natural ancient woodland. It features mature oak, ash, hazel, and field maple trees. The diverse ground flora includes dog's mercury and yellow archangel, supporting various fungi species.

Name: Totteridge Fields
Description: Totteridge Fields is a tranquil mosaic of traditionally managed wildflower meadows, hedgerows, and mature trees in north London. The site provides a vital habitat for various species, including bats, stag beetles, and migrating birds. Footpaths allow public access.

Name: Totternhoe
Description: Totternhoe nature reserve in Bedfordshire features chalk grassland, ancient woodland, and former limestone quarries. It supports diverse wildlife like rare butterflies and orchids. The site, managed by the Wildlife Trust, offers walking trails with panoramic views.

Name: Totternhoe (Medieval Quarrying)
Description: Totternhoe is a Wildlife Trust nature reserve in Bedfordshire, featuring a mosaic of habitats including chalk grassland, woodland, and medieval quarries. It supports diverse wildflowers, butterflies, and birds.

Name: Town Kelloe Bank
Description: Town Kelloe Bank is a nature reserve in County Durham, featuring grassland, scrub, and trees. It forms a wildlife corridor connecting Joe's Pond and Raisby Hill Grassland. Part of the reserve follows the route of the former Coxhoe railway line.

Name: Townclose Hills (Billy Goat Hill)
Description: Townclose Hills, also known as Billy Goat Hill, is a limestone grassland reserve in the Lower Magnesian Limestone belt. It features woodland, scrub, and grassland habitats. Nationally rare rigid brome grass and moonwort thrive here. Green woodpeckers, buzzards, and various butterflies inhabit the site. Footpaths offer views across the landscape.

Name: Townend (Heritage Orchard)
Description: Townend is a preserved 17th-century farmhouse in the Lake District, once home to the Browne family. Visitors explore its historical rooms, extensive library, and traditional Lakeland garden. The site includes a small heritage orchard and an historic barn, showcasing four centuries of rural life.

Name: Townhead Wood
Description: Townhead Wood is a mixed woodland and grassland reserve on the western shore of Loch Leven. Acquired in 2021, it connects existing protected areas. The site supports red squirrels, great spotted woodpeckers, and various woodland birds, enhancing local biodiversity.

Name: Townsend
Description: Townsend Reserve, on Portland, encompasses maritime grassland and a former limestone quarry. It supports rare plants, including Early Spider Orchid, and nationally important lichen communities. Migratory birds use the site for rest and foraging.

Name: Toys Hill (High Weald Woodland)
Description: Toys Hill is a high wooded ridge in Kent, part of the Wealden Greensand Ridge. It offers panoramic views across the Weald. The site features ancient woodlands and is rich in wildlife, with public footpaths for exploration.

Name: Trasna Island (Curlew Fledgling Success)
Description: Trasna Island, within the Lower Lough Erne Islands SPA, focuses on habitat restoration for breeding waders. LELP volunteers manage the site, which recently celebrated the successful fledging of three Curlew chicks. Work aims to support endangered bird populations.

Name: Tredegar House (Parkland Biodiversity)
Description: Tredegar House in Newport, Wales, features significant parkland biodiversity. The National Trust works to restore diverse habitats including ponds, wetlands, and species-rich meadows, supporting local wildlife.

Name: Tregonetha Downs
Description: Tregonetha Downs is a nature reserve in Cornwall, featuring species-rich grassland habitats. The site supports a diverse range of wildflowers and insects, notably the marsh fritillary butterfly. Active management, including grazing, maintains its biodiversity.

Name: Trelissick Maritime Woods
Description: Trelissick Maritime Woods offers tranquil garden and wooded parkland walks with panoramic views of the Fal estuary. Visitors can explore a variety of trails, from gentle strolls among trees and shrubs to longer hikes along the estuary. The site is located seven miles south of Truro.

Name: Trellwyn Fach (2026 Rainforest Flagship)
Description: Trellwyn Fach is a new 10-hectare reserve near Cenarth, owned by Welsh Wildlife Centre. Funded by Lost Rainforests of Britain and Size of Wales, it aims to restore temperate rainforest by 2026. The project focuses on expanding woodland and connecting ancient woods.

Name: Trench Wood (Feckenham Anchor)
Description: Trench Wood is an ancient woodland and SSSI in Worcestershire, managed by Worcestershire Wildlife Trust. Noted for spring bluebells and diverse fungi, the site supports rare butterflies like the pearl-bordered fritillary through ongoing woodland management practices.

Name: Tresco (Abbey Pool & Great Pool)
Description: Great Pool is a significant freshwater lake on Tresco, Isles of Scilly. It supports a diverse range of bird species, particularly migrating waterfowl. Nearby Abbey Pool also forms part of this important wetland system, contributing to the island's rich biodiversity.

Name: Treswell Wood
Description: Treswell Wood is an ancient woodland in Nottinghamshire, featuring oak standards and hazel coppice. It supports a rich ground flora, including bluebells, and a diverse range of breeding birds. The reserve is one of the county's oldest woodlands.

Name: Treswell Wood (Ancient Ash/Oak/Maple)
Description: Treswell Wood is a large ancient woodland in Nottinghamshire, featuring a rich ground flora with wood anemone, early purple orchid, and bluebell. Managed for both timber production and conservation, it provides diverse habitats and mature ash, oak, and field maple trees.

Name: Treswell Wood (Ancient Bluebell Wood)
Description: Treswell Wood is a 55-hectare ancient woodland in Nottinghamshire, rich in diverse flora and fauna. Notable species include bluebells, lily-of-the-valley, and several bat species. The site features broadleaf trees and conifer plantations, managed for conservation.

Name: Trevone Bay
Description: Trevone Bay is situated on the North Cornwall coast near Padstow, featuring dramatic cliffs, sandy beaches, and numerous coves. Part of the South West Coast Path runs along Trevose Head, offering coastal views of the Atlantic and opportunities to spot seabirds.

Name: Trevose Head (SSSI Grassland)
Description: Trevose Head is a prominent headland near Padstow, Cornwall, designated as a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI). It features coastal grassland, dramatic cliffs, and important seabird breeding sites. Visitors enjoy scenic walks and observe diverse wildlife.

Name: Tricketts Pond
Description: Tricketts Pond Nature Reserve is a small freshwater habitat near Eastwood, Nottinghamshire. The site features a pond, wet woodland, and marshland, supporting diverse wildlife. It is managed to conserve amphibians, dragonflies, and various bird species.

Name: Trillis Banks
Description: Trillis Banks is a nature reserve on the River Severn's steep bank, featuring species-rich grassland, ancient woodland, and hedgerows. Managed by Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust, it protects biodiversity and provides habitats for various flora and fauna.

Name: Trimdon Grange Quarry
Description: Trimdon Grange Quarry is a former limestone quarry now a Durham Wildlife Trust nature reserve near Trimdon. Its varied habitats include grassland, scrub, and woodland, supporting a diversity of plant and animal life. The site features calcicolous grassland and wetland areas, attracting rare invertebrates and birds.

Name: Trimley Marshes
Description: Trimley Marshes is a Suffolk Wildlife Trust nature reserve located on the Stour Estuary. It features freshwater lagoons, reedbeds, and grazing marsh, providing crucial habitats for a diverse range of bird species. The reserve supports overwintering and breeding waterfowl and waders, including avocets and marsh harriers.

Name: Trimley Marshes (Orwell Estuary)
Description: Trimley Marshes is a coastal grazing marsh and reedbed reserve located on the west bank of the Orwell Estuary. It provides a mosaic of habitats supporting diverse bird species, including waders and wildfowl. The site is managed to enhance biodiversity for resident and migratory birds.

Name: Tring Reservoirs
Description: Tring Reservoirs, comprising Startops End, Marsworth, Tringford, and Wilstone, is a significant site for overwintering wildfowl and migrating waders. Located at the head of the Grand Union Canal, the reserve's diverse habitats support water voles and dragonflies. Footpaths offer access for nature observation.

Name: Tring Reservoirs (National Nature Reserve)
Description: Tring Reservoirs, a National Nature Reserve and SSSI in Hertfordshire, supports diverse birdlife. The four reservoirs provide crucial habitats for wintering waterfowl and breeding birds. Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust manages the site, focusing on conservation and habitat preservation.

Name: Trinity Broads
Description: Trinity Broads is a complex of five freshwater bodies located in Norfolk, managed by Norfolk Wildlife Trust. The site is a haven for waterbirds and is surrounded by reedbeds and fen, supporting diverse plant life.

Name: Trinity Broads (Ormesby
Description: The Trinity Broads are a group of freshwater lakes in Norfolk, managed by the Norfolk Wildlife Trust. They form a Ramsar site and Special Area of Conservation, vital for diverse aquatic life and birds. Opportunities for fishing and limited boating exist on these unique waters.

Name: Trosley Country Park
Description: Trosley Country Park is a 170-acre site near Meopham on the North Downs. It offers expansive views and significant chalk grassland slopes. Kent County Council manages the park, while Kent Wildlife Trust maintains a vital chalk grassland area within it.

Name: Troublefield
Description: Troublefield is a 13-hectare wetland reserve south of Uddens in East Dorset, within the Stour Valley catchment. It features reedbed, fen, and wet woodland habitats. The reserve is known for dragonflies, damselflies, and diverse bird species. Access is limited.

Name: Troup Head (Gannet Colony)
Description: Troup Head is Scotland's largest mainland gannet colony, home to over 30,000 gannets during the breeding season. This cliff-top reserve also supports kittiwakes, fulmars, and guillemots. Seals are often visible below the cliffs.

Name: Trowell Marsh (Erewash Valley)
Description: Trowell Marsh Nature Reserve is a small but important wetland site nestled in the Erewash Valley, Nottinghamshire. It features a mosaic of habitats including reedbed, marshland, scrub, and open water, supporting diverse birdlife, amphibians, and insects. The site is managed for conservation, providing a tranquil natural space for wildlife observation.

Name: Trumland (Rousay)
Description: Trumland is an RSPB nature reserve on Rousay, Orkney. It features heathland, wetlands, and moorland habitats. The reserve is home to diverse bird species including hen harriers, red-throated divers, and arctic skuas. Visitors can explore walking trails.

Name: Trumpington Meadows
Description: Trumpington Meadows is a 58-hectare country park and nature reserve south of Cambridge. It features wildflower meadows, woodlands, and riverside habitats along the River Cam. Created as part of a housing development, it provides diverse environments.

Name: Tuckmill Meadows
Description: Tuckmill Meadows is a traditional hay meadow and pasture bordering the River Thame, near Tetsworth. It supports colourful wildflowers like great burnet and meadowsweet. Butterflies such as orange-tip visit the site. BBOWT manages the site for biodiversity.

Name: Tuckmill Meadows (Chalk Stream Fed)
Description: Tuckmill Meadows is a valuable wildflower meadow and wet woodland reserve near High Wycombe. It is fed by the River Wye, a rare chalk stream, supporting diverse plant and insect life, including water voles and various butterfly species.

Name: Tudhoe Mill Wood
Description: Tudhoe Mill Wood is a small, ancient woodland on the steep banks of the River Wear near Spennymoor. It features oak, ash, and elm trees with diverse ground flora including wild garlic and bluebells, supporting various bird species and mammals.

Name: Tummel Shingle Islands
Description: The reserve is an important site for breeding wading birds, especially common sandpipers, oystercatchers and ringed plovers. Its shingle islands are formed by the meandering River Tummel, creating dynamic habitats for diverse wildlife.

Name: Tunman Wood
Description: Tunman Wood is an ancient woodland nature reserve in Lincolnshire, managed by the Lincolnshire Wildlife Trust. It features oak, ash, and hazel trees, with a rich ground flora including dog's mercury and bluebells. It provides a habitat for various wildlife.

Name: Tunstall Forest
Description: Tunstall Forest is a mixed woodland in Suffolk, East of England, offering walking, cycling, and horse riding trails. Part of an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty, it provides diverse habitats for wildlife and is managed sustainably for timber and recreation.

Name: Tupsley Meadows
Description: Tupsley Meadows is a valuable green space in Hereford, comprising species-rich grassland and a small orchard. Managed by Herefordshire Wildlife Trust, it provides a habitat for various plants and insects, supporting local biodiversity within an urban setting.

Name: Turf Hide
Description: Turf Hide is an accessible wildlife viewing facility overlooking the tidal River Crouch. It offers views of saltmarsh, mudflats, and the river, hosting diverse bird species, including wintering waders and wildfowl. It is a key birdwatching spot within the River Crouch marshes.

Name: Turner's Wood (Pelsall)
Description: Turner's Wood is a small nature reserve in Pelsall, Walsall, comprising ancient woodland with mature oak and birch trees. It supports diverse flora and fauna, including bluebells and various bird species. A vital green space, it offers peaceful walks.

Name: Turners Puddle Heath
Description: Turners Puddle Heath is a Dorset Wildlife Trust reserve protecting lowland heath, mire, and bog. It supports scarce wildlife including nightjars, Dartford warblers, sand lizards, and smooth snakes, crucial for biodiversity in Dorset.

Name: Turnford and Cheshunt Pits
Description: Turnford and Cheshunt Pits, a nature reserve in the Lee Valley, features flooded gravel pits. It is an SSSI and SPA, supporting diverse birdlife including overwintering wildfowl and breeding birds. Birdwatching hides and walking paths are available.

Name: Tweed Forum - LIFE WADER Project
Description: The LIFE Wader project, led by Tweed Forum, improves breeding wader habitats in the Scottish Borders and Dumfries & Galloway. Running until May 2026, it aims to reverse the decline of threatened bird species by enhancing their local environments.

Name: Tweed Forum (Catchment Mastery Hub)
Description: Tweed Forum is a charitable trust working to protect and enhance the natural, cultural, and economic heritage of the River Tweed. The Catchment Mastery Hub provides resources and training for sustainable river management.

Name: Tweed Forum (Landscape Recovery)
Description: Tweed Forum is an award-winning charity dedicated to the integrated management of the River Tweed and its catchment area. It undertakes projects such as woodland creation, peatland restoration, and natural flood management to enhance biodiversity and improve the landscape.

Name: Twickenham Rough
Description: Twickenham Rough is a nature reserve in Twickenham, owned by Richmond Council. TCV volunteers undertake coppicing, scrub clearance, and path maintenance to manage the site's biodiversity.

Name: Two Mile Bottom
Description: Two Mile Bottom is a Forestry England site within Thetford Forest, Norfolk. It offers walking, cycling, and horse riding trails amidst woodlands near the Little Ouse River. It serves as a gateway to the wider forest experience.

Name: Two Tree Island
Description: Two Tree Island is a small island managed for wildlife on the Thames Estuary. It features saltmarsh, lagoons, and grazing marsh, important for avocets, lapwings, and diverse invertebrates. Public footpaths and bird hides are present.

Name: Two Tree Island (Leigh-on-Sea)
Description: Two Tree Island is a 270-hectare nature reserve in the Thames Estuary, near Leigh-on-Sea, Essex. It features saltmarsh, grassland, and scrub habitats, supporting diverse bird species including avocets, ringed plovers, and dunlins. The site offers coastal walks.

Name: Tyland Barn (Visitor Centre & Gardens)
Description: Tyland Barn is a Kent Wildlife Trust nature reserve and visitor centre near Maidstone. It features wildlife gardens, a learning centre, and accessible trails. Facilities include a shop, seasonal cafe, and picnic areas. Events and educational programs are offered on site.

Name: Tyle Colliery (Spoil-heap Habitat)
Description: Tyle Colliery is a former spoil tip near Pontarddulais, transformed into a rich wildlife habitat. Grasslands, heathland, and woodland support diverse birds, invertebrates, and reptiles. This site demonstrates a successful transition from industrial land to a biodiverse landscape.

Name: Tywardreath Marsh
Description: Tywardreath Marsh is a rare freshwater marsh behind Par Beach in Cornwall, featuring two ponds, grazed pasture, and reed-fringed ditches. It supports nationally rare fen orchids, snipe, water rail, and kingfishers. Cornwall Wildlife Trust manages the site to maintain its diverse wetland habitats.

Name: Ubley Warren
Description: Ubley Warren is a former lead mine in the Mendip Hills. It features a unique calcicolous grassland habitat, supporting a diverse range of plant and insect species. The reserve is known for its rare wildflowers and mining spoil heaps.

Name: Ubley Warren (The Industrial Scar Ark)
Description: Ubley Warren, within the Mendip Hills AONB, is a post-industrial landscape showing signs of lead mining from Roman times. Limestone grassland and ancient woodland support diverse flora like small scabious, plus invertebrates such as the common blue butterfly. Conservation efforts maintain its unique habitats.

Name: Uffmoor Wood
Description: Uffmoor Wood is an ancient woodland near Hagley, managed by the Woodland Trust for conservation and public access. It features diverse habitats, including broadleaf trees, conifers, and open glades. Visitors can explore a network of walking trails and observe various wildlife, including numerous bird species.

Name: Ufton Fields
Description: Ufton Fields Nature Reserve, a former quarry and railway cutting, is a diverse wildlife haven. It features wildflower-rich grasslands, ancient woodland, scrub, and ponds. The reserve supports over 20 butterfly species and 200 flowering plant species.

Name: Ughill Farm (Wildlife-Friendly Farming Flagship)
Description: Ughill Farm is a working farm within the Sheffield Peak District, spanning 160 hectares. The site prioritises wildlife-friendly farming practices, creating a mosaic of habitats including woodlands, wildflower meadows, and wetlands.

Name: Ullswater Catchment Restoration (Flood Mitigation)
Description: The National Trust manages the Ullswater catchment area, undertaking conservation work and restoring habitats. Efforts include tree planting and rewilding valleys to mitigate flood risk and enhance the natural landscape.

Name: Ulverscroft
Description: Ulverscroft is a secluded ancient woodland in Leicestershire, featuring a diverse array of birds, plants, and butterflies. The reserve is managed for wildlife, creating a mosaic of habitats including ancient woodland, wildflower meadows, and serene ponds.

Name: Umbra (Dunes & Magilligan)
Description: Umbra Nature Reserve is a significant dune system at Magilligan, County Londonderry. It hosts a unique flora, including rare orchids, and important fauna like natterjack toads and diverse bird species. The site holds Ramsar, SAC, and ASSI designations.

Name: Underhill Wood
Description: Underhill Wood is a small ancient woodland near Habberley, Shropshire. It features mixed broadleaf trees, including oak and hazel, supporting diverse wildlife. The reserve provides a habitat for various bird species and wildflowers.

Name: Upcott Cross
Description: Upcott Cross is a small wet woodland nature reserve in Herefordshire. The site features ancient woodland species like sessile oak, birch, and rowan. A stream runs through the reserve, supporting a diverse range of amphibians and invertebrates. This reserve is managed for biodiversity conservation.

Name: Uphill Hill
Description: Uphill Hill is a coastal limestone grassland near Weston-super-Mare with views of the Bristol Channel. It features ancient monuments, diverse flora including orchids, and supports various bird species. The site offers walking trails.

Name: Uppacott Wood
Description: Uppacott Wood is an ancient oak woodland situated on a steep valley side above the River Taw. This nature reserve supports diverse woodland birds and invertebrates. A public footpath crosses the site, offering access through its typical sessile oak habitat.

Name: Upper Conwy Catchment (Afon Eidda Project)
Description: The Upper Conwy Catchment is a landscape of ancient woodland, farmland, and blanket bog in the Afon Conwy valley. It supports diverse wildlife, including red squirrels and otters. The Afon Eidda Project focuses on restoring nature and improving water quality within the sub-catchment area.

Name: Upper Derwent Catchment (Moors for the Future)
Description: Located within the Peak District National Park, the Upper Derwent Catchment is a vital upland area of peatlands and reservoirs. Moors for the Future Partnership undertakes extensive restoration, re-wetting degraded blanket bog habitats to enhance biodiversity and capture carbon effectively.

Name: Upper Derwent Valley (NT Woodland & Peat)
Description: The National Trust cares for the Upper Derwent Valley, an area of picturesque woodlands and extensive peat moorlands. It encompasses the iconic Derwent, Howden, and Ladybower reservoirs, offering walking, cycling, and diverse wildlife discovery.

Name: Upper Dunsforth Carr
Description: Upper Dunsforth Carr Nature Reserve is a wetland site on the River Ure in North Yorkshire. It features wet woodland, fen, and reedbed, supporting diverse birds, dragonflies, and butterflies. The reserve is managed to enhance its rich biodiversity.

Name: Upper Minety (Braydon Expansion)
Description: Upper Minety, part of the Braydon Forest Project, integrates woodland, grassland, and wetland habitats. Managed by Wiltshire Wildlife Trust, the reserve supports diverse wildlife, including various butterflies, dragonflies, and bird species, enhancing local biodiversity.

Name: Upper Nethan Gorge
Description: Upper Nethan Gorge is a semi-ancient woodland site located near Crossford, South Lanarkshire. The reserve features steep, wooded slopes, riverine habitats, and ancient trees. It supports diverse wildlife, including otters and kingfishers, and offers public access via a marked trail.

Name: Upper Park Wood
Description: Upper Park Wood is an ancient woodland in South Yorkshire, managed by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust. It hosts diverse tree species like sessile oak, birch, and rowan, with a rich ground flora of bluebells and wood sorrel. The reserve supports a variety of bird life and woodland invertebrates.

Name: Upper Ray Meadows
Description: Upper Ray Meadows, on the Oxfordshire/Buckinghamshire border near Marsh Gibbon, is a complex of species-rich meadows, pastures, hedgerows, and ditches. It supports wildlife like otters, curlew, and many scarce plants.

Name: Upper Ray Meadows (Floodplain Restoration)
Description: Upper Ray Meadows is a series of traditional floodplain meadows and wetlands in Buckinghamshire. Managed for biodiversity, it supports diverse wildlife, including breeding waders and wildflowers, and aids natural flood management.

Name: Upper Titchfield Haven (Meon Valley)
Description: Upper Titchfield Haven is a Hampshire nature reserve in the Meon Valley. It protects fen, reedbed, and wet grassland habitats. The site supports diverse birdlife, dragonflies, and water voles. Accessible trails allow visitors to explore the area.

Name: Upper Welson Marsh
Description: Upper Welson Marsh is a valuable site for wildflowers and insects, nestled beside the River Wye near Hay-on-Wye. The reserve features wet grassland habitats supporting diverse plant species and a rich invertebrate community. It is managed to conserve its unique biodiversity.

Name: Upper Wharfedale (Dales Partnership)
Description: Upper Wharfedale is a glacial valley in the Yorkshire Dales. The National Trust manages 7,000 acres of upland, featuring hay meadows, limestone pavements, and ancient woodlands. It supports rare plants and wildlife like red squirrels. Conservation work maintains diverse habitats.

Name: Upton Broad and Marshes
Description: Upton Broad and Marshes is a significant wetland nature reserve within the Norfolk Broads. It features a large shallow broad, extensive reedbeds, and fens, supporting rare invertebrates and marshland birds. Management focuses on maintaining diverse habitats.

Name: Upton Fen and Marshes
Description: Upton Fen is a nature reserve near Acle, featuring fen, grazing marsh, and reedbeds. It supports a diverse range of wildlife, including swallowtail butterflies, rare plants, and various birds. Boardwalks provide access for visitors to explore the wetland habitats. The site is managed for biodiversity conservation.

Name: Upton Meadow
Description: Upton Meadow is a 0.8-hectare nature reserve near St Just-in-Roseland, Cornwall, featuring grassland, scrub, and wet woodland alongside the River Fal. Managed by Cornwall Wildlife Trust, it supports otters, dormice, and diverse wildflowers. A circular path allows visitor access.

Name: Upwood Meadows
Description: Upwood Meadows is a Site of Special Scientific Interest near Ramsey, Cambridgeshire. These two traditionally managed fenland meadows feature a rich diversity of wildflowers and grasses, supporting numerous insect species, including butterflies and bees. The site is open to the public for quiet enjoyment.

Name: Upwood Meadows (SSSI)
Description: Upwood Meadows SSSI is an unimproved neutral grassland in Cambridgeshire. Managed by Wildlife BCN, its two species-rich meadows, The Lings and Old Chapel Field, feature green-winged orchid, adder's-tongue fern, oxeye daisy, and yellow rattle.

Name: Uswayford (The High Forest Link)
Description: Uswayford, situated within the Cheviot Hills, is part of Northumberland National Park. It is central to The High Forest Link initiative, a strategic project aiming to enhance woodland connectivity across the landscape. This area supports crucial biodiversity and contributes to regional ecological resilience.

Name: Uxbridge Alderglade
Description: Uxbridge Alderglade is a nature reserve near Harefield, west London. Managed by Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust, it protects ancient woodland and wet alder woodland. The River Colne flows through, creating diverse habitats.

Name: Valley Lakes (Anglesey Wetland Hub)
Description: Valley Lakes, Anglesey, Wales, is a significant RSPB wetland habitat. It features shallow lakes, reedbeds, and wet grasslands, supporting diverse birdlife. It provides breeding grounds and overwintering areas for wildfowl and waders.

Name: Vane Farm (Loch Leven)
Description: Vane Farm sits on the southern shore of Loch Leven, a large freshwater lake in Scotland. It offers panoramic views and diverse habitats including woodlands and reedbeds. The reserve provides accessible trails, hides, and a visitor centre, supporting numerous bird species.

Name: Vann Lake
Description: Vann Lake is a 14.5-hectare Surrey Wildlife Trust reserve in Surrey, England, featuring a tranquil 2.5-hectare lake surrounded by woodland, grassland, mire, and heathland. It supports diverse wildlife, including wildfowl, kingfishers, and rare plants.

Name: Vealand Farm
Description: Vealand Farm, near Clawton in West Devon, is a nature reserve encompassing ancient oak woodland, wet grassland, and a stream. It provides diverse habitats for wildlife, including otters and Daubenton's bats. Grazing helps maintain the grasslands.

Name: Vealand Farm (Exceptional Diversity Node)
Description: Vealand Farm is a 20-hectare nature reserve near Bideford, North Devon, close to the River Torridge. It features diverse habitats including species-rich grassland, ponds, woodland, and an orchard, supporting otters, kingfishers, and barn owls.

Name: Veilstone Moor
Description: Veilstone Moor is a Devon Wildlife Trust nature reserve near Chulmleigh, featuring culm grassland, valley mire, and woodland. It hosts wildflowers such as meadow thistle and marsh violet, and birds including willow warbler and cuckoo. The site is managed for conservation.

Name: Vell Mill Daffodil Meadow
Description: Vell Mill Daffodil Meadow is a nature reserve near Newent, Gloucestershire. It showcases ancient woodlands and a notable display of wild daffodils each spring. The site is managed by Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust to protect its diverse flora and habitats.

Name: Vell Mill Daffodil Meadow (Pollinator Link)
Description: Vell Mill Daffodil Meadow is a Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust reserve near Hartpury. It displays extensive wild daffodils in spring, attracting pollinators. The meadow features a stream, diverse flora, and mature trees, providing habitats for various local wildlife.

Name: Velvet Bottom (Cheddar Complex)
Description: Velvet Bottom, in the Mendip Hills SSSI, is a tranquil valley with ancient woodland and grasslands. It supports diverse wildlife, including dormice and horseshoe bats. Visible spoil heaps reveal its lead mining history.

Name: Velvet Bottom (The Lead-Minings Lead)
Description: Velvet Bottom, part of the Mendip Hills SSSI, is a former lead mining site now supporting calcareous grassland and woodland. Notable for its diverse plant life, including several orchid species, and various butterfly populations. Remains of mining activity are visible.

Name: Venn Ottery
Description: Venn Ottery reserve features ancient woodland, unimproved grassland, and wetland habitats. It supports dormice, badgers, and numerous bird species. Public footpaths offer access to explore its varied landscapes.

Name: Verne Common
Description: Verne Common is a calcareous grassland nature reserve on the Isle of Portland. It supports diverse wildflowers, insects, and birds, including horseshoe vetch and the small blue butterfly. Grazing maintains the habitat, which offers views over Portland Harbour and Weymouth Bay.

Name: Vicar Water Country Park (Colliery Restoration)
Description: Vicar Water Country Park, north of Clipstone Village, is a former colliery site transformed into a Green Flag park. It now features woodlands, open heathland, and a large lake, offering diverse wildlife habitats and well-maintained paths for walkers and cyclists.

Name: Vinters Valley Nature Reserve
Description: Vinters Valley Nature Reserve is a peaceful haven of woodland, grassland, and wetland in Maidstone, Kent. The River Len flows through the valley, supporting diverse wildlife including birds, mammals, and invertebrates.

Name: Volehouse Moor (River Torridge Culm)
Description: Volehouse Moor is a nationally important culm grassland site in West Devon, within the Torridge Valley wetlands. The reserve features a mosaic of wet and dry grassland, mire, and heathland, supporting diverse plants and marsh fritillary butterflies. Management involves scrub clearance and grazing. No public access.

Name: Waen Fraith (Eryri Peat Restoration 2026)
Description: Waen Fraith is a peatland restoration site within Eryri National Park, Wales. The National Trust, in partnership with Admiral, is restoring 100 hectares to capture carbon and mitigate flood risk. This three-year project blocks ditches to re-wet the peat and remove invasive species.

Name: Waitby Greenriggs (Railway Flowers)
Description: Waitby Greenriggs is a nature reserve in Cumbria, a disused section of the former Stainmore railway line. It features limestone grassland habitats, rich in wildflowers and diverse invertebrates, supporting rare plant species.

Name: Waitrose Nature Trail
Description: Waitrose Nature Trail near Chelmsford, Essex, offers a circular walk through varied habitats including woodland, wetland, and meadow. Managed by Essex Wildlife Trust, the trail provides opportunities to observe diverse flora and fauna.

Name: Wakerley Great Wood
Description: Wakerley Great Wood is a large woodland in Northamptonshire, managed by Forestry England. It offers walking and cycling trails through diverse habitats, including ancient woodland and conifer plantations. The site provides various recreational activities and is known for its flora and fauna.

Name: Wakerley Great Wood (Jurassic Way)
Description: Wakerley Great Wood is a large ancient woodland in Northamptonshire, managed by Forestry England. It offers waymarked trails for walking, cycling, and horse riding, a play area, and forms part of the Jurassic Way long-distance path.

Name: Walborough
Description: Walborough is a coastal grazing marsh and reedbed in North Somerset, bordering the Severn Estuary. The reserve supports a diverse array of wildlife, including migrating birds and rare plants, forming part of the Walborough and Portbury Wharf area.

Name: Waldridge Fell SSSI
Description: Waldridge Fell SSSI is a lowland heath in County Durham, providing a vital habitat for various rare plants and insects. Designated as a Site of Special Scientific Interest, it represents one of the last remaining areas of this unique ecosystem.

Name: Walkeringham Nature Reserve
Description: Walkeringham Nature Reserve is a former sand and gravel quarry adjacent to the River Trent flood plain. It features a large lake, wet grassland, and scrub, providing habitats for a variety of birds, insects, and plant species. The site is managed for wildlife conservation.

Name: Wallasea Island (Partner)
Description: Wallasea Island, Essex, is a significant conservation and engineering project, forming Europe's largest man-made nature reserve. Created from Crossrail tunnel spoil, it features extensive mudflats, saltmarshes, and lagoons, providing vital habitats for diverse migratory and breeding bird species.

Name: Wallington (River Wansbeck Restoration)
Description: Wallington, a National Trust property in Northumberland, features a historic house, gardens, and expansive estate. It is currently undertaking a pioneering River Wansbeck restoration project, improving water quality, increasing flood storage, and creating wetland habitats.

Name: Wallington Estate (Wilder Wallington)
Description: Wilder Wallington is a National Trust project in Northumberland. The initiative rewilds 1,000 acres, restoring natural processes. It involves planting woodlands, restoring hedgerows, and reintroducing beavers to enhance biodiversity and resilience.

Name: Wallis Wood
Description: Wallis Wood is an ancient woodland reserve featuring coppiced sweet chestnut and oak standards on sandy clay soil. The site includes diverse ground flora with bluebells and wood anemones. A small stream and wet woodland areas are present, supporting various bird species.

Name: Walltown Crags (Connectivity Node)
Description: Walltown Crags, a connectivity node in Northumberland National Park, is part of the Hadrian's Wall Wetlands Project. It restores wetlands, peatlands, and grasslands, enhancing biodiversity and wildlife corridors.

Name: Walsall Canal (Wildlife Haven)
Description: The Walsall Canal runs through a mix of urban and green spaces in the West Midlands. It provides a habitat for various wildlife species, including waterbirds and insects. The canal supports local ecosystems and offers a linear green corridor.

Name: Waltham Brooks
Description: Waltham Brooks is a mosaic of habitats in the Western Rother flood plain. It features damp grassland, ditches, ponds, and wet woodland. The reserve supports a range of wildlife, including dragonflies, bats, and various bird species. Grazing maintains the wetland habitat.

Name: Waltham Brooks (Arun Valley)
Description: Waltham Brooks is a 150-acre nature reserve in the Arun Valley, West Sussex. This mosaic of wet grassland and riverside habitats is part of the extensive Arun flood plain. It supports diverse plants, animals, wintering wildfowl, and breeding waders. Managed by grazing cattle, footpaths offer visitor access.

Name: Walthamstow Marshes
Description: Walthamstow Marshes is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) in East London. It features a diverse mix of marshland, rivers, and ditches, supporting unique flora and fauna. Historically, it hosted Britain's first powered flight in 1909 and the nation's first aircraft factory.

Name: Walthamstow Reservoirs (Fishery & Wildlife)
Description: Walthamstow Wetlands is a 211-hectare nature reserve in London, home to diverse wildlife and nine operational reservoirs supplying water to the city. It offers opportunities for birdwatching, walking, and fishing amidst a varied natural environment.

Name: Walthamstow Wetlands (Partner)
Description: Walthamstow Wetlands is a 211-hectare urban wetland nature reserve in North East London. It comprises 10 reservoirs, supplying drinking water to the capital. Thames21 coordinates engagement events and volunteer habitat maintenance.

Name: Wandlebury Country Park (Cambridge Hills)
Description: Wandlebury Country Park is a historic parkland on the Gog Magog Hills near Cambridge, featuring Iron Age hillfort remains. It offers walking routes, nature trails, and views over the city, managed for wildlife and public enjoyment.

Name: Wandlebury Country Park (Cambridge PPF - Partner)
Description: Wandlebury Country Park, near Cambridge, features an Iron Age hillfort, ancient woodland, and chalk grassland. Managed by Cambridge PPF, it offers walking trails, wildlife habitats, and panoramic views. The site is open daily for public enjoyment and nature observation.

Name: Wandlebury Country Park (Cambridge PPF)
Description: Wandlebury Country Park, south of Cambridge, is a chalk grassland and woodland site with an Iron Age Hillfort. It hosts wildlife and walks, open daily from dawn to dusk. Cambridge Past, Present & Future owns and cares for the park.

Name: Wangford Warren
Description: Wangford Warren, a Suffolk Wildlife Trust reserve, features rare open inland sand dunes, a habitat typically coastal. It supports specialised flora and fauna adapted to sandy, nutrient-poor conditions. Active management maintains its unique character.

Name: Wangford Warren (Sand Dunes & Rare Mosses)
Description: Wangford Warren is a Suffolk Wildlife Trust nature reserve featuring nationally rare sand dunes and associated mosses, lichens, and grassland. This Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) supports diverse invertebrates, with a landscape continually shaped by sand movement.

Name: Wanlip Meadows
Description: Wanlip Meadows is a wet grassland reserve beside the River Soar, managed by Leicestershire and Rutland Wildlife Trust. It hosts breeding waders, wintering wildfowl, and diverse grassland habitats, ditches, and ponds. The site provides a rich environment for numerous bird species.

Name: Wanstead Park (Floating Reedbeds)
Description: Wanstead Park hosts floating reedbeds launched by Thames21 in 2025. These reedbeds improve water quality and create habitats for wildlife, supported by volunteers and the City of London Corporation.

Name: Wappenbury Wood
Description: Wappenbury Wood is an ancient woodland, home to diverse wildlife and flora. Visitors can explore various habitats, including ancient oak and hazel coppice, and a small pond. It is managed for conservation, supporting species like dormice and woodcocks.

Name: Wappenbury Wood (Ancient Woodland Giant)
Description: Wappenbury Wood is a large ancient woodland in Warwickshire. Dominated by oak and ash, it features hazel and hawthorn coppice, with bluebell carpets in spring. It supports a variety of birds, badgers, and a nationally important population of the rare violet click beetle.

Name: Warburg Nature Reserve
Description: Warburg Nature Reserve is a wildlife refuge in a deep, sheltered valley within the Chilterns. It features juniper glades, chalk grassland, and varied woodlands, supporting diverse wildflowers and local wildlife.

Name: Warburg Nature Reserve (Flagship Woodland)
Description: Warburg Nature Reserve is a hidden valley within the Chiltern Hills, Oxfordshire, managed by BBOWT. It features ancient beech woodlands, flower-rich grasslands, and juniper scrub, supporting rare orchids, butterflies, and diverse bird species.

Name: Warburton's Wood (Ancient Gorge Wood)
Description: Warburton's Wood, an ancient woodland in Cheshire, features a steep-sided gorge and a fast-flowing stream. It supports mature oak, ash, and elm, with ground flora like bluebell and wild garlic. Tawny owls, jays, and kingfishers inhabit the reserve.

Name: Waresley & Gransden Woods
Description: Waresley & Gransden Woods is an ancient woodland in Cambridgeshire, managed by Wildlife BCN. It is noted for its spring displays of bluebells and wood anemones, mature trees, and diverse wildlife. Waymarked trails are available for visitors.

Name: Waresley and Gransden Woods
Description: Waresley and Gransden Woods is an ancient woodland site in Cambridgeshire, comprising diverse broadleaf woodland, rides, and glades. It supports woodland birds, mammals, and invertebrates, managed for wildlife conservation and public access.

Name: Wark Forest (Vast Borderland Forest)
Description: Wark Forest is a large working forest located within Kielder Forest Park in Northumberland. Managed by Forestry England, it is known for its diverse wildlife, including red squirrels and ospreys. The area offers various outdoor activities.

Name: Warleigh Point
Description: Warleigh Point is a peninsula in Devon where the Rivers Tavy and Tamar converge, offering expansive views across the estuaries. This nature reserve encompasses ancient woodland, historic parkland, and vital saltmarsh habitats. It supports diverse wildlife, including various bird species and native flora.

Name: Warleigh Point (Tamar Estuary Ancient Wood)
Description: Warleigh Point is a peninsula of ancient woodland, meadows, and saltmarsh extending into the Tamar Estuary near Plymouth. Its tidal mudflats support feeding waders and wildfowl. The woodland is home to dormouse and various bird species. The site offers walking trails and estuarine views.

Name: Warley Place
Description: Warley Place is a nature reserve in Essex, located on the former grounds of a stately home. It features historic garden remains, ancient trees, ponds, and diverse wildlife including birds and insects. It is known for its spring flowers.

Name: Warley Place (Historic Garden Nature Reserve)
Description: Warley Place is an Essex Wildlife Trust nature reserve in Warley, Brentwood, Essex. It features the remains of a Victorian garden by Ellen Willmott, now managed for wildlife. Habitats include ancient woodland, wildflower meadows, and historic garden features, known for spring flowers and diverse insects.

Name: Warnborough Greens
Description: Warnborough Greens Nature Reserve comprises species-rich meadows and ancient woodland in North East Hampshire. It supports a variety of wildlife, including plants like ragged-robin and marsh-marigold, and birds such as tawny owl. The site is managed for conservation, with accessible paths.

Name: Warrens Wood
Description: Warren Wood is a former ancient woodland plantation near Maidstone, Kent. Dominated by mature oak, hornbeam, and hazel coppice, with some sycamore and ash. It features footpaths and an adjacent public bridleway, with part of it a Local Wildlife Site.

Name: Wart Barrow (Limestone Grassland)
Description: Wart Barrow is a Cumbria Wildlife Trust reserve, featuring steep limestone grassland and woodland. It provides habitat for diverse wildflowers, butterflies, and insects. Mature ash and hazel woodlands are present, and the site overlooks the Kent Estuary, offering views.

Name: Warton Crag
Description: Warton Crag is a prominent limestone hill and nature reserve in Lancashire, designated as a SSSI. Managed by Lancashire Wildlife Trust, it features diverse habitats including woodland, grassland, and limestone pavement. The site supports rare butterflies and offers panoramic views of Morecambe Bay.

Name: Wasdale & Eskdale (High Fell Recovery)
Description: Wasdale, in the Lake District, encompasses England's highest mountain, Scafell Pike, and deepest lake, Wastwater. The National Trust manages this valley, undertaking vital conservation work, including high fell path repairs and visitor access.

Name: Washburn Valley (Biodiversity Enhancement)
Description: Yorkshire Water, through its Washburn Valley Biodiversity Enhancement Project, improves 200 hectares for wildlife. Efforts include restoring peatland, creating new woodlands, and improving river habitats within the scenic Washburn Valley area.

Name: Washland
Description: Washland is a freshwater grazing marsh, forming part of the Trinity Broads system near Great Yarmouth. This reserve provides important habitat for diverse wading birds, dragonflies, and butterflies. The site floods seasonally, supporting a wide range of aquatic and grassland species.

Name: Waterford Heath
Description: Waterford Heath is a former sand and gravel quarry in Hertfordshire, now a varied nature reserve. It features acid grassland, wetland, heathland, woodland, and wildflower meadows. The site supports skylarks, green woodpeckers, dragonflies, and various other wildlife species.

Name: Waterford Heath (Restored Quarry)
Description: Waterford Heath is a former sand and gravel quarry, now restored for wildlife. Varied habitats include woodland, grassland, heathland, acid grassland, and ponds, supporting diverse species like migratory nightingales and invertebrates.

Name: Watersmeet (Deep Gorge Woodland)
Description: Watersmeet is a deep gorge woodland in Exmoor National Park, Devon, where the East Lyn River and Hoar Oak Water meet. Owned by the National Trust, it features scenic walking trails and a historic tea-garden, popular for its natural beauty and diverse wildlife.

Name: WaterWorks Centre Nature Reserve
Description: The WaterWorks Centre Nature Reserve, in East London, features wetland habitats, wildflower meadows, and open water, supporting diverse wildlife.
