Located adjacent to the Lee Navigation, the site offers walking routes and bird hides for visitors to observe nature, particularly a range of bird species.

Name: Watford Lodge (New Mills - Newt Habitat)
Description: Watford Lodge, near New Mills, is a crucial nature reserve for great crested newts. The site features a mix of broadleaf woodland, grassland, and ponds, providing diverse habitats. It also supports various birds, invertebrates, and wildflowers throughout the year.

Name: Watlington Hill
Description: Watlington Hill offers panoramic views across the Oxfordshire plain, situated high above Watlington. This site features a mosaic of chalk grassland and ancient woodland, supporting a variety of rare plants and insects, including the red kite.

Name: Watton Wick
Description: Watton Wick, acquired by Norfolk Wildlife Trust in 2021, is a wetland site in Norfolk. It features extensive reedbeds and ponds, supporting diverse wildlife including reed buntings, marsh harriers, and otters. Management focuses on biodiversity enhancement.

Name: Watts Wood
Description: Watts Wood is a small ancient woodland in Essex, managed by Essex Wildlife Trust. It features diverse tree species, including oak, hornbeam, and hazel, and supports various wildflowers and woodland birds, providing a valuable habitat.

Name: Wayland Wood
Description: Wayland Wood is an ancient woodland, 56.5 hectares, near Watton, Norfolk. It supports diverse flora like bluebells and wild garlic, and many bird species. The site offers trails for walking and nature observation.

Name: Wayland Wood (Ancient Forest of Arden type)
Description: Wayland Wood is an ancient woodland in Norfolk, known for its diverse flora and fauna. It features mature trees, a rich ground layer, and is associated with the 'Babes in the Wood' legend. Managed for conservation.

Name: Weald Country Park
Description: Weald Country Park is a large ancient deer park offering extensive woodlands, lakes and open grasslands. It supports diverse wildlife including fallow deer and various bird species. Visitors can explore a network of paths.

Name: Weavers Down Bog
Description: Weavers Down Bog, East Hampshire, is a nature reserve with wet and dry heathland, bog, and ancient woodland. It supports rare plants like sundews and sphagnum mosses, and birds such as Dartford warbler. This site protects important biodiversity.

Name: Weeleyhall Wood
Description: Weeleyhall Wood is an ancient woodland in north-east Essex, managed for conservation. It features diverse tree species, a seasonal pond, and a rich understory of wildflowers. The site supports various bird species and invertebrates, offering a quiet habitat for wildlife.

Name: Weeleyhall Wood (Bluebells & Butterflies)
Description: Weeleyhall Wood is an ancient woodland in Essex, known for its vibrant spring bluebells and diverse butterfly populations. It features mature oak, hornbeam, and hazel coppice, providing habitat for various wildlife. Accessible trails are available for visitors.

Name: Weeting Chalk Pit
Description: Weeting Chalk Pit is a former chalk quarry in Norfolk, managed by Norfolk Wildlife Trust. It features diverse habitats including steep chalk faces, scree slopes, and woodland. The site supports rare plants like moonwort and several orchid species, and is a designated SSSI.

Name: Weeting Heath
Description: Weeting Heath is a significant Norfolk Wildlife Trust reserve, known for supporting Stone Curlew, nightjars, and woodlarks. Its sandy soils host rare plants such as Moonwort fern and insects like the Dark Green Fritillary. Rabbit grazing helps maintain the open heathland habitats.

Name: Weeting Heath (Stone Curlew Site)
Description: Weeting Heath is a Breckland grass heath site, managed to protect stone curlews. It features a viewing hide, walking trails and diverse wildlife. Habitats include dry grassland, scrub, and scattered trees.

Name: Weetslade Country Park
Description: Weetslade Country Park is a former colliery site near Newcastle. It features grassland, woodland, and ponds, providing habitat for skylarks and roe deer. The park offers walking trails and panoramic views across Northumberland for visitors.

Name: Weetslade Country Park (Urban Link)
Description: Weetslade Country Park is a reclaimed colliery spoil heap in North Tyneside, managed by Northumberland Wildlife Trust. It offers diverse habitats for wildlife and provides recreational paths for walking and cycling within an urban fringe setting.

Name: Weir Wood Reservoir (Bird Sanctuary)
Description: Weir Wood Reservoir is a 150-acre SSSI in Sussex, managed by the Weir Wood Reservoir Conservation Trust. It supports diverse bird species, including overwintering wildfowl and migrating waders. Public access is limited, with bird hides available.

Name: Welcombe Hills
Description: Welcombe Hills, near Stratford-upon-Avon, features diverse habitats including wildflower-rich grasslands, ancient woodlands, and ponds. The site offers extensive views across the Avon Valley and includes historical landmarks like the Welcombe Lodge.

Name: Welcombe Hills & Clopton Park
Description: Welcombe Hills & Clopton Park is a Warwickshire nature reserve near Stratford-upon-Avon, featuring ancient woodlands, grassland, and diverse wildlife. Public footpaths offer views of the Avon valley and surrounding landscape.

Name: Wellington Gravel Pits
Description: Wellington Gravel Pits, a former aggregate quarry in Herefordshire, is now a diverse nature reserve. It features a deep central lake, shallow pools, reedbeds, scrub, and grassland, attracting wildfowl, butterflies, dragonflies, and various bird species.

Name: Wells Farm (Oxfordshire)
Description: Wells Farm is a nature reserve in West Oxfordshire, featuring limestone grassland and ancient woodland. It supports a diverse range of wildflowers, butterflies, and bird species. Visitors can explore waymarked trails. The site is managed for conservation.

Name: Wem Moss (Floating Bog)
Description: Wem Moss is a rare raised bog in north Shropshire, a designated National Nature Reserve. It supports diverse plant life, including sphagnum mosses and carnivorous plants, providing critical habitat for insects, birds, and amphibians.

Name: Wem Moss NNR (Lowland Raised Bog)
Description: Wem Moss NNR is a Site of Special Scientific Interest and England's largest actively growing raised bog. It supports unique flora like bog rosemary, sphagnum mosses, and sundews, alongside dragonflies and large heath butterflies.

Name: Wembury Bay (Marine & Coastal Conservation)
Description: Wembury Bay is a popular Devon coastal site, managed by the National Trust, featuring diverse habitats from sandy beaches to rocky shores. It offers rockpooling, beach walks, and a marine centre focused on education and marine conservation.

Name: Wendover Woods (Chilterns)
Description: Wendover Woods is a extensive woodland managed by Forestry England in the Chiltern Hills, Buckinghamshire. It features walking and cycling trails, a café, and a popular treetop adventure course. The site offers diverse habitats and panoramic views across the Aylesbury Vale.

Name: Wendover Woods (East District boundary)
Description: Wendover Woods, located in the Chiltern Hills, provides extensive woodland for recreation. Visitors enjoy walking, cycling, and a dedicated play trail. The site features a cafe and panoramic views. Forestry England manages this popular outdoor destination.

Name: Wenlock Edge
Description: Wenlock Edge is a limestone escarpment stretching for 19 miles in Shropshire. A woodland of ash, lime and hazel covers the ancient coral reef, providing habitat for diverse wildlife. It offers walking trails and is a designated Site of Special Scientific Interest managed by the National Trust.

Name: Wenlock Edge (Limestone Escarpment)
Description: Wenlock Edge is a 19-mile limestone escarpment in Shropshire, featuring ancient woodlands and grasslands. Designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI), it forms part of the Shropshire Hills Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. It supports diverse wildlife and geological features.

Name: Wentworth Castle Gardens (NT/Council Biodiversity)
Description: Wentworth Castle Gardens, in South Yorkshire, is a historic landscape with formal gardens, parkland, and architectural follies. Managed by the National Trust in partnership with Barnsley Council, it focuses on conservation and public access to heritage.

Name: Wentworth Castle Gardens (Parkland Restoration)
Description: Wentworth Castle Gardens features 500 acres of historic parkland and monuments, including woodland, lakes, and a Victorian conservatory. This Grade I listed landscape in South Yorkshire is actively undergoing significant restoration efforts to conserve its heritage.

Name: Werndryd (Dragonfly Bog Sanctuary)
Description: Werndryd is a wet heath and bog sanctuary in Radnorshire, Wales, managed by Radnorshire Wildlife Trust. It protects diverse dragonflies, damselflies, and unique bog plants. Cattle grazing sustains the habitat. An accessible boardwalk aids visitor exploration.

Name: Wernffrwd (Loughor Estuary Saltmarsh)
Description: Wernffrwd is a key saltmarsh and scrub habitat on the east bank of the Loughor Estuary. It supports important overwintering wildfowl and wader populations. The site is a Special Area of Conservation and Special Protection Area.

Name: Wesleat Moor (Avalon connectivity)
Description: Wesleat Moor is a traditional hay meadow, forming an important part of the landscape around Shapwick Heath National Nature Reserve. This reserve features diverse wetland habitats and a rich history, with evidence of human occupation dating back 10,000 years.

Name: Wessington Pasture
Description: Wessington Pasture is a 1.25-hectare traditionally managed flower-rich SSSI. It hosts green-winged orchid, adder's-tongue fern, yellow rattle, ancient trees, and a ridge and furrow field system. Hay is cut annually, sheep graze in autumn.

Name: West Bexington
Description: West Bexington is a coastal reserve in Dorset, featuring a shingle beach, freshwater marsh, and reedbeds. It supports diverse birdlife, including overwintering and migrating species, alongside various insects and plant communities.

Name: West Blean and Thornden Woods (Wildwood Bison Project)
Description: West Blean and Thornden Woods is a 1,200-hectare ancient semi-natural woodland in Kent. Managed by Kent Wildlife Trust, it is a Site of Special Scientific Interest and home to the Wilder Blean project, introducing European bison and other grazing animals for rewilding.

Name: West Burton Meadow
Description: West Burton Meadow is a traditional hay meadow near Burton Joyce, Nottinghamshire, close to the River Trent. It features diverse flora including great burnet, meadowsweet, ragged robin, and cuckoo flower. This Trent Valley site is an important stopover for migrating birds.

Name: West Burton Reedbed
Description: West Burton Reedbed is a Nottinghamshire Wildlife Trust wetland reserve. It features extensive reedbeds, open water, and wet grassland habitats. The site provides a vital refuge for diverse bird species, including bitterns and marsh harriers, crucial for wetland conservation.

Name: West Chevington (30x30 Flagship)
Description: West Chevington is a former opencast coal mine transformed into a mosaic of habitats including grassland, woodland, and wetlands. The site is a key refuge for birds, insects, and other wildlife in Northumberland, managed for conservation.

Name: West Chevington (Opencast Recovery)
Description: West Chevington, a former opencast coal mine, now supports diverse grassland, wetland, and woodland habitats. It is home to breeding waders, rare grassland plants, and various dragonflies.

Name: West Coker Fen
Description: West Coker Fen is a wet meadow and wetland area near Yeovil, managed by Somerset Wildlife Trust. It features diverse flora including southern marsh orchids and ragged robin. The site supports various insects, amphibians, and birds, reflecting its rich biodiversity.

Name: West Dean Woods
Description: West Dean Woods, a remnant of ancient woodland in the South Downs National Park near Chichester, features beech, oak, and ash trees with a hazel and hawthorn understorey. The site includes veteran trees, a dew pond, and ancient woodland indicator plants.

Name: West Dean Woods (Hazel Coppice & Daffodils)
Description: West Dean Woods is an ancient woodland in West Sussex, notable for its coppiced hazel and wild daffodils. The site supports diverse woodland flora and fauna, including rare butterflies and birds. It features marked trails for public access and nature observation.

Name: West Ham Park
Description: West Ham Park is a historic 7.7-hectare green space in Newham, East London. It features formal gardens, sports facilities, a children's playground, and an arboretum. The park is managed by the City of London Corporation and provides diverse recreational opportunities.

Name: West Haystoun
Description: West Haystoun is an important site for nature in Peeblesshire. Acquired in 2021, this small reserve safeguards ancient semi-natural woodland alongside newly planted areas. The site contributes to a vital woodland corridor and features a mix of broadleaved trees like oak, ash, and birch. It supports diverse wildlife and enhances riparian habitats along the River Tweed, improving biodiversity in the Borders region.

Name: West Muchlarnick (Rainforest Legacy)
Description: West Muchlarnick is a site in Cornwall, part of the Rainforest Legacy project, where Cornwall Wildlife Trust works to protect and expand temperate rainforest habitats. These unique ecosystems are rare in the UK, found in areas of high rainfall.

Name: West Muchlarnick (Rainforest Project)
Description: Cornwall Wildlife Trust leads a temperate rainforest restoration project across various Cornish sites. It aims to expand and connect these rare ecosystems, boosting biodiversity and climate resilience through active management and native tree planting.

Name: West Runton and Beeston Regis Heath
Description: West Runton and Beeston Regis Heath offers panoramic views of the the North Norfolk coast and a diverse landscape of heathland, woodland, and meadows. It is home to various wildlife species, including butterflies, birds, and deer. The site is popular for walking and nature observation.

Name: West Walk - Forest of Bere
Description: West Walk, within the Forest of Bere, is a woodland site in Hampshire. It offers marked walking, cycling, and horse riding trails through ancient woodland and heathland habitats. Managed by Forestry England, it supports biodiversity and provides recreational access.

Name: West Walk (Forest of Bere Edge)
Description: West Walk is a large woodland in Hampshire, bordering the Forest of Bere. It offers waymarked trails for walking, cycling, and horse riding, with diverse habitats including broadleaf and conifer areas. Facilities include a car park, toilets, and a children's play area.

Name: West Wickham Common
Description: West Wickham Common and Spring Park, managed by the City of London Corporation, offers heathland, woodland, ponds, and open green spaces in South London. This Green Flag awarded site is a Site of Special Scientific Interest, protecting ancient woodland and providing a refuge for wildlife.

Name: West Williamston (Limestone Shore)
Description: West Williamston is a diverse reserve featuring ancient woodland, limestone grassland, and rocky shoreline in Pembrokeshire. It hosts rich flora, including orchids, and supports various bird species. Well-maintained paths are available for visitors.

Name: West Wood
Description: West Wood is a small ancient woodland in Essex, managed by coppicing hornbeam and oak. This practice benefits ground flora like wood anemone and bluebells. The site supports various butterflies, including white admirals, and bird species such as nightingales.

Name: Westhay Moor NNR (Peatland Heart)
Description: Westhay Moor National Nature Reserve, managed by Somerset Wildlife Trust, is a crucial peatland habitat in the Somerset Levels. It supports rare plants, invertebrates, and important bird populations throughout the year, including wintering wildfowl.

Name: Weston Big Wood
Description: Weston Big Wood is a 60-hectare ancient woodland in North Somerset, boasting a rich variety of trees, plants, and wildlife. Managed by Avon Wildlife Trust, it features both ancient and secondary woodland, coppiced areas, and a grassland glade. Rare whitebeam trees grow on limestone cliffs.

Name: Weston Turville Reservoir
Description: Weston Turville Reservoir is a shallow, alkaline waterbody and a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) in Buckinghamshire. It supports diverse aquatic plants and invertebrates, attracting migratory waterfowl and surrounded by reedbeds and grasslands.

Name: Westwood Manor Estate
Description: Westwood Manor is a medieval house in Wiltshire, featuring a beautiful garden and topiary. The property reveals its changing uses, from a medieval hall to a Georgian clothier's home and a 20th-century family residence, with collections of furniture and musical instruments.

Name: Wetmoor (Dormouse Habitat)
Description: Wetmoor is a 14.7-hectare nature reserve near Lucton, Herefordshire, featuring traditional orchard, meadow, and ancient woodland. It provides an important habitat for dormice and mistletoe. The site is actively managed to support diverse wildlife populations.

Name: Wharram Quarry
Description: Wharram Quarry is a disused chalk quarry near Wharram-le-Street, managed by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust. The reserve protects chalk grassland, scrub, and woodland habitats, supporting wildflowers, butterflies, and birds for visitors to observe.

Name: Wheldrake Ings (Lower Derwent Valley)
Description: Wheldrake Ings, part of the Lower Derwent Valley NNR, is a vital wetland in Yorkshire, managed by YWT. It supports significant populations of wintering wildfowl and breeding wading birds. Its extensive floodplains offer diverse habitats for plants, invertebrates, and mammals.

Name: Whichford Wood (Ancient Bluebell Wood)
Description: Whichford Wood is an ancient woodland, a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI), located near Whichford, Warwickshire. It is known for its spring bluebells, mature oak and ash trees, and diverse ground flora. The site is managed for conservation, providing habitats for various wildlife.

Name: Whinlatter Forest (Mountain Forest)
Description: Whinlatter Forest, England's only true mountain forest, is nestled high in the Lake District National Park. It provides extensive walking and cycling trails, a Go Ape course, and a visitor centre. The forest is home to ospreys and red squirrels, offering stunning views.

Name: Whirlow Farm (Sheffield/Derby Borderlands)
Description: Whirlow Farm is an urban farm in Sheffield, bordering the Peak District. It provides educational experiences for children and young people, focusing on farming, food production, and environmental awareness. The site includes rare breed animals.

Name: Whisby Nature Park (Sand & Gravel Pits)
Description: Whisby Nature Park, near Lincoln, features lakes, woodland, and grassland habitats formed from former gravel pits. The site supports diverse wildlife including birds, insects, and mammals. It offers accessible paths, bird hides, and a visitor centre with a shop and cafe.

Name: Whistlestop (Matlock Bath)
Description: Whistlestop is a small nature reserve adjacent to Matlock Bath train station. It features a diverse mosaic of habitats, including riverside, wetland, and woodland, supporting local wildlife like kingfishers and otters on the River Derwent. The site is easily accessible for visitors.

Name: Whitacre Heath (River Tame)
Description: Whitacre Heath is a 50-hectare nature reserve in Warwickshire, bordering Staffordshire at the River Tame. It features diverse habitats including wet woodland, wildflower meadows, marshy grasslands, and reedbeds. Aquatic wildlife thrives in its pools.

Name: Whitacre Heath (SSSI Wetland)
Description: Whitacre Heath is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) wetland reserve in North Warwickshire. It features reedbeds, wet grassland, and open water. The site supports otters, bitterns, and diverse bird and insect species. Restoration efforts enhance biodiversity.

Name: Whitbarrow Scar (Upland Giant)
Description: Whitbarrow Scar is a designated National Nature Reserve and Special Area of Conservation in Cumbria. This site features impressive limestone pavements, ancient woodlands, and rich grasslands, supporting a diverse array of wildlife including rare butterflies and orchids.

Name: Whitcombe Hill
Description: Whitcombe Hill Nature Reserve in Dorset is a significant chalk grassland site managed by Dorset Wildlife Trust. It supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare butterflies and wildflowers. The reserve offers panoramic views across the Frome Valley, providing a crucial habitat for local wildlife.

Name: White Cliffs of Dover (Iconic Chalk Cliffs)
Description: Iconic chalk cliffs offer panoramic views across the English Channel, forming part of the Kent Downs AONB. This site is home to diverse wildlife and rich history, including wartime tunnels and a visitor centre. Coastal walks and educational activities are available.

Name: White Downs (Ranmore)
Description: White Downs is a mosaic of habitats, including ancient woodland, chalk grassland, and scrub. The reserve supports a variety of wildlife, such as orchids, butterflies, and deer. It offers walking trails with views across the Surrey Hills.

Name: White Field
Description: White Field is a small traditional hay meadow near Langport, managed by Somerset Wildlife Trust. It supports wildflowers like meadow saxifrage and green-winged orchid. Grazing occurs in late summer/autumn after hay cutting.

Name: White Hill
Description: White Hill is a chalk grassland nature reserve located on a steep south-west facing slope of the North Downs in Kent. It is an important habitat supporting a variety of insects, including the Adonis Blue butterfly, and different plant species. The site is part of the Kent Downs Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty.

Name: White Horse Hill
Description: High on a chalk downland ridge in Oxfordshire, the Bronze Age White Horse is carved into the hillside. Iron Age earthworks of Uffington Castle surround the chalk figure, while Dragon Hill and Wayland's Smithy are nearby.

Name: White Rocks (Doward)
Description: White Rocks is a limestone cliff with ancient woodland overlooking the River Wye in Herefordshire. It provides stunning views and supports rare plants and invertebrates. The reserve features rich ground flora and significant geological formations, with public access paths.

Name: White Water
Description: White Water is a former gravel quarry restored to a wildlife haven near Stockton-on-Tees. It features shallow wetland habitats and open water, providing important breeding and feeding grounds for various bird species, amphibians, and invertebrates.

Name: Whitecross Green Wood
Description: Whitecross Green Wood is an ancient woodland, restored from a conifer plantation, rich in butterflies and dragonflies. Varied habitats include wet woodland and wildflower meadows, supporting diverse wildlife. The reserve offers peaceful walks.

Name: Whitecross Green Wood (Bernwood Forest)
Description: Whitecross Green Wood is a significant fragment of ancient Bernwood Forest, featuring diverse habitats like semi-natural woodland, meadows, and ponds. It supports a wide array of wildlife, including butterflies, dragonflies, and birds. Visitors can explore well-maintained paths.

Name: Whiteford Burrows (The Neptune Legacy)
Description: Whiteford Burrows is a coastal reserve on the Gower Peninsula in South Wales. It features an expansive sandy beach, a dune system, saltmarsh, and woodland, supporting diverse wildlife. The area is known for its historic cast-iron lighthouse and provides habitats for many bird species and rare plants.

Name: Whiteford Hill
Description: Whiteford Hill offers open access to ancient woodland and chalk downland within the South Downs National Park. It provides spectacular views across the landscape, encompassing diverse wildlife habitats. This tranquil Hampshire site features walking trails.

Name: Whitehaven Coast (Coastal Grassland)
Description: The Whitehaven Coast offers dramatic cliff-top walks and panoramic views across the Irish Sea. This area encompasses important maritime grasslands, providing vital habitats for diverse wildlife and unique coastal flora, accessible via maintained public paths.

Name: Whitehouse Lagoon (Migratory Stopover)
Description: Whitehouse Lagoon is an international wetland and vital migratory stopover on Belfast Lough, part of RSPB Belfast's Window on Wildlife. It hosts diverse bird species year-round. Two hides offer observation points for visitors overlooking the lagoon.

Name: Whitehouse Meadow
Description: Whitehouse Meadow Nature Reserve is a small, species-rich grassland located in Hampshire, managed by the Wildlife Trust. It features a diverse range of wildflowers, including ragged-robin and sneezewort, supporting various insects. The site is actively managed for conservation.

Name: Whiteleaf Hill (Chalk Hillside)
Description: Whiteleaf Hill offers panoramic views across the Chilterns, featuring a prehistoric barrow and the striking Whiteleaf Cross carved into the chalk hillside. The site includes woodlands and open grasslands, supporting diverse wildlife. Popular for walking and wildlife spotting.

Name: Whitelee Moor National Nature Reserve
Description: Whitelee Moor National Nature Reserve spans 1,500 hectares of diverse upland habitats in the Cheviot Hills, Northumberland. It protects blanket bog, acid grassland, and heather moorland, supporting black grouse, dunlin, and otter.

Name: Whitelee Moor NNR (Borderland Peat)
Description: Whitelee Moor NNR is a vast area of blanket bog and wet heath in the Northumberland National Park. It supports important populations of red grouse, merlin, and golden plover. The site features extensive peatlands, contributing to carbon storage.

Name: Whitelee Wind Farm (Nature Project)
Description: Whitelee Wind Farm, south of Glasgow, hosts a large nature reserve managed by the Scottish Wildlife Trust. It features heathland, forest, and wetlands, providing habitats for raptors and red deer. Public trails are available.

Name: Whitlaw Mosses
Description: Whitlaw Mosses is a group of four nationally important raised bogs in the Scottish Borders, managed by the Scottish Wildlife Trust. The site supports diverse wetland plants, including sphagnum mosses, cotton grass, and cranberry. It hosts invertebrates like dragonflies and birds such as curlew and snipe, providing a vital habitat for specialist bog species.

Name: Whitmoor & Rickford Commons
Description: Whitmoor & Rickford Commons is a large expanse of lowland heath, grassland, and woodland, located just north of Guildford. It provides crucial habitats for many bird species, including nightjar, woodlark, and Dartford warbler. The site is actively managed for conservation.

Name: Whitmoor Common
Description: Whitmoor Common is a large expanse of heathland, woodland, and wetland near Guildford, Surrey. It supports rare birds like nightjars and woodlarks, and reptiles such as adders and sand lizards. The common is managed for its biodiversity, providing habitat for diverse wildlife.

Name: Whitwell Coppice
Description: Whitwell Coppice is a mixed woodland nature reserve south of Shrewsbury, bordering the River Severn. Ancient woodland features, including mature oak and hazel coppice, support diverse fungi, mosses, and invertebrates. Managed by Shropshire Wildlife Trust, it offers walks.

Name: Whixall Moss (Peatland Restoration)
Description: Whixall Moss, part of Fenn’s, Whixall & Bettisfield Mosses NNR, is a significant lowland raised bog spanning the England-Wales border. It hosts rare flora and fauna. Restoration efforts through the BogLIFE project enhance its unique biodiversity.

Name: Wicken Fen (The Vision Landscape)
Description: Wicken Fen is a national nature reserve where traditional fenland habitats are preserved, supporting over 9,000 species. Actively expanding, it features waterways, grazing marshes, and reedbeds within a significant wetland landscape.

Name: Wicken Fen National Nature Reserve
Description: Wicken Fen is the National Trust's oldest nature reserve and one of Europe's most important wetlands. It supports over 9,000 species, including many rare plants, birds and insects. Visitors can explore the fen on foot or by bike, or take a boat trip.

Name: Wigan Flashes (Nature Recovery Gateway)
Description: Wigan Flashes is a network of wetlands and woodlands in Wigan Borough. This Site of Special Scientific Interest offers vital habitat for diverse wildlife, including rare birds and newts. It provides public access for activities like walking, cycling, and birdwatching.

Name: Wigan Flashes LNR
Description: Wigan Flashes Local Nature Reserve features wetlands, woodlands, and grasslands south of Wigan. A mosaic of habitats supports diverse wildlife, including significant bird populations and nationally rare plants. The flashes were formed by industrial subsidence.

Name: Wightwick Manor (Arts & Crafts Gardens)
Description: Wightwick Manor is a Victorian house in Wolverhampton, West Midlands, showcasing the Arts and Crafts movement. It features interiors and collections by William Morris, De Morgan, and Rossetti. The gardens reflect the Arts and Crafts style.

Name: Wigpool (The Northern Forest Gateway)
Description: Wigpool, a former iron ore mine in the Forest of Dean, is now a Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust nature reserve. It features mixed woodland, grassland, and ponds, supporting diverse wildlife like dragonflies and birds. Public access is available.

Name: Wild Ennerdale (Pioneering Rewilding)
Description: Wild Ennerdale is a partnership initiative in the Ennerdale valley of the Lake District, Cumbria. It allows natural processes to shape the landscape, integrating sustainable forestry, farming, and conservation with minimal intervention.

Name: Wild Haweswater (Rewilding Project)
Description: Wild Haweswater is a partnership between United Utilities and the RSPB, restoring natural processes and improving biodiversity in the Haweswater valley. The project focuses on rewilding a large upland area, enhancing habitats for wildlife like red squirrels and rare birds.

Name: Wild Ingleborough (1200ha Flagship)
Description: Wild Ingleborough is a 1,200-hectare nature recovery project in the Yorkshire Dales National Park. Led by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust, it focuses on restoring uplands, rewilding landscapes, reintroducing species, and enhancing biodiversity.

Name: Wild Ingleborough (Landscape Scale Recovery)
Description: Wild Ingleborough is a landscape-scale nature recovery project across 12,000 hectares in the Yorkshire Dales National Park. Led by Yorkshire Wildlife Trust, it restores peatlands, creates woodlands, and manages grasslands to enhance biodiversity and natural processes.

Name: Wild Kielder (Forestry England Partnership)
Description: Wild Kielder is a partnership project within Kielder Forest and Water Park, Northumberland. It focuses on improving understanding of cultural heritage and wildlife through conservation initiatives and outreach activities.

Name: Wild Peak (Landscape Project)
Description: Wild Peak is a landscape-scale conservation project by Derbyshire Wildlife Trust, restoring nature across 35,000 hectares of the Peak District. It focuses on creating resilient habitats, enhancing biodiversity, and connecting people with the natural environment, particularly in the southern Peak.

Name: Wild Walsall (Heathland Recovery)
Description: Wild Walsall is restoring and managing threatened heathland habitats in the borough, aiming to create a network of wildlife-rich sites. Collaborating with Walsall Council and local communities, the project enhances biodiversity across areas like Stubbers Green and Park Lime Pits.

Name: Wilden Marsh (Stour Floodplain)
Description: Wilden Marsh, a 33.7-hectare nature reserve near Stourport-on-Severn, is a neutral grassland floodplain on the River Stour. It features wet and dry grassland, ditches, and woodland, supporting wading birds, wildfowl, and invertebrates.

Name: Wilder Blean (Partner)
Description: Wilder Blean is a pioneering rewilding project in Kent, encompassing 3,500 acres. It restores natural processes through the reintroduction of grazing animals like European bison, longhorn cattle, pigs, and ponies. The initiative aims to boost biodiversity and climate resilience.

Name: Wilder Community Green Spaces
Description: Wilder Community Green Spaces, a Tees Valley Wildlife Trust project, enhances local green spaces. It engages communities in creating havens for wildlife and people, focusing on habitat creation and management to boost biodiversity across urban areas.

Name: Wilder Little Duxmore
Description: Wilder Little Duxmore, on the Isle of Wight, is a 98-hectare rewilding project. Formerly intensive farmland, the site is transforming into diverse habitats like woodland, scrub, and grassland, restoring biodiversity and natural processes.

Name: Wilder Lugg (Natural Flood Management)
Description: The Wilder Lugg project implements natural flood management and habitat restoration within the Lugg catchment across Herefordshire and Powys. It enhances biodiversity, improves water quality, and builds climate change resilience.

Name: Wilder Nunwell
Description: Wilder Nunwell is a 40-hectare nature reserve on the Isle of Wight, managed by Hampshire & Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust. It comprises ancient woodland, species-rich grassland, and wetland habitats. A stream supports diverse wildlife. The site features walking trails.

Name: Wilderness Island
Description: Wilderness Island is a small nature reserve in east London, near Snaresbrook. It features woodland, a pond, and a section of the River Roding. The site provides a vital urban refuge, supporting diverse birds, invertebrates, and amphibians.

Name: Wildmoor Heath
Description: Wildmoor Heath is a lowland heathland and mire system in Berkshire. It supports rare species like silver-studded blue butterflies, Dartford warblers, and sundews. Management includes grazing and scrub clearance.

Name: Wildmoor Heath (Sandlings)
Description: Wildmoor Heath is a lowland heathland reserve managed by BBOWT. It provides habitat for rare species including nightjar, woodlark, Dartford warbler, adders, and sand lizards. The site is a significant part of the wider Sandlings heathland in Berkshire.

Name: Wilford Claypit
Description: Wilford Claypit is a small nature reserve near the River Trent. It features grassland, scrub, and woodland habitats, supporting various birds, insects, and small mammals. Formerly a brickworks, the site now offers a mosaic of habitats for local wildlife and visitors.

Name: Wilford Claypits (Urban Wetland)
Description: Wilford Claypits is an urban wetland near the River Trent in Nottingham, transformed from a former brickworks clay pit. It features a large lake, reedbeds, and grasslands, supporting diverse wildlife including birds and amphibians. Popular for walks.

Name: Willesborough Lees
Description: Willesborough Lees offers a varied mosaic of ancient woodland, grassland, and ponds, supporting diverse habitats. This Kent Wildlife Trust nature reserve is home to amphibians, insects, and woodland birds. It provides a valuable local green space for nature, with ongoing conservation efforts.

Name: Willesley Wood
Description: Willesley Wood is a small ancient woodland near Ashby-de-la-Zouch, Leicestershire. It features a diverse mix of tree species, including oak, ash, and hazel, supporting a rich understorey and varied wildlife. The site offers tranquil paths for visitors.

Name: Williamthorpe (Former Colliery Ponds)
Description: Williamthorpe Colliery Ponds Local Nature Reserve features diverse habitats like grasslands, wetlands, woodlands, and open water. It supports amphibians such as frogs, toads, and newts, and is a key site for dragonflies and damselflies during summer months.

Name: Willington Gravel Pits (Trent Valley/Derby interface)
Description: Willington Gravel Pits is a significant wetland nature reserve in the East Midlands, supporting nationally important populations of gadwall and goldeneye. The site provides habitat for various waterfowl and waders throughout the year. It features diverse landscapes of lakes, pools, and reedbeds.

Name: Willington Wetlands (Beaver Hub)
Description: Willington Wetlands, a Derbyshire nature reserve managed by Derbyshire Wildlife Trust, developed from former gravel pits. It features diverse wetland habitats, including open water and reedbeds. The site hosts a re-established beaver population within an enclosure, supporting varied birdlife.

Name: Willow Tree Farm
Description: Willow Tree Farm is a 32-hectare nature reserve near Bere Regis, Dorset. It comprises wet woodland, meadows, and reedbeds, supporting diverse wildlife. The site is managed for habitat maintenance and biodiversity.

Name: Willow Tree Fen
Description: Willow Tree Fen is a mosaic of reedbeds, fen, wet grassland and open water, supporting diverse wildlife. Formerly arable land, extensive restoration transformed the site from 2009. It now hosts breeding waders including lapwing and marsh harriers.

Name: Willow Wheel (Pembrokeshire Coastal Buffer)
Description: A 100-acre site adjacent to the Pembrokeshire Coast National Park. Welsh Wildlife Trust manages it to enhance biodiversity and improve public access, creating a vital buffer zone against coastal development. Restoration focuses on wildflower meadows and native woodland planting.

Name: Willowmead
Description: Willowmead is a small nature reserve in Hertford, Hertfordshire, managed by Herts and Middlesex Wildlife Trust. It features a meadow and a small area of woodland, supporting diverse plant species and local wildlife.

Name: Willsbridge Valley
Description: Willsbridge Valley nature reserve near Bristol features woodlands, meadows, and a stream. It supports diverse wildlife, including kingfishers. The site contains a historic mill and lime kiln, managed for conservation.

Name: Wilson’s Pits (Nene Valley)
Description: Wilson's Pits, a former gravel pit in the Nene Valley, is now a nature reserve featuring reedbeds and open water. It supports diverse bird species, including reed warblers and great crested grebes, alongside various dragonflies and orchids.

Name: Wilwell Farm Cutting
Description: Wilwell Farm Cutting is a former railway cutting near Nottingham, a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI). It features diverse grassland and woodland habitats, supporting wildflowers, insects, and birds. The site is managed for nature conservation.

Name: Wilwell Farm Cutting (Chalk-like Grassland)
Description: Wilwell Farm Cutting is a unique nature reserve near Ruddington, showcasing a rare example of chalk grassland on boulder clay. It supports diverse flora, including numerous wildflowers, and a rich invertebrate population, particularly butterflies and moths.

Name: Wimbledon Common (Independent)
Description: Wimbledon and Putney Commons span 1,140 acres of heathland, woodland, and grassland in South West London. Managed under an 1871 Act of Parliament, the site supports diverse wildlife and offers recreational opportunities for visitors.

Name: Wimpole Estate (Green Recovery Project & Wood Pasture)
Description: Wimpole Estate in Cambridgeshire is a National Trust property encompassing a mansion, gardens, parkland, and a working farm. It focuses on conservation efforts, including the Green Recovery Project and wood pasture restoration.

Name: Winchelsea Beach (Managed sections)
Description: Managed sections of Winchelsea Beach are part of Rye Harbour Nature Reserve. This shingle and saltmarsh habitat supports a range of bird species, including terns and ringed plovers. The area provides public access for wildlife observation and walking.

Name: Windmill Farm (Lizard Peninsula Hub)
Description: Windmill Farm is a coastal heathland and wetland reserve on the Lizard Peninsula, Cornwall. It features diverse habitats including mire, damp grassland, and dry heath. The reserve supports rare plants, invertebrates, and breeding choughs. Public access is available.

Name: Windmill Hill
Description: Windmill Hill is a young woodland with recent tree planting, offering open views across the surrounding countryside. It features mixed deciduous species and a pond, providing habitats for various wildlife. The site is accessible via public footpaths.

Name: Windmill Hill (Limestone Grassland)
Description: Windmill Hill is a species-rich limestone grassland, a former quarry now hosting diverse flora like cowslips and bee orchids. Managed for biodiversity, it provides a vital habitat in Warwickshire. The site offers views across the Avon Valley.

Name: Windyhills
Description: Windyhills is a grassland reserve in Aberdeenshire, managed for wildlife. It features diverse plant species and supports various invertebrates, including the great yellow bumblebee. The site also attracts raptors.

Name: Wing Water Treatment Works (Managed Grassland)
Description: Wing Water Treatment Works Managed Grassland is a lowland meadow reserve next to Rutland Water, managed for wildflowers and insects. The site features various grassland habitats, including neutral soils, supporting diverse invertebrate populations and bird species for feeding and breeding.

Name: Wingate Quarry (LNR Partner)
Description: Wingate Quarry is a former limestone quarry, now a Local Nature Reserve in County Durham. It features flower-rich grassland supporting bee, fragrant, and frog orchids. Butterflies like dingy skipper, common blue, and small copper thrive here.

Name: Wingate Quarry LNR
Description: Wingate Quarry LNR is a Site of Special Scientific Interest, featuring magnesian limestone grassland and scrub. It hosts diverse flora, including early purple orchids, and butterflies such as dingy skipper and brown argus.

Name: Winks Meadow
Description: Winks Meadow is a valuable remnant of ancient grassland, rich in wildflowers. This Suffolk Wildlife Trust reserve features a spring-fed stream, providing diverse habitats for insects and amphibians. Grazed traditionally, it supports species like green-winged orchid and adder's-tongue fern.

Name: Winks Meadow (Species-rich Grassland)
Description: Winks Meadow is a valuable ancient grassland remnant near Gislingham, Suffolk. Managed for its species-rich plant communities, it features chalky boulder clay habitat supporting wildflowers like green-winged orchid and cowslip, along with diverse insects and birdlife.

Name: Winmarleigh Moss (Lancashire Carbon Farm)
Description: Winmarleigh Moss is a Lancashire Wildlife Trust nature reserve and carbon farm. This raised bog is part of the Great North Bog initiative. Restoration efforts focus on rewetting the peatland to store carbon and support species like sphagnum mosses, cotton grass, and dragonflies.

Name: Winnall Moors
Description: Winnall Moors is a rare mosaic of fen, wet grassland, rivers, and disused watercress beds alongside the River Itchen in Winchester. It provides a vital green space within the city and is a significant wetland nature reserve.

Name: Winnall Moors Nature Reserve (Itchen Valley)
Description: Winnall Moors is a chalk stream water meadow nature reserve in Winchester, bordering the River Itchen. It supports diverse wildlife, including otters, water voles, and kingfishers. Habitats include fen, wet grassland, and reedbed.

Name: Winsford Gardens (Penge Green Gym)
Description: Winsford Gardens is a Victorian public park in Penge, South East London, operating as a Green Gym since 2011. Volunteers meet weekly to perform conservation and gardening tasks. The group manages and maintains the park's Green Flag award.

Name: Winterton Dunes
Description: Winterton Dunes is a nationally important dune system and beach in Norfolk, home to one of the largest grey seal colonies in England. It supports diverse flora and fauna, including natterjack toads and various bird species. Managed for conservation, it sees high visitor numbers.

Name: Winterton Dunes (managed sections)
Description: Winterton Dunes is a dynamic coastal habitat, home to rare natterjack toads and important for nesting little terns. Managed sections feature shifting sand dunes, marram grass, and diverse flora, adapting to the coastal environment.

Name: Wisbech Tilney St Lawrence
Description: Wisbech Tilney St Lawrence is a Norfolk Wildlife Trust reserve. This former arable field has been reverted to grassland, creating a valuable flood-prone wetland habitat. It supports diverse flora and fauna, including rare plants and wading birds.

Name: Wisley & Ockham Commons & Chatley Heath
Description: Wisley, Ockham Commons, and Chatley Heath form a large expanse of ancient heathland, acid grassland, and woodland in Surrey. It is home to diverse wildlife, including rare birds and insects, with ponds supporting various amphibians. Public access for walking offers extensive views.

Name: Wistow Wood
Description: Wistow Wood is an ancient woodland in Leicestershire, known for its mature oak and ash trees. It hosts a rich ground flora, with bluebells, wild garlic, and wood anemone. The site supports diverse birds and invertebrates, with public footpaths.

Name: Withdean Woods
Description: Withdean Woods is a small, semi-natural woodland in a Brighton valley. It provides a wildlife haven with a stream, ancient trees, and diverse flora and insects. The site is managed for conservation.

Name: Withdean Woods (Brighton Hillside Woodland)
Description: Withdean Woods is a sloping ancient woodland and designated Local Nature Reserve in Brighton. It features diverse trees, shrubs, and wildflowers, providing a rich habitat for wildlife. Managed to enhance biodiversity, it offers walking paths for visitors.

Name: Witley and Milford Commons (Heathland NNR)
Description: Witley and Milford Commons is a heathland National Nature Reserve in Surrey. It features diverse habitats including heathland, woodland, and ponds, supporting varied wildlife. Visitors can explore walking trails.

Name: Wivenhoe Woods
Description: Wivenhoe Woods is a mixed ancient woodland near Colchester, Essex. It features mature oak, hornbeam, and sweet chestnut trees. In spring, the woodland floor displays bluebells and wood anemones. Birdlife is abundant along the circular path.

Name: Wokefield Common
Description: Wokefield Common is a large nature reserve in Berkshire, featuring woodlands, heathland, and mires. It is a key habitat for all six native reptile species and diverse dragonflies. Much of the site holds SSSI status due to its valuable mosaic of habitats.

Name: Wolborough Fen
Description: Wolborough Fen is a small, spring-fed nature reserve near Newton Abbot, Devon. It safeguards a unique fen habitat, supporting rare plants and insects. Owned and managed by Devon Wildlife Trust, the site features a boardwalk and viewing platform for visitors.

Name: Wolla Bank Pit
Description: Wolla Bank Pit, near Chapel St Leonards, is a former sand and gravel pit, now a wetland habitat. The reserve features open water, reedbeds, and willow scrub, supporting diverse wildlife like birds and dragonflies.

Name: Wolston Lane (River Leam Hub)
Description: Wolston Lane nature reserve, near Coventry, is a small wetland habitat alongside the River Leam. It features reedbeds, ponds, and wet grassland, supporting species like water voles and otters. The site contributes to flood management and water quality.

Name: Wood Lane (Wetland & Bird Reserve)
Description: Wood Lane is a reserve created on the site of a former sand and gravel quarry. It now features open water, reedbeds, wet woodland, and grassland, attracting a diverse range of bird species. The site includes bird hides and accessible paths.

Name: Wood of Cree (Ancient Oakwood)
Description: RSPB Scotland's largest ancient woodland, Wood of Cree protects oak, birch, and alder trees. It features stunning displays of bluebells in spring. The reserve is a vital habitat for various birds, including pied flycatchers, redstarts, and wood warblers, offering rich wildlife watching opportunities.

Name: Woodah Farm (Wilder Woodah Hub)
Description: Wilder Woodah is a 40-hectare rewilding site near Newton Abbot, South Devon, acquired by Devon Wildlife Trust in 2022. It creates a mosaic of habitats for wildlife, including woodland and wetlands. The site also serves as a community hub for nature connection and rewilding education.

Name: Woodchester Park (Woodland & Lakes)
Description: Woodchester Park is a secluded valley in the Cotswolds featuring a Victorian Gothic mansion, five lakes, and extensive woodlands. The estate is managed for nature conservation, offering walking routes amidst diverse habitats and historical landscape features.

Name: Woodford Halse
Description: Woodford Halse is a nature reserve near the village of Woodford Halse in Northamptonshire. Once railway sidings, it now features grasslands, scrub, and ponds, providing habitats for various wildlife. Notable species include bee orchids and small copper butterflies.

Name: Woodford Halse (Disused Railway)
Description: Woodford Halse Disused Railway is a nature reserve in Northamptonshire, formed from two sections of old Great Central Railway embankment. It features species-rich grassland, scrub, and woodland, supporting diverse orchids and butterflies. The site offers a habitat for wildlife.

Name: Woodhall Spa Airfield
Description: Woodhall Spa Airfield is a wildlife haven on the former RAF bomber base. Acid grassland, heathland, and wetland support diverse flora and fauna, including rare invertebrates. Habitats are managed for biodiversity, preserving both natural heritage and wartime history.

Name: Woodham Fen
Description: Woodham Fen is a mosaic of saltmarsh, grazing marsh, and ditches near the River Crouch. It supports diverse wildlife, including breeding waders and migrating birds. The reserve helps protect and manage important coastal habitats.

Name: Woodham Fen (Rare Salt-marsh Plants Inland)
Description: Woodham Fen is a small, nationally important nature reserve. It hosts rare and unusual saltmarsh flora, uniquely found inland. Grazing marsh and ditches provide habitat for diverse invertebrates and birds.

Name: Woodhouse Washlands (Floodplain Mosaic)
Description: Woodhouse Washlands is a flood-relief scheme and wildlife reserve managed by Sheffield & Rotherham Wildlife Trust. It features a mosaic of habitats including wet woodland, reedbed, and species-rich grassland, supporting diverse bird and insect life.

Name: Woodland Trust - Chalkney Wood
Description: Chalkney Wood is a 55-hectare (136-acre) woodland near Earls Colne, Essex, owned and managed by the Woodland Trust. It features ancient woodland, coppiced sweet chestnut, and a mix of broadleaf and conifer trees. The wood provides habitats for diverse wildlife and offers walking trails.

Name: Woodland Trust - Joyden's Wood
Description: Joyden's Wood, near Bexley, Kent, is an ancient woodland managed by the Woodland Trust. It features diverse tree species and supports varied wildlife. Public footpaths offer walking and recreation. Historical earthworks are also present.

Name: Woodland Trust - Round Wood
Description: Round Wood is a 7.5-hectare maturing woodland in Doncaster, South Yorkshire. It features a diverse mix of native broadleaf trees and accessible paths. The wood provides a valuable habitat for local wildlife and offers public access.

Name: Woods Mill
Description: Woods Mill is a nature reserve near Henfield, West Sussex, and Sussex Wildlife Trust's headquarters. It features woodlands, meadows, and a lake, supporting diverse wildlife. The site offers environmental education and marked trails for visitors.

Name: Woods Mill (Wetland & Woodland Mosaic)
Description: Woods Mill is Sussex Wildlife Trust's headquarters and a Site of Special Scientific Interest near Henfield. It encompasses diverse wetland, woodland, and grassland habitats, offering nature trails and wildlife observation. The site supports educational activities and conservation efforts in Sussex.

Name: Woodside
Description: Woodside, near Harlow, is an ancient woodland managed by Essex Wildlife Trust. It features varied tree species, hazel coppice, and a pond, supporting diverse wildlife, including woodland birds and invertebrates. Public access is available for quiet enjoyment.

Name: Woodside (Farmland Rewilding)
Description: Woodside is a 40-hectare site near Shipley in Derbyshire. The Derbyshire Wildlife Trust is rewilding former farmland to create a mosaic of habitats. This work aims to boost biodiversity, establishing meadows, wetlands, and new woodlands to support diverse species.

Name: Woodside (Woolhope Dome)
Description: Woodside is an ancient woodland within the Woolhope Dome, featuring diverse habitats like old ponds, a stream, and traditional orchard areas. It supports dormice, woodpeckers, bluebells, and fungi. Managed by Herefordshire Wildlife Trust, the site is tranquil.

Name: Woodston Ponds (Peterborough)
Description: Woodston Ponds is a series of shallow ponds, seasonal pools, and wet grassland in Peterborough. It supports diverse wetland invertebrates, including rare species like dragonflies and damselflies. The site is also a good location for birdwatching, attracting ducks and wading birds.

Name: Woodthorpe Meadow
Description: Woodthorpe Meadow is an urban nature reserve in Nottingham, managed by Nottinghamshire Wildlife Trust. It protects grassland habitats, supporting wildflowers, insects, and other wildlife. It is important for local biodiversity.

Name: Woodwalton Fen
Description: Woodwalton Fen, a National Nature Reserve since 1984, is one of the UK's oldest, purchased in 1910. It is a vital fenland wilderness, home to rare wetland insects, plants, and the swallowtail butterfly. Habitats include fen, reedbed, woodland, and grassland.

Name: Woodwalton Fen (NNR)
Description: Woodwalton Fen is a National Nature Reserve within the Great Fen project. It features wet woodland, reedbed, and unimproved fen grassland, providing habitats for diverse wildlife. This internationally important site is actively managed to preserve its unique fenland ecosystems.

Name: Woodwalton Marsh
Description: Woodwalton Marsh is a wet grassland SSSI in Cambridgeshire, managed for breeding waders like lapwing and snipe. This involves water level control and summer cattle grazing. Adjacent to Woodwalton Fen, the site supports rare plants and diverse invertebrates.

Name: Woody Bay (Exmoor Coast)
Description: Woody Bay is a secluded cove on the Exmoor Coast in North Devon, featuring a pebble beach, cliffs, and a waterfall. Part of the National Trust's managed Exmoor coastal area, it offers dramatic scenery and access via coastal footpaths.

Name: Woolacombe & Morte Point
Description: Woolacombe & Morte Point is a dramatic headland on the North Devon coast with spectacular views. The area features rugged cliffs, sandy beaches, and coastal footpaths, managed for conservation and public access.

Name: Woolbeding Countryside (National Trust Recovery)
Description: Woolbeding Countryside spans 500 acres near Midhurst, West Sussex. It features ancient woodlands, wildflower meadows, and open parkland. Visitors explore waymarked trails, enjoying diverse wildlife and panoramic views across the Rother Valley.

Name: Woolbeding Countryside (River Rother & Parkland)
Description: Woolbeding Countryside, near Midhurst in West Sussex, comprises parkland and the River Rother. It features diverse habitats including water meadows, ancient woodland, and arable fields, offering walking routes and views of the South Downs. The National Trust manages the estate for conservation and public access.

Name: Woolsthorpe Manor (Newton's Orchard)
Description: Woolsthorpe Manor, Isaac Newton's birthplace, features the room where he formulated gravity and light theories. The famous apple tree, inspiring his gravity insight, remains in the orchard. This National Trust site is a Grade I listed building.

Name: Woorgreens (Heathland & Nightjar Hub)
Description: Woorgreens Nature Reserve, 104 hectares in Gloucestershire's Forest of Dean, features acid grassland, heather moorland, and a lake. It supports nightjars and dragonflies. Gloucestershire Wildlife Trust manages the site for habitat restoration.

Name: Wootton Wawen Marsh
Description: Wootton Wawen Marsh is a wetland site with a mix of habitats. It features fen, marsh, and grassland areas, supporting diverse plant and animal life. Reedbeds provide cover for birds, while ditches and pools host amphibians and insects. It is one of Warwickshire's best remaining fen meadows.

Name: Worcester & Birmingham Canal (Urban Biodiversity Spine)
Description: The Worcester & Birmingham Canal links Worcester and Birmingham, passing through urban and rural landscapes. A new project, the 'Urban Biodiversity Spine', focuses on enhancing biodiversity and community engagement within Worcester, working in partnership with local organisations.

Name: Wotton and Abinger Commons
Description: Wotton and Abinger Commons are a Site of Special Scientific Interest covering woodland and heathland in Surrey. Managed by Surrey Wildlife Trust, the commons offer diverse habitats including ancient woodland, open heath, and acidic grassland, supporting species such as woodlark and nightjar. Trails invite exploration.

Name: Wrabness
Description: Wrabness Nature Reserve on the Stour Estuary encompasses diverse habitats including saltmarsh, grazing marsh, and woodland. It supports a variety of wildlife, notably overwintering birds and migratory species. Public footpaths offer views.

Name: Wrabness Nature Reserve (Stour Estuary)
Description: Wrabness Nature Reserve, on the Stour Estuary, features saltmarsh, reedbeds, woodlands, and grasslands. It is a vital site for breeding and overwintering wildfowl and waders, offering walking trails and birdwatching.

Name: Wreay Woods
Description: Wreay Woods is an ancient woodland nature reserve, traversed by the River Petteril, near Carlisle. It features diverse tree species, vibrant wildflowers, and supports a rich variety of wildlife, including badgers, otters, kingfishers, and woodland birds.

Name: WWT London - Dragonfly Festival Hub
Description: WWT London Wetland Centre in Barnes hosts the Dragonfly Festival. Visitors explore its 105-acre urban wetland, engaging in pond dipping, guided walks, and crafts. The event highlights dragonfly life cycles and habitats, showcasing WWT's conservation work.

Name: WWT Welney Wetland Centre
Description: WWT Welney Wetland Centre is located in the Ouse Washes, a major wetland landscape in the Fens. The centre provides crucial habitat for thousands of migrating birds, including swans, geese, and ducks. Visitors can observe wildlife from hides and attend daily bird feeds.

Name: Wybunbury Moss (National Partner)
Description: Wybunbury Moss is a National Nature Reserve and a rare example of a raised bog in Cheshire. It features diverse Sphagnum mosses, rare plants, and invertebrates on a floating raft. Managed by Cheshire Wildlife Trust, natural processes are prioritised.

Name: Wye National Nature Reserve
Description: Wye National Nature Reserve is a chalk grassland site in Kent, recognised for its rich biodiversity. Managed by Kent Wildlife Trust, it features rare orchids and wildflowers, supporting various insect species. The reserve offers walking trails and panoramic views of the surrounding landscape, providing a habitat for wildlife.

Name: Wyelands (River Meadow Hub)
Description: Wyelands River Meadows is a 67-hectare Local Nature Reserve and SSSI near Tintern, managed by Gwent Wildlife Trust. It features floodplain pastures along the River Wye, home to otters, kingfishers, and water voles. Paths, gates and a footbridge are available.

Name: Wyeswood Common (Large-scale Restoration)
Description: Wyeswood Common spans 72 hectares of ancient woodland, meadows, and regenerating woodland in Monmouthshire. The site provides a mosaic habitat for diverse wildlife, including Dormice and rare Pearl-bordered Fritillary butterflies. Restoration efforts focus on heathland and wildflower meadows.

Name: Wykeham Forest (Raptor Viewpoint)
Description: Wykeham Forest, near Scarborough, is a mosaic of woodland and open space. It features the Raptor Viewpoint, offering views of birds of prey, and a diverse range of flora and fauna. The forest provides trails for walking and cycling.

Name: Wyming Brook (SSSI Ravine)
Description: Wyming Brook is a steep-sided ravine near Sheffield, a designated SSSI with ancient woodland and a fast-flowing brook. Managed by Sheffield & Rotherham Wildlife Trust, it provides important habitat for diverse wildlife, including woodland birds. The site offers public access and conservation.

Name: Wyre Forest
Description: Wyre Forest, managed by Forestry England, is a large ancient woodland in Worcestershire and Shropshire. It features diverse walking and cycling trails, a Go Ape course, and a visitor centre with a cafe and play area, offering accessible outdoor recreation.

Name: Wyre Forest (Forestry England Section)
Description: Wyre Forest is a large, ancient woodland managed by Forestry England, straddling the Worcestershire and Shropshire border. It is a National Nature Reserve, offering walking and cycling trails, a play area, and a visitor centre.

Name: Wyrley & Essington Canal (Local Nature Reserve)
Description: The Wyrley & Essington Canal is a Local Nature Reserve in the West Midlands, offering habitats for dragonflies, damselflies, and water voles. It provides routes for walking, cycling, and boating, forming part of the national cycle network.

Name: Wyver Lane (Belper Wetlands)
Description: Wyver Lane, a Derbyshire Wildlife Trust nature reserve near Belper, showcases diverse wetland habitats along the River Derwent. Ponds, reedbeds, and wet grassland support various bird species, otters, and amphibians. It is managed for wildlife conservation.

Name: Y Graig (Limestone Escarpment)
Description: Y Graig is a prominent limestone escarpment in North Wales, part of the Clwydian Range and Dee Valley AONB. It features ancient woodland and limestone grassland, supporting diverse flora, including rare plants, and fauna like peregrine falcons. Conservation efforts manage these important habitats.

Name: Yarley Fields
Description: Yarley Fields, a Somerset Wildlife Trust nature reserve, contains species-rich grassland with traditional hay meadows. It supports diverse flowering plants, grasses, and provides habitat for pollinators and wildlife, managed to enhance biodiversity. Public access available.

Name: Yarley Fields (Levels Overlook)
Description: Yarley Fields offers panoramic views across the Somerset Levels and Moors from the Mendip Hills. This species-rich grassland reserve links important habitats, supporting diverse wildflowers, insects, and birds. It provides a key habitat corridor.

Name: Yarty Moor
Description: Yarty Moor is a lowland heath and mire site in the Blackdown Hills AONB, designated an SSSI. It features diverse habitats including wet heath and sphagnum bog, supporting rare plants and invertebrates. Grazing helps maintain its open character.

Name: Yatleton Farm
Description: Yatleton Farm nature reserve in Herefordshire features ancient woodland, wildflower meadows, and traditional orchards. Managed for biodiversity, it supports dormice, woodland birds, and other local wildlife. The site provides a valuable habitat.

Name: Yearsley Woods (Howardian Hills)
Description: Yearsley Woods is a tranquil mixed woodland located near Yearsley village, within the Howardian Hills AONB. It offers numerous walking, cycling, and horse riding trails. Two free car parks provide access to the site, which is open all year round.

Name: Yeavering Bell (The Hill of the Goats)
Description: Yeavering Bell is a prominent Iron Age hill fort in the Cheviot Hills, Northumberland National Park. It is the largest hill fort in the region, featuring well-preserved ramparts and visible settlement remains. The site offers expansive views.

Name: Yellands Meadow
Description: Yellands Meadow is a valuable species-rich wet grassland and marshy area near Grassington. It is home to diverse wildflowers, including globe-flower and marsh violet, supporting various insect life. The site forms part of a wider network of reserves managed for conservation.

Name: Yetholm Loch
Description: Yetholm Loch is a shallow, nutrient-rich loch set amongst farmland in the Scottish Borders. It supports a variety of wetland plants, invertebrates and birds, including breeding waders and wintering wildfowl.

Name: Ynys-hir (The Dyfi Estuary Hub)
Description: Ynys-hir is an internationally important reserve on the Dyfi Estuary. It features saltmarsh, wet grassland, and woodland, providing habitat for migratory birds and other wildlife. Visitors can explore trails and hides.

Name: Ynyscedwyn (Tawe Valley Industrial Site)
Description: Ynyscedwyn, a former industrial site in the Tawe Valley, now features a mix of woodland, grassland, and wetland habitats. The reserve supports diverse wildlife, including otters and various bird species, alongside its industrial heritage.

Name: Yockletts Bank
Description: Yockletts Bank is an ancient woodland nature reserve in Kent, featuring mature broadleaf trees and diverse ground flora. It is renowned for its spring bluebells and orchids, providing a vital habitat for woodland birds and invertebrates. Kent Wildlife Trust manages the site, focusing on conservation to maintain its ecological value and biodiversity.

Name: Yockletts Bank (Orchid Wood)
Description: Yockletts Bank is a significant ancient woodland on the Kent Downs AONB, known for its diverse flora. The reserve features hazel coppice, oak standards, and a rich ground layer of orchids and other wildflowers, providing vital habitat.

Name: Yoesden (Butterflies & Beechwood)
Description: Yoesden nature reserve in the Chilterns features significant chalk grassland and ancient beech woodland. It is a critical site for rare butterflies, including the Duke of Burgundy, and supports diverse wildflowers and other insects, offering important habitat.

Name: Ystradfawr (Marshy Pasture & Peat)
Description: Ystradfawr is a nature reserve situated north of Llandovery, Carmarthenshire. It comprises a mosaic of habitats, including marshy grassland, purple moor-grass, and deep peat. The site supports diverse flora and fauna, characteristic of a healthy wetland environment, and is important for its rare plants and invertebrates.

Name: Zennor Head & Gurnard's Head
Description: Dramatic coastal stretch on the Penwith peninsula, from Zennor Head to Gurnard's Head. Features granite cliffs, coves, and ancient field systems. Home to a variety of wildlife, including seabirds and grey seals.
